topics.title: The RISC Design Philosophy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The RISC Design Philosophy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**RISC** stands for **Reduced Instruction Set Computer**. It is a CPU design philosophy based on the insight that a small, highly optimized set of instructions, combined with a load–store architecture and aggressive pipelining, yields superior performance over complex instruction sets. The term was coined by **David Patterson (Berkeley RISC project, 1980)** and **John Hennessy (Stanford MIPS project, 1981)**.

The formal KTU 2024 syllabus definition is:

> RISC is a microprocessor design philosophy that favors a **simple, small, and highly orthogonal instruction set**, where each instruction is of fixed length, executes in a **single clock cycle (ideally)**, and operates only on registers — memory access being performed exclusively via dedicated *load* and *store* instructions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Under the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, students must be able to:
> - **CO1**: Understand the architectural principles behind advanced microcontrollers (Bloom Level: *Understand / Remember*).
> - Distinguish RISC from CISC and identify the design rationale behind modern ARM-based controllers used in PECPT631.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Specialist vs. The Generalist"

Imagine a **Swiss Army Knife** vs. a **Surgeon's Scalpel**.

| Tool Type | Analogy | Instruction Set |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| **Swiss Army Knife** | Does everything (saw, scissors, file, knife) but each tool is mediocre. Many tools = many complex instructions. | **CISC** (Complex Instruction Set Computer) |
| **Surgeon's Scalpel** | One sharp, precise tool that performs one incision perfectly and very fast. | **RISC** |

A CISC processor (like the legacy Intel 8086) tried to provide "one instruction to do everything" — but the hardware had to decode complex multi-step operations internally, making each instruction slow. A RISC processor (like ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, AVR) keeps each instruction *stupid-simple* so the hardware can run it at maximum clock speed, then uses **pipelining** to overlap many of them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Modern microcontrollers — including the **ARM Cortex-M** family (STM32, LPC1768, ATSAM3X) used throughout PECPT631 — are all RISC machines. Understanding this philosophy is the foundation for understanding ARM registers, instruction timing, and assembly programming in later modules.

### 1.3 Critical Design Metrics in RISC

| Metric | Typical RISC Value | Significance |
|--------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Instruction Length** | **Fixed (32-bit in ARM/MIPS)** | Simplifies fetch and decode stages |
| **Registers in Register File** | **32 (or more, e.g., ARMv7-M = 13 GPR + special)** | Reduces memory traffic |
| **Addressing Modes** | **≤ 5** (register, immediate, base+offset, PC-relative, auto-index) | Simpler decoder hardware |
| **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** | **Target = 1.0** | Enables single-cycle execution |
| **Memory Access** | **Load/Store only** | Decouples computation from memory |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Foundational Principles of RISC

The RISC philosophy rests on **four pillars**. Each one solves a specific bottleneck identified in CISC designs of the 1970s:

**Pillar 1 — Simplicity of Instructions**
- Every instruction performs exactly one primitive operation (add, shift, compare, branch).
- No instruction combines a memory access with an ALU operation — that is delegated to two separate instructions.

**Pillar 2 — Fixed Instruction Length & Format**
- All instructions occupy the **same number of bits (typically 32 bits)**.
- This makes the **fetch and decode stages deterministic** → enables deep pipelining.
- Contrast: x86 instructions range from 1 to 15 bytes.

**Pillar 3 — Load/Store (Register-Register) Architecture**
- ALU operates **only on registers**.
- Only two instructions touch memory: `LDR` (load) and `STR` (store).
- This separation allows the CPU to overlap ALU work with memory fetches.

**Pillar 4 — Large Register File & Register Windowing**
- A typical RISC has **≥ 32 general-purpose registers**.
- Berkeley RISC pioneered **register windows** (overlapping sets of registers for procedure calls) to reduce saving/restoring on every call — this idea later influenced SPARC and is conceptually echoed in modern ARM's *banked registers* on context switch.

**Pillar 5 — Hardwired Control (Microcode-free)**
- Control unit is implemented as **combinational logic / FSM**, not microcode ROM.
- This eliminates the microcode-fetch delay, reducing CPI toward 1.

**Pillar 6 — Pipeline-First Design**
- RISC was designed *after* pipelining was understood. Therefore, every architectural decision (fixed length, register-register ops) was made to **facilitate pipelining**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar

- **Why fixed length?** A variable-length instruction means the decoder cannot know where the *next* instruction begins without parsing the current one — this kills pipelining. Fixed length → **instruction pointer advances by a constant offset every cycle**.
- **Why load/store only?** It allows the **memory stage** of the pipeline to be physically separate from the **execute stage**, doubling throughput.
- **Why hardwired control?** Microcode ROM access takes **1–3 extra cycles**, breaking the CPI = 1 goal.
- **Why so many registers?** Compilers can keep more variables in registers, avoiding slow stack-memory accesses (an insight proven by Patterson's measurements on early Berkeley RISC).

### 2.3 RISC vs. CISC — Engineering Comparison

| Design Aspect | RISC | CISC |
|---------------|------|------|
| Instruction set size | **Small (< 150)** | Large (200–1000+) |
| Instruction length | **Fixed** | Variable |
| Execution time | **1 cycle (most)** | Multi-cycle, variable |
| Memory access | **Load/Store only** | Any instruction can access memory |
| Control logic | **Hardwired** | Microcoded (often) |
| Register file | **Large (≥32)** | Small (8–16) |
| Code density | Lower (more instructions per task) | Higher |
| Pipeline friendliness | Excellent | Difficult |
| Examples | **ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, AVR, SPARC** | x86 (8086, 80286, early Intel) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Reality:** Since the 1990s, x86 (CISC) processors internally *translate* complex instructions into RISC-like micro-operations (μops) before execution. So the philosophical line has blurred — but the embedded/microcontroller world (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, RISC-V) is **purely RISC**, which is what KTU's PECPT631 emphasizes.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

These formulas are essential for performance-comparison problems in ESE.

| # | Formula / Relation | Notation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | $T_{CPU}$ = CPU execution time, $IC$ = instruction count, $CPI$ = cycles per instruction, $T_{clk}$ = clock period | Seconds |
| 2 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency in Hz | Million Instructions Per Second |
| 3 | $\text{MFLOPS} = \dfrac{\text{Number of FP ops}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}}$ | Floating-point ops rate | — |
| 4 | $\text{Amdahl's Law: } S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | $S$ = speedup, $f$ = fraction enhanced, $n$ = enhancement factor | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $f_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Clock frequency = inverse of period | Hz |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput (Pipelined)} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{1}$ | Ideal CPI after pipelining = 1 cycle/instruction | Instr/sec |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU numerical problems, when comparing RISC vs CISC, you are typically given $IC_{RISC}$, $CPI_{RISC}$, $IC_{CISC}$, $CPI_{CISC}$ and clock frequencies. You must compute $T_{CPU}$ for each using formula (1) and state which architecture is faster. **Always show units.** Examiners explicitly check unit cancellation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The RISC philosophy underpins virtually every modern embedded system:

1. **ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers** — STM32F4, NXP LPC1768, TM4C123 (used in KTU lab courses). All ARM Cortex-M cores are RISC, **Thumb-2 instruction set**.
2. **ESP32 / ESP8266** — Tensilica Xtensa LX6 RISC core, WiFi/BT SoCs.
3. **RISC-V** — Open-standard RISC ISA dominating IoT, AI accelerators, and education.
4. **AVR (Arduino)** — Classic 8-bit RISC microcontroller.
5. **Automotive ECUs, drones, robotics** — Use RISC cores for deterministic real-time response.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the CPU Performance Equation

The most fundamental relationship in computer architecture, derived from first principles:

**Step 1 — Define clock period.**
The clock oscillates with frequency $f_{clk}$ (in Hz). One clock period is:

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the number of clock cycles.**
For a program consisting of $IC$ (instruction count) instructions, with an average CPI (cycles per instruction) of $CPI$, the total clock cycles required is:

$$
\text{Total Clock Cycles} = IC \times CPI
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the clock period to get wall-clock time.**
Each cycle takes $T_{clk}$ seconds, so:

$$
T_{CPU} = (IC \times CPI) \times T_{clk}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $T_{clk}$ to express in terms of frequency.**

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clk}}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive MIPS (Millions of Instructions Per Second).**
MIPS measures the rate of instruction execution, normalized to millions:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{IC}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{IC}{(IC \times CPI / f_{clk}) \times 10^{6}} = \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A RISC processor runs at **100 MHz** and executes a program with **2 million instructions** at an average CPI of **1.2**. A CISC processor runs at **80 MHz** with **1.2 million instructions** at an average CPI of **3.5**. Compare their performance.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute CPU time for RISC.**

$$
T_{RISC} = \frac{IC_{RISC} \times CPI_{RISC}}{f_{clk,RISC}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{6} \times 1.2}{100 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{RISC} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{6}}{1.0 \times 10^{8}} = 0.024 \text{ seconds} = 24 \text{ ms}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute CPU time for CISC.**

$$
T_{CISC} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 3.5}{80 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{4.2 \times 10^{6}}{8.0 \times 10^{7}} = 0.0525 \text{ seconds} = 52.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Compute speedup ratio.**

$$
S_{RISC/CISC} = \frac{T_{CISC}}{T_{RISC}} = \frac{52.5}{24} = 2.1875
$$

**Conclusion:** The RISC processor is **~2.19× faster** for this workload, even though it executes more total instructions (because each one is faster).

### 3.3 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (for Enhancements)

When optimizing a fraction $f$ of a program by a factor of $n$:

**Step 1:** Original execution time = $T$.

**Step 2:** Enhanced execution time = $(1-f) \cdot T + \dfrac{f}{n} \cdot T$.

**Step 3:** Speedup $S$ is the ratio:

$$
S = \frac{T}{(1-f) \cdot T + \dfrac{f}{n} \cdot T} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}
$$

**Step 4:** As $n \to \infty$ (ideal enhancement), the limit is:

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1-f}
$$

> This shows that **no matter how fast you make the optimized fraction, the unoptimized part bottlenecks total speedup** — a core reason RISC designs kept *all* instructions simple (high $f$) rather than only making a few faster.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping RISC Principles to ARM Cortex-M (PECPT631 Target)

| RISC Pillar | Implementation in ARM Cortex-M (Thumb-2) | Engineering Benefit |
|-------------|-------------------------------------------|---------------------|
| Fixed instruction length | Thumb-2: 16-bit & 32-bit mix, but each instruction's *first decode* is deterministic | Predictable fetch |
| Load/Store only | `LDR`, `STR`, `LDRB`, `STRH` — only ops touching memory | Clean pipeline separation |
| Large register file | 13 general-purpose (R0–R12) + SP, LR, PC | Compiler optimization headroom |
| Hardwired control | FSM-based decoder (no microcode in M-profile) | Low gate count, low power |
| Single-cycle execution | Most ALU ops complete in 1 cycle at pipeline stage | Deterministic timing for RTOS |
| Pipeline-first | 3-stage (M0) / 5-stage (M3/M4) pipeline | High throughput |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RISC Design Philosophy — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observation 1970s: CISC designs had complex instructions] --> B[Insight by Patterson and Hennessy 1980 1981]
    B --> C[RISC Design Philosophy]
    C --> D[Fixed Length Instructions]
    C --> E[Load Store Architecture]
    C --> F[Large Register File]
    C --> G[Hardwired Control]
    C --> H[Single Cycle Execution Target]
    D --> I[Simplified Fetch and Decode]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Deep Pipelining Becomes Possible]
    J --> K[High Instruction Throughput]
    K --> L[Modern RISC Microcontrollers: ARM Cortex M, RISC V, AVR]
```

### 4.2 RISC Pipeline — Instruction Flow (5-Stage)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IF[IF Instruction Fetch] --> ID[ID Instruction Decode and Register Fetch]
    ID --> EX[EX Execute ALU Operation]
    EX --> MEM[MEM Memory Access Load or Store]
    MEM --> WB[WB Write Back to Register File]
    IF -.overlaps.-> ID
    ID -.overlaps.-> EX
    EX -.overlaps.-> MEM
    MEM -.overlaps.-> WB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed overlap arrows represent **pipelined concurrency** — while instruction $i$ is in MEM stage, instruction $i+1$ is in EX, instruction $i+2$ is in ID, and so on. This is exactly the **CPI = 1 throughput** promised by the RISC philosophy. KTU examiners frequently test this concept with a diagram and ask the student to label overlapping instructions.

### 4.3 RISC vs. CISC Decoder Complexity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph RISC_PATH [RISC Processor]
    R1[Fixed 32 bit instruction] --> R2[Hardwired Combinational Decoder]
    R2 --> R3[Direct to ALU or Memory Unit]
    end

    subgraph CISC_PATH [CISC Processor]
    C1[Variable length 1 to 15 byte instruction] --> C2[Microcode ROM Lookup]
    C2 --> C3[Multi cycle Micro routine Execution]
    C3 --> R3
    end

    style RISC_PATH fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#1890ff
    style CISC_PATH fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#fa8c16
```

### 4.4 PECPT631 Context — ARM Cortex-M Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PC[Program Counter PC] --> FETCH[Fetch Unit Thumb 2]
    FETCH --> DEC[Hardwired Decoder]
    DEC --> REGFILE[Register File R0 to R12]
    DEC --> ALU[ALU Pipeline Stage]
    REGFILE --> ALU
    ALU --> MEMIF[Load Store Interface]
    MEMIF --> MEMBUS[AHB or APB Bus to Memory]
    ALU --> WB[Write Back Stage]
    WB --> REGFILE
    ALU --> BR[Branch Unit updates PC]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. Define RISC. List any four characteristics of a RISC processor.** *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

RISC stands for **Reduced Instruction Set Computer**. It is a microprocessor architecture philosophy that uses a small, highly optimized set of instructions, where each instruction is of fixed length and executes in a single clock cycle.

**Four Characteristics:**
1. **Fixed-length instructions** (e.g., 32-bit in ARM) — simplifies pipelining.
2. **Load/Store architecture** — only `LDR` and `STR` access memory; all ALU operations use registers only.
3. **Large register file** — typically 32 or more general-purpose registers to reduce memory accesses.
4. **Hardwired (microcode-free) control unit** — combinational logic decoder reduces CPI toward 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "RISC means fewer instructions than CISC" — this is *not* strictly true. RISC means the instruction set is *reduced in complexity*, not necessarily in count. ARM has thousands of instructions in Thumb-2; CISC x86 has fewer than ARM in some metrics. Use the term **"reduced complexity"** to be safe.

---

**Q2. Compare RISC and CISC architectures on any three parameters.** *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RISC | CISC |
|-----------|------|------|
| **Instruction length** | Fixed (typically 32 bits) | Variable (1–15 bytes in x86) |
| **Memory access** | Only Load/Store instructions can access memory | Any instruction may access memory |
| **CPI** | Target ≈ 1 cycle per instruction | Multi-cycle, varies per instruction |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** In 3-mark "compare" questions, **always use a table** and mention *one-sentence justification* for each row. A bare table without justification loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Module-1 carries one full question (14 marks) with internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q(a)** Explain the **RISC design philosophy** in detail. Discuss the key principles that distinguish RISC from CISC. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) design philosophy emerged in the early 1980s from research at **UC Berkeley (Patterson)** and **Stanford (Hennessy)**. It is built on the central premise that **architectural simplicity enables higher performance** through deeper pipelining and faster clock cycles.

**Key Principles:**

1. **Simple, fixed-format instructions** — Every instruction is 32 bits (in classic ARM/MIPS). The opcode, source, and destination fields occupy fixed bit positions. This eliminates variable-length parsing and allows the decoder to operate in a single combinational stage.

2. **Load/Store architecture** — Arithmetic and logical instructions operate *only* on registers. Memory is accessed exclusively through dedicated `LDR` (load register) and `STR` (store register) instructions. This decouples ALU execution from memory latency.

3. **Large register file (≥32 registers)** — A wide register file reduces memory traffic by allowing compilers to keep the most-used variables in registers. Berkeley RISC introduced *register windows* — overlapping register sets that automatically save/restore on procedure calls, eliminating push/pop overhead.

4. **Hardwired control** — The control unit is implemented as a finite-state machine with combinational logic, not as a microcoded ROM. Microcode introduces 1–3 extra cycles of indirection; removing it is essential to achieve CPI = 1.

5. **Pipeline-first design** — RISC was architected *with* pipelining in mind, not adapted to it. Each principle above exists to make pipelining feasible: fixed length → predictable fetch; load/store → separated MEM stage; simple ops → single-cycle EX stage.

6. **Orthogonal instruction set** — Any operation can use any register as operand, simplifying compiler register allocation.

**Distinction from CISC:** CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) attempted to reduce the *semantic gap* between high-level languages and machine code by providing powerful multi-cycle instructions (e.g., string copy, loop control). The hardware cost of decoding and microcoding these made CPI high (often 4–10 cycles per instruction). RISC flips the design priority — keep instructions simple, let the *compiler* and *pipeline* do the work.

---

**Q(b)** A **RISC processor** runs at **200 MHz** clock and has an average **CPI of 1.5** for a benchmark of **10 million instructions**. A **CISC processor** runs at **100 MHz** with an average **CPI of 4.0** for the same benchmark but uses only **4 million instructions** (because each CISC instruction does more work). Compute the CPU execution time for both and determine which is faster. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute CPU time for the RISC processor.**

Using the fundamental performance equation:

$$
T_{CPU,RISC} = \frac{IC_{RISC} \times CPI_{RISC}}{f_{clk,RISC}} = \frac{10 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5}{200 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{CPU,RISC} = \frac{15 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.075 \text{ seconds} = 75 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CPU time for the CISC processor.**

$$
T_{CPU,CISC} = \frac{IC_{CISC} \times CPI_{CISC}}{f_{clk,CISC}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6} \times 4.0}{100 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{CPU,CISC} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^{8}} = 0.16 \text{ seconds} = 160 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute speedup ratio of RISC over CISC.**

$$
S = \frac{T_{CPU,CISC}}{T_{CPU,RISC}} = \frac{160}{75} \approx 2.133
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
The **RISC processor is approximately 2.13× faster** for this benchmark, despite executing **2.5× more instructions**, because its much higher clock frequency and lower CPI more than compensate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** A common student error is to divide instructions by clock frequency directly (treating CPI = 1 implicitly) and skipping the multiplication. KTU examiners **specifically deduct 2 marks** for this mistake. Always write the full formula $T = IC \times CPI / f$ explicitly. Also: do not forget to state **units** — writing "0.075" instead of "0.075 seconds" loses a mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Adapted]`

**Q(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **5-stage RISC pipeline**. Show how overlapping of instructions achieves a throughput of CPI = 1. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The classic RISC pipeline consists of **five stages**, each performing a specific sub-task in one clock cycle:

| Stage | Full Name | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| **IF** | Instruction Fetch | Read next instruction from memory at address in PC |
| **ID** | Instruction Decode | Decode opcode, read source registers from register file |
| **EX** | Execute | ALU operation or address calculation |
| **MEM** | Memory Access | Read (for `LDR`) or write (for `STR`) memory |
| **WB** | Write Back | Write ALU result or loaded value back to destination register |

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cycle1 [Cycle 1]
    A1[IF: I1]
    end
    subgraph Cycle2 [Cycle 2]
    A2[ID: I1]
    B1[IF: I2]
    end
    subgraph Cycle3 [Cycle 3]
    A3[EX: I1]
    B2[ID: I2]
    C1[IF: I3]
    end
    subgraph Cycle4 [Cycle 4]
    A4[MEM: I1]
    B3[EX: I2]
    C2[ID: I3]
    D1[IF: I4]
    end
    subgraph Cycle5 [Cycle 5]
    A5[WB: I1]
    B4[MEM: I2]
    C3[EX: I3]
    D2[ID: I4]
    E1[IF: I5]
    end
```

**Explanation of Overlap:**
- In **Cycle 1**, instruction I1 enters the IF stage.
- In **Cycle 2**, while I1 moves to ID, instruction I2 enters IF — both happen in the same clock cycle.
- By **Cycle 5**, the pipeline is *full*: I1 is in WB, I2 in MEM, I3 in EX, I4 in ID, I5 in IF.
- After this point, **one instruction completes (WB stage) every clock cycle** → throughput = 1 instruction/cycle → effective CPI = 1.
- Without pipelining, each instruction would take 5 cycles sequentially, giving an effective CPI = 5.

**Why RISC Enables This:**
Pipelining requires that each stage's operation be independent and complete in one cycle. The RISC design choices — **fixed instruction length, register-register ALU ops, simple addressing modes** — are exactly what allow this. A CISC instruction with variable length and embedded memory access cannot be cleanly split into uniform pipeline stages.

---

**Q(b)** A program consists of **80% ALU operations** (CPI = 1) and **20% load/store operations** (CPI = 2). The clock frequency is **50 MHz**. Find the average CPI, MIPS rating, and total execution time for **20 million instructions**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute the weighted average CPI.**

$$
CPI_{avg} = (0.80 \times 1) + (0.20 \times 2) = 0.80 + 0.40 = 1.20
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MIPS rating using $f_{clk}$ in Hz.**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{6}}{1.20 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{50}{1.20} \approx 41.67 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total execution time.**

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{IC \times CPI_{avg}}{f_{clk}} = \frac{20 \times 10^{6} \times 1.20}{50 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{24 \times 10^{6}}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 0.48 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification.**
Instruction mix breakdown:
- ALU instructions: $0.80 \times 20 \times 10^6 = 16 \times 10^6$ instructions × 1 cycle each = $16 \times 10^6$ cycles.
- Load/Store instructions: $0.20 \times 20 \times 10^6 = 4 \times 10^6$ instructions × 2 cycles each = $8 \times 10^6$ cycles.
- Total cycles = $24 \times 10^6$, giving total time = $24 \times 10^6 / (50 \times 10^6) = 0.48$ s ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse *MIPS* (instruction throughput) with *MHz* (clock frequency). MIPS = clock frequency / CPI. If the question gives $f_{clk} = 50$ MHz but you forget to multiply CPI by $10^6$ in the denominator, your MIPS answer will be **wrong by six orders of magnitude**. Always carry units through.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RISC = Reduced Instruction Set *Computer***, emphasizing simplicity, not necessarily low instruction count.
- **Five design pillars:** fixed-length instructions, load/store architecture, large register file, hardwired control, pipelining-first design.
- **Origin:** Patterson (Berkeley RISC I, 1980) and Hennessy (Stanford MIPS, 1981).
- **Performance equation (must memorize):** $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI / f_{clk}$
- **MIPS formula:** $\text{MIPS} = f_{clk} / (CPI \times 10^6)$
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/n]$ — bottlenecks dominate, justify RISC keeping *all* instructions simple.
- **ARM Cortex-M** (used throughout PECPT631) is a **modern RISC implementation** with Thumb-2 instruction set, hardwired decoder, and 3- or 5-stage pipeline.
- **Modern hybrid note:** Today's x86 CPUs internally translate CISC instructions into RISC-like μops — but the embedded/microcontroller world (ARM, AVR, RISC-V) remains *purely* RISC.
- **Exam traps to avoid:**
  - Don't confuse "reduced instruction set" with "few instructions" — say "reduced complexity."
  - Always show the full $T_{CPU}$ formula; don't skip CPI.
  - Use tables for compare-type questions; one-sentence justification per row.
  - Label all Mermaid diagrams with stage names, not just cycles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Design Philosophy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ARM Design Philosophy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ARM Design Philosophy** refers to the foundational architectural principles and engineering trade-offs adopted by ARM Holdings (originally **Acorn RISC Machine**, later **Advanced RISC Machine**) when designing the ARM processor family. It is a **Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC)** philosophy that deliberately trades complex hardware-decoded instructions for a small, highly optimized, and frequently executed instruction set, combined with a large register file, load-store architecture, and fixed-length instructions to maximize **instructions per cycle (IPC)**, minimize power dissipation, and enable superior **performance-per-watt** metrics for embedded and mobile systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *ARM Design Philosophy* encompasses the principles of RISC architecture, the load-store model, fixed instruction encoding, large uniform register banks, conditional execution, and the strategic use of mode-switching (ARM/Thumb/Thumb-2) to balance performance, power, and code density in embedded applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swiss Army Knife vs. Specialist Toolkit"

Imagine two kitchens preparing 10,000 identical sandwiches:

- **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** is like a chef with one giant multi-function "sandwich-master" machine. It can make a sandwich in *one* complex operation, but the machine is heavy, expensive, slow to start, and needs maintenance. If one part breaks, the whole workflow halts.
- **ARM (RISC)** is like a chef with five lightweight, razor-sharp knives. Each knife does *one* thing — slice bread, spread butter, add filling, etc. — but it does it **very fast** and **very efficiently**. The chef can finish 10,000 sandwiches using just 3–4 simple, pipelined steps, with far less energy per sandwich.

This is the heart of ARM's design: **small, fast, simple instructions executed in a deeply pipelined fashion**, rather than a few large, complex instructions.

## 1.3 The Seven Pillars of ARM's Design Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven Foundational Principles (MUST memorize for KTU exams):**
> 
> 1. **Large Uniform Register File** — 16 user-visible registers (in user mode) visible to all instructions, eliminating register saving/restoring overhead.
> 2. **Load-Store Architecture** — Only `LDR`/`STR` instructions access memory; all data processing operates on registers.
> 3. **Fixed Instruction Width** — 32-bit ARM instructions, 16-bit Thumb instructions (and mixed Thumb-2) for predictable decode and pipelining.
> 4. **Simple, Orthogonal Addressing Modes** — Few modes, applied consistently across instructions.
> 5. **Conditional Execution** — Almost every instruction can be conditionally executed, eliminating most short branches.
> 6. **Hardware Implementation of Shifters** — Barrel shifter integrated into the data path; shifts are free (zero cycles).
> 7. **Mode-Switching for Density vs. Performance** — Thumb (16-bit) reduces code size; ARM (32-bit) maximizes performance.

## 1.4 Key Physical & Architectural Metrics

| Metric | Typical ARM Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Register file size** | **16 × 32-bit** (user mode) | Reduces stack traffic |
| **Instruction width** | **32-bit (ARM), 16-bit (Thumb)** | Fixed for easy decode |
| **CPI (ideal)** | **~1.0** | Single-cycle execution target |
| **Pipeline depth** | **3 to 13 stages** (varies by core) | Higher clock frequency |
| **Power per instruction** | **< 0.5 mW/MIPS** (typical) | Mobile-grade efficiency |
| **Conditional codes (flags)** | **4 (N, Z, C, V)** | Every ALU op updates flags |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Large Uniform Register File

In traditional accumulator-based or CISC designs, the CPU has only a few internal registers (e.g., 8086 had 4 general-purpose registers `AX, BX, CX, DX`). Every procedure call required **pushing many registers onto the stack** — a memory operation that is slow and power-hungry.

ARM's solution: **16 general-purpose 32-bit registers** (`R0`–`R15`) plus a **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)**. This means:
- The compiler can keep more variables in registers.
- Procedure-call overhead is minimized.
- Register allocation by compilers is dramatically improved (fewer spills).

> **Engineering Reality:** The "16 register" choice was not arbitrary. It fits in a **5-bit field** within a 32-bit instruction, leaving ample encoding space for two source operands, one destination, the opcode, and condition codes.

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Load-Store Architecture

In CISC, an instruction like `ADD AX, [BX+SI]` performs **memory fetch + ALU operation** in one cycle. This is elegant in code, but the CPU must contain complex microcode to sequence memory and ALU operations.

ARM insists on **separation of concerns**:

$$
\underbrace{\text{LDR } R_0, [R_1]}_{\text{Memory access stage}} \quad \rightarrow \quad \underbrace{\text{ADD } R_2, R_3, R_0}_{\text{ALU operates on registers only}}
$$

**Why this is faster overall:**
- ALU instructions can execute in a **single cycle** because the data path is register-to-register.
- Memory access is decoupled and can use **separate load/store units** in parallel with ALU execution (Harvard-style separation).
- Pipelining is trivial: every instruction is a predictable shape.

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Fixed Instruction Width

All ARM instructions are **32 bits wide** (in ARM state). This has profound hardware benefits:

$$
\text{Instruction fetch bandwidth} = \text{instruction width} \times \text{fetch rate}
$$

With fixed width, the **fetch unit can pre-fetch** the next instruction while decoding the current one — a prerequisite for **deep pipelining**. The address of the next instruction is always `PC + 4`, eliminating the variable-length decode complexity of x86.

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Conditional Execution

Almost every ARM instruction has a **4-bit condition code field** in bits `[31:28]`. The `CPSR` flags determine whether the instruction actually executes:

| Code | Mnemonic | Meaning | Flag Test |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| `0000` | `EQ` | Equal | Z = 1 |
| `0001` | `NE` | Not equal | Z = 0 |
| `0010` | `CS/HS` | Carry set | C = 1 |
| `0011` | `CC/LO` | Carry clear | C = 0 |
| `0100` | `MI` | Minus | N = 1 |
| `0101` | `PL` | Plus | N = 0 |
| `0110` | `VS` | Overflow | V = 1 |
| `0111` | `VC` | No overflow | V = 0 |
| `1000` | `HI` | Higher (unsigned) | C=1 & Z=0 |
| `1001` | `LS` | Lower or same | C=0 or Z=1 |
| `1010` | `GE` | Greater or equal (signed) | N = V |
| `1011` | `LT` | Less than (signed) | N ≠ V |
| `1100` | `GT` | Greater than | Z=0 & N=V |
| `1101` | `LE` | Less or equal | Z=1 or N≠V |
| `1110` | `AL` | Always (default) | — |
| `1111` | `NV` | Never (deprecated) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trivia:** Conditional execution eliminates an entire **branch instruction** (which would normally be needed after a compare). This saves 1 fetch + 1 decode + 1 execution cycle per short forward branch — a massive power saving in tight loops.

## 2.5 Pillar 5 — Embedded Barrel Shifter (Hardware Shifters)

Every data-processing instruction can specify a **barrel shift** on its second operand **for free** (zero extra cycles). Example:

```asm
ADD   R0, R1, R2, LSL #4      ; R0 = R1 + (R2 << 4)   — shift is FREE
```

The barrel shifter sits on the data path between registers and ALU, executing in **parallel** with the ALU's own logic. This eliminates separate `SHL`, `SHR` instructions and converts them into **operands** of existing instructions.

## 2.6 Pillar 6 — Mode Switching (ARM ↔ Thumb)

ARM is one of the few architectures with **two instruction encodings** switchable at runtime via the `BX`/`BLX` branch instructions:

| Mode | Instruction Width | Code Density | Performance | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ARM** | 32-bit | Lower | Higher (full register access) | Performance-critical code, ISR prologs |
| **Thumb** | 16-bit | ~65% smaller | ~10–15% slower | Tight loops, size-critical firmware |
| **Thumb-2** | 16/32-bit mixed | Best of both | Near-ARM | Modern ARM Cortex-M devices |

> **Why this matters:** Embedded systems are often **memory-bandwidth and flash-size limited**. Thumb mode cuts code size by ~35%, which directly reduces flash cost and power (fewer memory fetches).

## 2.7 Pillar 7 — Reduced Complexity for Low Power

By minimizing the number of instruction formats, the number of addressing modes, and the number of special registers, the **transistor count** of the decode unit is drastically reduced. This directly translates to:
- **Lower dynamic power** ($P = \alpha C V^2 f$)
- **Lower static leakage** (fewer transistors)
- **Higher achievable clock frequency** (shorter critical path)

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for ARM Design Philosophy**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{CPU}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | Basic CPU performance equation |
| 2 | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \, C \, V^2 \, f$ | Dynamic power (justifies RISC's low power) |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/k}$ | **Amdahl's Law** — speedup limit |
| 4 | $E_{\text{per-op}} = C_{\text{eff}} \cdot V^2$ | Energy per instruction |
| 5 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}$ | Million instructions per second |
| 6 | $\text{CodeSize}_{\text{Thumb}} \approx 0.65 \times \text{CodeSize}_{\text{ARM}}$ | Empirical Thumb density ratio |
| 7 | $\text{IPC} = \dfrac{N_{\text{inst}}}{N_{\text{cycles}}}$ | Inverse of CPI |
| 8 | $\text{Density} = \dfrac{\text{Program functionality}}{\text{Code size in bytes}}$ | Definition of code density |
| 9 | $\text{Pipeline throughput} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{clk}}}$ | Ideal (1 instr/cycle) |
| 10 | $N_{\text{reg-bits}} = \log_2(16) = 4$ | 5-bit fields encode 32 registers |

> **Note on Notation:** All `|x|` absolute values in exam work should be written as `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` to avoid LaTeX rendering issues.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why 16 Registers? (Encoding Constraint Analysis)

ARM is a 32-bit RISC machine. The instruction layout for a data-processing instruction is:

$$
\underbrace{\text{Cond}}_{4 \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{000}_{\text{opcode type}} \;\; \underbrace{I}_{\text{imm flag}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{4 \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{S}_{1 \text{ bit}} \;\; \underbrace{Rn}_{4 \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{Rd}_{4 \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Operand2}}_{12 \text{ bits}}
$$

**Step-by-step:**

- Total instruction width = **32 bits**.
- Bits consumed by condition code = **4**.
- Bits consumed by opcode + flags = **9** (typical).
- Bits consumed by `Rn` (source 1) = **4**.
- Bits consumed by `Rd` (destination) = **4**.
- Remaining for `Operand2` = `32 − 4 − 9 − 4 − 4 = 11 bits`.

The 12-bit `Operand2` field encodes a source register `Rm` (4 bits) **and** a shift specification (8 bits). This requires `Rm` to be selectable from **16 registers** (2⁴ = 16). 

**Conclusion:** The choice of 16 user registers is the **maximum** that fits the operand encoding while still allowing flexible shift specifications. This is a textbook example of design constraints driving architectural decisions.

## 3.2 Derivation: Performance Comparison — ARM vs. CISC (Hypothetical Loop)

Consider the canonical C statement:

```c
for (i = 0; i < 100; i++) sum += a[i];
```

Assume:
- CISC machine: each iteration takes **3 complex instructions × 4 cycles avg = 12 cycles**.
- ARM machine: each iteration takes **5 simple instructions × 1 cycle avg = 5 cycles** (ideal).

**Step 1: Total cycles for 100 iterations (CISC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{CISC}} &= 100 \times 3 \times 4 \\
&= 1200 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total cycles for 100 iterations (ARM, ARM-mode):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ARM}} &= 100 \times 5 \times 1 \\
&= 500 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total cycles for 100 iterations (ARM, Thumb-mode):**

Thumb CPI is slightly higher (1.15 typical) due to extra shifts/masks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Thumb}} &= 100 \times 5 \times 1.15 \\
&= 575 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Speedup of ARM over CISC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{ARM/CISC}} &= \frac{1200}{500} = 2.4\times
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: But considering code size:**

If each CISC instruction is 4 bytes and each ARM instruction is 4 bytes but each Thumb is 2 bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size}_{\text{ARM}} &= 5 \times 100 \times 4 = 2000 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Size}_{\text{Thumb}} &= 5 \times 100 \times 2 = 1000 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Size}_{\text{CISC}} &= 3 \times 100 \times 4 = 1200 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Performance-per-byte metric (energy proxy):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Eff}_{\text{ARM}} &= \frac{2.4}{1.0} = 2.4 \\
\text{Eff}_{\text{Thumb}} &= \frac{1200/575}{1000/2000} = \frac{2.087}{0.5} = 4.17
\end{aligned}
$$

**Thumb wins on performance-per-byte**, which is *exactly* why mobile/embedded ARM SoCs use Thumb-2.

## 3.3 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to Conditional Execution

Suppose **30%** of a program's instructions are short forward branches that ARM converts to conditional instructions (saving the branch + 1 branch-mispredict cycle).

**Step 1: Fraction enhanced (f) = 0.30, speedup of enhancement (k) = ∞ (we save the whole branch).**

**Step 2: Theoretical overall speedup (assuming branches are free):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{max}} &= \frac{1}{(1-f) + f/k} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.30/\infty} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.70} \approx 1.43
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Realistic case (k = 2, i.e., branch costs 1 cycle we save):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{real}} &= \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.30/2} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.15} = \frac{1}{0.85} \approx 1.18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation:**

Even with conservative estimates, ARM's conditional execution philosophy delivers an **18–43% raw speedup** on branch-heavy code — and saves **power** by reducing fetch traffic.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ARM vs. CISC

| Parameter | ARM (RISC) | CISC (e.g., x86) | Design Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Instructions per program | More (≈ 1.3–1.5×) | Fewer | RISC pays in count |
| Cycles per instruction | ≈ 1.0 (pipelined) | 2–15 | RISC wins on CPI |
| Instruction width | Fixed 32 bits | Variable 1–15 bytes | RISC simplifies fetch |
| Memory accesses | Load/Store only | Any instruction | RISC decouples |
| Register count | 16 (or 31 in privileged) | 8 (x86-32) | RISC reduces spills |
| Decoder complexity | Hardwired (small) | Microcode (large) | RISC = low power |
| Code density | Lower (ARM) / Higher (Thumb) | High | Thumb addresses this |
| Power per MHz | ~0.5 mW | ~5–10 mW | **RISC dominates mobile** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ARM Philosophy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Code] --> B{Compiler Selection}
    B -->|Performance Critical| C[ARM 32-bit Mode]
    B -->|Size Critical| D[Thumb 16-bit Mode]
    B -->|Mixed| E[Thumb-2 16/32-bit]

    C --> F[Fixed 32-bit Decode]
    D --> G[Fixed 16-bit Decode]
    E --> H[Variable Decode Unit]

    F --> I[Register File 16 x 32-bit]
    G --> I
    H --> I

    I --> J[Barrel Shifter - 0 cycle]
    J --> K[ALU with Flag Update]
    K --> L[CPSR - Condition Flags]
    L --> M{Condition Code Check bits 31-28}
    M -->|True| N[Writeback to Register]
    M -->|False| O[Instruction NOPed - No power spent]

    P[Load Store Unit] --> I
    I --> P
    P --> Q[Memory Bus]

    R[Pipeline Fetch Unit] --> F
    R --> G
    R --> H

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style I fill:#fff4e1
    style J fill:#ffe1f5
    style K fill:#e1ffe1
    style M fill:#f5e1ff
    style O fill:#ffe1e1
```

## 4.2 RISC Design Philosophy Tree (Conceptual Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[ARM Design Philosophy RISC Foundation]

    Root --> P1[Pillar 1: Load Store Architecture]
    Root --> P2[Pillar 2: Fixed Width Instructions]
    Root --> P3[Pillar 3: Large Register File 16 x 32]
    Root --> P4[Pillar 4: Conditional Execution]
    Root --> P5[Pillar 5: Hardware Barrel Shifter]
    Root --> P6[Pillar 6: Orthogonal Addressing]
    Root --> P7[Pillar 7: Mode Switching ARM/Thumb]

    P1 --> B1[Benefit: Single Cycle ALU ops]
    P1 --> B2[Benefit: Easy Pipelining]

    P2 --> C1[Benefit: Predictable Fetch]
    P2 --> C2[Benefit: Deep Pipelines Possible]

    P3 --> D1[Benefit: Reduced Stack Traffic]
    P3 --> D2[Benefit: Compiler Friendly]

    P4 --> E1[Benefit: Branchless Short Code]
    P4 --> E2[Benefit: Saves Power on Fetch]

    P5 --> F1[Benefit: Shifts Free Zero Cycle]
    P5 --> F2[Benefit: Operand not Instruction]

    P6 --> G1[Benefit: Simple Decoder]
    P6 --> G2[Benefit: Small Silicon Area]

    P7 --> H1[Benefit: Density vs Speed Tradeoff]
    P7 --> H2[Benefit: Embedded Flash Savings]

    style Root fill:#1e90ff,color:#ffffff
    style P1 fill:#ffe4b5
    style P2 fill:#ffe4b5
    style P3 fill:#ffe4b5
    style P4 fill:#ffe4b5
    style P5 fill:#ffe4b5
    style P6 fill:#ffe4b5
    style P7 fill:#ffe4b5
    style B1 fill:#e0f8e0
    style C1 fill:#e0f8e0
    style D1 fill:#e0f8e0
    style E1 fill:#e0f8e0
    style F1 fill:#e0f8e0
    style G1 fill:#e0f8e0
    style H1 fill:#e0f8e0
```

## 4.3 Instruction Pipeline Flow Showing RISC Regularity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Instr 1: LDR R0 R1] --> F1[Fetch]
    I2[Instr 2: ADD R2 R3 R0] --> F2[Fetch]
    I3[Instr 3: STR R4 R0] --> F3[Fetch]
    I4[Instr 4: CMP R5 R6] --> F4[Fetch]

    F1 --> D1[Decode]
    F2 --> D2[Decode]
    F3 --> D3[Decode]
    F4 --> D4[Decode]

    D1 --> E1[Execute - Memory]
    D2 --> E2[Execute - ALU]
    D3 --> E3[Execute - Memory]
    D4 --> E4[Execute - ALU + Flags]

    E1 --> W1[Writeback]
    E2 --> W2[Writeback]
    E3 --> W3[Writeback]
    E4 --> W4[Writeback to CPSR]

    style F1 fill:#fffacd
    style F2 fill:#fffacd
    style F3 fill:#fffacd
    style F4 fill:#fffacd
    style D1 fill:#add8e6
    style D2 fill:#add8e6
    style D3 fill:#add8e6
    style D4 fill:#add8e6
    style E1 fill:#90ee90
    style E2 fill:#90ee90
    style E3 fill:#90ee90
    style E4 fill:#90ee90
```

> **Reading the Pipeline:** Notice how, in a single clock cycle (cycle 4), four *different* instructions are simultaneously in four *different* pipeline stages. This is only possible because ARM's **uniform instruction width and uniform data-processing model** keep the pipeline balanced — a direct consequence of RISC design philosophy.
