Mastery of ARM fundamentals is foundational for **RTOS porting**, **firmware optimisation**, **assembly-level debugging**, and **IoT edge-AI applications**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The 32-bit ARM Data-Processing Instruction Encoding Every ARM data-processing instruction is encoded into exactly **32 bits**. The fields are laid out as follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bit positions:}\quad & [31:28]\;[27:26]\;[25]\;[24:21]\;[20]\;[19:16]\;[15:12]\;[11:0] \\ & \langle\text{cond}\rangle\;\langle 00\rangle\;\langle I\rangle\;\langle\text{opcode}\rangle\;\langle S\rangle\;\langle Rn\rangle\;\langle Rd\rangle\;\langle\text{Operand2}\rangle \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step explanation of each field:** - **Bits [31:28] — Condition code (`cond`)** 4 bits, allowing $2^4 = 16$ possible conditional suffixes. `0b1110` is the default "always execute" (AL). - **Bits [27:26] — Instruction category identifier** For data-processing, these are fixed to `00`. (Other values identify multiply, load/store, branch, etc.) - **Bit [25] — Immediate flag (`I`)** `I = 0` → Operand2 is a shifted register. `I = 1` → Operand2 is an immediate constant. - **Bits [24:21] — Opcode (`opcode`)** 4 bits specify the operation: `0100` = ADD, `0010` = SUB, `0001` = EOR, `1100` = ORR, etc. - **Bit [20] — Set-condition-codes (`S`)** If `S = 1`, the N, Z, C, V flags in CPSR are updated according to the result. - **Bits [19:16] — First source register (`Rn`)** 4 bits select any of the 16 registers. For instructions like `MOV`, this field is unused. - **Bits [15:12] — Destination register (`Rd`)** 4 bits; the result is written here. - **Bits [11:0] — Operand2** When `I = 0`: split into `[11:7] shift, [6:5] shift type, [4] = 0, [3:0] Rm`. When `I = 1`: 8-bit immediate `[11:8] rotate, [7:0] imm8` → produces a 32-bit constant. ### 3.2 Derivation: Decoding an Immediate Constant in ARM ARM's clever encoding generates a **full 32-bit immediate from only 12 bits**. The rule is: $$ \text{Immediate32} \;=\; \text{imm8} \;\text{rotated right by}\; 2 \times \text{rot} $$ where `imm8` is an 8-bit value (0–255) and `rot` is a 4-bit value (0–15), giving shifts of 0, 2, 4, …, 30 bits. **Worked example:** Decode the 12-bit field `0xF30` (`rot4 = 0b1011`, `imm8 = 0x30`). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{rot} &= 11 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{rotation amount} = 2 \times 11 = 22 \text{ bits} \\ \text{imm8} &= 0 \times 30 = 0 \times 0011\,0000 = 48 \\ \text{Immediate32} &= \text{48 rotated right by 22 bits} \\ &= 0 \times 0011\,0000\;|\;0 \times 0000\,0000\;|\;0 \times 0000\,0000\;|\;0 \times 0000 \\ &\;\;\text{after right rotation by 22 bits:}\;\;0 \times 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,1100\,0000\,0000 \\ &= 0 \times 00000C00 = 3072 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is why every legal ARM immediate is expressible as an **8-bit value rotated right by an even amount** — useful trick for exam questions! ### 3.3 Derivation: PC-Relative Branch Target Calculation In a classic ARM pipeline, the Program Counter (`PC = R15`) reads **two instructions ahead** of the one currently being decoded. Therefore, at the moment a branch is evaluated, the PC holds `current_addr + 8`. **Branch target formula:** $$ \text{Target Address} \;=\; (\text{PC at decode}) \;+\; \text{sign\_extend}_{32}(\text{offset24}) \;\times\; 4 $$ $$ \text{Target Address} \;=\; \text{PC}_{\text{at\_exec}} \;+\; \text{signed\_offset} \;\times\; 4 $$ where $\text{offset24}$ is a 24-bit signed value giving a reach of $\pm 2^{23} \times 4 = \pm 32\,\text{MB}$. **Worked example:** A `B` instruction at address `0x00008000` contains an offset field value of `0x00100020` (signed → +1,048,608). What is the target address? $$ \begin{aligned} \text{offset24} &= 0 \times 00100020 \;\;(\text{MSB} = 0,\; \text{positive}) \\ \text{signed\_offset} &= 1{,}048{,}576 \;+\; 32 = 1{,}048{,}608 \\ \text{PC at execution} &= 0 \times 00008000 + 8 = 0 \times 00008008 \\ \text{Target} &= 0 \times 00008008 + (1{,}048{,}608 \times 4) \\ &= 0 \times 00008008 + 4{,}194{,}432 \\ &= 0 \times 00410008 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Derivation: Logical Shift Left as Multiplication by Power of 2 The barrel shifter's LSL operation provides a hardware-accelerated way to perform multiplication: $$ \begin{aligned} X \;\text{LSL}\; n \;\; &\equiv\;\; X \times 2^n \\ \text{Example:}\;\; 0 \times 00000005 \;\text{LSL}\; 3 &= 0 \times 00000028 \\ &= 5 \times 2^3 = 5 \times 8 = 40 \;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Proof for $n = 1$:** Shifting a binary number left by 1 appends a zero to the right of the representation; in base-2 arithmetic, appending a zero is equivalent to multiplying by 2. By induction, shifting by $n$ appends $n$ zeros, multiplying by $2^n$. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Operand2 Addressing Modes | Mode | Syntax Example | Effective Second Operand | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | Immediate | `ADD R0, R1, #0x40` | Constant | Adding small constants | | Register | `ADD R0, R1, R2` | R2 unchanged | Pure register add | | Logical Shift Left | `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #n` | R2 << n | Multiplication by $2^n$ | | Logical Shift Right | `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSR #n` | R2 >> n (unsigned) | Unsigned division by $2^n$ | | Arithmetic Shift Right | `ADD R0, R1, R2, ASR #n` | R2 >> n (signed) | Signed division by $2^n$ | | Rotate Right | `ADD R0, R1, R2, ROR #n` | 32-bit rotation | Bit-field extraction | | Rotate Right Extended | `ADD R0, R1, R2, RRX` | 33-bit rotation via C flag | Carry propagation | | Register-shift register | `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL R3` | R2 << R3 | Variable shift (rare) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 ARM Data-Processing Instruction Format (Bit-Level Layout) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Bits 31:28
cond
(4 bits)"] --> B["Bits 27:26
category
(2 bits = 00)"] B --> C["Bit 25
I flag
(1 bit)"] C --> D["Bits 24:21
opcode
(4 bits)"] D --> E["Bit 20
S flag
(1 bit)"] E --> F["Bits 19:16
Rn
(4 bits)"] F --> G["Bits 15:12
Rd
(4 bits)"] G --> H["Bits 11:0
Operand2
(12 bits)"] H --> H1["if I=0
shift, type, Rm"] H --> H2["if I=1
rotate, imm8"] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Instruction Execution ```mermaid flowchart TD IM[Memory
32-bit instruction] --> F[Fetch Stage
reads PC] F --> D[Decode Stage
extract cond opcode Rd Rn Operand2] D --> B{Is it
Load or Store?} B -- Yes --> LS[Load Store Unit
calculates address
reads/writes memory] B -- No --> BS[Barrel Shifter
shifts Operand2] BS --> ALU[ALU
performs data-processing op] LS --> REG[Register Bank
R0 to R15] ALU --> REG REG --> CC[CPSR Update
if S=1] CC --> EX[Execute Stage
writes back to Rd
or updates PC for branch] ``` ### 4.3 ARM Conditional Execution Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Instruction Fetched
with cond field] --> B{cond matches
CPSR flags?} B -- Yes --> C[Execute Instruction
normal pipeline] B -- No --> D[Instruction treated
as a NOP
no register write] D --> E[Next Instruction] C --> E ``` ### 4.4 Addressing Mode Pre/Post-Indexed Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Load Store Instruction
LDR Rd, base offset] --> B{Pre-indexed
with WB?} B -- Yes Plain --> C[EA = base + offset
then write Rd] B -- Pre-indexed WB --> D[EA = base + offset
base = EA
then Rd = memory EA] B -- Post-indexed WB --> E[Md = memory base
Rd = Md
base = base + offset] C --> F[Memory Access] D --> F E --> F ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Explain the role of the barrel shifter in ARM architecture. Why is it called a "free" operation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **barrel shifter** is a dedicated hardware component in the ARM data path that can shift or rotate a 32-bit operand by any amount from 0 to 31 positions in a **single clock cycle**. It operates transparently as part of `Operand2` for any data-processing instruction. **(1 mark)** Definition of barrel shifter with single-cycle capability. **(1 mark)** Mention of LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX operations. **(1 mark)** Explanation of "free" — no additional execution cycles consumed; e.g., `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #3` performs the shift and add in one cycle. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List any six condition codes used in ARM instructions. What is their significance?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The ARM condition codes are 4-bit fields appended to almost every instruction. They allow instructions to execute only when the CPSR flags satisfy a specified relationship. **(1 mark)** Six examples: EQ, NE, GT, LT, GE, LE (or HI, LO, CS, CC, MI, PL). **(1 mark)** Significance: enables conditional execution without branch instructions, reducing pipeline flushes. **(1 mark)** Example: `ADDNE R0, R1, R2` adds only when Z = 0. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the three categories of ARM instructions with suitable examples. Discuss the role of the Program Counter (R15) and Link Register (R14) in branch operations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **data-processing instruction format** of ARM. Explain each field with its width. Decode the instruction `ADD R3, R4, R5, LSL #2` into its 32-bit binary form, assuming the condition is `AL` and `S = 0`. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Three categories of ARM instructions (7 marks):** ARM instructions are broadly classified into three groups based on the operations they perform: **(i) Data-Processing Instructions (2 marks)** These operate exclusively on registers (no memory access). They include arithmetic (ADD, SUB, RSB, ADC, SBC, MUL), logical (AND, ORR, EOR, BIC), comparison (CMP, TST, TEQ, CMN), and data-movement (MOV, MVN) instructions. *Example:* `SUB R0, R1, R2` performs R0 ← R1 − R2. **(ii) Branch Instructions (2 marks)** Used to alter program flow. - `B label` — simple branch (relative jump). - `BL label` — branch with link (saves return address in R14/LR). - `BX Rm` — branch and optionally exchange instruction set. **Role of R15 (PC) and R14 (LR) (3 marks):** - **R15 — Program Counter (1 mark):** Holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched; due to the 3-stage pipeline it actually reads `current_addr + 8` during execution. - **R14 — Link Register (1 mark):** On execution of `BL`, the return address (`PC + 4`) is automatically saved into R14. This allows the called routine to return via `MOV PC, LR`. - **Branch target formula (1 mark):** `Target = (PC + 8) + (signed offset × 4)`, giving a range of ±32 MB. --- **(b) Instruction format and decoding (7 marks):** **Data-processing format (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} &[31:28]\;[27:26]\;[25]\;[24:21]\;[20]\;[19:16]\;[15:12]\;[11:0] \\ &\text{cond}\;\;\;\; 00 \;\;\;\;\; I \;\;\; \text{opcode}\;\; S \;\;\;\; \text{Rn}\;\;\;\; \text{Rd}\;\;\;\; \text{Operand2} \end{aligned} $$ - `cond` = condition code (4 bits) - `27:26` = `00` for data-processing - `I` = `0` for register Operand2, `1` for immediate - `opcode` = operation code (4 bits) - `S` = set-condition flag - `Rn` = first source register (4 bits) - `Rd` = destination register (4 bits) - `Operand2` = 12-bit flexible second operand **Decoding `ADD R3, R4, R5, LSL #2` (4 marks):** | Field | Value | Bits | Binary | |---|---|---|---| | `cond` (AL) | 14 | [31:28] | `1110` | | category | data-processing | [27:26] | `00` | | `I` | register Operand2 | [25] | `0` | | `opcode` (ADD) | 4 | [24:21] | `0100` | | `S` | no update | [20] | `0` | | `Rn` (R4) | 4 | [19:16] | `0100` | | `Rd` (R3) | 3 | [15:12] | `0011` | | Operand2: shift=2, type=LSL, Rm=R5 | — | [11:0] | `00010 00 0 0101` | **Final 32-bit encoding:** $$ \text{ADD R3, R4, R5, LSL \#2} \;=\; 0\text{x}E0843102 $$ Binary: `1110 0000 1000 0100 0011 0001 0000 0010` --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **load/store architecture** of ARM. Discuss the different **addressing modes** with syntax examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a relevant example, explain **conditional execution** in ARM. How does it improve pipeline efficiency compared to traditional branch-based conditional code? Decode the immediate `#0xFF00` and show how it is encoded in the 12-bit immediate field of ARM. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Load/Store Architecture and Addressing Modes (7 marks):** **Load/Store Architecture (2 marks):** ARM is a load/store architecture, meaning **only `LDR` and `STR` instructions access memory**. All arithmetic and logical operations are performed exclusively on register operands. This separation simplifies the pipeline and enables deterministic execution timing — crucial for embedded and real-time systems. **Addressing Modes (5 marks):** (i) **Pre-indexed (1 mark):** `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` Effective address = R1 + 4; R1 unchanged. (ii) **Pre-indexed with writeback (1.5 marks):** `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!` Effective address = R1 + 4; **R1 ← R1 + 4** (writeback). (iii) **Post-indexed (1.5 marks):** `LDR R0, [R1], #4` Effective address = R1; R0 ← MEM[R1]; **then R1 ← R1 + 4**. (iv) **Register-offset** (1 mark): `LDR R0, [R1, R2]` — offset = R2. (v) **Scaled register-offset** (1 mark): `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #2]` — useful for array indexing. --- **(b) Conditional Execution and Immediate Decoding (7 marks):** **Conditional execution (3 marks):** Every ARM instruction contains a 4-bit condition field in bits [31:28]. If the CPSR flags do not match the condition, the instruction is decoded as a **NOP** — it takes 1 cycle but performs no register write. *Example:* ```assembly CMP R0, #10 ; Sets Z=1 if R0 == 10 ADDNE R1, R1, #1 ; Executes only if R0 != 10 ``` Without conditional execution, this would require: ```assembly CMP R0, #10 BNE skip ADD R1, R1, #1 skip: ``` This requires **3 instructions vs 2**, and the branch causes a **pipeline flush**. **Pipeline efficiency improvement (1 mark):** Conditional execution eliminates short forward branches, reducing pipeline flushes by up to 50% in tight code and improving cache utilisation. **Decoding immediate `#0xFF00` (3 marks):** The immediate `0xFF00` must be expressible as an 8-bit value rotated right by an even amount. $$ \begin{aligned} 0 \times \text{FF00} &= 0 \times \text{FF} \;\text{rotated right by}\; 24 \text{ bits} \\ \text{rot} &= 24/2 = 12 \;=\; 0 \times \text{C} \\ \text{imm8} &= 0 \times \text{FF} \end{aligned} $$ **12-bit encoded field:** `[rot4][imm8]` = `[1100][1111 1111]` = `0xCFF` The full 32-bit ADD instruction with this immediate would have bits [11:0] = `0xCFF`, with `I = 1` to indicate immediate form. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the pipeline offset of +8 in PC-relative branches** — this costs 2 marks on almost every branch-target question. > 2. **Confusing LSL with multiplication by 2 vs shifting left by 1** — always state `LSL #n` equals `× 2^n`. > 3. **Treating pre- and post-indexed addressing as identical** — they differ in **when** the base register is updated. > 4. **Writing `|x|` instead of `\vert x\vert` in tables** — breaks markdown rendering. > 5. **Forgetting that `CMP` and `TST` implicitly set the `S` flag** — students often omit this explanation. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **ARM is RISC:** load/store, 32-bit fixed instructions, 16 registers (R0–R15). - **R13 = SP**, **R14 = LR**, **R15 = PC** (always reads +8 in classic ARM pipeline). - **CPSR** holds flags N, Z, C, V plus control bits I, F, T, M[4:0]. - **Condition codes** (`EQ, NE, GT, LT, GE, LE, HI, LO, CS, CC, MI, PL, VS, VC, AL`) make most instructions conditional — eliminates many short branches. - **Barrel shifter** is "free" — LSL/LSR/ASR/ROR/RRX on Operand2 in 1 cycle. - **Data-processing syntax:** `{}{S} Rd, Rn, Operand2`. - **Operand2** = either immediate (8-bit value rotated right by even amount) or shifted register. - **Branch target:** `PC + 8 + signed_offset × 4`; range ±32 MB. - **Load/Store addressing:** pre-indexed, pre-indexed + writeback (`!`), post-indexed. - **Data-processing encoding bits:** `cond[31:28] 00 I opcode[24:21] S Rn Rd Operand2[11:0]`. - **Thumb mode** (T-bit) halves instruction size to 16 bits for code density. - **Endianness** is configurable; little-endian is default. - **Most exam-favourite:** decode an instruction, explain addressing modes, explain barrel shifter benefits, compare with CISC. - **Pipeline implication:** every branch costs at least 1 cycle for the flush — minimise branches via conditional execution. - **Modes:** User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System — encoded in CPSR M[4:0]. - **Banks of registers** in privileged modes (especially FIQ has more banked registers to avoid saving on entry). - **Block data transfer:** `LDM`/`STM` with suffixes `IA, IB, DA, DB` for stack/queue ops.

topics.title: Barrel Shifter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Barrel Shifter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Barrel Shifter** is a high-speed **combinational digital logic circuit** capable of shifting or rotating an $N$-bit data word by any arbitrary number of bit positions (ranging from $0$ to $N-1$) within a **single clock cycle**. It is implemented as a combinational (non-sequential) network of multiplexers, eliminating the need for multiple clock cycles that would be required by a conventional serial shift register.

In the context of the **ARM Cortex architecture** (the primary focus of *PECPT631 - Advanced Microcontrollers*), the barrel shifter is a **32-bit hardware unit** embedded inside the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) that performs shifts and rotations as part of the *second operand preprocessing* before any arithmetic/logic operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The barrel shifter is the *enabling hardware feature* that allows ARM's signature "shifted register operand" mechanism, which makes every data-processing instruction able to incorporate a shift in **zero additional cycles**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **cylindrical rotating drum with 32 numbered slots**, where each slot holds one bit of data. The drum has **32 exit ports** arranged around it. By rotating the drum by any number of slots (say 4 positions), all 32 bits shift simultaneously and emerge from the corresponding ports — **all in one twist of the drum**.

In simpler terms:
- A **normal shift register** is like a queue of people passing a ball one person at a time — slow.
- A **barrel shifter** is like having a magic conveyor belt that instantly moves the entire row by any distance in a single step — parallel and instantaneous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A standard shift register requires $N$ clock cycles to shift an $N$-bit word by one position. A barrel shifter requires **exactly one propagation delay** (typically a few nanoseconds) regardless of the shift amount.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Number of bits per stage**: $1$ bit shift increment
- **Number of control bits**: $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ lines (for $N$-bit shifter)
- **Propagation delay**: Proportional to $\log_2 N$ (number of MUX stages)
- **Hardware cost**: $N \times \log_2 N$ 2-to-1 multiplexers for an $N$-bit shifter
- **Word size in ARM Cortex-M**: **32 bits**

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam answers, always state the relevant bit-width (e.g., *32-bit for ARM Cortex*) and the exact number of multiplexers used in your circuit design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Taxonomy — The Five Shift Operations

A barrel shifter can implement **five canonical operations**:

1. **Logical Shift Left (LSL):** Bits move left; vacated LSBs filled with $0$; MSBs shifted out are discarded.
2. **Logical Shift Right (LSR):** Bits move right; vacated MSBs filled with $0$; LSBs shifted out are discarded.
3. **Arithmetic Shift Right (ASR):** Bits move right; vacated MSBs filled with the **sign bit** (preserves the sign in two's complement); LSBs discarded.
4. **Rotate Right (ROR):** Bits move right in a **circular** fashion; LSBs shifted out wrap around to become MSBs.
5. **Rotate Right Extended (RRX):** A special **33-bit rotate** that includes the C flag (carry) of the CPSR; only supported as a 1-bit rotation.

### 2.2 Architectural Logic — How It Works
The barrel shifter is constructed as a **cascade of $\log_2 N$ stages**, where each stage $i$ is controlled by the $i$-th bit of the shift amount. Each stage either passes the data unchanged or shifts it by $2^i$ positions using a row of 2-to-1 multiplexers.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- **Step 1:** The $N$-bit input word $D[31:0]$ enters the first stage.
- **Step 2:** Stage $0$ examines control bit $S_0$. If $S_0 = 1$, the data is shifted by $2^0 = 1$ position; otherwise, passed unchanged.
- **Step 3:** Stage $1$ examines control bit $S_1$ and conditionally shifts by $2^1 = 2$ positions.
- **Step 4:** Stage $2$ examines control bit $S_2$ and conditionally shifts by $2^2 = 4$ positions.
- **Step 5:** Continue until Stage $\log_2 N - 1$.
- **Step 6:** The final output $Y[31:0]$ is available after a single combinational propagation delay.

The "Why" behind this design: Because any shift amount $0 \leq k \leq N-1$ can be **uniquely represented as a binary sum** $k = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 N - 1} b_i \cdot 2^i$, the cumulative shifts across stages are mathematically equivalent to a single shift by $k$ positions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Control lines required | $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | Number of select bits for shift amount |
| Total 2-to-1 MUXes | $N \cdot \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | Hardware cost of full shifter |
| Propagation delay | $t_{pd} \cdot \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | Where $t_{pd}$ is MUX delay |
| Shift representation | $k = \sum b_i \cdot 2^i$ | Binary decomposition of shift $k$ |
| Logical Shift Left ($Y$) | $Y = D \cdot 2^k$ | Equivalent to unsigned multiplication |
| Logical Shift Right ($Y$) | $Y = \lfloor D / 2^k \rfloor$ | Equivalent to unsigned division |
| Arithmetic Shift Right ($Y$) | $Y = \lfloor D_{\text{signed}} / 2^k \rceil$ | Sign-preserving division |
| Rotate Right ($Y$) | $Y = (D \gg k) \, \vert \, (D \ll (N - k))$ | Circular wrap-around |
| ARM Shift range | $0 \leq k \leq 31$ | For 32-bit data words |
| Power consumption | $\propto N \cdot \log_2 N \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2 f$ | Capacitive switching model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on absolute values in formulas:** Since markdown tables break on the pipe `\vert` character inside cells, we use parentheses $\vert \cdot \vert$ or describe magnitude via words where required.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers:** Every `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `ORR`, `EOR` instruction can use a shifted second operand (e.g., `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #2` computes $R0 = R1 + 4 \cdot R2$).
- **DSP operations:** Efficient fixed-point multiplication/division by powers of two.
- **Cryptographic engines:** Bit permutations in AES S-boxes and DES key scheduling.
- **Communication protocols:** Bit stuffing/unstuffing in UART, CAN, USB.
- **Graphics processing:** Fast pixel rotations and texture coordinate transforms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hardware Cost of an $N$-bit Barrel Shifter

**Goal:** Show that a barrel shifter requires $N \cdot \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ 2-to-1 multiplexers.

**Given:**
- Word length $N$
- Each stage $i$ shifts by $2^i$ positions
- A single stage of shifting requires $N$ 2-to-1 MUXes (one per output bit)
- Number of stages required: $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ to cover all shift amounts $0$ to $N-1$

**Derivation:**

Let $S$ be the total number of 2-to-1 multiplexers.

$$
S = (\text{MUXes per stage}) \times (\text{Number of stages})
$$

Since each stage requires exactly $N$ MUXes (one for each output bit):

$$
S = N \times \lceil \log_2 N \rceil
$$

**Verification for $N = 4$:** A 4-bit shifter has $\lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$ stages.

$$
S = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ MUXes}
$$

This matches the standard 4-bit barrel shifter literature. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shift Amount Decomposition

**Goal:** Prove that any shift amount $k$ in range $[0, N-1]$ can be achieved through cumulative binary stages.

**Given:**
- $0 \leq k \leq N - 1$
- Control signals $S_0, S_1, \ldots, S_{m-1}$ where $m = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$

**Derivation:**

For any integer $k$ in the specified range, the **binary representation theorem** guarantees:

$$
k = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} S_i \cdot 2^i
$$

where each $S_i \in \{0, 1\}$.

The cumulative effect of stages operating sequentially is:

$$
Y[n] = \begin{cases} D[n - k] & \text{if } n \geq k \\ 0 & \text{otherwise (logical shift)} \end{cases}
$$

Since Stage $i$ contributes a shift of $S_i \cdot 2^i$ when its control bit is active, the total shift equals:

$$
k_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} S_i \cdot 2^i = k
$$

This holds for any $k \in [0, N-1]$ without exception, validating the architecture. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Propagation Delay Analysis

**Goal:** Compute the worst-case combinational delay of the $N$-bit barrel shifter.

**Given:**
- Each 2-to-1 MUX has propagation delay $t_{pd}$
- The shifter has $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ cascaded stages

**Derivation:**

In the **worst case**, all stages contribute to the signal path. The delay accumulates linearly along the cascade:

$$
t_{\text{delay}} = \sum_{i=0}^{\lceil \log_2 N \rceil - 1} t_{pd} = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil \cdot t_{pd}
$$

**For ARM Cortex-M ($N = 32$):**

$$
t_{\text{delay}} = \log_2 32 \cdot t_{pd} = 5 \cdot t_{pd}
$$

**Comparison with serial shift register** (which requires $N$ cycles):

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{N}{t_{\text{delay}} / t_{\text{clock}}} = \frac{N \cdot t_{\text{clock}}}{\log_2 N \cdot t_{pd}}
$$

For $N = 32$ with matched $t_{\text{clock}} \approx t_{pd}$, the speedup is $32 / 5 = \textbf{6.4}\times$ in cycles. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Example Trace for 8-bit Shift Right by 3

**Input:** $D[7:0] = 10110101_2$, Shift Right by $k = 3$

| Stage | Shift by | Control Bit | Output After Stage |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Input | — | — | $10110101$ |
| Stage 0 | $2^0 = 1$ | $S_0 = 1$ | $01011010$ |
| Stage 1 | $2^1 = 2$ | $S_1 = 1$ | $00010110$ |
| Stage 2 | $2^2 = 4$ | $S_2 = 0$ | $00010110$ (passed) |
| **Final** | **3** | $011_2$ | $\mathbf{00010110}$ |

**Verification:** Logical Shift Right by 3: bits $10110101 \rightarrow 00010110$. Correct. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic $N$-bit Barrel Shifter — Block Architecture (Mermaid)

The diagram below illustrates the cascaded multiplexer architecture for a generic $N$-bit barrel shifter, where each stage handles one bit of the binary-encoded shift amount.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputBus["INPUT DATA D N bit"] --> stage0["STAGE 0 MUX ARRAY SHIFT BY 1"]
    s0["SEL BIT S0"] --> stage0
    stage0 --> stage1["STAGE 1 MUX ARRAY SHIFT BY 2"]
    s1["SEL BIT S1"] --> stage1
    stage1 --> stage2["STAGE 2 MUX ARRAY SHIFT BY 4"]
    s2["SEL BIT S2"] --> stage2
    stage2 --> stage3["STAGE 3 MUX ARRAY SHIFT BY 8"]
    s3["SEL BIT S3"] --> stage3
    stage3 --> stage4["STAGE 4 MUX ARRAY SHIFT BY 16"]
    s4["SEL BIT S4"] --> stage4
    stage4 --> outputBus["OUTPUT DATA Y N bit"]
```

**Caption:** Cascaded multiplexer stages for a 32-bit shifter ($\log_2 32 = 5$ stages).

### 4.2 4-bit Barrel Shifter — Detailed Per-Bit MUX Structure (Mermaid)

This diagram zooms into a single bit-slice (bit position $n$) of the 4-bit barrel shifter, showing how the multiplexer inputs are wired from neighboring bit positions or from zero/ground.

```mermaid
graph TB
    bitNminus3["D n minus 3"] --> mux0A["MUX STAGE 0 INPUT 0"]
    bitNminus4["D n minus 4"] --> mux0B["MUX STAGE 0 INPUT 1"]
    s0Bit["SEL S0"] --> mux0Sel["MUX STAGE 0 SELECT"]
    mux0Out["MUX STAGE 0 OUTPUT"] --> mux1A["MUX STAGE 1 INPUT 0"]
    mux0OutShifted["MUX STAGE 0 OUTPUT SHIFTED PATH"] --> mux1B["MUX STAGE 1 INPUT 1"]
    s1Bit["SEL S1"] --> mux1Sel["MUX STAGE 1 SELECT"]
    mux1Out["MUX STAGE 1 FINAL OUTPUT Y n"]
```

**Caption:** Bit-slice of the 4-bit barrel shifter showing signal routing.

### 4.3 ARM Barrel Shifter — Functional Integration in the ALU (Mermaid)

This diagram illustrates how the barrel shifter is integrated within the ARM Cortex-M ALU datapath, processing the second operand before it reaches the arithmetic core.

```mermaid
graph LR
    rm["REGISTER Rm"] --> shiftUnit["32 BIT BARREL SHIFTER"]
    immShift["SHIFT IMMEDIATE OR REGISTER"] --> shiftUnit
    shiftUnit --> aluCore["ALU ARITHMETIC LOGIC CORE"]
    rn["REGISTER Rn"] --> aluCore
    aluCore --> rd["REGISTER Rd DESTINATION"]
    cpsr["CPSR FLAGS CARRY"] --> shiftUnit
    shiftUnit --> cpsrUpdate["UPDATE C FLAG"]
    cpsrUpdate --> cpsr
```

**Caption:** ARM data-processing instruction datapath with embedded barrel shifter.

### 4.4 Operation Selection Matrix (Mermaid State Diagram)

The diagram below maps the shift opcodes to their operational behavior on the input data word.

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["INPUT D AND SHIFT AMOUNT k"] --> decision{"SHIFT TYPE OPCODE"}
    decision -->|"LSL"| lslOp["SHIFT LEFT FILL 0 RIGHT"]
    decision -->|"LSR"| lsrOp["SHIFT RIGHT FILL 0 LEFT"]
    decision -->|"ASR"| asrOp["SHIFT RIGHT FILL SIGN BIT LEFT"]
    decision -->|"ROR"| rorOp["ROTATE RIGHT CIRCULAR"]
    decision -->|"RRX"| rrxOp["ROTATE RIGHT WITH CARRY 33 BIT"]
    lslOp --> out["OUTPUT Y"]
    lsrOp --> out
    asrOp --> out
    rorOp --> out
    rrxOp --> out
```

**Caption:** Decision tree for selecting the barrel shifter operation mode.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`
Define a barrel shifter. Mention two advantages it has over a conventional serial shift register.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A barrel shifter is a **combinational digital circuit** that can shift or rotate an $N$-bit data word by any number of bit positions in a **single clock cycle**, using a network of multiplexers controlled by $\log_2 N$ select lines.

**Advantages over serial shift register:**
1. **Single-cycle operation:** Shifts by any amount complete in one clock cycle, while a serial shift register requires $N$ cycles for an $N$-bit shift.
2. **Higher throughput:** Suitable for high-performance processors like ARM where shifts must complete before the ALU operation in the same cycle.
3. **Reduced software overhead:** No need for iterative shift loops in firmware.

**Marking scheme:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for advantages. **Course Outcome:** CO2, **Bloom's Level:** Remember/Understand.

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
List the five shift operations supported by the ARM Cortex-M barrel shifter.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **LSL** — Logical Shift Left
2. **LSR** — Logical Shift Right
3. **ASR** — Arithmetic Shift Right
4. **ROR** — Rotate Right
5. **RRX** — Rotate Right Extended (through Carry flag)

**Marking scheme:** 1 mark for naming any 5; 3 marks if all correct with brief description. **Course Outcome:** CO2, **Bloom's Level:** Remember.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question B1 — Choice A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Design a **4-bit logical barrel shifter** that performs a right shift by $0$, $1$, $2$, or $3$ positions. Draw the logic diagram using 2-to-1 multiplexers and explain its operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and operation of the 32-bit barrel shifter used in the ARM processor**. Discuss how it enables the "shifted operand" feature in ARM data-processing instructions with an example. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B1(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Architecture Specification (2 marks):**
For a 4-bit shifter, we need:
- Input $D[3:0]$, Output $Y[3:0]$
- $\lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$ control lines $S_1 S_0$
- Total MUXes: $4 \times 2 = 8$ (4 per stage)

**Step 2 — Stage 0 Design (Shift by 1) (2 marks):**

Each output bit $Y_i$ for $i \in \{0,1,2,3\}$ is computed as:

$$
Y_i^{(0)} = \begin{cases} D_i & \text{if } S_0 = 0 \text{ (no shift)} \\ D_{i+1} & \text{if } S_0 = 1 \text{ (shift right by 1)} \end{cases}
$$

For $i = 3$ (MSB), $D_{i+1} = 0$ to fill the vacated position.

**Step 3 — Stage 1 Design (Shift by 2) (2 marks):**

$$
Y_i^{(1)} = \begin{cases} Y_i^{(0)} & \text{if } S_1 = 0 \text{ (no shift)} \\ Y_{i+2}^{(0)} & \text{if } S_1 = 1 \text{ (shift right by 2)} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (1 mark):**
- Shift by 0 ($S_1 S_0 = 00$): $Y = D = d_3 d_2 d_1 d_0$
- Shift by 1 ($01$): $Y = 0 d_3 d_2 d_1$
- Shift by 2 ($10$): $Y = 0 0 d_3 d_2$
- Shift by 3 ($11$): $Y = 0 0 0 d_3$

**Logic Diagram (1 mark — drawn as Mermaid):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    d3["D3"] --> m1A["MUX0"]
    d2["D2"] --> m1B["MUX0"]
    d3 --> m1A2["MUX0 MSB FILL 0"]
    s0["S0"] --> m1Sel["MUX0 SEL"]
    d2["D2"] --> m2A["MUX1"]
    d1["D1"] --> m2B["MUX1"]
    s0 --> m2Sel["MUX1 SEL"]
    d1 --> m3A["MUX2"]
    d0 --> m3B["MUX2"]
    s0 --> m3Sel["MUX2 SEL"]
    d0 --> m4A["MUX3"]
    gnd["0"] --> m4B["MUX3 LSB FILL"]
    s0 --> m4Sel["MUX3 SEL"]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Barrel Shifter Design:**
> Students commonly lose marks by: (i) forgetting to fill vacated MSBs with $0$ for logical shifts, (ii) drawing only one MUX instead of $N$ per stage, (iii) failing to label control lines $S_0, S_1$ clearly, (iv) omitting the truth table verification step.

---

### Model Solution for Question B1(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — ARM Barrel Shifter Architecture (3 marks):**
The ARM Cortex-M processor contains a **dedicated 32-bit barrel shifter** inside the data-processing datapath. It is positioned **immediately before the ALU**, processing the second operand ($Rm$) of any instruction before the arithmetic operation begins.

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    rm["Rm SECOND OPERAND 32 BIT"] --> bshift["ARM BARREL SHIFTER 32 BIT"]
    shiftCtrl["SHIFT SPECIFIER IMM OR REG"] --> bshift
    bshift --> alu["ALU ADDSUBS ANDORR EOR"]
    rn["Rn FIRST OPERAND"] --> alu
    alu --> rd["Rd DESTINATION"]
    cpsr["CPSR CARRY FLAG"] --> bshift
    bshift --> flagOut["UPDATED CARRY FLAG"]
```

**Step 2 — Shift Operations (2 marks):**
The ARM barrel shifter supports **LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX** as described in Section 1.3.

**Step 3 — Shifted Operand Feature — Example (2 marks):**
Consider the ARM instruction: `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #2`

- The barrel shifter left-shifts $R2$ by $2$ bits ($R2 \times 4$).
- The ALU then computes $R0 = R1 + (R2 \times 4)$.
- **All in a single cycle**, with no additional latency.

This dramatically reduces instruction count for array indexing, address arithmetic, and DSP-style multiply-by-constant operations.

**Course Outcome:** CO2, CO3. **Bloom's Level:** Apply/Analyze.

---

**Question B2 — Choice B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the design of an **8-bit barrel shifter** capable of performing **logical shift right, circular rotate right, and arithmetic shift right**. Show the equations governing the multiplexer selection at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **barrel shifter with a conventional shift register** in terms of speed, hardware, power, and application suitability. Explain why ARM processors specifically use a barrel shifter. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B2(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Stage Count and MUX Count (2 marks):**
For $N = 8$, stages required = $\lceil \log_2 8 \rceil = 3$ (shift by 1, 2, 4). Total MUXes = $8 \times 3 = 24$.

**Step 2 — Operation Selection via Opcode (2 marks):**
A **2-bit mode signal** $M[1:0]$ selects between the three operations:
- $M = 00$: Logical Shift Right (fill $0$)
- $M = 01$: Rotate Right (circular)
- $M = 10$: Arithmetic Shift Right (fill sign bit $D_7$)

**Step 3 — MUX Equations for Bit $i$ at Stage 0 (Shift by 1) (2 marks):**

$$
Y_i^{(0)} = \begin{cases} 
D_i & S_0 = 0 \\
F_i^{(M)} & S_0 = 1
\end{cases}
$$

where $F_i^{(M)}$ is the shifted-and-filled value depending on mode $M$:

- Logical: $F_i^{(00)} = 0$ if $i = 7$, else $F_i^{(00)} = D_{i+1}$
- Rotate: $F_i^{(01)} = D_{(i+1) \bmod 8}$
- Arithmetic: $F_i^{(10)} = D_7$ if $i = 7$, else $F_i^{(10)} = D_{i+1}$

**Step 4 — Full Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
graph TB
    dBus["INPUT D 7 TO 0"] --> stageA["STAGE 0 SHIFT BY 1 MODE MUX"]
    mode["MODE M 1 0"] --> stageA
    stageA --> stageB["STAGE 1 SHIFT BY 2 MODE MUX"]
    s1["S1"] --> stageB
    stageB --> stageC["STAGE 2 SHIFT BY 4 MODE MUX"]
    s2["S2"] --> stageC
    stageC --> outBus["OUTPUT Y 7 TO 0"]
```

---

### Model Solution for Question B2(b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Barrel Shifter | Conventional Shift Register |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Shift Time | **1 clock cycle** for any shift | $N$ clock cycles per shift |
| Hardware | $N \cdot \log_2 N$ MUXes | $N$ flip-flops |
| Speed | **Very high** (combinational) | Lower (sequential) |
| Power | Higher (more switching) | Lower |
| Area | Larger silicon footprint | Smaller |
| Operation | Any shift amount, single step | Step-by-step, $1$ bit per cycle |
| Use Case | CPUs, ALUs, DSP cores | Serial interfaces, slow I/O |

**Why ARM uses a barrel shifter (3 marks):**

1. **RISC philosophy:** ARM is a RISC architecture optimized for single-cycle execution of every instruction. A barrel shifter is essential because it allows the *second operand* to be shifted *within the same cycle* as the ALU operation.
2. **Code density:** Instructions like `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #2` replace multiple `MOV`+`ADD` sequences, saving code space.
3. **DSP and addressing performance:** Efficient array indexing ($A[i] = B[2i]$) and fixed-point arithmetic rely on fast shifts.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Comparison Questions:**
> Students often write vague qualitative statements ("barrel shifter is faster") without **quantitative justification**. Always include numerical comparisons such as *"$N$ cycles vs 1 cycle"* and *"$N \cdot \log_2 N$ vs $N$ flip-flops"* to earn full marks.

**Course Outcome:** CO2, CO4. **Bloom's Level:** Analyze/Evaluate.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- A **barrel shifter** is a combinational circuit that shifts/rotates $N$-bit data by any amount in **one cycle**.
- It uses **$\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ cascaded stages**, each stage shifting by $2^i$ positions.
- Total **2-to-1 MUX count = $N \cdot \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$**.
- ARM Cortex-M has a **32-bit barrel shifter** inside the ALU.
- Five supported operations: **LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX**.
- RRX is a **33-bit rotate** that includes the **C flag**.
- ASR preserves the **sign bit**; LSL/LSR fill with **$0$**.
- Shift amount $k$ is **binary-decomposed**: $k = \sum S_i \cdot 2^i$.
- Speedup over serial shift register = **$N / \log_2 N$** factor.
- Example instruction: `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #2` computes $R0 = R1 + 4 \cdot R2$ in **one cycle**.
- Hardware cost grows as $O(N \log N)$; speed is $O(\log N)$ per shift.
- **ARM-specific:** The C (carry) flag is **updated** with the last bit shifted out.
- **Critical exam tip:** Always draw **$N$ MUXes per stage**, label all select lines, and include a **truth table** for verification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification and Explanation of Instructions with Examples - Data Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification and Explanation of Instructions with Examples - Data Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Processing Instructions** are a major class of instructions in the **ARM Cortex-M (Thumb-2)** instruction set that operate on data held in **registers** (operands located in the general-purpose register file `R0`–`R12`), performing **arithmetic, logical, shift, rotate, multiplication, and comparison** operations, and writing the result back to a destination register (or updating the **Application Program Status Register – APSR** flags without writing a result, in the case of test/comparison instructions).

According to the **ARMv7-M Architecture Reference Manual (ARM ARM)**, data processing instructions are broadly divided into the following sub-classes:

1. **Data Movement Instructions** — `MOV`, `MVN`
2. **Arithmetic Instructions** — `ADD`, `ADC`, `SUB`, `SBC`, `RSB`, `RSC`, `MUL`, `MLA`
3. **Logical Instructions** — `AND`, `ORR`, `EOR`, `BIC`, `ORN`
4. **Shift and Rotate Instructions** — `LSL`, `LSR`, `ASR`, `ROR`
5. **Comparison / Test Instructions** — `CMP`, `CMN`, `TST`, `TEQ`
6. **Multiply and Multiply-Accumulate Instructions** — `MUL`, `MLA`, `MLS`, `SMULL`, `UMULL`, `SMLAL`, `UMLAL`

> [!NOTE]
> **APSR (Application Program Status Register)** is a 32-bit register that holds the four condition flags: **N (Negative)**, **Z (Zero)**, **C (Carry)**, and **V (Overflow)**. By default, data processing instructions do **not** update these flags. The flag-update behaviour is explicitly enabled by appending the **'S' suffix** to the mnemonic (e.g., `ADDS`, `SUBS`, `ANDS`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> Imagine a **kitchen mixer-grinder** as the CPU and the ingredients in a **bowl** as the data stored in registers (`R0`–`R12`). A **Data Processing instruction** is like a **recipe card** that tells the chef: *"Take ingredient A from bowl 1 and ingredient B from bowl 2, perform an operation (chop, mix, roast, or taste), and put the result back in a bowl — and optionally, write on a small notepad whether the dish is salty, sweet, or burnt."* The "small notepad" here is the **APSR flags register**, and the act of writing on it is enabled by the **'S' suffix**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions

- All data processing instructions operate on **32-bit words** by default.
- Operand size can be reduced to **8-bit (Byte)** or **16-bit (Halfword)** using the `B` or `H` suffix (e.g., `ADDSB`, `ADDH` — these are not present in Cortex-M; instead, `AND` style operations on bytes/halfwords occur via `LDRB`/`LDRH`).
- The condition under which the instruction executes is encoded by a **4-bit condition code field** (`cond`) in the 32-bit encoding. Default is `AL` (Always).
- **Barrel Shifter**: A key hardware component in ARM that allows the second operand to be **shifted or rotated for free** (in a single cycle) before the ALU operation, encoded directly in the instruction word.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic Encoding Template (T1 / T2 Format)

A typical data processing instruction in **Thumb-2** uses either a **16-bit Thumb encoding (T1)** or a **32-bit Thumb-2 encoding (T2)**. The **second operand (Op2)** is unique to ARM — it is a **flexible operand** that may be:

- An **immediate constant** (rotated 8-bit value, 12-bit modified immediate in Thumb-2)
- A **register**
- A **register shifted by an immediate** (e.g., `R1, LSL #5`)
- A **register shifted by a register** (e.g., `R1, LSL R2`)

The **general assembly syntax** is:

```
<opcode>{<cond>}{S} Rd, Rn, <Op2>
```

Where:
- `opcode` = `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `ORR`, `EOR`, `BIC`, `MOV`, `MVN`, `CMP`, `TST`, …
- `cond` = condition code (`EQ`, `NE`, `LT`, `GT`, `CS`, `CC`, …) — optional
- `S` = "set flags" suffix — optional
- `Rd` = destination register
- `Rn` = first source register (often omitted in `MOV`/`MVN`/`CMP`/`TST`/`TEQ`)
- `Op2` = flexible second operand

## 2.2 Sub-Classification & Operational Description

### (A) Data Movement Instructions
| Mnemonic | Operation | Result Written? | Flags Updated? |
|----------|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| `MOV Rd, Op2` | `Rd ← Op2` | Yes | Optional (with `S`) |
| `MVN Rd, Op2` | `Rd ← ~Op2` (bitwise NOT) | Yes | Optional (with `S`) |
| `MOVS` / `MVNS` | As above, plus APSR update | Yes | **Yes** |

> [!NOTE]
> `MOV` and `MVN` do **not** read `Rn`; the syntax is `MOV Rd, Op2` (only 2 operands).

### (B) Arithmetic Instructions
| Mnemonic | Operation | Flags with 'S' |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| `ADD Rd, Rn, Op2` | `Rd = Rn + Op2` | N, Z, C, V |
| `ADC Rd, Rn, Op2` | `Rd = Rn + Op2 + C` | N, Z, C, V |
| `SUB Rd, Rn, Op2` | `Rd = Rn − Op2` | N, Z, C, V |
| `SBC Rd, Rn, Op2` | `Rd = Rn − Op2 + C − 1` | N, Z, C, V |
| `RSB Rd, Rn, Op2` | `Rd = Op2 − Rn` (reverse subtract) | N, Z, C, V |
| `RSC Rd, Rn, Op2` | `Rd = Op2 − Rn + C − 1` | N, Z, C, V |
| `MUL Rd, Rm, Rs` | `Rd = (Rm × Rs)[31:0]` | N, Z |
| `MLA Rd, Rm, Rs, Rn` | `Rd = (Rm × Rs + Rn)[31:0]` | N, Z |
| `MLS Rd, Rm, Rs, Rn` | `Rd = Rn − (Rm × Rs)[31:0]` | — |
| `SMULL RdLo, RdHi, Rm, Rs` | Signed 64-bit multiply | N, Z |
| `UMULL RdLo, RdHi, Rm, Rs` | Unsigned 64-bit multiply | N, Z |
| `SMLAL RdLo, RdHi, Rm, Rs` | Signed MAC into 64-bit | N, Z |
| `UMLAL RdLo, RdHi, Rm, Rs` | Unsigned MAC into 64-bit | N, Z |

### (C) Logical & Bit-Clear Instructions
| Mnemonic | Operation |
|----------|-----------|
| `AND Rd, Rn, Op2` | Bitwise AND |
| `ORR Rd, Rn, Op2` | Bitwise OR (set bits) |
| `EOR Rd, Rn, Op2` | Bitwise XOR (toggle bits) |
| `BIC Rd, Rn, Op2` | `Rd = Rn AND (~Op2)` — bit clear |
| `ORN Rd, Rn, Op2` | `Rd = Rn OR (~Op2)` |
| `TST Rn, Op2` | `Rn AND Op2`, **result discarded** (test) |
| `TEQ Rn, Op2` | `Rn XOR Op2`, **result discarded** (test) |

### (D) Comparison Instructions (always update flags, never write Rd)
| Mnemonic | Operation | Flags Updated |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| `CMP Rn, Op2` | `Rn − Op2` (discard result) | N, Z, C, V |
| `CMN Rn, Op2` | `Rn + Op2` (discard result) | N, Z, C, V |

### (E) Shifts & Rotates (used as Op2, or standalone as instructions)
| Mnemonic | Operation |
|----------|-----------|
| `LSL Rd, Rm, Rs/#n` | Logical Shift Left |
| `LSR Rd, Rm, Rs/#n` | Logical Shift Right (fills with 0) |
| `ASR Rd, Rm, Rs/#n` | Arithmetic Shift Right (sign-extends) |
| `ROR Rd, Rm, Rs/#n` | Rotate Right |
| `RRX Rd, Rm` | Rotate Right with Carry (1-bit through C flag) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (APSR Flag Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas define how the **APSR flags** are computed for arithmetic and logical data processing instructions (where the **result** is the 32-bit destination value):

| Flag | Formula | Meaning |
|------|---------|---------|
| **N** (Negative) | $N = \text{Result}_{31}$ | 1 if MSB of result is 1 (signed-negative) |
| **Z** (Zero) | $Z = (\text{Result} == 0)$ | 1 if result is exactly zero |
| **C** (Carry) | $C = \text{Carry out from the ALU}$ | 1 if unsigned overflow (ADD) or **borrow NOT** occurred (SUB) |
| **V** (Overflow) | $V = (\text{carry into MSB}) \oplus (\text{carry out of MSB})$ | 1 if signed overflow occurred |

For **SUB / CMP** operations, the **Carry flag C = NOT(Borrow)** — i.e., C is set to **1 when there is NO borrow**, which is the opposite of many textbooks. This is the classic ARM quirk that KTU examiners love to test.

$$ C_{SUB} = \overline{\text{Borrow}} $$

$$ C_{ADD} = \text{CarryOut} $$

For **Shifts**, the last bit shifted **out** is written to the C flag:

$$ C_{LSL/LSR/ASR/ROR} = \text{last bit shifted out} $$

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **CMP / TST** form the backbone of every **conditional branch** in ARM assembly (`BEQ`, `BNE`, `BGT`, …).
- **BIC** is heavily used to **clear individual bits** in peripheral configuration registers (e.g., clearing a GPIO pin mask).
- **ORR** is used to **set bits** (e.g., enabling an interrupt in NVIC).
- **Multiply-Accumulate (MLA, SMLAL)** instructions accelerate **DSP kernels** such as FIR filters, FFT butterflies, and matrix multiplication, exploiting the single-cycle 32-bit MAC hardware in Cortex-M3/M4 cores.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Addition with Barrel Shifter

**Problem:** Evaluate the following instruction and state the APSR flags.

```
ADD R3, R1, R2, LSL #4
```

Given: `R1 = 0x0000_0007` and `R2 = 0x0000_0002`.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Decode the Barrel-Shifted Operand:**
The second operand `R2, LSL #4` means "load `R2`, then logical-shift-left by 4 positions." The barrel shifter applies the shift **for free in a single cycle**, and the shifted value is fed directly to the ALU.

$$
\text{Op2} = R2 \ll 4 = 0x0000\_0002 \ll 4
$$

$$
\text{Op2} = 0x0000\_0020 \quad (32_{10})
$$

**Step 2 — Perform the ALU Addition:**

$$
R3 = R1 + \text{Op2} = 0x0000\_0007 + 0x0000\_0020
$$

$$
R3 = 0x0000\_0027 \quad (39_{10})
$$

**Step 3 — Compute APSR Flags (assuming the `S` suffix is present, e.g., `ADDS`):**

- **N (Negative):** $N = \text{Result}_{31} = 0$ → **N = 0**
- **Z (Zero):** $Z = (0x0000\_0027 == 0) = \text{false}$ → **Z = 0**
- **C (Carry):** For ADD, C is the carry-out of bit 31. Since $7 + 32 = 39$ fits comfortably in 32 bits → **C = 0**
- **V (Overflow):** Operands are both small positive numbers; the result is small and positive → no signed overflow → **V = 0**

> [!NOTE]
> Final result: `R3 = 0x00000027`, `APSR = N=0, Z=0, C=0, V=0`.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Subtraction with Borrow (Carry Flag Inversion)

**Problem:** Evaluate `SUBS R4, R5, R6`, with `R5 = 0x0000_0004` and `R6 = 0x0000_000A`.

**Step 1 — Perform the Subtraction:**

$$
R4 = R5 - R6 = 4 - 10 = -6
$$

In **32-bit two's complement** representation, $-6$ is:

$$
-6_{10} = 0xFFFF\_FFFA
$$

So `R4 = 0xFFFFFFFA`.

**Step 2 — Compute APSR Flags:**

- **N:** $N = \text{Result}_{31} = 1$ → **N = 1**
- **Z:** $Z = (0xFFFFFFFA == 0) = \text{false}$ → **Z = 0**
- **C (Carry):** In ARM, C is **NOT Borrow**. Since `R5 < R6`, a borrow *does* occur. Therefore, **C = 0** (this is the famous ARM quirk).
- **V:** Subtracting two small positive numbers gave a small negative number — no signed overflow → **V = 0**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly set C = 1 here because they think "C = 1 means there's a carry." In ARM, for `SUB`, **C = 1 means NO borrow** (i.e., the unsigned subtraction succeeded without underflow). Setting C = 0 here is correct.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Logical BIC (Bit Clear)

**Problem:** Execute `BIC R7, R8, R9`, with `R8 = 0xFFFF_FFFF` and `R9 = 0x0000_00F0`.

**Step 1 — Compute the Bitwise NOT of Op2:**

$$
\tilde{\text{Op2}} = \overline{R9} = \overline{0x0000\_00F0} = 0xFFFF\_FF0F
$$

**Step 2 — Apply AND with the First Operand:**

$$
R7 = R8 \text{ AND } \tilde{\text{Op2}} = 0xFFFF\_FFFF \text{ AND } 0xFFFF\_FF0F
$$

$$
R7 = 0xFFFF\_FF0F
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** `BIC` is a *mask-out* operation. It clears the bits that are 1 in the second operand, while leaving all other bits of the first operand unchanged. Here, bits 4–7 of `R8` are cleared.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — 32-bit Multiply and APSR Result

**Problem:** Evaluate `MULS R3, R1, R2`, with `R1 = 0x0001_0000` and `R2 = 0x0000_0004`.

**Step 1 — Multiply the Operands:**

$$
R3 = R1 \times R2 = 0x0001\_0000 \times 0x0000\_0004
$$

$$
R3 = 0x0004\_0000 \quad (262144_{10})
$$

**Step 2 — MUL Truncation Rule:**
The result is the **low 32 bits** of the full 64-bit product. In this case, the product fits in 32 bits, so no truncation occurs.

**Step 3 — APSR Flags (only N and Z are affected by MUL):**

- **N:** $N = 0x00040000_{31} = 0$ → **N = 0**
- **Z:** Result is non-zero → **Z = 0**
- **C:** Unaffected (kept as previous value).
- **V:** Unaffected (kept as previous value).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Comparison Driving a Conditional Branch

**Problem:** Show how `CMP` and a subsequent `BGT` interact.

```asm
    CMP   R0, R1     ; compute R0 − R1, update APSR
    BGT   label_gt   ; branch if signed R0 > R1
```

**Step-by-Step Branch Logic:**

The assembler encodes `BGT` as `B cond` with `cond = 0110` (GT = "Greater Than"). The condition `GT` evaluates to true when:

$$
\text{GT} = (Z == 0) \text{ AND } (N == V)
$$

In words: the result was **not zero** AND the **sign of the result matches the sign of any overflow** — i.e., the signed comparison `R0 > R1` holds.

**For our example:** if `R0 = 5`, `R1 = 3`:
- $5 - 3 = 2$ → Z = 0, N = 0, V = 0
- `N == V` → 0 == 0 → **true**
- `Z == 0` → **true**
- `GT` evaluates to **true** → branch is taken.

---

## 3.6 Summary Table — Quick-Reference of Common Data Processing Patterns

| Goal | Recommended Instruction | Example |
|------|-------------------------|---------|
| Add 1 to a register | `ADD` with immediate 1 | `ADD R0, R0, #1` |
| Subtract 1 from a register | `SUB` with immediate 1 | `SUB R0, R0, #1` |
| Test if register is zero | `CMP Rn, #0` (or `TST Rn, Rn`) | `CMP R0, #0` |
| Clear bit 5 of a register | `BIC` with mask 0x20 | `BIC R0, R0, #0x20` |
| Set bit 3 of a register | `ORR` with mask 0x08 | `ORR R0, R0, #0x08` |
| Toggle bit 7 of a register | `EOR` with mask 0x80 | `EOR R0, R0, #0x80` |
| Multiply two 32-bit numbers, keep low 32 bits | `MUL` | `MUL R0, R1, R2` |
| Multiply-accumulate (DSP) | `MLA` | `MLA R0, R1, R2, R3` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Data Processing Pipeline (Block Diagram)

Below is the block-level functional architecture of how a **data processing instruction** flows through the ARM Cortex-M3 execution unit, highlighting the role of the **barrel shifter** and the **APSR flag pipeline**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INSTRUCTION FETCH FROM ROM] --> B[INSTRUCTION DECODE]
    B --> C[READ Rn FROM REGISTER FILE]
    B --> D[READ Op2 OR FETCH IMMEDIATE]
    D --> E[BARREL SHIFTER LSL LSR ASR ROR]
    C --> F[ALU ADDER LOGIC XOR AND]
    E --> F
    F --> G[RESULT BUS]
    G --> H[WRITE BACK TO Rd IN REGISTER FILE]
    F --> I[FLAG COMPUTE UNIT]
    I --> J[APSR N Z C V]
    J --> K[CONDITIONAL BRANCH EVALUATOR]
    K --> L[BRANCH DECISION]
    H --> M[NEXT INSTRUCTION FETCH]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Data Processing Instructions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[DATA PROCESSING INSTRUCTIONS]
    root --> A1[DATA MOVEMENT]
    root --> A2[ARITHMETIC]
    root --> A3[LOGICAL]
    root --> A4[COMPARISON AND TEST]
    root --> A5[SHIFT AND ROTATE]
    root --> A6[MULTIPLY AND MAC]

    A1 --> A1a[MOV]
    A1 --> A1b[MVN]

    A2 --> A2a[ADD]
    A2 --> A2b[ADC]
    A2 --> A2c[SUB]
    A2 --> A2d[SBC]
    A2 --> A2e[RSB]
    A2 --> A2f[RSC]

    A3 --> A3a[AND]
    A3 --> A3b[ORR]
    A3 --> A3c[EOR]
    A3 --> A3d[BIC]
    A3 --> A3e[ORN]
    A3 --> A3f[TST]
    A3 --> A3g[TEQ]

    A4 --> A4a[CMP]
    A4 --> A4b[CMN]

    A5 --> A5a[LSL]
    A5 --> A5b[LSR]
    A5 --> A5c[ASR]
    A5 --> A5d[ROR]
    A5 --> A5e[RRX]

    A6 --> A6a[MUL]
    A6 --> A6b[MLA]
    A6 --> A6c[MLS]
    A6 --> A6d[SMULL]
    A6 --> A6e[UMULL]
    A6 --> A6f[SMLAL]
    A6 --> A6g[UMLAL]
```

## 4.3 APSR Flag Decision Flow for ADD / SUB

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[EXECUTE ADD OR SUB] --> q1[IS RESULT ZERO?]
    q1 -- YES --> setZ[SET Z = 1]
    q1 -- NO --> clrZ[Z REMAINS 0]
    q2[IS MSB OF RESULT 1?]
    q2 -- YES --> setN[N = 1]
    q2 -- NO --> clrN[N REMAINS 0]
    q3[ADD CARRY OUT OR SUB NO BORROW?]
    q3 -- YES --> setC[C = 1]
    q3 -- NO --> clrC[C = 0]
    q4[SIGNED OVERFLOW DETECTED?]
    q4 -- YES --> setV[V = 1]
    q4 -- NO --> clrV[V = 0]
    finish[UPDATE APSR REGISTER]
```

## 4.4 Operand-2 (Op2) Flex-Operand Decoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inst[INSTRUCTION WORD] --> dec[OPERAND 2 DECODER]
    dec --> modeA[MODE 1 IMMEDIATE 12 BIT]
    dec --> modeB[MODE 2 REGISTER]
    dec --> modeC[MODE 3 REGISTER SHIFTED BY IMMEDIATE]
    dec --> modeD[MODE 4 REGISTER SHIFTED BY REGISTER]
    modeA --> alu[FEED ALU]
    modeB --> shift[BARREL SHIFTER]
    modeC --> shift
    modeD --> shift
    shift --> alu
    alu --> result[RESULT TO Rd]
    alu --> flags[FLAGS TO APSR]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]:** Differentiate between `MOV` and `MVN` instructions in ARM Cortex-M with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `MOV` | `MVN` |
|--------|-------|-------|
| Operation | Copies the operand into the destination register | Copies the bitwise complement of the operand |
| Logical meaning | $Rd = Op2$ | $Rd = \overline{Op2}$ |
| Use case | Move a value, set a register to zero (`MOV R0, #0`) | Generate bitwise NOT; e.g., `MVN R0, #0` sets R0 to `0xFFFFFFFF` |
| Example | `MOV R1, R2` → `R1 = R2` | `MVN R1, R2` → `R1 = ~R2` |
| Flag update | Optional via `S` suffix | Optional via `S` suffix |

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]:** What is the role of the barrel shifter in ARM data processing instructions? Why is it considered "free"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **barrel shifter** is a dedicated hardware unit inside the ARM ALU that performs **shift or rotate operations on the second operand (Op2) of a data processing instruction before the main ALU computation**. It is called "free" because it is **not a separate instruction** — the shift amount is encoded in the instruction word itself and executed in the **same clock cycle** as the ALU operation. For example, `ADD R3, R1, R2, LSL #4` shifts `R2` left by 4 bits and adds the result to `R1` in a single cycle, eliminating the need for separate shift instructions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]:**

> **(a)** Classify the data processing instructions of the ARM Cortex-M (Thumb-2) instruction set. Explain any **five arithmetic instructions** with their assembly syntax, operations performed, and the effect on the **APSR flags**. (7 marks)

> **(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **role of the barrel shifter** in ARM data processing. Evaluate the result and APSR flags for the instruction sequence below: (7 marks)

```asm
MOV   R0, #0x10
MOV   R1, #0x0F
SUBS  R2, R0, R1
ADD   R3, R0, R1, LSL #2
```

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Classification (3 marks) + Five Arithmetic Instructions (4 marks):**

**Classification:** Data processing instructions are classified into the following six groups:
1. **Data Movement** — `MOV`, `MVN`
2. **Arithmetic** — `ADD`, `ADC`, `SUB`, `SBC`, `RSB`, `RSC`
3. **Logical** — `AND`, `ORR`, `EOR`, `BIC`, `ORN`
4. **Shift and Rotate** — `LSL`, `LSR`, `ASR`, `ROR`, `RRX`
5. **Comparison / Test** — `CMP`, `CMN`, `TST`, `TEQ`
6. **Multiply and MAC** — `MUL`, `MLA`, `MLS`, `SMULL`, `UMULL`, `SMLAL`, `UMLAL`

**Five Arithmetic Instructions (detailed):**

1. `ADD Rd, Rn, Op2` → $Rd = Rn + Op2$; flags: N, Z, C, V
2. `ADC Rd, Rn, Op2` → $Rd = Rn + Op2 + C$; flags: N, Z, C, V (used in multi-word addition)
3. `SUB Rd, Rn, Op2` → $Rd = Rn - Op2$; flags: N, Z, C, V (C = 1 means no borrow)
4. `RSB Rd, Rn, Op2` → $Rd = Op2 - Rn$ (reverse subtract); flags: N, Z, C, V
5. `MUL Rd, Rm, Rs` → $Rd = (Rm \times Rs)[31:0]$; flags: N, Z (only)

**Part (b) — Barrel Shifter (3 marks) + Evaluation (4 marks):**

**Barrel Shifter:** The barrel shifter is a **dedicated combinational logic block** within the ARM ALU that performs **shifts and rotations on Operand-2** of a data processing instruction **in parallel (in the same cycle)** with the main ALU operation. It supports **LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, and RRX** operations, with shift amounts specified either as an immediate (5-bit value, 0–31) or by a register. The result of the barrel shifter is fed directly to the ALU, eliminating the need for separate shift instructions and thus saving program memory and execution time.

**Evaluation Step-by-Step:**

Step 1 — `MOV R0, #0x10`
- $R0 = 0x10 = 16_{10}$

Step 2 — `MOV R1, #0x0F`
- $R1 = 0x0F = 15_{10}$

Step 3 — `SUBS R2, R0, R1`
- $R2 = 0x10 - 0x0F = 0x01$
- N: MSB of 0x01 is 0 → N = 0
- Z: result is non-zero → Z = 0
- C: no borrow (16 > 15) → C = 1
- V: no signed overflow → V = 0

Step 4 — `ADD R3, R0, R1, LSL #2`
- $R1 \text{ LSL } 2 = 0x0F \times 4 = 0x3C = 60_{10}$
- $R3 = 0x10 + 0x3C = 0x4C = 76_{10}$
- (no `S` suffix → flags are **not updated**)

**Final Results:**
- R0 = 0x10
- R1 = 0x0F
- R2 = 0x01, APSR = N=0, Z=0, C=1, V=0
- R3 = 0x4C

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]:**

> **(a)** Explain the **APS flags (N, Z, C, V)** in detail. For each of the following instructions, compute the result and the APSR flag values. (7 marks)

```asm
MOV   R0, #0xC0000000
MOV   R1, #0x40000000
ADDS  R2, R0, R1
SUBS  R3, R1, R0
```

> **(b)** Explain the **`BIC`** and **`EOR`** instructions with syntax, operation, and one real-world embedded example each. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — APSR Flags Explanation (2 marks) + Evaluation (5 marks):**

The four APSR condition flags are:
- **N (Negative):** Set to bit 31 of the result. Indicates a negative value in two's complement signed representation.
- **Z (Zero):** Set to 1 when the result is exactly 0; otherwise 0.
- **C (Carry):** For `ADD`, C is the carry-out of bit 31 (unsigned overflow indicator). For `SUB`, C = 1 means **no borrow** occurred.
- **V (Overflow):** Set to 1 when signed overflow is detected (carry into MSB ≠ carry out of MSB).

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

**Instruction 1:** `ADDS R2, R0, R1`
- $R2 = 0xC0000000 + 0x40000000$
- In binary, the high nibble is $0xC + 0x4 = 0x10$ → a carry occurs out of bit 31
- $R2 = 0x00000000$ (only the carry-out is preserved; the 32-bit result wraps to 0)

Flag analysis:
- N = 0x00000000 (bit 31 = 0)
- Z = 1 (result is zero)
- C = 1 (carry out of bit 31 occurred)
- V = 1 (signed overflow: $−2^{31} + 2^{30} = −2^{30}$, but the result $0$ has a different sign — overflow detected)

**Instruction 2:** `SUBS R3, R1, R0`
- $R3 = 0x40000000 - 0xC0000000$
- This is a 32-bit subtraction: $0x40000000 - 0xC0000000 = 0x80000000$ (after borrowing)
- Interpreted as signed: $R3 = 2^{30} - (-2^{30}) = +2^{31}$, but in 32-bit signed form this is $-2^{31}$ (a negative number). Signed overflow has occurred.

Flag analysis:
- N = 1 (bit 31 of $0x80000000$ is 1)
- Z = 0
- C = 0 (borrow occurred because $0x40000000 < 0xC0000000$)
- V = 1 (signed overflow)

**Part (b) — BIC and EOR explanation (7 marks):**

**BIC (Bit Clear):**
- **Syntax:** `BIC Rd, Rn, Op2`
- **Operation:** $Rd = Rn \text{ AND } (\overline{Op2})$
- **Effect:** Clears (resets to 0) the bits in `Rn` that correspond to 1-bits in `Op2`.
- **Real-world example:** Clearing a specific interrupt-enable bit in the NVIC `ISER` register's mirror:
  ```asm
  BIC R0, R0, #0x04   ; clears bit 1 (mask 0b100) in R0
  ```
  If `R0 = 0xFFFFFFFF`, after `BIC` it becomes `0xFFFFFFFB`.

**EOR (Exclusive OR):**
- **Syntax:** `EOR Rd, Rn, Op2`
- **Operation:** $Rd = Rn \oplus Op2$
- **Effect:** Toggles the bits in `Rn` that correspond to 1-bits in `Op2`.
- **Real-world example:** Toggling a single GPIO output pin (read-modify-write):
  ```asm
  EOR R1, R1, #0x80   ; toggles bit 7 of R1
  ```
  This pattern is the foundation of the classic **read-modify-write bit-banding** technique used to flip LEDs, relay drivers, or any single-bit peripheral flag without disturbing other bits.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 'S' suffix** for flag updates (very common; `ADD` does *not* update flags by default, only `ADDS` does).
> 2. **Inverting the Carry flag logic for SUB** — Remember C = 1 means **no borrow**, not "borrow occurred."
> 3. **Confusing `BIC` with `SUB`** — `BIC` is a *bitwise* clear, not arithmetic.
> 4. **Forgetting to truncate `MUL` result** to 32 bits — the high 32 bits of a 32×32 product are silently dropped. Use `SMULL`/`UMULL` if the full 64-bit result is required.
> 5. **Writing Rd in CMP/TST/CMN/TEQ** — these instructions never write to a destination register; their result is **discarded**, only the flags are updated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Data processing instructions operate on **register operands** (R0–R12) and are executed by the **ALU + Barrel Shifter**.
- [x] The **'S' suffix** is mandatory to update APSR flags (N, Z, C, V).
- [x] **`MOV`** copies; **`MVN`** copies the bitwise complement.
- [x] **Arithmetic instructions**: `ADD`, `ADC`, `SUB`, `SBC`, `RSB`, `RSC`, plus multiply family.
- [x] **Logical instructions**: `AND`, `ORR`, `EOR`, `BIC`, `ORN`; tests: `TST`, `TEQ`.
- [x] **Comparison**: `CMP` (subtract, discard result), `CMN` (add, discard result).
- [x] **Barrel Shifter** is free — shift amount encoded in the instruction, executed in 1 cycle.
- [x] **Carry flag in SUB** = 1 means **no borrow** (counter-intuitive — remember it!).
- [x] **`MUL` affects only N and Z flags**, never C or V.
- [x] **`MLA`** performs multiply-accumulate: $Rd = (Rm \times Rs) + Rn$.
- [x] **`SMULL/UMULL`** produce a **64-bit result** in two registers (RdLo:RdHi).
- [x] **`BIC`** is the standard way to **clear bits** in peripheral registers; **`ORR`** to **set bits**; **`EOR`** to **toggle bits**.
- [x] All data processing instructions can be **conditionally executed** using a 4-bit condition code (`EQ, NE, CS, CC, MI, PL, VS, VC, HI, LS, GE, LT, GT, LE, AL`).
- [x] The 16 most commonly used condition codes are encoded in the 4 MSBs of the instruction word, with `AL` (0xE) being the default (always execute).
- [x] **Immediate constants** in Thumb-2 use the **12-bit modified immediate** format (an 8-bit value + 4-bit rotate-right), allowing many useful constants to be encoded directly.
- [x] **Pipeline impact**: data processing instructions take **1 cycle** in the execute stage on Cortex-M3 (multi-cycle for `MUL` and `MLA`).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Branch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Branch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **ARM Cortex-M architecture** (the core study platform for PECPT631 – Advanced Microcontrollers, KTU 2024 Scheme), a **Branch instruction** is a class of control-flow instructions that alters the sequential execution of the program counter (`PC`), redirecting instruction fetch from its linear, sequential default behavior to a target address determined either by a signed PC-relative offset or by the contents of a general-purpose register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> A *branch* instruction modifies the program flow by updating the `R15` (Program Counter) to either an absolute address (loaded from a register) or a **PC-relative offset** from the current instruction. Branches form the foundation of all high-level language constructs such as `if-else`, `switch`, `while`, `for`, and `function calls`.

In the **Thumb-2 instruction set** used by Cortex-M3/M4 processors, branch instructions are classified into six functional families:

| S.No | Instruction Family | Mnemonic | Function |
|------|--------------------|----------|----------|
| 1 | Unconditional Branch | `B` | Unconditional jump to label |
| 2 | Conditional Branch | `B<cond>` | Jump only if condition flag satisfied |
| 3 | Branch with Link | `BL` | Save return address in `LR`, then branch |
| 4 | Branch and Exchange | `BX` / `BLX` | Branch + optionally switch instruction set |
| 5 | Compare and Branch | `CBZ` / `CBNZ` | Test register and branch in one cycle |
| 6 | Table Branch | `TBB` / `TBH` | Branch table indexing for `switch`/jump tables |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Junction

Imagine a train traveling along a single-track railway line. By default, the train moves forward, station to station, in a fixed sequence — this is **sequential execution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Railway Network**
> - The **mainline track** = the linear address sequence of memory.
> - A **junction (point/switch)** = a *branch instruction*.
> - The **signal light at the junction** = the *condition flags* (N, Z, C, V).
> - A **memory of the previous junction** = the **Link Register (`LR`)** used by `BL`.
> - A **train timetable index** = **Table Branch** (`TBB`/`TBH`).
> 
> Just as a junction may be *automatic* (unconditional branch) or *signal-controlled* (conditional branch), a branch instruction either *always redirects* execution or does so *only when conditions permit*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Architectural Metrics

- **Program Counter (PC)**: Register `R15`. In ARM Cortex-M, the **LSB of the PC always reads as 0** because instructions are 16-bit or 32-bit aligned.
- **Link Register (LR)**: Register `R14`, holds the return address after a `BL`.
- **APSR Condition Flags**: **`N`** (Negative), **`Z`** (Zero), **`C`** (Carry), **`V`** (Overflow) — collectively drive all conditional execution.
- **Default instruction encoding on Cortex-M3/M4**: **Thumb-2** (mix of 16-bit and 32-bit encodings).
- **Branch range**: Up to **±16 MB** for full 32-bit `B/BL` encodings.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Fire Insight:** Always remember that on entry to an exception/ISR, hardware automatically pushes the return address into `LR`. So in ISRs you do **not** need a `BL` to call helpers — they behave like a regular function thanks to the **Tail-Chaining** hardware optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Why and How

### 2.1.1 Why Do We Need Branch Instructions?

A processor executing purely sequential code can only do one thing: count upward through memory. Real programs require:

1. **Decision Making** — `if (x == 0) then ... else ...`
2. **Loops** — `while`, `for`, `do-while`
3. **Subroutines / Function Calls** — reusable code blocks
4. **Switch / Case** dispatch — multi-way jumps
5. **Exception Handling** — fault service routines

> **Branch instructions are the silicon-level mechanism that enables ALL higher-level control structures.**

### 2.1.2 How Does a Branch Execute Internally?

When the CPU fetches a branch instruction:

1. **Fetch Stage (F)** — Branch opcode is fetched from memory at `PC`.
2. **Decode Stage (D)** — Offset field is sign-extended and shifted left by 1 (since offsets are in half-word units).
3. **Branch Target Calculation** — `Target = (PC_current) + (Offset × 2)`.
4. **Execute Stage (E)** — Condition flags are evaluated if conditional. On success, `PC` is updated to the target; on failure, the instruction is treated as a **NOP** (no operation).
5. **Pipeline Flush** — The pipeline (Cortex-M3/M4 is 3-stage) is flushed, and refilling begins at the new address.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD TABLE — Memorize This for KTU University Exam**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Range / Units |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Branch target address | $Target = PC + \text{Offset}$ | Offset in **bytes** |
| 2 | 32-bit `B/BL` offset range | $-16\,777\,216 \le \text{Offset} \le +16\,777\,214$ | **±16 MB** in steps of 2 |
| 3 | 16-bit `B` offset range (cond) | $-256 \le \text{Offset} \le +254$ | **±254 bytes** in steps of 2 |
| 4 | `CBZ/CBNZ` offset range | $0 \text{ to } +126$ (forward), $0 \text{ to } -130$ (backward) | Steps of **2** |
| 5 | `TBB` index range | $0 \text{ to } 255$ (byte entries) | **256 entries max** |
| 6 | `TBH` index range | $0 \text{ to } 65535$ (halfword entries) | **65 536 entries max** |
| 7 | LR after `BL` | $LR = \text{address of instruction after BL}$ | Used by `BX LR` to return |
| 8 | `BX` target address bit [0] | Bit 0 selects instruction set (0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb) | Cortex-M ignores bit 0 in branches (always Thumb) |
| 9 | APSR flags updated by | `CMP`, `TST`, `SUBS`, `ADCS`, logical ops | N, Z, C, V |
| 10 | Pipeline refill penalty | **3 cycles** for taken branch (Cortex-M3/M4) | 3-stage pipeline |

## 2.3 Condition Code Reference (KTU Exam Favourite)

The suffix `<cond>` appended to `B` selects one of **16 condition codes**. These are evaluated against APSR flags `N`, `Z`, `C`, `V`.

| Suffix | Mnemonic | Condition Tested | APSR Flag Check |
|--------|----------|------------------|-----------------|
| `EQ` | Equal | $Z = 1$ | Z set |
| `NE` | Not Equal | $Z = 0$ | Z clear |
| `CS` / `HS` | Carry Set / Unsigned ≥ | $C = 1$ | C set |
| `CC` / `LO` | Carry Clear / Unsigned < | $C = 0$ | C clear |
| `MI` | Minus (Negative) | $N = 1$ | N set |
| `PL` | Plus (Non-Negative) | $N = 0$ | N clear |
| `VS` | Overflow Set | $V = 1$ | V set |
| `VC` | Overflow Clear | $V = 0$ | V clear |
| `HI` | Unsigned > | $C = 1 \;\text{AND}\; Z = 0$ | compound |
| `LS` | Unsigned ≤ | $C = 0 \;\text{OR}\; Z = 1$ | compound |
| `GE` | Signed ≥ | $N = V$ | compound |
| `LT` | Signed < | $N \neq V$ | compound |
| `GT` | Signed > | $Z = 0 \;\text{AND}\; N = V$ | compound |
| `LE` | Signed ≤ | $Z = 1 \;\text{OR}\; N \neq V$ | compound |
| `AL` | Always (default) | unconditional | — |
| `NV` | Never (deprecated) | never true | — |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bare-Metal Firmware**: Every ISR returns via `BX LR` after branch-with-link capture.
- **RTOS Scheduling**: Context switchers use direct register-indirect branches to switch between task stacks.
- **Motor Control Loops**: Tight inner current-control loops use `CBZ` for zero-cross detection on ADC values.
- **Bootloader Design**: `BL` to application entry point is fundamental to firmware update mechanisms.
- **Protocol State Machines**: UART/SPI drivers use `B<cond>` chains to dispatch on state variables.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Connection:** Modern automotive ECUs (engine control units) running AUTOSAR rely heavily on `BLX` for fast function-pointer-based dispatch in OSEK/ORTI interrupt service routines. The branch mechanism is the heartbeat of real-time embedded control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Branch Target Address Calculation

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a 32-bit `B` instruction at memory address `0x0800_0100` (typical STM32 flash base). The Thumb-2 encoding carries an immediate value `imm32` representing the branch offset.

In **Thumb-2**, the encoding semantics are:

$$
\text{Offset}_{\text{bytes}} = \text{SignExtend}_{32}(\text{imm32}) \times 2
$$

Because the fetched instruction is already 4 bytes (the 32-bit `B`), the **PC value used in the target calculation is `PC + 4`** (architectural definition for ARM).

### 3.1.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given:**
- Current `B` instruction located at address `0x0800_0100`
- The 32-bit encoded offset field reads `0x00FF_FFFC` (a 24-bit signed value scaled to bytes by hardware)

**Step 1 — Extract raw offset field from instruction word.**

$$
\text{imm32}_{\text{raw}} = 0x00\text{FF}\text{FFFC}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to signed 32-bit integer.**

Since the MSB of the 24-bit field is `0`, no sign extension is needed; the value is positive.

$$
\text{imm32}_{\text{signed}} = +16\,777\,212
$$

**Step 3 — Architectural PC value (PC reads 4 bytes ahead on Thumb-2).**

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{arch}} = 0x0800\_0100 + 4 = 0x0800\_0104
$$

**Step 4 — Apply target formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Target} &= \text{PC}_{\text{arch}} + \text{imm32}_{\text{signed}} \\
&= 0x0800\_0104 + 16\,777\,212 \\
&= 0x0800\_0104 + 0x00\text{FF}\_\text{FFFC} \\
&= 0x0800\_0104 + 0x00\text{FF}\_\text{FFFC}
\end{aligned}
$$

Performing the hex addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x0800\_0104 \\
+ \; 0x00\text{FF}\_\text{FFFC} \\
\hline
0x0900\_0100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Target Address} = 0x0900\_0100}
$$

**Step 6 — Range verification.**

Maximum positive offset is `0x00FF_FFFE`, giving:

$$
\text{Target}_{\max} = \text{PC}_{\text{arch}} + 0x00\text{FF}\_\text{FFFE}
$$

For `PC = 0x0800_0104`, this is `0x0900_0102` — confirming the **+16 MB ceiling**.

Minimum (negative) offset is `0xFF00_0000`, giving:

$$
\text{Target}_{\min} = 0x0800\_0104 - 16\,777\,216 = 0x0700\_0104
$$

This confirms the **−16 MB floor**.

## 3.2 Derivation: CBZ Offset Encoding

`CBZ` (Compare and Branch if Zero) and `CBNZ` (Compare and Branch if Non-Zero) are **16-bit instructions** with limited range:

- Encoding: `cbz Rn, label` consumes 2 bytes
- The offset is an unsigned 6-bit value `i`, scaled by 2, **but the architectural PC advances by 2** (not 4) because the instruction itself is 16-bit.

The formula is:

$$
\text{Target} = \text{PC} + (2 \times i)
$$

where $0 \le i \le 63$.

### Worked Example

`CBZ R3, skip_loop` located at `0x2000_0000`. The encoded `i = 20`.

**Step 1 — Architectural PC value (16-bit instruction, so PC = address + 2):**

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{arch}} = 0x2000\_0000 + 2 = 0x2000\_0002
$$

**Step 2 — Compute byte offset:**

$$
\text{Offset}_{\text{bytes}} = 2 \times 20 = 40 = 0x28
$$

**Step 3 — Compute target:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Target} &= 0x2000\_0002 + 0x28 \\
&= 0x2000\_002\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum forward reach check:**

$$
\text{MaxForward} = \text{PC}_{\text{arch}} + 2 \times 63 = 0x2000\_0002 + 126 = 0x2000\_0080
$$

This is the **+126 byte forward limit** quoted in the KTU formula sheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Asymmetric Range Insight:** Backward branches can reach `−130 bytes` because the PC advancement differs at backward targets. The asymmetry arises from the encoding format — a frequent KTU viva question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Branch Family Decision Table

Use this matrix to choose the correct branch in KTU exam answers:

| Situation | Best Choice | Reason |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Simple unconditional jump < 2 KB | `B label` (16-bit form) | Saves flash, single cycle |
| Jump > 2 KB | `B.W label` (32-bit form) | Full ±16 MB range |
| Function call, return needed | `BL subroutine` | Saves return in LR |
| Return from subroutine | `BX LR` | Standard ARM calling convention |
| Branch on zero/non-zero register | `CBZ` / `CBNZ` | Fuses compare + branch |
| Dispatch on index value | `TBB` / `TBH` | Compact jump table |
| Conditional jump > 254 bytes | `B<cond>.W` (32-bit form) | Long conditional branch |
| Conditional short jump (saves cycles) | `B<cond>` 16-bit + `IT` block | Avoids flushes via predication |
| Switch instruction set (Cortex-A) | `BX`/`BLX` with bit 0 = 1 | Not used on Cortex-M (always Thumb) |

## 3.4 Sub-Routine Link Mechanism — Exhaustive Walkthrough

Consider this assembly snippet:

```armasm
BL   myFunction      ; 32-bit encoding
NEXT:                ; next instruction here
...
myFunction:
   ; do work
   BX   LR           ; return
```

**Execution trace:**

1. `BL myFunction` is at address, say, `0x0800_0200`. It is a 32-bit instruction.
2. After fetch, **LR ← 0x0800_0204** (the address of the next instruction, `NEXT:`).
3. The hardware sets **PC ← address of myFunction**.
