4. `myFunction` executes.
5. When `BX LR` executes, PC ← LR = `0x0800_0204`, and execution resumes at `NEXT:`.

> **Why `PC + 4` and not `PC + 2` for 32-bit Thumb instructions?**
> ARM architectural definition states that the PC returned for a 32-bit Thumb instruction always points to the **next 32-bit-aligned address after the current instruction**. Since 32-bit instructions are 4 bytes, `LR = PC_current + 4`.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Branch Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fetch Instruction at PC] --> B{Is it a Branch Instruction?}
    B -- No --> C[Execute Normally] --> Z[Increment PC by 2 or 4]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is it Conditional B cond?}
    D -- No --> E[Extract Offset Field]
    D -- Yes --> F{APSR Flags Match Condition?}
    F -- No --> G[Treat as NOP] --> Z
    F -- Yes --> E
    E --> H{Encoding Width?}
    H -- 16-bit --> I[Sign Extend imm32]
    H -- 32-bit --> J[Sign Extend imm32]
    I --> K[Target = PC_arch + Offset x 2]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Is it BL or BLX?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Save LR = PC_arch_next]
    L -- No --> N[Skip LR update]
    M --> O[Load New PC = Target]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Flush Pipeline 3 cycles]
    P --> Q[Resume Fetch at Target]
```

## 4.2 Subroutine Call and Return — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Caller[Caller Code at PC] -->|BL subroutine| BLExec[BL Execution Unit]
    BLExec -->|Update| LRReg[LR Register R14]
    BLExec -->|Jump to| SubStart[Subroutine Entry]
    SubStart --> SubBody[Subroutine Body]
    SubBody -->|BX LR or POP PC| RetMech[Return Mechanism]
    LRReg -->|Reads| RetMech
    RetMech -->|Restores| Caller
```

## 4.3 Conditional Branch Evaluation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Decode B cond Instruction] --> ReadFlags[Read APSR Flags N Z C V]
    ReadFlags --> Lookup[Lookup Condition Code Table]
    Lookup --> Evaluate{Condition True?}
    Evaluate -- True --> Take[Update PC = PC + Offset]
    Evaluate -- False --> NoOp[NOP - PC unchanged]
    Take --> Flush[Pipeline Flush]
    Flush --> FetchNew[Fetch at New PC]
    NoOp --> Continue[Continue Sequential Flow]
```

## 4.4 IT (If-Then) Block Execution Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ITInsn[IT xxxx Condition] --> C1[Execute Conditionally]
    C1 --> C2[Execute Conditionally]
    C2 --> C3[Execute Conditionally]
    C3 --> Out[Exit IT Block]
    Out --> Norm[Resume Normal Predication]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The `IT` (If-Then) instruction in Thumb-2 allows up to **4 conditional instructions** to follow without flushing the pipeline. This is one of the key performance advantages of the ARM Cortex-M core over traditional 8-bit microcontrollers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the difference between `B`, `BL`, and `BX` branch instructions in ARM Cortex-M. Give one example use-case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`B label` — Unconditional Branch (1 mark)**
  Used for unconditional jumps within the program. Does **not save a return address**. Example: jumping to the top of a loop.
  ```armasm
  B   infinite_loop
  ```

- **`BL subroutine` — Branch with Link (1 mark)**
  Used to call subroutines/functions. **Saves the return address in `LR (R14)`**, enabling later return. Example:
  ```armasm
  BL  Delay_ms
  ```

- **`BX Rm` — Branch and Exchange (1 mark)**
  Branches to the absolute address contained in register `Rm`. Used to return from subroutines by branching to `LR`.
  ```armasm
  BX  LR
  ```
  Note: The LSB of `Rm` historically selected instruction set (ARM/Thumb); on Cortex-M, only Thumb is supported, and bit 0 must be 1.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List any six condition codes used with the `B<cond>` instruction in ARM Cortex-M. State the APSR flag combination tested by each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Suffix | Condition | APSR Test |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| `EQ` | Equal | $Z = 1$ |
| `NE` | Not Equal | $Z = 0$ |
| `GT` | Signed Greater Than | $Z = 0 \;\text{AND}\; N = V$ |
| `LT` | Signed Less Than | $N \neq V$ |
| `HI` | Unsigned Higher | $C = 1 \;\text{AND}\; Z = 0$ |
| `CS` / `HS` | Carry Set / Unsigned ≥ | $C = 1$ |

*(Any six correct entries with flags — full 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **execution flow of a `BL` subroutine call** in ARM Cortex-M. Show how the Link Register is updated and how the return is performed using `BX LR`. Discuss the role of the **PC-relative offset** in branch target calculation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 32-bit `B` (unconditional branch) instruction is located at address `0x0800_1234` in the flash memory of an STM32 Cortex-M4 microcontroller. The instruction encodes an immediate offset field of `0x00FF_FE00`. Calculate the **branch target address**. Also state the **maximum positive and negative offsets** that this instruction can encode, and verify whether the calculation falls within the legal range. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — What is `BL`? (1 mark)**

`BL` (Branch with Link) is a **32-bit Thumb-2 instruction** that performs a PC-relative branch while simultaneously saving the **return address** into the **Link Register (`LR`, register `R14`)**.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram / Execution Flow (3 marks)**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PC points to BL myFunc at 0x0800_0100] --> B[Decode BL Instruction]
    B --> C[Calculate Target = PC_arch + Offset]
    C --> D[LR = PC_arch + 4 = 0x0800_0104]
    D --> E[Flush Pipeline]
    E --> F[PC = Target = address of myFunc]
    F --> G[Execute myFunc body]
    G --> H{BX LR encountered?}
    H -- Yes --> I[PC = LR = 0x0800_0104]
    I --> J[Resume execution after original BL]
    H -- No --> G
```

**Step 3 — Role of PC-Relative Offset (2 marks)**

The PC-relative offset is a **signed 24-bit value** (after encoding), scaled by 2 to produce a **byte offset**. This offset is **position-independent**, meaning the code can be relocated in memory without modification — crucial for relocatable firmware, bootloaders, and Position-Independent Code (PIC).

**Step 4 — Return Mechanism (1 mark)**

The subroutine returns by executing `BX LR`, which loads `PC` with the contents of `LR`, restoring execution to the instruction immediately following the original `BL`.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Instruction address: `0x0800_1234`
- Encoded immediate field: `0x00FF_FE00` (24-bit value)
- 32-bit `B` instruction → architectural PC = instruction address + **4 bytes**

**Step 1 — Compute architectural PC (1 mark)**

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{arch}} = 0x0800\_1234 + 4 = 0x0800\_1238
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret immediate field (1 mark)**

The encoded immediate is a 24-bit value. Since the MSB (`bit 23`) is `0`, it is a **positive signed value**:

$$
\text{imm32} = 0x00\text{FF}\_\text{FE}00 = 16\,776\,960 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply branch target formula (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Target} &= \text{PC}_{\text{arch}} + \text{imm32} \\
&= 0x0800\_1238 + 0x00\text{FF}\_\text{FE}00 \\
&= 0x0900\_1038
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Hex addition verification (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\phantom{+}\; 0x0800\_1238 \\
&+\; 0x00\text{FF}\_\text{FE}00 \\
&\hline
&\phantom{+}\; 0x0900\_1038
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Range verification (1 mark)**

Maximum positive offset: `0x00FF_FFFE` (≈ +16 MB)
Calculated offset: `0x00FF_FE00` < `0x00FF_FFFE` ✓ **Within legal range**

**Step 6 — Final answer (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\text{Branch Target Address} = 0x0900\_1038}
$$

---

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Compare and Branch (`CBZ`/`CBNZ`)** instructions in ARM Cortex-M. State the encoding format, target calculation formula, and forward/backward range. Discuss their advantage over separate `CMP` + `B<cond>` instruction sequences. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following ARM assembly snippet. Trace the execution, showing the contents of `LR` and `PC` at each step, and explain why this code does **not** correctly return from the subroutine `myFunc`.

```armasm
0x0800_0000:   MOV   R0, #5
0x0800_0004:   BL    myFunc
0x0800_0008:   ADD   R1, R0, #1       ; <-- expected return here
0x0800_000C:   B     .

myFunc:
0x0800_0010:   PUSH  {R0, LR}
0x0800_0012:   MOV   R0, #10
0x0800_0014:   ADD   R0, R0, #1       ; R0 = 11
0x0800_0016:   POP   {R0, R1}         ; <-- BUG HERE
0x0800_0018:   BX    R1               ; <-- returns using corrupted value
```

Identify the bug, rewrite the corrected code, and justify each correction. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Definition of `CBZ`/`CBNZ` (2 marks)**

`CBZ` (Compare and Branch on Zero) and `CBNZ` (Compare and Branch on Non-Zero) are **16-bit Thumb instructions** that perform a **compare-and-branch in a single instruction**. They test a low register (`R0`–`R7`) without affecting APSR flags.

- `CBZ Rn, label` — branch to `label` if `Rn == 0`
- `CBNZ Rn, label` — branch to `label` if `Rn != 0`

**Step 2 — Encoding format (1 mark)**

```
[15:8]  opcode   [7:3]  Rn   [2:0]  i (offset high)
followed by another 16-bit halfword for the remaining offset bits
```

**Step 3 — Target formula and range (2 marks)**

$$
\text{Target} = \text{PC} + (2 \times i), \quad 0 \le i \le 63
$$

| Direction | Range |
|-----------|-------|
| Forward (positive offset) | **0 to +126 bytes** |
| Backward (negative offset) | **0 to −130 bytes** |

**Step 4 — Advantages over `CMP` + `B<cond>` (2 marks)**

- **Single instruction** → saves code space (2 bytes vs 4 bytes).
- **Does not affect APSR flags** → preserves prior condition state.
- **Fused micro-operation** → in many Cortex-M implementations, executes in fewer cycles than separate compare + branch.
- **Pipeline-friendly** — no flush if branch not taken, conditional branch prediction works well.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the bug (2 marks)**

The `PUSH {R0, LR}` saves R0 and LR onto the stack. The matching `POP {R0, R1}` **mismatches the push order**: it pops the saved R0 back into R0 (correct), but pops the **saved LR value into R1**, then branches using `BX R1`. Since R1 did not originally contain the return address, execution jumps to an unpredictable address.

**Step 2 — Trace execution (2 marks)**

| Step | Instruction | R0 | R1 | LR | PC |
|------|-------------|----|----|----|----|
| 1 | `BL myFunc` (at 0x0800_0004) | 5 | — | **0x0800_0008** | 0x0800_0010 |
| 2 | `PUSH {R0, LR}` | 5 | — | 0x0800_0008 | 0x0800_0012 |
| 3 | `MOV R0, #10` | 10 | — | 0x0800_0008 | 0x0800_0014 |
| 4 | `ADD R0, R0, #1` | 11 | — | 0x0800_0008 | 0x0800_0016 |
| 5 | `POP {R0, R1}` ← BUG | 5 | **0x0800_0008** | 0x0800_0008 | 0x0800_0018 |
| 6 | `BX R1` | 5 | 0x0800_0008 | 0x0800_0008 | **0x0800_0008** |

Actually the trace happens to *return correctly by coincidence*, but if R1 had any other value (e.g., garbage from a previous routine), the program would crash. The bug is the **register list mismatch** — `POP` should restore to the **same registers that were pushed**, in the **same order**.

**Step 3 — Corrected code (2 marks)**

```armasm
myFunc:
    PUSH  {R0, LR}      ; save R0 and LR
    MOV   R0, #10
    ADD   R0, R0, #1
    POP   {R0, LR}      ; restore R0 and LR — KEY FIX
    BX    LR             ; return using LR
```

**Step 4 — Justification (1 mark)**

The corrected `POP {R0, LR}` restores LR to its saved value, and `BX LR` returns to the instruction following the original `BL`. Register push/pop lists must be **mirror images**, and the return register must always be `LR`, not an arbitrary register.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Branch Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting PC + 4 rule** — Many students compute the target as `PC + offset` instead of `(PC + 4) + offset` for 32-bit Thumb-2 instructions. **Cost: 1 mark** in derivations.
> 
> 2. **Confusing `CBZ` range with `B` range** — `CBZ` has ±130 byte range, NOT ±16 MB. Mixing these loses **2 marks**.
> 
> 3. **Wrong LR update** — Some students state `LR = address of BL` instead of `LR = address of next instruction after BL`. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 
> 4. **Confusing `BX` with `BLX`** — `BX` does NOT save LR; only `BLX` does. **Cost: 1–2 marks.**
> 
> 5. **Sign-extension errors** — When the immediate MSB is 1, students forget sign extension in 32-bit subtraction. Always convert to **signed 32-bit** before arithmetic.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the architectural PC rule** — On Cortex-M, PC reads as `current + 4` for 32-bit Thumb and `current + 2` for 16-bit Thumb. **Cost: 2 marks** in numerical problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — `Branch` Module**

- 🔹 A **branch instruction** changes the Program Counter away from sequential execution.
- 🔹 **Six families** in Cortex-M: `B`, `B<cond>`, `BL`, `BX`/`BLX`, `CBZ`/`CBNZ`, `TBB`/`TBH`.
- 🔹 **Branch target** = `PC_arch + (Offset × 2)` for PC-relative branches.
- 🔹 **`BL` saves return address** into `LR (R14)`. Return via `BX LR`.
- 🔹 **`CBZ`/`CBNZ`** do **NOT** modify APSR flags (unlike `CMP`).
- 🔹 **32-bit `B` range**: **±16 MB** (offset −16,777,216 to +16,777,214).
- 🔹 **16-bit `B<cond>` range**: **±254 bytes**.
- 🔹 **`CBZ`/`CBNZ` range**: **+126 forward / −130 backward**.
- 🔹 **Pipeline penalty** for taken branch on Cortex-M3/M4 = **3 cycles**.
- 🔹 **`TBB`** indexes a byte-sized branch table (≤ 256 entries).
- 🔹 **`TBH`** indexes a halfword-sized branch table (≤ 65,536 entries).
- 🔹 Condition flags: **N, Z, C, V** — set by data-processing instructions, read by conditional branches.
- 🔹 **Common pitfalls**: PC + 4 rule, sign extension, push/pop list matching.
- 🔹 **Always restore LR after PUSH before BX LR** — mismatched PUSH/POP causes subtle bugs.
- 🔹 **Thumb-2 mixes 16-bit and 32-bit encodings** — assembler chooses width automatically.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Topic: Branch | PECPT631 — Advanced Microcontrollers | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load-Store
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Load-Store # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Load-Store Architecture (Register-Register Architecture)** is a CPU design philosophy, adopted by all ARM-family processors, in which **memory can be accessed ONLY through dedicated `LDR` (Load Register) and `STR` (Store Register) instructions**. All arithmetic, logical, and data-processing operations are strictly confined to operands residing in the **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)**. No data-processing instruction may directly read from or write to main memory. In the ARM instruction set, this translates to three fundamental classes: | Class | Allowed Operand Sources | Memory Access? | |---|---|---| | **Data Processing** (ADD, SUB, AND, ORR…) | Register only | ❌ No | | **Load (LDR, LDRB, LDRH, LDRSB, LDRSH, LDM, LDRT…)** | Memory → Register | ✅ Yes | | **Store (STR, STRB, STRH, STM, STRT…)** | Register → Memory | ✅ Yes | | **Branch (B, BL, BX, BLX)** | PC-relative | ❌ No | The ARM7TDMI — the historical anchor of PECPT631 — has **37 visible 32-bit registers** (31 general-purpose + 6 status registers), physically arranged as a **register bank** that is *bank-switched* according to the current processor mode. This register-rich design is precisely what makes the **2-address, register-register Load-Store model** practical and fast. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workbench & Warehouse" Model > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine a carpenter's workshop. > - The **Warehouse** = Main Memory (slow, far away, large capacity). > - The **Workbench** = CPU Registers (small, fast, on the desk). > - The carpenter **cannot cut wood lying in the warehouse**. He must first **load** planks onto the workbench (this is an `LDR`). > - After sawing and planing on the workbench (data-processing instructions), he must **store** the finished piece back to the warehouse (this is an `STR`). > - In CISC (e.g., legacy 8051, x86), the carpenter could shout instructions to a warehouse assistant to "add the two boxes directly" — i.e., a single `ADD [mem1], [mem2]` instruction. In RISC Load-Store (ARM), the carpenter must do it himself, but his workbench is so close that the round-trip is essentially free in terms of clock cycles. This is why ARM code looks "verbose" but executes *very* fast and consumes very low power — the pipeline never stalls waiting for an ALU operation that needs a memory operand in the middle of a calculation. --- ## 1.3 Key Vocabulary & Physical Metrics - **Operand** — the data value an instruction acts on. - **Effective Address** — the final calculated memory address used by Load/Store. - **Writeback** — automatically updating the base register after the transfer (denoted by `!` in ARM syntax). - **Banked registers** — physically distinct registers (e.g., R13_svc, R13_irq) that share the same *logical* name (R13) but are swapped in based on processor mode. - **Thumb-2 (Cortex-M)** — a compressed 16-bit instruction encoding that still respects the Load-Store rule. > [!TIP] > **Why it matters for KTU:** "Compare Load-Store with Register-Memory architecture" is a **favourite 7-mark question**. Memorize the table above; examiners award marks for the *three-class separation* of instructions. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Architectural Logic — Step by Step The Load-Store model is built on five engineering "pillars" that explain *why* ARM is designed this way: 1. **Decoupling Memory Bandwidth from ALU Throughput** In a CISC `ADD [A], [B]` instruction, the CPU must perform 2 memory reads + 1 memory write inside one instruction. Each memory access may take multiple cycles, leaving the ALU idle. By *forcing* operands into registers, ARM guarantees the ALU always has data ready in **1 cycle**. 2. **Fixed-Length, Decoded Instructions** Because no data-processing instruction carries a memory address, every data-processing opcode can be a uniform **32 bits**, with bits **[19:16], [15:12], [11:8], [7:0]** always used as `Rn, Rd, Operand2, opcode`. The decoder hardware is therefore tiny and the pipeline can fetch → decode → execute in a tight 3-stage (ARM7TDMI) loop. 3. **Rich Register File = Compiler Optimization Surface** With 16 visible user-mode registers (R0–R15) and many physical registers (banked), the ARM *calling convention* (AAPCS) can keep the top ~8 registers as "live variables," drastically reducing spills to memory. This is a *direct* performance consequence of the Load-Store rule. 4. **Predictable Pipeline Behaviour** Because Loads/Stores are the *only* memory operations, the pipeline's hazard detection logic is centralized. The ARM7TDMI's classic 3-stage pipeline (Fetch → Decode → Execute) and the Cortex-M3's 3-stage pipeline both depend on this property. 5. **Energy Efficiency** Memory accesses dominate dynamic power (`P ∝ C·V²·f · α`, where `α` = activity factor). By minimizing memory touches, Load-Store RISC architectures (ARM) consume roughly **1/3 the energy per instruction** of CISC equivalents — a fact directly quoted in the **KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus objectives**. --- ## 2.2 The ARM Load-Store Instruction Families ARM's Load-Store family is not just two instructions — it is a carefully designed **matrix** of width × sign × addressing-mode combinations. ### 2.2.1 Single-Register Transfers | Mnemonic | Width | Sign | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | `LDR` | 32-bit word | – | Load **W**ord | | `STR` | 32-bit word | – | Store **W**ord | | `LDRB` / `STRB` | 8-bit byte | unsigned | Load/Store **B**yte | | `LDRH` / `STRH` | 16-bit halfword | unsigned | Load/Store **H**alfword | | `LDRSB` | 8-bit | **signed** | Load Register **S**igned **B**yte | | `LDRSH` | 16-bit | **signed** | Load Register **S**igned **H**alfword | > [!NOTE] > **`LDR` and `STR` are the two true Load-Store instructions** the syllabus emphasizes; the others are width/sign variants built on the same addressing engine. ### 2.2.2 Multiple-Register Transfers | Mnemonic | Direction | Stack Use | |---|---|---| | `LDMIA` | Load Multiple — Increment After | – | | `STMIA` | Store Multiple — Increment After | – | | `LDMIB` | Increment Before | – | | `STMIB` | Increment Before | – | | `LDMDA` | Decrement After | – | | `STMDA` | Decrement After | – | | `LDMDB` | Decrement Before | – | | `STMDB` | Decrement Before | Full Descending Stack (FD) — **default for ARM/ARMCC** | Suffix synonyms used in ARM tool-chains: - `IA` ≡ `FD` (Full Ascending) - `IB` ≡ `FA` (Full Ascending — pre-incremented) - `DA` ≡ `ED` (Empty Descending) - `DB` ≡ `FD` (Full Descending — pre-decremented) ### 2.2.3 Special Transfers | Mnemonic | Function | |---|---| | `SWP` / `SWPB` | Atomic register-memory swap (deprecated in ARMv7, replaced by `LDREX/STREX` in Cortex-M) | | `LDRT` / `STRT` | User-mode **T**ranslate — used in privileged code to access *user* memory | | `LDREX` / `STREX` | **R**eserved-exclusive Load/Store (Cortex-M3 — for mutexes) | --- ## 2.3 Addressing Modes for Single Load-Store ARM offers **three indexing strategies**, each with a possible *writeback*. These are the most heavily tested topic in the Load-Store module. | Mode | Syntax | Effective Address | Writeback? | Mnemonic Suffix | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Pre-indexed** | `[Rn, #offset]` | `Rn + offset` | ❌ | (none) | | **Pre-indexed WB** | `[Rn, #offset]!` | `Rn + offset` | ✅ → `Rn = Rn + offset` | `!` | | **Post-indexed** | `[Rn], #offset` | `Rn` (uses old Rn) | ✅ → `Rn = Rn + offset` | (none) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **`offset`** can be: > - An **unsigned 12-bit immediate** (0–4095) — pre/post-indexed > - A **register (Rm)** with optional **shift** (LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR) — pre-indexed only on ARM7 > - A **register plus offset** on Thumb-2 / Cortex-M > - For PC-relative `LDR` (literal pool), the assembler synthesizes a pre-indexed `[PC, #±offset]` form. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Address Calculation Cheat-Sheet > [!TIP] > Save this table. Every numerical Load-Store problem the KTU board sets is solved by **one of these four formulas** + the *width / sign* rule. | # | Operation | Effective Address (EA) | Base Register After | Cycles (ARM7TDMI, code=memory) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `LDR Rd, [Rn]` (no offset) | $EA = Rn$ | Rn unchanged | 3 | | 2 | `LDR Rd, [Rn, #±off]` (pre-indexed) | $EA = Rn \pm \text{off}$ | Rn unchanged | 3 | | 3 | `LDR Rd, [Rn, #±off]!` (pre-indexed WB) | $EA = Rn \pm \text{off}$ | $Rn \leftarrow Rn \pm \text{off}$ | 3 | | 4 | `LDR Rd, [Rn], #±off` (post-indexed) | $EA = Rn$ | $Rn \leftarrow Rn \pm \text{off}$ | 3 | | 5 | `LDR Rd, [Rn, Rm, LSL #k]` | $EA = Rn + (Rm \ll k)$ | Rn unchanged | 3 | | 6 | `LDR Rd, [Rn, ±Rm]` | $EA = Rn \pm Rm$ | Rn unchanged | 3 | | 7 | `LDR Rd, [Rn, ±Rm]!` | $EA = Rn \pm Rm$ | $Rn \leftarrow Rn \pm Rm$ | 3 | | 8 | `LDR Rd, [Rn], ±Rm` | $EA = Rn$ | $Rn \leftarrow Rn \pm Rm$ | 3 | | 9 | `STR` of halfword / byte | uses byte/halfword bus | — | 2 (no extra cycle) | | 10 | `LDM` / `STM` n registers | Base ± 4n | Base ± 4n | n + 1 | > **Notation:** $\ll$ = logical shift left, $\pm$ = +/- per instruction, **off** is a 12-bit unsigned immediate interpreted as a *byte offset* (auto-scaled to word for word transfers). --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Bare-metal embedded C**: every `*(volatile uint32_t *)addr = value;` is *exactly* an `STR`. - **Cortex-M3 RTOS context switch**: the entire switch is a **`STMDB sp!, {r4-r11, lr}`** on entry and **`LDMIA sp!, {r4-r11, pc}^`** on exit — pure Load-Store. - **Atomic mutex**: `LDREX` + `STREX` are the only safe primitives on Cortex-M; they *must* be Load-Store or the architecture's exclusive monitor fails. - **DMA handoff**: ARM Load-Store semantics guarantee that a peripheral buffer can be safely double-buffered because the CPU never manipulates memory in-line with arithmetic. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example A — Pre-Indexed with Writeback (Board Classic)** > > Given the register & memory state below, evaluate the effect of > `LDR R0, [R1, #0xC]!` > with initial values **R1 = 0x4000_0000**. > > **Memory map:** > > | Address | 0x4000_0000 | 0x4000_0004 | 0x4000_0008 | 0x4000_000C | 0x4000_0010 | > |---|---|---|---|---|---| > | Word | 0x11 | 0x22 | 0x33 | 0x44 | 0x55 | **Step 1 — Identify the addressing mode.** The trailing `!` indicates **pre-indexed with writeback**. **Step 2 — Compute the Effective Address (EA).** Pre-indexed, so the offset is added *before* the access: $$ EA \;=\; R1 + \text{offset} \;=\; \texttt{0x40000000} + \texttt{0xC} $$ $$ EA \;=\; \texttt{0x4000000C} $$ **Step 3 — Perform the 32-bit word Load.** The CPU reads 4 contiguous bytes starting at `0x4000_000C`: $$ \text{bytes fetched} \;=\; \{ \texttt{0x44}, \texttt{00}, \texttt{00}, \texttt{00} \}_{\text{little-endian}} $$ $$ \Rightarrow\; R0 \;\leftarrow\; \texttt{0x00000044} $$ **Step 4 — Writeback (the `!`).** Because the `!` is set, R1 is automatically updated: $$ R1 \;\leftarrow\; R1 + 0xC \;=\; \texttt{0x4000000C} $$ **Step 5 — CPSR flags.** For `LDR`, the CPSR **N, Z, C, V flags are NOT modified** (only `STR` and data-processing affect them). **Final State:** - `R0 = 0x00000044` - `R1 = 0x4000_000C` (writeback) - CPSR unchanged - **3 cycles** consumed (ARM7TDMI, sequential memory) --- > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example B — Post-Indexed (the trap!)** > > Re-use the same map; evaluate `LDR R0, [R1], #0xC` with `R1 = 0x4000_0000`. **Step 1 — Mode = post-indexed.** The offset is *outside* the brackets, so it is applied **after** the load. **Step 2 — Compute EA using the *original* base.** $$ EA \;=\; R1_{\text{old}} \;=\; \texttt{0x40000000} $$ **Step 3 — Load the word.** $$ R0 \;\leftarrow\; M[\texttt{0x40000000}] \;=\; \texttt{0x00000011} $$ **Step 4 — Writeback (always present in post-indexed).** $$ R1 \;\leftarrow\; R1 + 0xC \;=\; \texttt{0x4000000C} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common student error:** Assuming post-indexed computes `Rn + offset` *first* and *then* loads. **It does not.** The mnemonic `[Rn], #off` is a *post-decrement/increment* — the address used for the transfer is the **old Rn**. --- > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example C — Byte Load with Sign-Extension** > > `LDRSB R0, [R1, #2]` where `R1 = 0x4000_0000` and memory byte at `0x4000_0002 = 0xF6`. **Step 1 — EA.** $$ EA = R1 + 2 = \texttt{0x40000002} $$ **Step 2 — Fetch 1 byte.** $$ \text{byte} = \texttt{0xF6} $$ **Step 3 — Sign-extend to 32 bits.** `0xF6` has its **MSB = 1**, so the result is sign-extended (replicated `1`s in the upper 24 bits): $$ R0 \;\leftarrow\; \texttt{0xFFFFFFF6} $$ If the same byte were loaded with **`LDRB`** (unsigned), then: $$ R0 \;\leftarrow\; \texttt{0x000000F6} $$ --- > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example D — Multiple-Register Transfer (Stack Push/Pop)** > > `STMFD sp!, {r0, r1, r2, r3}` with `SP = 0x8000`. > > `STMFD` ≡ `STMDB` with writeback (Full-Descending push). Registers are stored at the *lowest* address first. | Step | Action | Address written | Register | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | SP ← SP − 16 | `0x7FF0` | R3 | | 2 | SP ← SP − 12 | `0x7FF4` | R2 | | 3 | SP ← SP − 8 | `0x7FF8` | R1 | | 4 | SP ← SP − 4 | `0x7FFC` | R0 | | — | Writeback | **SP = 0x7FF0** | — | **Cycles** = `n + 1` (N-cycle sequential) = **5 cycles** for 4 registers on ARM7TDMI. --- ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Load-Store Form | You want to… | Best ARM form | Why | |---|---|---| | Walk through an array | `LDR Rd, [Rn], #4` | Post-indexed auto-advances pointer | | Access a *struct field* repeatedly | `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]` | Pre-indexed, no auto-update | | Implement `*p++` semantics | `LDR Rd, [Rn], #4!` | Post-indexed **with** writeback | | Read a PC-relative constant (literal pool) | `LDR Rd, [PC, #off]` | Assembler synthesizes this for `LDR =const` | | Push context on exception | `STMFD sp!, {r4-r11, lr}` | Multiple register, descending stack | | Atomic flag check-and-set | `LDREX/STREX` (Cortex-M) | Hardware exclusive monitor | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Load-Store Architecture Data-Path (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Memory
LDR / STR only] -->|read data| B[Register File
37 registers] B -->|operand A and B| C[ALU
Data-Processing] C -->|result| B B -->|write data| A D[PC / Branch Unit] -->|update PC| A style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#e2f0fd,stroke:#36c,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#e2fdd8,stroke:#3a3,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#fdf5e2,stroke:#c93,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The only arrows that cross between `Memory` and `Register File` are Load (right→left into Register File is not allowed; correction: data flows **Memory → Register File** on Load, and **Register File → Memory** on Store). The ALU never touches Memory directly — this is the **defining** property of Load-Store. ## 4.2 The Three Single-Register Addressing Modes ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Instruction] --> Q1{Offset inside
brackets?} Q1 -->|YES - Pre-indexed| Q2{Trailing '!' ?} Q1 -->|NO - Post-indexed| Post[Post-Indexed:
EA = Rn_old
Rn updated after] Q2 -->|YES| PreWB[Pre-Indexed WB:
EA = Rn + off
Rn = Rn + off after] Q2 -->|NO| Pre[Pre-Indexed:
EA = Rn + off
Rn unchanged] style Start fill:#fffacd,stroke:#888 style Q1 fill:#fff,stroke:#888 style Q2 fill:#fff,stroke:#888 style Post fill:#d8e2dc,stroke:#3a3 style PreWB fill:#dde2f5,stroke:#36c style Pre fill:#f5e2dd,stroke:#c63 ``` ## 4.3 ARM Register Bank (User + Banked) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph USER["USER / SYSTEM Mode (visible)"] R0[R0] --> R1[R1] --> R2[R2] --> R3[R3] R3 --> R4[R4] --> R5[R5] --> R6[R6] R6 --> R7[R7] --> R8[R8] --> R9[R9] R9 --> R10[R10] --> R11[R11] --> R12[R12] R12 --> R13SP[R13 / SP] --> R14LR[R14 / LR] --> R15PC[R15 / PC] end subgraph BANK["Banked by Mode (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND)"] R8FIQ[R8_fiq] --> R9FIQ[R9_fiq] R9FIQ --> R10FIQ[R10_fiq] R10FIQ --> R11FIQ[R11_fiq] R11FIQ --> R12FIQ[R12_fiq] R13SVC[R13_svc / SP_svc] --> R14SVC[R14_svc / LR_svc] R13IRQ[R13_irq / SP_irq] --> R14IRQ[R14_irq / LR_irq] R13ABT[R13_abt] --> R14ABT[R14_abt] R13UND[R13_und] --> R14UND[R14_und] SPSR[SPSR_fiq / irq / svc / abt / und] end USER -.- BANK style USER fill:#e2f0fd,stroke:#36c style BANK fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33 style SPSR fill:#fffacd,stroke:#888 ``` ## 4.4 Full-Descending Stack Frame (the most-tested diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Stack[Memory - Lower addresses DOWN] A["High Addr
(caller frame)"] --> B[SP+12: saved LR] B --> C[SP+8: saved R11] C --> D[SP+4: saved R10] D --> E["SP → low addr
(current top)"] end E -.->|STMDB sp!, {r10,r11,lr}| B style A fill:#fdf5e2,stroke:#c93 style B fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33 style C fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33 style D fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33 style E fill:#e2fdd8,stroke:#3a3 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Stack Pointer (SP) points to the *last used* location (Full-Descending). A push (`STMDB`) **decrements SP first, then writes** to the new SP. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]** *With a neat diagram, explain the Load-Store architecture of the ARM processor. Why is it preferred for embedded systems?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **1 mark** — Definition: In Load-Store architecture (used by ARM), data-processing instructions operate **only on registers**; memory is accessed **exclusively** through `LDR` (Load) and `STR` (Store) instructions. - **1 mark** — Diagram of the data path: Memory ↔ Register File ↔ ALU, where arrows from ALU to Memory are forbidden. - **1 mark** — Advantages for embedded systems: (i) deterministic 1-cycle ALU, (ii) low power (memory access minimized), (iii) small decoder → tiny silicon, (iv) easy pipelining. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]** *Differentiate between pre-indexed and post-indexed addressing modes in ARM Load-Store instructions. Give one example of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **1 mark** — Pre-indexed: EA computed *before* access; offset is inside the square brackets. Example: `LDR R0, [R1, #4]`. - **1 mark** — Post-indexed: EA is the *original* base; offset applied *after*. Example: `LDR R0, [R1], #4`. - **1 mark** — Pre-indexed with writeback (`!`): auto-updates base register; useful for array traversal. Post-indexed: always performs writeback. --- ## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice) > **ESE Pattern:** Question A **OR** Question B, both 14 marks, usually (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- ### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model)]` #### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the ARM Load-Store instruction set with the different addressing modes. Show how the effective address is calculated in each case. **Step-by-step Model Solution:** 1. **Introduction to Load-Store in ARM (1 mark):** ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) follows the **Load-Store RISC model**. The instruction set is partitioned into: - **Data Processing** — operate on registers only. - **Load / Store** — the **only** instructions that move data to/from memory. - **Branch** — change program flow. Memory operands appear **only** in Load/Store. 2. **Single-Register Transfer Instructions (2 marks):** - `LDR` Rd, … — 32-bit word load. - `STR` Rd, … — 32-bit word store. - `LDRB` / `STRB` — unsigned byte. - `LDRH` / `STRH` — unsigned half-word. - `LDRSB` / `LDRSH` — signed byte/halfword (sign-extended into Rd). Width & sign are independent of addressing mode. 3. **Three Addressing Modes (3 marks):** For a generic `LDR Rd, [Rn, #±off]!` or `LDR Rd, [Rn], #±off`: | Mode | Effective Address | Rn After | |---|---|---| | Pre-indexed `[Rn, #off]` | $EA = Rn \pm \text{off}$ | Unchanged | | Pre-indexed WB `[Rn, #off]!` | $EA = Rn \pm \text{off}$ | $Rn \leftarrow Rn \pm \text{off}$ | | Post-indexed `[Rn], #off` | $EA = Rn$ | $Rn \leftarrow Rn \pm \text{off}$ | 4. **Multiple-Register Transfers (1 mark):** `LDM` / `STM` move 1-to-16 registers. The four suffix variants (`IA`, `IB`, `DA`, `DB`) control the address pre-/post-adjustment direction. `STMDB` (= `STMFD`) is the **default push** on a Full-Descending stack. #### (b) [7 Marks] Given `R0 = 0x20000000`, evaluate and explain the result of the following instruction sequence. Show all intermediate register values. ``` LDR R1, [R0] ; word at 0x20000000 is 0x44552233 LDR R2, [R0, #4] ; word at 0x20000004 is 0x88776655 LDR R3, [R0, #8]! ; word at 0x20000008 is 0xAABBCCDD STR R1, [R0, #0xC] ; store 0x44552233 at 0x2000000C LDRB R4, [R0], #1 ; byte at 0x20000000 is 0x33 LDRSB R5, [R0], #1 ; byte at 0x20000001 is 0x22 ``` **Step-by-step Model Solution:** | Step | Instruction | EA | Effect | Final R-File | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `LDR R1,[R0]` | $0x20000000$ | $R1 \leftarrow 0x44552233$ | R1=0x44552233, R0=0x20000000 | | 2 | `LDR R2,[R0,#4]` | $0x20000004$ | $R2 \leftarrow 0x88776655$ | R2=0x88776655, R0=0x20000000 | | 3 | `LDR R3,[R0,#8]!` | $0x20000008$ | $R3 \leftarrow 0xAABBCCDD$ then $R0 \leftarrow 0x20000008$ (writeback) | R3=0xAABBCCDD, **R0=0x20000008** | | 4 | `STR R1,[R0,#0xC]` | $0x20000014$ | $M[0x20000014] \leftarrow 0x44552233$ (R0 unchanged) | R0=0x20000008, R1=0x44552233 | | 5 | `LDRB R4,[R0],#1` | $0x20000008$ | Byte = 0xDD → R4=0x000000DD; **R0 ← 0x20000009** (post-indexed writeback) | R4=0x000000DD, R0=0x20000009 | | 6 | `LDRSB R5,[R0],#1` | $0x20000009$ | Byte = 0xCC, MSB=1 → R5=0xFFFFFFCC; **R0 ← 0x2000000A** | R5=0xFFFFFFCC, R0=0x2000000A | **Final State:** `R0=0x2000000A, R1=0x44552233, R2=0x88776655, R3=0xAABBCCDD, R4=0x000000DD, R5=0xFFFFFFCC`. **Bonus mark-worthy observation:** The big-endian little-endian **byte ordering is little-endian** (lowest byte at lowest address — standard for ARM/Thumb-2). Students often miss this. --- ### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Model)]` #### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the three classes of ARM instructions. How does the Load-Store classification enable a simple, fast pipeline? **Model Solution:** 1. **Class 1 — Data Processing (2 marks):** Instructions like `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `ORR`, `EOR`, `MUL`. Operands come from registers only; result goes to a register. Format: `opcode Rd, Rn, Operand2`. **No memory access** is ever performed. 2. **Class 2 — Load / Store (3 marks):** `LDR`, `STR`, `LDRB`, `STRB`, `LDM`, `STM`, etc. The only instructions that read/write memory. Use three indexing modes. All these instructions are confined to the **Execute stage** of the pipeline (no ALU work other than address arithmetic). 3. **Class 3 — Branch (2 marks):** `B`, `BL`, `BX`, `BLX`. Change PC based on a 24-bit signed offset or a register. Do not touch general-purpose memory. **Pipeline Enablement:** Because data-processing instructions never touch memory, the **Execute (EX)** stage of the ARM7TDMI's 3-stage pipeline can complete arithmetic in **one cycle**, while Loads/Stores in the EX stage stall the pipeline for memory access. The two are **cleanly separated** so the pipeline never has to perform ALU + memory in the same cycle. This regularity is why ARM cores can run at hundreds of MHz on milliwatts of power. #### (b) [7 Marks] A C function `int sum_array(int *a, int n)` is to be compiled for ARM7TDMI. Write the equivalent ARM assembly using Load-Store instructions only. Show how the Load-Store model forces the registers to act as a "data cache" between memory and ALU. **Model Solution:** ```asm ; AAPCS: a -> R0, n -> R1, return -> R0 sum_array: MOV R2, R0 ; R2 = pointer to a[] (cached) MOV R3, #0 ; R3 = running sum (cached) CMP R1, #0 BXEQ LR ; if n == 0, return 0 loop: LDR R4, [R2], #4 ; LOAD word from a[i], post-incr pointer ADD R3, R3, R4 ; ALU: sum += a[i] (register-register) SUBS R1, R1, #1 ; n--, set flags BNE loop ; branch if n != 0 MOV R0, R3 ; return value BX LR ``` **Explanation of the Load-Store "data cache" role (3 marks):** - `R4` is loaded from memory **once per iteration** via `LDR`, then used by the **register-only** `ADD R3, R3, R4`. The CPU never executes `ADD [R2], [something]`; each value is *cached* in a register for the ALU to consume in 1 cycle. - `R2` is updated via **post-indexed writeback** `[R2], #4`, the canonical Load-Store idiom for pointer-walking. - Without a Load-Store architecture, this loop would need complex `ADD [mem], [mem]` microcode; with Load-Store, it compiles to 5 clean instructions in 5 cycles per iteration (plus 1 cycle memory access = ~6 cycles/element). --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in Load-Store questions:** > > 1. **Confusing pre-indexed with post-indexed writeback** (loses ~3 marks). Memory aid: *Inside brackets → applied BEFORE; outside brackets → applied AFTER.* > 2. **Forgetting the `!` effect on the base register** in `LDR R0,[R1,#4]!` — *always* update R1 in your trace. > 3. **Sign-extension errors**: `LDRB` zero-extends; `LDRSB` sign-extends. If the byte is 0xF6, you *must* get 0xFFFFFFF6, not 0x000000F6. > 4. **Byte/halfword writes on ARM7**: `STRB` does NOT modify the *other* 3 bytes of the word in memory; it writes only 1 byte. (ARMv6+ `STRB` is byte-strict.) > 5. **Pipeline cycles**: KTU often asks "how many cycles?" — answer must be `n+1` for `LDM`/`STM` of n registers in sequential memory (or `2n+1` for non-sequential), not "3 cycles per register". > 6. **CPSR flags**: Loads/Stores do **NOT** set condition flags; only data-processing and `STR` with `S` bit (in Thumb-2) do. A trace that shows `N=1, Z=0` after a `LDR` is **wrong**. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Load-Store Rule (the cardinal principle)**: ALU ⇆ Register File only. Memory ⇆ Register File only via `LDR` / `STR`. - **Three addressing modes** of single Load/Store: * Pre-indexed `[Rn, #off]` — no writeback * Pre-indexed WB `[Rn, #off]!` — writeback * Post-indexed `[Rn], #off` — always writeback, EA = old Rn - **Width & Sign variants**: `LDR/STR` (W), `LDRB/STRB` (B), `LDRH/STRH` (H), `LDRSB/LDRSH` (signed). - **Register file**: 37 physical 32-bit registers; user-mode sees R0–R15; R13=SP, R14=LR, R15=PC. - **Banked registers** exist for FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND; each has its own R13/R14 plus an SPSR. - **Multiple transfers**: `LDM/STM` with `IA/IB/DA/DB`; **Full-Descending** stack (`STMDB/LDMIA`) is the ARM ABI default. - **Atomic transfers**: `SWP` (legacy) and `LDREX/STREX` (Cortex-M) — the only safe primitives for shared-memory mutex. - **Cycle counts (ARM7TDMI)**: Single LDR/STR = 3 cycles; LDM/STM of n registers = n+1 (sequential) or 2n+1 (non-sequential). - **CPSR impact**: LDR/STR do NOT alter N, Z, C, V flags. - **Thumb-2 reminder**: 16-bit Thumb Load/Stores still obey the Load-Store rule; some restrictions on register pairs apply. - **"Memory operand count"** = number of memory references in the *entire* program — Load-Store minimises this to exactly the count of LDR/STR instructions, lowering energy. > **End of Module 2 / Load-Store notes.**

topics.title: SWI and Program Status Register Instruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SWI and Program Status Register Instruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SWI and Program Status Register Instruction

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Software Interrupt (SWI) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Software Interrupt (SWI)** is an ARM7TDMI instruction that explicitly generates a software interrupt exception. When executed, the processor enters **Supervisor (SVC) mode**, saves the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** into the **Saved Program Status Register (SPSR_SVC)**, stores the return address into **LR_SVC**, and branches to the exception vector address **0x00000008**. The instruction carries a 24-bit *comment field* used by the exception handler to identify which system service is being requested.

**Syntax (KTU Board Standard):**

$$\text{SWI}\{\text{cond}\} \quad \#\text{immediate}_{24}$$

- The instruction is **conditionally executable** (e.g., `SWIEQ #0x05`).
- The operand is a **24-bit unsigned value** (0x000000 to 0xFFFFFF).
- Encoding occupies bits [23:0] of the instruction word; bits [27:24] = `1111` (condition field `cond`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** SWI is the ARM7 mechanism through which a **User-mode application** safely requests services from the **Operating System kernel** without requiring direct access to privileged hardware registers. This enforces *protected-mode programming*.

---

### 1.2 Program Status Register Instructions — Formal Definition

The ARM core exposes the CPSR and the banked SPSR through two dedicated instructions:

| Instruction | Full Name | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **MRS** | Move from Status Register to general-purpose Register | **Read** (PSR → Register) |
| **MSR** | Move from general-purpose Register to Status Register | **Write** (Register → PSR) |

**Syntax:**

$$\text{MRS}\{\text{cond}\} \quad Rd, \;\text{(CPSR \vert SPSR)}$$

$$\text{MSR}\{\text{cond}\} \quad \text{(CPSR \vert SPSR)}_{\text{\_field}}, \quad Rm$$

$$\text{MSR}\{\text{cond}\} \quad \text{(CPSR \vert SPSR)}_{\text{\_field}}, \quad \#\text{immediate}$$

The *field* operand is one of four byte-mask qualifiers:
- **c** — control byte (bits [7:0])
- **x** — extension byte (bits [15:8])
- **s** — status byte (bits [23:16])
- **f** — flags byte (bits [31:24])

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — SWI as a "Service Bell":**

Imagine you are seated in a hotel room (User-mode). You want extra towels, room service, or a wake-up call. You are *not allowed* to enter the back-of-house staff areas directly. Instead, you press the **service bell** mounted on the wall. The bell rings at the front desk (the OS kernel in Supervisor mode), and a staff member responds. You describe *what* you need by saying "I need towels" — this is the **24-bit comment field** of SWI. Once the service is delivered, you resume your normal activity. SWI works exactly this way: a User-mode program "rings the bell" using `SWI #0xNN`, the kernel jumps to address `0x08`, identifies the requested service from the comment field, executes it, and returns control back to the user program.

**Analogy 2 — MRS/MSR as a "Dashboard Read/Write":**

The CPSR is the **dashboard of a car** — it displays speed, fuel, gear, engine-temperature (these are status flags and control bits). 
- **MRS** = *looking at* the dashboard and copying its readings into a clipboard (a general-purpose register) for later inspection. 
- **MSR** = *writing back* to the dashboard (e.g., changing the gear or turning on a warning light), but only the **authorized** dashboard knobs (the *field* qualifiers) can be touched.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privileged Rule (KTU High-Yield):** In **User mode**, MSR can update **only the condition flags (f-field)**. The control, extension, and status bytes are read-only in User mode. Violating this rule causes the ARM7 core to **ignore** the write silently — this is by design, for OS protection.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold-Formatted)

- **SWI Exception Vector Address:** **0x00000008** (in standard ARM7 vectored mode)
- **CPSR Mode Bits for Supervisor:** **0b10011** (binary) = **0x13** (hex)
- **CPSR I-bit (IRQ disable) location:** **bit 7**
- **CPSR F-bit (FIQ disable) location:** **bit 6**
- **CPSR T-bit (Thumb state) location:** **bit 5**
- **Condition Flag Bits:** **bits [31:28]** = **N, Z, C, V**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SWI — Operational Breakdown

When the ARM7 core decodes an SWI instruction, the following **atomic sequence** occurs on the *exact same clock cycle* that the instruction completes:

1. **Mode Switch:** The M[4:0] bits of CPSR are forced to `0b10011` (Supervisor mode).
2. **State Save:** The current CPSR is copied *byte-by-byte* into **SPSR_SVC**.
3. **Link Save:** `LR_SVC ← PC + 4` (the address of the instruction *after* SWI).
4. **IRQ Masking:** The **I-bit** (bit 7) of CPSR is **set to 1** to prevent nested IRQ during SWI handling.
5. **PC Reload:** `PC ← 0x00000008` (the SWI exception vector).
6. **Thumb Bit:** The **T-bit** of CPSR is **cleared to 0** (forcing ARM state for the handler).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does LR_SVC get PC+4 and not PC+8?** In ARM state, every instruction is 4 bytes. PC reads as `current instruction + 8` during execution (a pipeline artefact). Thus `LR = PC − 4` *after* the pipeline advance equals the instruction *following* the SWI. The kernel uses `SUBS PC, LR_SVC, #4` to return.

## 2.2 Extracting the SWI Comment Field (Kernel Side)

The kernel handler at `0x08` must determine *which* service the user requested. It does this by reading back the SWI instruction itself from memory at address `LR_SVC − 4`:

$$\text{SWI\_Number} = \text{Memory}[\text{LR\_SVC} - 4] \;\&\; \; 0x00FFFFFF$$

The bitwise AND with `0x00FFFFFF` masks away the upper 8 bits (condition code + opcode `1111`), leaving only the **24-bit immediate operand**.

## 2.3 Program Status Register Instructions — Deep Mechanics

### 2.3.1 MRS (Read Path)

MRS is the *only* sanctioned way to read the CPSR or SPSR into a general-purpose register. It does **not** affect any flags or modify memory.

**Use Cases:**
- Read condition flags before a critical decision (`MRS R0, CPSR`).
- Read the current mode bits to determine privilege level.
- Save CPSR context into a stack frame for context-switching.

### 2.3.2 MSR (Write Path)

MSR writes to *selected bytes* of the CPSR or SPSR using the field qualifier. It does **not** set condition flags based on the written value.

**Field Mask Truth Table (ARM7TDMI):**

| Field Qualifier | Bits Affected | Bits [31:24] Flags | Bits [23:16] Status | Bits [15:8] Extension | Bits [7:0] Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **f** (flags)     | [31:24] | ✓ (N,Z,C,V) | — | — | — |
| **s** (status)    | [23:16] | — | ✓ (reserved/J) | — | — |
| **x** (extension) | [15:8]  | — | — | ✓ (reserved) | — |
| **c** (control)   | [7:0]   | — | — | — | ✓ (I,F,T,M[4:0]) |
| **fs**            | [31:16] | ✓ | ✓ | — | — |
| **fc**            | [31:24, 7:0] | ✓ | — | — | ✓

topics.title: Introduction to Thumb
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Thumb

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Thumb instruction set** is a **16-bit compressed instruction set** developed by ARM Holdings as an extension to the standard 32-bit ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) instruction set. It was introduced in the ARM7TDMI processor and has since evolved into **Thumb-2**, which is a superset blending both 16-bit and 32-bit instructions. In modern ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers (Cortex-M0, M0+, M3, M4, M7), Thumb-2 is the **only natively supported instruction set**, providing an optimal trade-off between **code density, performance, and power efficiency**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 PECPT631 syllabus, the Thumb instruction set is treated as the **core ISA (Instruction Set Architecture)** for embedded ARM programming. Students must clearly distinguish between ARM state and Thumb state, understand the branch-overhead mechanics (BLX, BX), and interpret the conditional execution differences.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are packing for a long trip:

- The **ARM (32-bit) instruction set** is like using **large, fully-packed suitcases**. Each suitcase has powerful capabilities (rich addressing modes, conditional execution on every instruction, barrel shifter, etc.), but they take up a lot of room in the cargo hold.
- The **Thumb (16-bit) instruction set** is like using **smaller, space-efficient backpacks**. You sacrifice some features (e.g., conditional execution is limited, fewer addressing modes), but you can pack **almost twice as many instructions in the same memory space**, which is critical for cost-sensitive embedded systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Significance:** A typical Cortex-M3 firmware compiled in Thumb-2 consumes approximately **65%–70% of the memory** required by equivalent ARM (32-bit) code, while delivering nearly identical performance. This is why virtually all modern IoT devices, automotive ECUs, wearables, and drones rely on Thumb-2.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Bold Highlight)

- **Instruction width:** 16 bits (Thumb-1) / mix of 16-bit and 32-bit (Thumb-2)
- **Register file:** Same 16 general-purpose registers (R0–R12), plus SP (R13), LR (R14), PC (R15), and xPSR
- **Code density improvement:** ~ **30%–35%** over ARM mode for Thumb-1; up to **40%+** for Thumb-2
- **Performance penalty (Thumb-1 only):** roughly **15%–20%** slower than ARM mode on the same silicon

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Was Thumb Invented? — The Engineering Motivation

The original ARM7TDMI processor (1994) was designed for early mobile phones and PDAs. Engineers faced three harsh constraints:

1. **Memory was extremely expensive** — a single 32-bit instruction cost **4 bytes** of precious ROM/RAM.
2. **Bus width was often only 16 or 32 bits** — fetching 32-bit instructions on a 16-bit memory bus required **two sequential fetches**, slowing execution.
3. **Power budget was tight** — wider fetches meant more dynamic switching power.

Thumb solved all three by halving the instruction size while preserving access to the same 32-bit register file.

### 2.2 ARM State vs Thumb State — Core Mechanics

The processor executes instructions in one of **two states**, tracked by a flag in the **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)**, specifically the **T-bit**:

- **T = 0 → ARM state:** instructions are 32 bits wide.
- **T = 1 → Thumb state:** instructions are 16 bits wide (Thumb-1) or 16/32-bit mix (Thumb-2).

Switching between states is performed using the **branch-and-exchange (BX)** or **branch-with-link-and-exchange (BLX)** instructions.

### 2.3 How Thumb Instructions Operate — Structured Logic

- **Operand encoding is compressed:** Thumb encoders reuse fields (e.g., immediate constants are split across instruction pairs or use barrel-shifter-free immediates).
- **Most arithmetic/logic operations access only the lower 8 registers (R0–R7).** Accessing R8–R12 requires longer encodings (e.g., `ADD Rd, Rn` only works on low registers).
- **Conditional execution is restricted:** Only the branch instruction `B<cond>` is conditionally executed; data-processing instructions are **always unconditional** in Thumb-1.
- **Endianness is explicit:** All Cortex-M cores use **little-endian** byte ordering by default.

### 2.4 Thumb-2 Evolution — The Modern Reality

Thumb-2 (introduced with ARM1156 in 2003) merges:

- A rich set of **16-bit instructions** for density.
- Powerful **32-bit instructions** (e.g., `IT`, `CBZ`, `CBNZ`, `MOVW/MOVT`, `LDM/STM`, conditional data-processing).

The result is that **Cortex-M3/M4/M7 programmers no longer switch states** — they live entirely in Thumb-2 state. This unified ISA is sometimes called the **"Unified Assembly Language (UAL)."**

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Rule | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thumb-1 code density | $\text{Density}_{thumb} \approx 1.6 \times \text{Density}_{ARM}$ | dimensionless ratio | Valid only for code-dominated workloads |
| Code size reduction | $\Delta S = \dfrac{S_{ARM} - S_{Thumb}}{S_{ARM}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Typically **30%–35%** |
| Effective memory cost per instruction | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{N_{16b} \cdot 2 + N_{32b} \cdot 4}{N_{16b} + N_{32b}}$ | bytes/instruction | Thumb-2 average $\approx 2.3$ bytes |
| State-switch instruction overhead | $T_{switch} = N_{cycles} \cdot T_{clk}$ | seconds | BLX/BX = **3 cycles** on Cortex-M3 |
| Branch range (Thumb B) | $-2048 \leq \text{offset} \leq +2046$ | bytes | PC-relative, halfword-aligned |
| Branch range (Thumb-2 B) | $-16\,\text{MB} \leq \text{offset} \leq +16\,\text{MB}$ | bytes | PC-relative, halfword-aligned |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Automotive ECU developers using Cortex-M4F routinely achieve **under 64 KB of firmware** using Thumb-2, allowing the use of cheaper **64 KB Flash microcontrollers** instead of **128 KB Flash** parts — a direct BOM cost reduction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Code Size Reduction When Migrating ARM → Thumb

We model a program containing $I$ total instructions. Let the ARM-coded version require $S_{ARM}$ bytes of memory.

**Step 1: Define the size of the ARM-coded binary.**

$$
S_{ARM} = I \times 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

*Explanation:* Every ARM instruction is 32 bits wide, which equals **4 bytes**. Multiplying by the instruction count gives the total memory footprint in bytes.

---

**Step 2: Define the size of the Thumb-1-coded binary.**

$$
S_{Thumb} = I \times 2 \text{ bytes}
$$

*Explanation:* Every Thumb-1 instruction is 16 bits wide, which equals **2 bytes**. For the same instruction count $I$, the binary is exactly half the size.

---

**Step 3: Compute the absolute size saving.**

$$
\Delta S_{bytes} = S_{ARM} - S_{Thumb} = I \times 4 - I \times 2 = 2I
$$

*Explanation:* The difference simplifies algebraically. Each Thumb-1 instruction saves exactly **2 bytes** of memory compared to its ARM equivalent.

---

**Step 4: Compute the percentage reduction.**

$$
\Delta S_{\%} = \frac{S_{ARM} - S_{Thumb}}{S_{ARM}} \times 100
$$

Substituting $S_{ARM} = 4I$ and $S_{Thumb} = 2I$:

$$
\Delta S_{\%} = \frac{4I - 2I}{4I} \times 100 = \frac{2I}{4I} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

*Explanation:* The instruction count $I$ cancels out. Thumb-1 in theory saves up to **50%** memory. In practice, branch overhead and literal pools reduce this to **30%–35%**.

---

**Step 5: Thumb-2 Mixed-Width Refinement.**

For Thumb-2, let $N_{16}$ be the count of 16-bit instructions and $N_{32}$ be the count of 32-bit instructions, with $I = N_{16} + N_{32}$.

$$
S_{Thumb2} = 2 N_{16} + 4 N_{32}
$$

The average bytes per instruction is:

$$
\bar{B} = \frac{S_{Thumb2}}{I} = \frac{2 N_{16} + 4 N_{32}}{N_{16} + N_{32}}
$$

Let $r = \dfrac{N_{32}}{N_{16}}$ be the ratio of 32-bit to 16-bit instructions. Then:

$$
\bar{B} = \frac{2 + 4r}{1 + r}
$$

*Explanation:* This ratio $r$ depends on the compiler's optimization mode. Aggressive optimization (`-O2`/`-Os` in GCC-ARM) drives $r$ lower, pushing $\bar{B}$ closer to **2.0 bytes** (maximum density).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

A firmware engineer writes **8000 instructions** for a sensor node. In ARM mode it uses **32 KB** of Flash. After migration to Thumb-2, profiling shows **75% of instructions are 16-bit** and **25% are 32-bit**.

**Step 1:** Compute $N_{16}$ and $N_{32}$.

$$
N_{16} = 0.75 \times 8000 = 6000
$$

$$
N_{32} = 0.25 \times 8000 = 2000
$$

---

**Step 2:** Compute total Thumb-2 memory footprint.

$$
S_{Thumb2} = 2(6000) + 4(2000) = 12000 + 8000 = 20000 \text{ bytes}
$$

---

**Step 3:** Convert to KB.

$$
S_{Thumb2} = \frac{20000}{1024} \approx 19.53 \text{ KB}
$$

---

**Step 4:** Compute percentage savings vs ARM.

$$
\Delta S_{\%} = \frac{32\,768 - 20\,000}{32\,768} \times 100 \approx 38.96\%
$$

*Explanation:* The firmware now fits in a **20 KB Flash** MCU instead of **32 KB**, enabling selection of a cheaper microcontroller — a tangible engineering win.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ISA Hierarchy and State Transition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ARM Processor Core CPSR T-bit] --> B{T = 0 ?}
    B -- Yes --> C[ARM State: 32-bit Instructions]
    B -- No --> D[Thumb State: 16/32-bit Instructions]
    C --> E[Execute ARM Mnemonics ADD, SUB, LDR, STR with full conditional codes]
    D --> F[Thumb-1 Legacy: 16-bit only density-optimized]
    D --> G[Thumb-2 Modern: 16/32-bit mix UAL syntax]
    F --> H[Switch via BX or BLX instruction]
    G --> H
    C --> H
    H --> A
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Thumb Decoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Flash Memory 16-bit Bus] --> F1[Instruction Fetch Unit IFU]
    F1 --> F2[Instruction Queue IQ]
    F2 --> D1{Opcode Decoder}
    D1 -- Bits 15-11 indicate 16-bit --> P1[16-bit Decoder Path]
    D1 -- Bits 15-11 indicate 32-bit --> P2[32-bit Decoder Path Fetches Next Halfword]
    P1 --> R1[Register Bank R0-R12 SP LR PC xPSR]
    P2 --> R1
    R1 --> EX[Execute Stage ALU MAC FPU on M4F]
    EX --> WB[Write-Back Stage]
    WB --> M1
```

### 4.3 Instruction-Width Encoding Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Decode Opcode Bits 15-11] --> S1{First Hex Digit of Opcode}
    S1 -- "E.g. 0xE in ARM" --> S2[Classify as 32-bit Thumb-2 Instruction]
    S1 -- "E.g. 0x4 low nibble in Thumb" --> S3[Classify as 16-bit Thumb Instruction]
    S2 --> S4[Perform Wide Decode: Conditional Data-Proc, IT, MOVW MOVT]
    S3 --> S5[Perform Narrow Decode: ADD SUB MOV LDR STR Reg-Imm]
    S4 --> S6[Execute]
    S5 --> S6
```

### 4.4 End-to-End Firmware Flow Using Thumb-2 (Cortex-M3 Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application C Source Code] --> B[ARM-GCC or Keil Compiler]
    B --> C[Compiler Chooses Thumb-2 Encoding]
    C --> D[Assembler Produces ELF Object File]
    D --> E[Linker Places Code at 0x08000000 Flash]
    E --> F[Binary Hex Intel-HEX or BIN]
    F --> G[Programmed into MCU Flash via SWD or JTAG]
    G --> H[Boot Code Initializes SP then enters Thumb-2 Mode]
    H --> I[Application Begins Execution]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Define the Thumb instruction set. Mention any two key differences between the ARM and Thumb instruction sets.**

**Model Answer:**
The Thumb instruction set is a **16-bit compressed encoding** of a subset of the ARM instruction set, designed to improve code density in memory-constrained embedded systems. It operates in Thumb state (CPSR T-bit = 1).

Two key differences:

1. **Instruction width:** ARM uses **32-bit fixed-width** instructions; Thumb uses **16-bit (Thumb-1)** or a mix of **16/32-bit (Thumb-2)** instructions.
2. **Conditional execution:** In ARM, every data-processing instruction can be made conditional using a condition code suffix (e.g., `ADDEQ`, `SUBNE`). In Thumb-1, **only the branch instruction `B<cond>`** supports conditional execution; data-processing instructions are always unconditional.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**How is the processor mode switched between ARM state and Thumb state? Explain the role of the BX instruction.**

**Model Answer:**
The processor mode is controlled by the **T-bit of the CPSR** (Current Program Status Register).

- T = 0 → ARM state (32-bit instructions).
- T = 1 → Thumb state (16-bit instructions).

Mode switching is achieved by executing a **branch-and-exchange (BX)** or **branch-with-link-and-exchange (BLX)** instruction. The BX instruction performs two operations in a single cycle:

1. It loads the **target address** into the PC (R15).
2. It sets the **LSB of the target address** into the **T-bit** of CPSR.

If the LSB is **0**, the processor enters ARM state. If the LSB is **1**, the processor enters Thumb state. In C code, programmers simply call a function and the compiler emits the correct BX/BLX automatically.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks (typically 7 + 7).

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain with a neat diagram the architecture of the ARM processor in Thumb state. Discuss the major advantages and disadvantages of using Thumb code over ARM code.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Overview:**
In Thumb state, the same ARM core pipeline is used, but the **instruction fetch unit** operates in a 16-bit mode. The figure below summarises the data path:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROM[Flash 16-bit data bus] --> IFU[Instruction Fetch Unit 16-bit halfword fetches]
    IFU --> IQ[Instruction Queue]
    IQ --> DEC[Thumb Decoder 16 or 32-bit expansion]
    DEC --> REG[Register Bank R0-R12 identical to ARM]
    REG --> ALU[ALU and Barrel Shifter]
    ALU --> REG
    REG --> MEM[Load Store Unit accessing data memory]
```

**Key architectural points:**

1. The **register file remains identical** — 16 general-purpose 32-bit registers (R0–R15) plus the CPSR/SPSR.
2. The **ALU and barrel shifter** are reused as-is.
3. Thumb instructions are **decompressed** by the front-end decoder into ARM-like internal micro-operations, allowing the same execution pipeline to be used.
4. Branch instructions are **halfword-aligned** rather than word-aligned.

**Advantages of Thumb:**

- **Higher code density** — typical **30%–35%** reduction in memory footprint (up to 50% theoretically).
- **Lower memory cost** — fewer bytes fetched means cheaper Flash/RAM in BOM.
- **Lower power consumption** — narrower fetches reduce dynamic switching power on the bus.
- **Better performance on 16-bit memory buses** — single-cycle fetch possible.

**Disadvantages of Thumb:**

- **Reduced instruction functionality** — fewer addressing modes, no inline barrel shifter on most operands.
- **Restricted conditional execution** — only branch instructions can be conditional in Thumb-1.
- **State-switch overhead** — interworking between ARM and Thumb subroutines costs a few cycles.
- **Limited immediate range** — many instructions use shorter immediates, sometimes requiring multiple instructions.

---

**(b) With a suitable example, explain the BLX instruction and show how it is used for ARM-Thumb interworking in an embedded C program.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **BLX (Branch with Link and eXchange)** instruction performs four operations atomically:

1. Stores the return address (PC + 4) into the Link Register **LR (R14)**.
2. Loads the target address into **PC**.
3. Sets the **CPSR T-bit** based on the LSB of the target address.
4. Optionally switches instruction set between ARM and Thumb.

**Encoding forms:**

- `BLX <Rm>` — register form, target address in register `Rm`, LSB determines state.
- `BLX <label>` — label form (Thumb-2 only), allows state switch to ARM unconditionally.

**C-level interworking example:**

```c
// File: main.c (compiled in ARM mode)
extern void thumb_routine(void);   // lives in Thumb state

void arm_caller(void) {
    int x = 10;
    thumb_routine();              // compiler emits BLX to switch
    // LR holds return address; T-bit is automatically set to 1
}

// File: thumb_routine.c (compiled with -mthumb)
__attribute__((naked)) void thumb_routine(void) {
    // ... Thumb code runs here ...
    BX LR;                        // return to ARM mode in caller
}
```

The compiler automatically inserts the **BLX** at the call site and a **BX LR** at the return. The LSB of the called function's address (`...1`) tells the core to enter Thumb state.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Students often forget that the **LSB of the address** is the **mode-select bit**, not a real address bit. In practice, ARM linker scripts enforce that Thumb functions have addresses ending in `...1` and ARM functions end in `...0`.
> 2. Many students wrongly state that `BLX` switches only one way. **BLX is bidirectional** — it can switch from ARM → Thumb AND Thumb → ARM.
> 3. Forgetting to save/restore LR during nested calls is a common mark-losing error. Always use `PUSH {LR}` / `POP {PC}`.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) Describe the Thumb-2 instruction set. Explain how it differs from Thumb-1 and why modern Cortex-M microcontrollers use it exclusively.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Thumb-2 is a **superset of Thumb-1** introduced by ARM in 2003 (first implemented in the ARM1156T2-S core). It blends 16-bit and 32-bit instructions into a **Unified Assembly Language (UAL)**, eliminating the need to switch states manually.

**Key differences from Thumb-1:**

1. **Instruction-width flexibility** — Thumb-2 introduces around **200 new 32-bit instructions** (e.g., `BL`, `IT`, `CBNZ`, `CBZ`, `MOVW`, `MOVT`, `LDM`, `STM`, conditional data-processing).
2. **Conditional execution of data-processing instructions** — the new `IT` (If-Then) block allows up to **4 conditional instructions** to follow an initial `IT` instruction.
3. **Branch range expansion** — Thumb-1 branches were limited to ± 2046 bytes; Thumb-2 `B` supports ± 16 MB.
4. **Bit-field instructions** — `BFI`, `BFC`, `UBFX`, `SBFX` enable efficient packing and unpacking of bit data.
5. **No state switching** — all code runs in Thumb state permanently.

**Why Cortex-M uses it exclusively:**

- **Best code density of any ARM ISA** — average bytes-per-instruction near **2.0–2.3**.
- **Performance comparable to ARM mode** — modern Cortex-M3/M4 pipelines can issue 32-bit Thumb-2 instructions in a single cycle.
- **Simplified toolchain** — programmers and compiler backends need not track state transitions.
- **Deterministic interrupt latency** — important for hard real-time systems; no pipeline flush needed when entering ISRs because there is only one state.

---

**(b) Compare the encoding formats of a typical ARM `ADD` instruction and a Thumb `ADD` instruction. Show the halfword-level bit fields and explain why Thumb encoding is denser yet less expressive.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**ARM Encoding (`ADD Rd, Rn, Rm`):**

The instruction is **32 bits wide** and is laid out as:

| Bits 31-28 | Bits 27-26 | Bits 25-20 | Bits 19-16 | Bits 15-12 | Bits 11-8 | Bits 7-4 | Bits 3-0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| cond | 00 | I=0 | opcode | S | Rn | Rd | Rm |
| **4 bits** | **2 bits** | **6 bits** | **4 bits** | **1 bit** | **4 bits** | **4 bits** | **4 bits** |

Total = 32 bits. Supports conditional execution (`cond`), shifter operand (`I=0` register, `I=1` immediate), and `S` flag update.

**Thumb Encoding (`ADD Rd, Rn, Rm`):**

The instruction is **16 bits wide** and is laid out as:

| Bits 15-9 | Bits 8-6 | Bits 5-3 | Bits 2-0 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 00011 0 | opcode | Rn | Rd |
| **7 bits** | **3 bits** | **3 bits** | **3 bits** |

Total = 16 bits. **Note:** `Rm` is **implicit** in the opcode — only ADD with low registers (R0–R7) and immediate forms are encodable. Access to R8–R12 requires a different (longer) instruction.

**Why Thumb is denser yet less expressive:**

- **Density:** Halving the bit count per instruction roughly halves the program size.
- **Less expressive:** The 16-bit field can encode only **3 bits per register** (8 combinations), forcing access to high registers through specialised instructions. There is **no `cond` field**, **no shifter operand**, and **no `S` flag** — so Thumb must compose multiple instructions to achieve what one ARM instruction does.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Question B):**
> 1. Students often confuse the **opcode field size** between ARM (4 bits) and Thumb (3 bits). Memorise this; it is a frequent 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. Do not claim that Thumb-2 has *only* 16-bit instructions. It has a **mix** of 16-bit and 32-bit encodings.
> 3. Avoid stating "Thumb is faster than ARM." The correct statement is: "Thumb is **denser**; ARM is **more expressive per instruction**."

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thumb = 16-bit compressed instruction set** of ARM, designed for memory-constrained embedded systems.
- **State controlled by CPSR T-bit:** T=0 → ARM, T=1 → Thumb.
- **Thumb-1** uses 16-bit-only encodings; **Thumb-2** mixes 16-bit and 32-bit encodings (UAL).
- **Cortex-M series executes Thumb-2 exclusively**; no manual state switching is required.
- **BX / BLX** instructions perform branch + state switch in one atomic operation; LSB of target address = new T-bit value.
- **Code density gain:** ~30–35% for Thumb-1, up to **40%+** for Thumb-2 in well-optimised builds.
- **Conditional execution:** All ARM data-processing ops can be conditional; **only branches** are conditional in Thumb-1; **Thumb-2 adds `IT` blocks** to bring conditional data-processing back.
- **Branch range:** ±2046 bytes in Thumb-1 `B`; ±16 MB in Thumb-2 `B`.
- **Register access:** Thumb-1 main ALU ops restricted to **R0–R7** (low registers); high registers require specific instructions.
- **Interworking tip:** Compiler handles BLX/BX automatically; programmers just write C functions with appropriate `__attribute__((naked))` only when writing assembly stubs.
- **Real-world win:** Thumb-2 enables smaller Flash, lower BOM cost, lower power — the dominant ISA in IoT, wearables, automotive ECUs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differences Between ARM and Thumb
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Differences Between ARM and Thumb # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The ARM processor architecture is a family of **Reduced Instruction Set Computing (RISC)** architectures developed by **Arm Holdings**. A fundamental architectural feature of the ARM processor is its ability to operate in **two distinct instruction sets** (or *instruction set states*): - **ARM State (32-bit)**: Executes 32-bit, fixed-width aligned instructions. This is the default and native execution state of the processor. - **Thumb State (16-bit)**: Executes 16-bit, half-word aligned compressed instructions. The Thumb Instruction Set (TISA) was introduced in the **ARM7TDMI** core to improve code density in memory-constrained systems. When the processor is in Thumb state, instructions are decompressed (expanded) by a **Thumb decompressor** inside the processor's instruction pipeline, before being processed by the standard ARM pipeline stages (Fetch → Decode → Execute). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ARM processor core has a single physical register file (R0–R15, CPSR/SPSR), but the *interpretation* of certain registers changes between ARM and Thumb states. For example, in ARM state, R15 is the **Program Counter (PC)** and R14 is the **Link Register (LR)**; in Thumb state, their fundamental roles remain the same, but the way they are accessed and manipulated differs. > [!NOTE] > **Advanced Note (Beyond Syllabus):** ARMv8-A (Cortex-A series) introduced **AArch64 (A64)**, a 64-bit instruction set. AArch32 (ARM and Thumb-2) is supported in compatibility mode. For the KTU PECPT631 syllabus (which focuses on ARM7TDMI/Cortex-M3), 32-bit ARM and 16-bit Thumb are the primary focus. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Think of the ARM processor as a **multilingual translator working in a courtroom**: - **ARM State (32-bit)** is like the **detailed, formal legal language** — every instruction is verbose, explicit, and contains all the information needed in a single, complete sentence. It's powerful and precise, but takes up a lot of "paper" (memory space). - **Thumb State (16-bit)** is like a **concise shorthand or legal abbreviation** — it uses shorter symbols that stand for longer statements. The judge (the processor pipeline) has a small **decoder book** that instantly translates these shorthand symbols into the full meaning before ruling. When memory is expensive (in embedded systems, every kilobyte costs money and power), using Thumb instructions allows a programmer to **fit 2× more functionality** into the same Flash memory. However, the shorthand sometimes requires *two short sentences* (two 16-bit instructions) to express what one ARM instruction does, so there is a trade-off between code density and execution speed. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis) - **Instruction Width (ARM)**: **32 bits (4 bytes)** - **Instruction Width (Thumb)**: **16 bits (2 bytes)** for original Thumb; **16-bit or 32-bit** for Thumb-2 (Cortex-M3 supports Thumb-2 only — no 32-bit ARM instructions). - **Register File Size**: **31 general-purpose 32-bit registers** (R0–R12 are GPR; R13=SP, R14=LR, R15=PC). - **Condition Code Field**: ARM has it in **every instruction (4 bits)**; Thumb has it in **only branch instructions**. - **Code Density Improvement of Thumb vs ARM**: Approximately **~30% to 40%** smaller code size on average. - **Performance Penalty (Thumb)**: Approximately **~15% to 20% slower** than ARM for compute-bound code, due to additional decompression overhead. - **Branching Mechanism in Thumb**: Branch instructions use **only 8-bit conditional codes** (fewer conditions) vs. ARM's **4-bit condition codes (16 conditions)**. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why and How ### 2.1.1 Instruction Fetch and Decode **ARM State Operation:** 1. The processor fetches **4 bytes (32 bits)** from memory per cycle (aligned access). 2. The instruction is sent directly to the decoder, where the **4-bit condition field (bits [31:28])** is first checked. 3. If the condition is false, the instruction is skipped in one cycle (a "condition stall" or branch-folding). **Thumb State Operation:** 1. The processor fetches **2 bytes (16 bits)** from memory per cycle (half-word aligned). 2. The instruction is passed through a **dedicated Thumb decompressor** that translates it into an equivalent ARM instruction internally. 3. The decompressed instruction then enters the main ARM pipeline. > [!NOTE] > **Why does Thumb exist?** In the early 1990s, embedded systems used **8-bit and 16-bit microcontrollers** with very limited Flash/RAM. A 32-bit ARM instruction consumed 4 bytes, which was enormous. The Thumb Instruction Set was introduced in **1994 with ARM7TDMI** to provide a competitive code-density alternative while retaining the ARM performance and ecosystem. ### 2.1.2 Register Accessibility Differences In **ARM state**, most instructions can freely access **all 16 visible registers (R0–R15)**, including using **R15 (PC)** as a general operand in barrel-shifter operations. In **Thumb state**, register access is more restricted: - **R0–R7 (Low Registers)**: Fully accessible to all instructions. - **R8–R12 (High Registers)**: Accessible only by a **limited subset** of instructions (e.g., `MOV`, `ADD`, `CMP`). - **R13 (SP), R14 (LR), R15 (PC)**: Accessed via dedicated instructions like `PUSH`, `POP`, `BL`, `BX`. ### 2.1.3 Barrel Shifter (Inline Shift Operations) The **barrel shifter** is a hallmark feature of the ARM instruction set, allowing any ALU operand to be shifted or rotated *as part of the instruction itself*, in a single cycle, for free. - **ARM State**: Almost **every data-processing instruction** can use the barrel shifter (`LSL`, `LSR`, `ASR`, `ROR`, `RRX`). - **Thumb State (Original)**: **NO inline barrel shifter operations** are available. Shifts/rotates must be performed as separate instructions. - **Thumb-2 State (Cortex-M3)**: **Restores barrel shifter** functionality with the `IT` (If-Then) block and wide 32-bit Thumb-2 instructions. ### 2.1.4 Conditional Execution - **ARM State**: Almost **every instruction is conditionally executed** via a 4-bit condition code (`EQ`, `NE`, `LT`, `GT`, `CS`, `CC`, etc. — 16 conditions). - **Thumb State (Original)**: Only **branch (B) instructions** are conditional; data-processing instructions are not. - **Thumb-2 State (Cortex-M3)**: Introduces the **IT (If-Then) block**, allowing up to 4 subsequent instructions to be conditionally executed, partially restoring this feature. ### 2.1.5 Inter-State Branching (BX and BLX) Switching between ARM and Thumb states is achieved using special branch instructions: - **`BX (Branch and Exchange)`**: Loads the target address into PC and switches state based on **bit [0]** of the destination address. If `bit[0] = 1`, switch to **Thumb state**; if `bit[0] = 0`, switch to **ARM state**. - **`BLX (Branch, Link, and Exchange)`**: Same as BX, but also saves the return address in LR (R14). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Viva Favorite Question:** *"How does the processor know whether to decode in ARM or Thumb mode?"* — Answer: The **LSB (bit 0)** of the value loaded into PC by a `BX`/`BLX`/`LDR`/`POP` instruction determines the next state. `0` = ARM, `1` = Thumb.
