| Branch range | ±32 MB | ±2 KB (forward), ±4 MB (`BL` 32-bit) |

**2. Justification — IoT sensor node (2 marks)**

**Use Thumb** (or Thumb-2 on Cortex-M):

- Sensor nodes are **flash-bound** (tiny on-chip flash, 16–256 KB).
- Thumb's 35% code-density reduction directly **lowers BOM cost** and **extends battery life** (fewer fetches).
- Performance is not critical (sampling at Hz, not MHz).
- Cortex-M3/M4 cores are Thumb-2-only anyway.

**3. Justification — DSP filter (2 marks)**

**Use ARM state** (on ARM9/Cortex-A, where both states exist):

- DSP inner loops are **performance-critical** (every cycle matters for FIR/FFT).
- ARM's 32-bit instructions give **higher IPC** and the **barrel shifter** is free.
- The cycle savings per instruction outweigh the extra code bytes.
- Optionally, use **VFP/NEON co-processor** in ARM state for SIMD.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks on ARM-Thumb interworking questions:**
> 1. **Confusing `+4` and `+2` offsets in `BLX` return address** — Thumb uses `+2`, ARM uses `+4`. The `+1` in the target address (`func+1`) is *separate* from the offset.
> 2. **Forgetting the LSB-as-state-flag convention** — A student answers "we set bit 0 of the address" without explaining that bit 0 is *not part of the real address*, it's a state indicator that gets masked off.
> 3. **Drawing `CPSR` with T-bit in the wrong position** — T-bit is **bit 5**, not bit 0, not bit 31. Know the exact bit position.
> 4. **Confusing `BX` with `BLX`** — `BX` is a *branch* (no return); `BLX` is a *call* (saves return address in LR). They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Stating that Cortex-M3 supports ARM state** — **It does not.** Cortex-M3/M4/M7 are Thumb-2-only. "ARM-Thumb interworking" on these cores means *16-bit/32-bit Thumb-2 interworking*, which is transparent to the user.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **ARM-Thumb interworking** is the dynamic switching between 32-bit ARM and 16-bit Thumb instruction sets within one program.
- ✅ The **T-bit (CPSR bit 5)** is the hardware switch: `T=0` → ARM, `T=1` → Thumb.
- ✅ `BX Rm` branches to `Rm` and sets `CPSR[T] = Rm[0]`; the LSB of `Rm` is **not** part of the real address.
- ✅ `BLX Rm` does a state-switching **subroutine call** (saves `LR` with the return-state flag in `LR[0]`).
- ✅ **Veneers** are linker-generated ARM-state trampolines (8 bytes, `BX Rm`) used when the `BL` offset cannot reach a Thumb function directly.
- ✅ Thumb code is **~65% the size** of ARM code (35% flash savings).
- ✅ ARM state has **all 16 registers directly accessible** and **conditional execution on every instruction**; Thumb (1) restricts high registers and conditions to branches.
- ✅ **Cortex-M3/M4/M7 cores do not have ARM state** — they use Thumb-2, so "interworking" there is a software concern, not a hardware state switch.
- ✅ State-switching costs **2–3 pipeline cycles** per `BX`/`BLX` — only worthwhile for functions larger than ~30 instructions.
- ✅ `LR[0]` is the **return-state flag**: the `BX LR` at the end of a function uses it to restore the caller's state.
- ✅ For pure Thumb (Thumb-1) code, branch range is only **±2 KB** — `BL` (32-bit in Thumb-2) extends this to ±4 MB.
- ✅ **ATPCS** (ARM-Thumb Procedure Call Standard) governs register usage, stack alignment, and return-state conventions across state-switching calls.
- ✅ **Linker flag:** `--interwork` (or `--crosser`) tells `armlink` to generate veneers automatically.
- ✅ On **Cortex-M reset**, the T-bit is set to 1 (Thumb-2 only); on **ARM7/ARM9 reset**, the T-bit is 0 (ARM state) unless configured otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Structure of ARM Assembly Module
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** General Structure of ARM Assembly Module

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **ARM Assembly Module** is a structured, text-based source code file written in the **ARM Unified Assembly Language (UAL)** that defines the complete program — including executable code, constant data, initialized variables, and memory layout information — required by the ARM assembler to generate a relocatable object file for an ARM target processor (typically **ARM7TDMI** for the LPC2148 microcontroller family used in KTU laboratories).

The module is a logically self-contained unit that is processed by the assembler to produce **machine code**, **symbol tables**, and **relocation information** for the linker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "An ARM assembly module is a sequential collection of assembly lines, each line being either a label definition, an assembler directive, a machine instruction, a pseudo-operation, or a comment — bounded by the `AREA` directive and terminated by the `END` directive."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" Intuition

Think of an ARM Assembly Module exactly like a **structured recipe card** used by a professional chef:

| Recipe Card Component | ARM Assembly Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Title of the dish** | `AREA` directive | Declares what kind of "section" this is (code, data, stack) |
| **"Start Here" arrow** | `ENTRY` directive | Tells the CPU where execution begins — like the chef's first step |
| **Ingredients list** | Data directives (`DCD`, `DCB`, `SPACE`) | Reserves and initializes memory — the chef's pre-measured items |
| **Step-by-step cooking steps** | Instructions (`MOV`, `ADD`, `LDR`, `STR`) | The actual work performed by the CPU |
| **Sticky notes / margin notes** | Comments after `;` | Human-readable explanations, ignored by assembler |
| **"End of recipe" marker** | `END` directive | Marks the physical end of the source module |

Just as a recipe card has a strict format (so any chef can read it), ARM assembly requires a **strict structure** so the assembler tool can reliably parse and convert it into machine code.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key ARM Assembly Standards:**
> - **Instruction width**: **32 bits (fixed)** for ARM mode, **16 bits (Thumb/Thumb-2)** for compressed mode.
> - **Label naming**: Alphanumeric + `_` + `$`; **case-sensitive**; cannot begin with a digit.
> - **Comment delimiter**: Semicolon `;` (entire remaining line ignored).
> - **String delimiter**: Double quote `" "` (for character strings).
> - **Byte order in memory**: **Little-endian** by default on LPC2148 and most ARM7TDMI targets.
> - **Line length**: Up to **255 characters** per source line (assembler standard).

---

## 1.4 Structural Building Blocks — The Three Pillars

Every ARM assembly module is built from exactly **three logical categories** of lines:

1. **Assembler Directives** (Pseudo-operations) — Control the assembler's behavior, memory layout, and sectioning. They do **not** generate machine code (with one or two exceptions like `DCD`).
2. **Machine Instructions** — Mnemonics that translate directly into 32-bit (ARM) or 16-bit (Thumb) opcodes executed by the processor.
3. **Labels and Comments** — Symbolic names for addresses and human-readable annotations.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Heuristic**: If a line starts with a dot `.` or an all-uppercase keyword like `AREA`, `ENTRY`, `END`, `EQU`, `DCD` — it is a **directive**. If it starts with lowercase like `mov`, `add`, `ldr`, `b` — it is an **instruction**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Single ARM Assembly Line

The grammar of every line in an ARM assembly module follows this canonical form:

```
[label[:]]  [instruction | directive | pseudo-op]  [operands]  [; comment]
```

**Component-by-component breakdown:**

| Slot | Optional? | Example | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `label` | Optional | `Loop:` `START` `Delay_50ms` | Symbolic name (address) bound to current location; ends with `:` if scope is local within the AREA. |
| Mnemonic | Required (one of) | `MOV R0, #5` / `AREA MyCode, CODE, READONLY` | Either a **machine instruction** or an **assembler directive**. |
| Operands | Depends | `R0, #5` | Comma-separated arguments; literals prefixed with `#` for numeric constants. |
| Comment | Optional | `; initialize counter` | Begins with `;`, extends to end-of-line, **ignored** by assembler. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Whitespace Rules (Board-Favorite Question):**
> - At least **one whitespace** (space or tab) MUST separate the label from the mnemonic.
> - Operands MUST be separated by commas **with no spaces** around the comma itself (e.g., `MOV R0, R1` not `MOV R0 , R1`).
> - Tabs and spaces are treated as a single token separator.

---

## 2.2 The Four Mandatory Structural Directives

A valid KTU-style ARM assembly module **must** contain these four directive categories to be assembled successfully:

### (a) `AREA` Directive — The Section Declarator

```
AREA    SectionName, Attributes
```

- Defines a named memory section with specific attributes.
- An assembly module can contain **multiple `AREA` blocks**.
- **Common attribute combinations**:

| Attribute Set | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `CODE, READONLY` | Executable code section in Flash ROM |
| `DATA, READWRITE` | Initialized read/write data (typically RAM) |
| `NOINIT, READWRITE` | Uninitialized RAM area (zeroed at startup) |

### (b) `ENTRY` Directive — The Program Entry Point

```
ENTRY
```

- Marks **the first instruction** the processor will execute after reset.
- Only **ONE `ENTRY`** per assembly module is allowed; marks the program's logical start.

### (c) `END` Directive — The Module Terminator

```
END   [label]
```

- Optional operand: address label indicating where to begin execution (alternative to `ENTRY`).
- **MANDATORY**: Every source file must end with `END`. Lines after `END` are ignored.

### (d) `ALIGN` Directive — Alignment Control

```
ALIGN   [n]
```

- Aligns the current location counter to a boundary of $2^n$ bytes.
- Default: `ALIGN 2` aligns to a **word (4-byte) boundary**.
- Essential before placing `DCD` literals or building literal pools.

---

## 2.3 Data Definition Directives (Memory Reservation)

| Directive | Full Name | Size | Example | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `DCD` | Define Constant Data | **32-bit (Word)** | `count DCD 100` | Reserves a word and initializes with constant |
| `DCW` | Define Constant Halfword | **16-bit** | `val DCW 0x1234` | Half-word storage |
| `DCB` | Define Constant Byte | **8-bit** | `msg DCB "Hello", 0` | Byte storage / string literal |
| `DCWU` | DCW Unaligned | 16-bit | — | No alignment enforced |
| `SPACE` | Reserve Space | Byte-array | `buf SPACE 256` | Reserves uninitialized bytes (filled with 0) |
| `FILL` | Fill Memory | Byte-array | `FILL 8, 0xFF, 1` | Fills n bytes with value v |
| `EQU` | Equate (constant) | — | `BUFSIZE EQU 0x100` | Defines a symbolic constant (no memory) |
| `RN` | Register Name | — | `TEMP RN 5` | Aliases a register number |

---

## 2.4 Symbol & Visibility Directives

| Directive | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `EXPORT <label>` | Makes a symbol **visible to the linker** (globally accessible) |
| `IMPORT <label>` | Declares an **external symbol** defined in another module |
| `EXTERN <label>` | Similar to `IMPORT`, but more flexible with absolute addresses |
| `GLOBAL <label>` | Equivalent to `EXPORT` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Forgetting `EXPORT` makes a function invisible to other modules in a multi-file project — a common linker error in KTU lab examinations.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Alignment | Address $\mod 2^n = 0$ for `ALIGN n` | Word alignment means address divisible by 4 |
| Total AREAs in Module | $\geq 1$ CODE area, optionally + DATA areas | At least one `CODE, READONLY` |
| Comment Indicator | `;` starts a comment | Rest of line ignored |
| Literal Pool Size | Determined by `LTORG` directive | Pool inserted at 4 KB boundary by default |
| Endianness | Little-endian: $A, A+1, A+2, A+3$ stores $\text{LSB} \rightarrow \text{MSB}$ | Default on LPC2148 |
| Word Data Storing | `DCD 0x12345678` → byte0=0x78, byte1=0x56, byte2=0x34, byte3=0x12 | Little-endian storage |
| Stack Growth Direction | **Full-descending** (default for ARM) | SP decrements on PUSH |
| Label Scope | Local to AREA unless `EXPORT`ed | Case-sensitive |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The general ARM assembly module structure is foundational for:
- **Embedded firmware development** (LPC2148, STM32, SAMSUNG S3C2440 — all KTU lab targets).
- **Bootloader code** that must initialize stack pointers, copy `.data` from Flash to RAM, and zero `.bss` — all expressed via `AREA`/`ENTRY`/`END` structure.
- **Interrupt Vector Tables** — typically defined in a `CODE, READONLY` AREA at absolute address `0x00000000`.
- **Mixed C-Assembly projects** — `EXPORT` and `IMPORT` enable inter-language symbol sharing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical ARM Assembly Module — A Complete Annotated Example

Below is the **standard "Hello-World equivalent"** (LED blink) ARM assembly module demonstrating the full general structure expected in KTU examinations:

```
;=====================================================
; File       : led_blink.s
; Purpose    : Demonstrate General Structure of ARM
;              Assembly Module (KTU PECPT631 - M3)
; Target     : ARM7TDMI (LPC2148)
;=====================================================

                AREA    Reset_Handler, CODE, READONLY
                ENTRY                           ; <-- Marks program entry point
                
;--- Vector Table (simplified) -----------------------
                LDR     PC, =Reset_Addr         ; Reset vector
                LDR     PC, =Undef_Addr         ; Undefined instruction
                LDR     PC, =SWI_Addr           ; Software interrupt
                ; ... other vectors ...

Reset_Addr      DCD     Start                    ; Address of Start label
Undef_Addr      DCD     Undef_Handler
SWI_Addr        DCD     SWI_Handler

;--- Main Program Section ---------------------------
                EXPORT  Start                    ; Make 'Start' visible to linker
Start
                MOV     R0, #0x00000001          ; Step 1: Initialize bit mask
                LDR     R1, =IO0DIR             ; Step 2: Load address of GPIO0 direction reg
                STR     R0, [R1]                ; Step 3: Configure P0.0 as OUTPUT
                
Loop
                LDR     R1, =IO0SET             ; Step 4: Address of set register
                STR     R0, [R1]                ; Step 5: Set P0.0 HIGH (LED ON)
                BL      Delay                   ; Step 6: Call delay subroutine
                LDR     R1, =IO0CLR             ; Step 7: Address of clear register
                STR     R0, [R1]                ; Step 8: Clear P0.0 (LED OFF)
                BL      Delay                   ; Step 9: Call delay
                B       Loop                    ; Step 10: Infinite loop

;--- Delay Subroutine --------------------------------
Delay
                MOV     R2, #0x00FF             ; Load counter value
Delay_Loop
                SUBS    R2, R2, #1              ; Decrement and set flags
                BNE     Delay_Loop              ; Branch if not zero
                MOV     PC, LR                  ; Return to caller

;--- Interrupt / Exception Handlers ------------------
Undef_Handler   B       Undef_Handler           ; Trap
SWI_Handler     B       SWI_Handler             ; Trap

;--- Data Section for Constants ----------------------
                AREA    Constants, DATA, READONLY
IO0DIR          EQU     0xE0028008              ; Symbolic constant (no memory)
IO0SET          EQU     0xE0028004
IO0CLR          EQU     0xE002800C

                END                            ; <-- Mandatory module terminator
```

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Walkthrough (Logical Flow)

**Step 1 — Module Initialization:**
The `AREA Reset_Handler, CODE, READONLY` directive declares a section named `Reset_Handler` that holds executable code placed in **Flash ROM**.

**Step 2 — Entry Declaration:**
`ENTRY` marks the first instruction executed after the processor reset vector is loaded. Only one `ENTRY` per assembly module is permitted.

**Step 3 — Vector Table Population:**
The `DCD Reset_Addr` defines a 32-bit word constant whose value is the **address of the `Start` label**. The processor's vector table loader reads these to populate the initial PC.

**Step 4 — Code Body:**
Standard ARM mnemonics (`MOV`, `LDR`, `STR`, `BL`, `B`) execute the algorithm.

**Step 5 — Subroutine Convention:**
`BL Delay` saves return address in `LR` (R14) and branches to `Delay`. The subroutine restores it with `MOV PC, LR`.

**Step 6 — Constant Definition:**
`EQU` defines symbolic constants that occupy **no memory**; they are replaced textually by the assembler.

**Step 7 — Module Termination:**
`END` tells the assembler that no further source lines follow — **mandatory**.

---

## 3.3 The Assembly-to-Object Translation Process

The conversion of an ARM assembly module into a relocatable object file proceeds through these rigorous stages:

$$
\text{Source Module} \xrightarrow{\text{Tokenizer}} \text{Tokens} \xrightarrow{\text{Parser}} \text{Abstract Syntax Tree}
$$

$$
\text{AST} \xrightarrow{\text{Resolver}} \text{Resolved IR} \xrightarrow{\text{Code Generator}} \text{Machine Code (ELF/AXF)}
$$

$$
\text{Machine Code} \xrightarrow{\text{Linker}} \text{Executable Image} \xrightarrow{\text{Locator}} \text{Hex/Binary for Flash}
$$

### Step-by-Step Translation Logic:

**Step 1 — Lexical Analysis:**
Each line is tokenized into `[label]`, `[mnemonic]`, `[operand1, operand2, ...]`, `[comment]`.

**Step 2 — Symbol Resolution:**
- Labels become entries in the **symbol table** with their current location counter (LC) value.
- `EQU` constants are added to a **literal pool**.
- `IMPORT`/`EXPORT` symbols are flagged for linker resolution.

**Step 3 — Location Counter Update:**

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{new}} = \text{LC}_{\text{old}} + \text{instruction size}
$$

$$
\text{For ARM mode: instruction size} = 32 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Directive Expansion:**
- `DCD 0x12345678` → emits 4 bytes: `78 56 34 12` (little-endian).
- `SPACE 256` → advances LC by 256 bytes, emits zero-initialized region.
- `ALIGN 2` → pads to next 4-byte boundary.

**Step 5 — ELF/AXF Generation:**
Outputs relocatable object containing:
- **`.text`** section → machine code
- **`.data`** section → initialized variables
- **`.bss`** section → uninitialized variables (`SPACE`-reserved)
- **Symbol table** with all exported/imported labels.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Section Types

| Section Attribute | Storage Location | Writable? | Typical Content |
|---|---|---|---|
| `AREA X, CODE, READONLY` | Flash ROM (0x00000000+) | No | Instructions, vector table |
| `AREA Y, DATA, READWRITE` | RAM (0x40000000+) | Yes | Initialized global variables |
| `AREA Z, NOINIT, READWRITE` | RAM | Yes | Uninitialized buffers, stack, heap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Map for LPC2148:**
> - **Flash ROM**: $\text{0x00000000}$ to $\text{0x0007FFFF}$ (512 KB)
> - **SRAM**: $\text{0x40000000}$ to $\text{0x40007FFF}$ (32 KB)
> - Vector table resides at $\text{0x00000000}$ in Flash.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ARM Assembly Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ARM Assembly Source File .s] --> B[Lexer Tokenizer]
    B --> C{Token Classifier}
    C -->|Directive| D[Directive Handler]
    C -->|Instruction| E[Instruction Encoder]
    C -->|Label| F[Symbol Table Builder]
    C -->|Comment| G[Discard]
    
    D --> D1[AREA Handler]
    D --> D2[ENTRY Handler]
    D --> D3[END Handler]
    D --> D4[DCD DCB SPACE Handler]
    D --> D5[EQU RN Handler]
    
    E --> E1[ARM Mode 32 bit Encoder]
    E --> E2[Thumb Mode 16 bit Encoder]
    
    F --> F1[Local Symbol Table]
    F --> F2[Export Symbol Table]
    F --> F3[Import Symbol Table]
    
    D1 --> H[Location Counter LC]
    D4 --> H
    D5 --> H
    E1 --> H
    E2 --> H
    F1 --> H
    F2 --> H
    
    H --> I[ELF or AXF Object File]
    I --> J[Linker]
    J --> K[Executable Image]
    K --> L[Locator]
    L --> M[Intel HEX for Flash Programming]
```

## 4.2 Logical Sequence of Module Sections

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Source File Start] --> A1[AREA SectionName, CODE, READONLY]
    A1 --> A2[Optional Vector Table with DCD]
    A2 --> A3[ENTRY Directive]
    A3 --> A4[Main Code Block with Labels]
    A4 --> A5[Subroutine Definitions]
    A5 --> A6[Exception Handlers B Self]
    A6 --> B1[AREA Constants, DATA, READONLY]
    B1 --> B2[EQU Directive Definitions]
    B2 --> C1[AREA Buffers, NOINIT, READWRITE]
    C1 --> C2[SPACE Reservations]
    C2 --> End[END Directive - Mandatory]
    End --> Done[Module Terminated]
```

## 4.3 Line-by-Line Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Line 1: AREA declaration] --> L2[Line 2: ENTRY]
    L2 --> L3[Line 3: Vector DCDs]
    L3 --> L4[Line 4: Code Mnemonics]
    L4 --> L5[Line 5: Subroutine CALL]
    L5 --> L6[Line 6: Subroutine Definition]
    L6 --> L7[Line 7: Return]
    L7 --> L8[Line 8: Constant AREA]
    L8 --> L9[Line 9: END]
    
    L1 -.allocates CODE section.-> M1[Flash ROM]
    L3 -.stores 32-bit addresses.-> M2[Vector Table]
    L6 -.linked via BL.-> M3[Subroutine Pool]
    L8 -.placed in read-only data.-> M4[Constant Pool]
    L9 -.terminates assembly.-> M5[Object File Ready]
```

## 4.4 Memory Map Resulting from a Typical Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Flash[Flash ROM 0x00000000]
        V0[0x00 Reset Vector DCD]
        V1[0x04 Undef Vector DCD]
        V2[0x08 SWI Vector DCD]
        C1[0x40+ Code Section]
        C2[Subroutines]
        C3[Constants via EQU replaced inline]
    end
    
    subgraph RAM[SRAM 0x40000000]
        D1[Initialized Data]
        D2[Uninitialized BSS SPACE buffers]
        D3[Stack Pointer Initial Value]
    end
    
    C1 -.initializes.-> D1
    D2 -.zeroed at startup.-> RAM
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Q: List the FOUR mandatory assembler directives required in any ARM assembly module and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **`AREA`**: Declares a named memory section with attributes (e.g., `CODE, READONLY`).
2. **`ENTRY`**: Marks the first instruction to be executed after processor reset.
3. **`END`**: Terminates the assembly source file; any text after `END` is ignored.
4. **`ALIGN`**: Aligns the current location counter to a specified boundary ($2^n$ bytes).

*(Each directive: 0.75 marks × 4 = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Differentiate between `DCD` and `SPACE` directives with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `DCD` | `SPACE` |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Define Constant Data | Reserve Space |
| Size | 32-bit (4 bytes) | Byte-array (n bytes) |
| Initialization | Initialized to specified value | Initialized to **zero** |
| Example | `count DCD 100` | `buf SPACE 256` |
| Memory Class | `.data` | `.bss` |
| Use Case | Look-up tables, constants | Buffers, scratch areas |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**(a) Explain the general structure of an ARM assembly module with a neat block diagram. List all mandatory and optional assembler directives used in a typical module. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The general structure of an ARM assembly module consists of the following logical blocks arranged in sequence:

**Block 1 — Section Declaration (`AREA`):**
The module begins with the `AREA` directive that defines a memory section. Example:

```
AREA MyProgram, CODE, READONLY
```

**Block 2 — Vector Table (Optional but Typical):**
For ARM7TDMI targets, a vector table of `DCD` entries is placed at the section's beginning:

```
LDR PC, =Reset_Handler
DCD Reset_Handler   ; Stores 32-bit address
```

**Block 3 — Entry Point (`ENTRY`):**
Marks the program's first executable instruction.

**Block 4 — Code Body:**
Contains labels, instructions, and subroutine definitions. Example:

```
Start   MOV R0, #1
        LDR R1, =GPIO_BASE
        STR R0, [R1]
```

**Block 5 — Constant Data Section:**
Separate `AREA ... DATA, READONLY` block with `EQU` and `DCD` definitions.

**Block 6 — Module Termination (`END`):**
Mandatory closing directive.

**List of Directives:**

| Mandatory | Optional |
|---|---|
| `AREA`, `ENTRY`, `END` | `ALIGN`, `EQU`, `EXPORT`, `IMPORT`, `DCD`, `DCB`, `SPACE`, `RN`, `LTORG`, `PRESERVE8` |

*(Block explanation: 4 marks; Directive listing: 2 marks; Example: 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Write a complete ARM assembly module to toggle an LED connected to P0.15 of LPC2148. Use proper AREA, ENTRY, END directives. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```
;==================================================
; Program : LED Toggle on P0.15 (LPC2148)
; Module  : General Structure Demonstration
;==================================================

            AREA    LED_Toggle, CODE, READONLY
            ENTRY

            EXPORT  __main

;--- Hardware Register Addresses -------------------
IO0DIR      EQU     0xE0028008      ; P0 Direction Register
IO0SET      EQU     0xE0028004      ; P0 Set Register
IO0CLR      EQU     0xE002800C      ; P0 Clear Register
LED_MASK    EQU     (1 << 15)       ; P0.15 mask = 0x00008000

;--- Main Program ----------------------------------
__main
            LDR     R0, =IO0DIR
            MOV     R1, #LED_MASK
            STR     R1, [R0]        ; Configure P0.15 as OUTPUT

LOOP
            LDR     R0, =IO0SET
            STR     R1, [R0]        ; LED ON
            BL      DELAY

            LDR     R0, =IO0CLR
            STR     R1, [R0]        ; LED OFF
            BL      DELAY
            B       LOOP

;--- Delay Subroutine ------------------------------
DELAY
            MOV     R2, #0x00100000
DELAY_L
            SUBS    R2, R2, #1
            BNE     DELAY_L
            MOV     PC, LR

            END
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- Correct AREA/ENTRY/END usage: 1 mark
- EQU definitions: 1 mark
- Direction register setup: 1 mark
- Set/Clear register logic: 2 marks
- Delay subroutine: 1 mark
- Proper syntax & comments: 1 mark
= **7 marks**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a) With a suitable example, explain the following ARM assembler directives: `AREA`, `ENTRY`, `END`, `EQU`, `EXPORT`, `IMPORT`, `DCD`, and `SPACE`. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Directive | Syntax | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `AREA` | `AREA name, attr` | Declares a section | `AREA MyCode, CODE, READONLY` |
| `ENTRY` | `ENTRY` | Marks entry point | Placed after AREA, before code |
| `END` | `END` | Ends source file | Last line of module |
| `EQU` | `name EQU expr` | Defines symbolic constant | `BUFSIZE EQU 256` |
| `EXPORT` | `EXPORT label` | Exposes symbol globally | `EXPORT MyFunction` |
| `IMPORT` | `IMPORT label` | References external symbol | `IMPORT __main` |
| `DCD` | `label DCD value` | Reserves 32-bit word, initialized | `val DCD 0x12345678` |
| `SPACE` | `label SPACE n` | Reserves n bytes (zero-initialized) | `buf SPACE 1024` |

**Illustrative Example Module:**
```
            AREA    Demo, CODE, READONLY
            ENTRY
            EXPORT  Start
START       MOV     R0, #10          ; Load constant
            LDR     R1, =MyVar       ; Load address
            STR     R0, [R1]         ; Store to memory
            B       START            ; Infinite loop

            AREA    MyData, DATA, READWRITE
MyVar       DCD     0                 ; Initialized word
Buffer      SPACE   256              ; 256-byte uninitialized buffer
            END
```

*(Directive explanation: 4 marks; Example module: 3 marks = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Explain the role of the `AREA` directive and discuss the difference between `CODE, READONLY`, `DATA, READWRITE`, and `NOINIT, READWRITE` section attributes. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of `AREA`:**
The `AREA` directive instructs the assembler to group subsequent instructions or data into a named memory section. The linker combines like-named sections across modules and the locator assigns them final physical addresses based on the target memory map.

**Comparison of Attributes:**

**(i) `CODE, READONLY`:**
- Stores in **Flash ROM**.
- Contains **executable instructions** and read-only data (e.g., lookup tables, string literals).
- **Write-protected** at runtime — cannot be modified by program.
- Example: `AREA MyCode, CODE, READONLY`

**(ii) `DATA, READWRITE`:**
- Stored in **RAM** (initialized).
- Contains **modifiable** variables with **non-zero initial values**.
- Loaded from Flash to RAM at startup (copy-down initialization).
- Example: `AREA MyVars, DATA, READWRITE`

**(iii) `NOINIT, READWRITE`:**
- Stored in **RAM** (uninitialized).
- Reserved as **scratch space** — not zeroed by C runtime startup.
- Used for large buffers to avoid Flash-to-RAM copy overhead.
- Example: `AREA BigBuf, NOINIT, READWRITE`

**Engineering Significance:**
Choosing the correct attribute reduces startup time, optimizes memory usage, and prevents accidental code corruption.

*(AREA role: 2 marks; Comparison: 4 marks; Significance: 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in ARM Assembly Structure Questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing `END` directive** — Assembler throws an error; **−1 mark minimum**.
> 2. **Forgetting `EXPORT` on `__main`** when linking with C startup code — linker fails to find entry; question considered incomplete.
> 3. **Using `EQU` for runtime values** — `EQU` is for compile-time constants only; attempting `COUNTER EQU R0` is illegal.
> 4. **Mixing `DCD` with wrong alignment** — Always place `DCD` after `ALIGN` directive, or specify in a `DATA` AREA to ensure proper word alignment.
> 5. **Wrong case in mnemonics** — While ARM mnemonics are case-insensitive, **labels ARE case-sensitive**. Defining `Start` and calling `start` produces linker errors.
> 6. **Comments before `ENTRY`** — Comments are fine, but DO NOT place `ENTRY` after vector DCDs if the vector table needs to be at the absolute address — use `__Vectors` at `0x00000000`.
> 7. **Conflating `IMPORT` and `EXTERN`** — `IMPORT` resolves to absolute address; `EXTERN` allows relocation. Students frequently lose marks by not distinguishing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — ARM Assembly Module Structure:**
>
> ✅ An ARM assembly module is bounded by `AREA` and `END` directives.
>
> ✅ **Every line** follows the grammar: `[label] [mnemonic/directive] [operands] [;comment]`.
>
> ✅ Comments begin with `;` and extend to end-of-line — assembler ignores them.
>
> ✅ `AREA SectionName, Attributes` is mandatory; common attributes are `CODE, READONLY` / `DATA, READWRITE` / `NOINIT, READWRITE`.
>
> ✅ `ENTRY` marks the program's first instruction after reset — **only ONE per module**.
>
> ✅ `END` is **mandatory** and terminates the source file.
>
> ✅ `DCD` reserves and initializes a **32-bit word**; `DCW` reserves 16 bits; `DCB` reserves bytes/strings.
>
> ✅ `SPACE n` reserves **n bytes of uninitialized** memory (zero-filled).
>
> ✅ `EQU` defines a **symbolic compile-time constant** — no memory allocated.
>
> ✅ `EXPORT` makes a label globally visible; `IMPORT` references an external label.
>
> ✅ `ALIGN n` aligns the location counter to a $2^n$-byte boundary (default `ALIGN 2` = word alignment).
>
> ✅ ARM assembly is **little-endian** by default on LPC2148.
>
> ✅ Labels are **case-sensitive**; mnemonics are case-insensitive.
>
> ✅ The vector table is typically the first thing in a `CODE, READONLY` AREA, filled with `DCD` entries.
>
> ✅ Subroutine return uses `MOV PC, LR` (ARM mode) — critical for `BL`-based calls.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembler Directives - AREA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assembler Directives - AREA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AREA Directive (ARM Assembler):** The `AREA` directive is a fundamental assembler directive in the ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) assembly language that **instructs the assembler to define a named, logically grouped section (region) of code or data** within an assembly source file. Every ARM assembly program must contain at least one `AREA` directive to mark a logically distinct block that the linker can later locate, group, and place into the target memory map.

**General Syntax:**

$$ \texttt{AREA}\ \ \textit{section\_name}\texttt{,}\ \textit{attr}_1\texttt{,}\ \textit{attr}_2\texttt{,}\ \ldots $$

where:
- **section_name** → a symbolic name given to the region (must be unique within the file).
- **attr_1, attr_2, …** → comma-separated attributes that describe the type and access rights (e.g., `CODE, READONLY`).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Rooms in a House"**
> Think of your final **executable program** as a **large house**, and the **memory map** as the plot of land on which the house is built. The `AREA` directive is like the **architect's instruction** to build a **labeled room** (a "Kitchen", a "Bedroom", a "Garage") on that land. Each room has:
> - A **name** (e.g., `MyAsmCode`, `StackTop`),
> - A **purpose** (Code or Data),
> - **Accessibility rules** (Read-Only or Read/Write),
> - A **location** decided later by the linker.
>
> Just as a builder cannot construct a house without first drawing **room boundaries**, an ARM assembler cannot produce a valid object file without `AREA` directives defining code/data sections.

### 1.3 Why AREA is Mandatory

- ARM object files are organized into **sections** (also called *areas* or *regions*). The linker (`armlink`) needs at least **one section** to place.
- It allows **mixing of C and assembly** by matching section names (e.g., `|.text|`, `|.data|`).
- It supports **scatter-loading**, where different regions are placed at predefined physical addresses.

**Default / Reserved Section Names (used in C-interworking):**

| Section Name | Purpose |
|--------------|---------|
| `|.text|` | Executable code (default C code section) |
| `|.data|` | Initialized global/static variables |
| `|.bss|` | Uninitialized global/static variables |
| `|.constdata|` | Read-only constants |

> **Bold emphasis:** The keywords **`CODE`**, **`DATA`**, **`READONLY`**, and **`READWRITE`** are the **four most frequently tested attributes** in KTU 2024 Scheme board examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the AREA Directive

The `AREA` directive is a **logical-only** directive — it does **not** emit any machine instruction. It purely acts as a *section header* for the assembler/linker pipeline.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Parsing Stage:** Assembler reads `AREA section_name, attrs` and creates a new internal section record.
2. **Attribute Binding:** Attributes (`CODE`/`DATA`, `READONLY`/`READWRITE`, `ALIGN=n`, etc.) are tagged onto the section.
3. **Content Collection:** All following instructions/data are appended to the section until the next `AREA` or `END` directive.
4. **Symbolic Linking:** The section is exported to the linker with its name and attributes.
5. **Placement:** The linker (with the help of a scatter file) places the section in physical memory at link time.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Attribute Cheat Sheet

| Attribute | Category | Meaning | Typical Use |
|-----------|----------|---------|-------------|
| `CODE` | Type | Marks the section as containing **machine instructions** | Program code |
| `DATA` | Type | Marks the section as containing **data values** (no instructions) | Variables, look-up tables |
| `READONLY` | Access | Section can only be **read** at runtime | Code, constants |
| `READWRITE` | Access | Section can be **read and written** at runtime | Variables, buffers |
| `ALIGN=n` | Alignment | Align section start to **2ⁿ byte** boundary | DMA buffers, vector tables |
| `COMMON` | Linker | Section is a **common block** (like Fortran COMMON) | Shared data across files |
| `NOINIT` | Data | Do not zero-initialize at startup | Uninitialized RAM regions |
| `SECTIONNAME` | Naming | Allow non-standard section name (with $\vert$ delimiters) | Custom linker sections |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Pairing:** The type attribute (`CODE` or `DATA`) **must always be paired** with an access attribute (`READONLY` or `READWRITE`). An invalid combination such as `CODE, READWRITE` is **rejected by the assembler**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Firmware Development:** Separate boot-loader code, application code, and calibration data into distinct `AREA` blocks for reliable in-field updates.
- **RTOS Porting:** Place interrupt vector tables in fast on-chip SRAM using a dedicated `AREA` with `ALIGN=9` (512-byte boundary, ARM-cortex vector table rule).
- **Memory-Mapped I/O:** Use `AREA MyReg, CODE, READONLY` for placing lookup tables in Flash to save RAM.
- **Mixed C/ASM Projects:** Use reserved names `|.text|` and `|.data|` to seamlessly interleave assembly routines with `main.c`.
- **Safety-Critical Systems (ISO 26262, IEC 61508):** Place safety code and non-safety code in physically separate memory regions via scatter files — declared using `AREA`.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow for Choosing AREA Attributes

The following analytical matrix (truth-table style) helps students decide the correct AREA attribute set based on the **content** and **access intent**:

| Content Type | Runtime Access? | Correct AREA Line | Reason |
|--------------|-----------------|-------------------|--------|
| ARM Instructions | Read-only | `AREA Prog, CODE, READONLY` | Code is executed, never written |
| Lookup Table (const) | Read-only | `AREA LUT, DATA, READONLY` | Data but immutable |
| Global Variable (RW) | Read/Write | `AREA Vars, DATA, READWRITE` | Variable must be modifiable |
| Interrupt Vector Table | Read-only, 512-byte aligned | `AREA Vectors, CODE, READONLY, ALIGN=9` | Cortex-M vector table rule |
| Uninitialized Buffer (BSS-like) | Read/Write, no init | `AREA BUF, DATA, READWRITE, NOINIT` | Saves startup time |
| Shared Block (multi-file) | Read/Write, common | `AREA SHARE, COMMON, READWRITE` | Merged across object files |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Building a Multi-Section Assembly File

**Problem Statement:** Write an ARM assembly program that contains:
- An executable code section named `MyAsm` (read-only).
- A data section named `MyData` containing two initialized 32-bit words: `0x12345678` and `0xCAFEBABE`.

**Step 1 — Declare the Code AREA**

We begin by telling the assembler "this region will hold code":

```assembly
        AREA    MyAsm, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY                           ; Marks first instruction for execution
        MOV     R0, #1                  ; Load constant 1 into R0
        LDR     R1, =MyData            ; Load address of MyData section
        LDR     R2, [R1]               ; R2 = first word of MyData
        LDR     R3, [R1, #4]           ; R3 = second word of MyData
        ADD     R4, R2, R3             ; R4 = sum of the two words
        BX      LR                      ; Return from subroutine
        END                             ; End of source file
```

**Detailed Line-by-Line Logic:**

| Line | Instruction/Directive | Purpose |
|------|----------------------|---------|
| 1 | `AREA MyAsm, CODE, READONLY` | Creates a **read-only code section** named `MyAsm` |
| 2 | `ENTRY` | Declares this as the **program entry point** |
| 3 | `MOV R0, #1` | Load immediate value **1** into **R0** |
| 4 | `LDR R1, =MyData` | Pseudo-instruction: load the **address** of label `MyData` into R1 |
| 5 | `LDR R2, [R1]` | Read 32-bit word from memory at `[R1]` (offset 0) |
| 6 | `LDR R3, [R1, #4]` | Read 32-bit word from memory at `[R1+4]` (offset 4) |
| 7 | `ADD R4, R2, R3` | Add the two words, store result in R4 |
| 8 | `BX LR` | Branch to address in **Link Register** (return) |
| 9 | `END` | Signals the **assembler** that source file has ended |

**Step 2 — Declare the Data AREA**

We now add the data section. Note that `AREA` can be reused **multiple times** in the same file with different names:

```assembly
        AREA    MyData, DATA, READWRITE
MyData DCD     0x12345678               ; Define Constant - 32-bit word #1
        DCD     0xCAFEBABE              ; Define Constant - 32-bit word #2
```

**Memory Layout Produced (Little-Endian, default for ARM Cortex):**

| Offset | Byte 0 | Byte 1 | Byte 2 | Byte 3 | 32-bit Word |
|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|-------------|
| 0x00 | 0x78 | 0x56 | 0x34 | 0x12 | 0x12345678 |
| 0x04 | 0xBE | 0xBA | 0xFE | 0xCA | 0xCAFEBABE |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Verification of the Attribute Pairing Rule

**Test the rule:** "`CODE` must pair with `READONLY` and `DATA` with `READWRITE`."

$$ \text{Code Region} \rightarrow \text{Type} = \texttt{CODE} \Rightarrow \text{Access} = \texttt{READONLY}\ \text{(mandatory)} $$

$$ \text{Data Region (variable)} \rightarrow \text{Type} = \texttt{DATA} \Rightarrow \text{Access} = \texttt{READWRITE}\ \text{(mandatory)} $$

**Counter-Example (will be rejected by the assembler):**

```assembly
        AREA    BadSection, CODE, READWRITE     ;  ERROR: code cannot be writable
        AREA    BadSection2, DATA, READONLY     ;  ERROR: data declared read-only but no storage
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 AREA Directive — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **logical flow** of how the `AREA` directive is processed through the ARM toolchain (assembler → linker → loader → target memory):

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[ARM Assembly Source File .s] --> ASM[ARM Assembler armasm]
    ASM --> SEC[Section Object File .o]
    SEC --> LNK[ARM Linker armlink]
    LNK --> SCAT[Scatter File .sct]
    LNK --> ELF[Final ELF Executable .axf]
    ELF --> LOAD[Image Loader / Debugger]
    LOAD --> MEM[Target Memory Map]
    SEC --> SEC1[AREA MyAsm]
    SEC1 --> A1[Type CODE]
    SEC1 --> A2[Access READONLY]
    SEC --> SEC2[AREA MyData]
    SEC2 --> B1[Type DATA]
    SEC2 --> B2[Access READWRITE]
    SEC2 --> B3[Optional ALIGN 3]
    MEM --> M1[Flash Region 0x00000000]
    MEM --> M2[SRAM Region 0x20000000]
    SEC1 -. Placed into .-> M1
    SEC2 -. Placed into .-> M2
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right AREA Attributes

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start: Choose AREA attributes] --> Q1{What is the content?}
    Q1 -->|Instructions| QT{Type = CODE}
    Q1 -->|Variables| DT{Type = DATA}
    QT --> QA{Will runtime modify it?}
    QA -->|No| RO[Access = READONLY]
    QA -->|Yes| ERR1[ERROR: Illegal combo]
    DT --> DA{Will runtime modify it?}
    DA -->|No| RO2[Access = READONLY for constants]
    DA -->|Yes| RW[Access = READWRITE]
    RO --> AL{Need special alignment?}
    RO2 --> AL
    RW --> AL
    AL -->|Yes, 512B for Vectors| ALN[Add ALIGN 9]
    AL -->|Yes, 4B general| ALN2[Add ALIGN 2]
    AL -->|No| DONE[Final AREA line ready]
    ALN --> DONE
    ALN2 --> DONE
```

### 4.3 Physical Memory Mapping of a Typical Multi-AREA Program

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FLASH[FLASH Memory 0x00000000]
        F1[AREA Boot CODE READONLY]
        F2[AREA VectorTable ALIGN 9]
        F3[AREA App CODE READONLY]
        F4[AREA ConstTab DATA READONLY]
    end
    subgraph SRAM[SRAM Memory 0x20000000]
        S1[AREA Vars DATA READWRITE]
        S2[AREA Stack DATA READWRITE NOINIT]
        S3[AREA Heap DATA READWRITE]
    end
    F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4
    S1 --> S2 --> S3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the AREA directive in ARM assembly language. List any two attributes used with it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `AREA` directive is an assembler directive that defines a **named, logical section** of code or data in an ARM assembly source file. It does not generate any machine code by itself; instead, it groups instructions or data into a section that the linker (`armlink`) can later place in memory. The general syntax is:

$$ \texttt{AREA}\ \textit{name}\texttt{,}\ \textit{attribute1}\texttt{,}\ \textit{attribute2} $$

**Two important attributes:**
1. `CODE` / `DATA` → defines whether the section contains **instructions** or **data**.
2. `READONLY` / `READWRITE` → defines whether the section can be **only read** or **read and written** at runtime.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for syntax, 1 mark for two attributes.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the difference between `AREA MyCode, CODE, READONLY` and `AREA MyData, DATA, READWRITE`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `AREA MyCode, CODE, READONLY` | `AREA MyData, DATA, READWRITE` |
|--------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Type | Holds **machine instructions** | Holds **data values** |
| Access | **Read-only** at runtime | **Read & Write** at runtime |
| Typical Location | **Flash memory** (non-volatile) | **SRAM** (volatile) |
| Example Use | Program routines, ISRs | Global variables, buffers |

*(Award 1.5 marks for each correct side-by-side comparison.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** "Within each module, students must answer one full 14-mark question (with sub-parts) from the chosen module. The question carries an internal choice (OR)."

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the syntax of the ARM `AREA` directive. Discuss the significance of the `CODE`, `DATA`, `READONLY`, and `READWRITE` attributes with suitable examples.

**Model Solution:**

The `AREA` directive in ARM assembly is used to **define a named section** of code or data. Its syntax is:

$$ \texttt{AREA}\ \textit{section\_name}\texttt{,}\ \textit{attr}_1\texttt{,}\ \textit{attr}_2 $$

**Step 1 — `CODE` Attribute (1 mark):**
The `CODE` attribute marks the section as containing **machine-executable instructions**. Without it, the assembler will reject the section if any `ADD`, `MOV`, `BX`, etc. are placed inside.

**Step 2 — `DATA` Attribute (1 mark):**
The `DATA` attribute marks the section as containing **data values** (such as `DCD`, `DCB`, `DCW`, or `SPACE`). Code cannot be placed inside a `DATA` section.

**Step 3 — `READONLY` Attribute (1.5 marks):**
Indicates that the section cannot be modified during program execution. The linker places it typically in **Flash memory**.

**Step 4 — `READWRITE` Attribute (1.5 marks):**
Indicates that the section can be both read and written. The linker places it typically in **SRAM**.

**Step 5 — Examples (2 marks):**

```assembly
        AREA    SampleCode, CODE, READONLY      ;  Code section in Flash
        MOV     R0, #10
        ADD     R1, R0, #20
        BX      LR

        AREA    SampleData, DATA, READWRITE    ;  Data section in SRAM
        DCD     0x11111111
        DCD     0x22222222
        END
```

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an ARM assembly program to define:
- A code section named `MainProg` containing a routine to add two 32-bit numbers stored in memory and store the result.
- A data section named `Numbers` containing the two 32-bit operands `0x00000005` and `0x0000000A`.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Declare the data section first (or after code; both valid):**

```assembly
        AREA    Numbers, DATA, READWRITE
Num1    DCD     0x00000005           ;  First operand = 5
Num2    DCD     0x0000000A           ;  Second operand = 10
Result  DCD     0                    ;  Result storage
```

**Step 2 — Declare the code section:**

```assembly
        AREA    MainProg, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY                            ;  Program entry point
        LDR     R0, =Num1               ;  R0 = address of Num1
        LDR     R1, [R0]                ;  R1 = value of Num1 = 5
        LDR     R2, =Num2
        LDR     R3, [R2]                ;  R3 = value of Num2 = 10
        ADD     R4, R1, R3              ;  R4 = 5 + 10 = 15
        LDR     R5, =Result
        STR     R4, [R5]                ;  Store 15 into Result
        BX      LR                      ;  Return
        END
```

**Step 3 — Expected Output in Memory (3 marks):**

| Label | Address (e.g.) | Value (Hex) | Value (Dec) |
|-------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| Num1 | 0x20000000 | 0x00000005 | 5 |
| Num2 | 0x20000004 | 0x0000000A | 10 |
| Result | 0x20000008 | 0x0000000F | 15 |

*(Award 2 marks for each section declaration, 2 marks for the instruction logic, 1 mark for the END directive.)*

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of sections in ARM assembly. How does the `AREA` directive interact with the linker (armlink) and the scatter file to place code and data in the target memory?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of Sections (2 marks):**
A *section* (also called an *area* or *region*) is a **named, contiguous block** of code or data in an ARM object file. Sections are the **fundamental units of placement** for the ARM linker. They allow the programmer to logically group related items and let the linker place them physically in memory.

**Step 2 — Role of `AREA` Directive (2 marks):**
The `AREA` directive is the **assembler-level command** that creates a section. It defines the **name** and **attributes** (`CODE`, `DATA`, `READONLY`, `READWRITE`, `ALIGN`, etc.) of the section in the object file (`.o`). Each section in the source file becomes a separate section in the object file.

**Step 3 — Interaction with `armlink` (1.5 marks):**
`armlink` reads the **object files** produced by `armasm`, identifies the sections declared via `AREA`, and merges sections of the same name and attributes across multiple object files.

**Step 4 — Interaction with Scatter File (1.5 marks):**
A **scatter file** (`.sct`) tells the linker **where in physical memory** to place each section. Example:

```c
LOAD_REGION 0x00000000 0x00080000     ; Flash region
{
    ER_CODE 0x00000000 0x00080000      ; Execution region
    {
        *.o (MyAsm, +First)            ;  Place MyAsm code section first
    }
    ER_DATA +0
    {
        *.o (MyData)                    ;  Place MyData section after code
    }
}

LOAD_REGION 0x20000000 0x00010000     ; SRAM region
{
    RW_RAM 0x20000000
    {
        *.o (MyVars)                    ;  Place MyVars in SRAM
    }
}
```

This shows how the **assembler** (`AREA`) and **linker** (scatter) cooperate to position code in Flash and data in SRAM.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design an ARM assembly program with two AREA sections to:
- Store an array of 5 bytes in a `DATA, READONLY` section.
- Compute the **sum** of the array elements in a `CODE, READONLY` section and store the result in a `READWRITE` variable.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Declare the Read-Only Data Section (1.5 marks):**

```assembly
        AREA    ConstArray, DATA, READONLY
Arr     DCB     0x10, 0x20, 0x30, 0x40, 0x50   ;  5 bytes
```

**Step 2 — Declare the Read-Write Variable Section (1.5 marks):**

```assembly
        AREA    Globals, DATA, READWRITE
Sum     DCD     0                                ;  Sum variable (32-bit)
```

**Step 3 — Declare the Code Section (3 marks):**

```assembly
        AREA    SumCode, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        LDR     R0, =Arr                ;  R0 = base address of Arr
        MOV     R1, #5                  ;  R1 = loop counter
        MOV     R2, #0                  ;  R2 = running sum
LOOP    LDRB    R3, [R0], #1            ;  R3 = *R0, then R0 = R0+1
        ADD     R2, R2, R3              ;  R2 = R2 + R3
        SUBS    R1, R1, #1              ;  decrement counter
        BNE     LOOP                    ;  branch if not zero
        LDR     R4, =Sum
        STR     R2, [R4]                ;  store final sum
        BX      LR
        END
```

**Step 4 — Trace the Execution (1 mark):**

| Iteration | R0 (addr) | R3 (byte) | R2 (sum) | R1 (counter) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|--------------|
| Initial | &Arr | – | 0 | 5 |
| 1 | &Arr+1 | 0x10 | 0x10 | 4 |
| 2 | &Arr+2 | 0x20 | 0x30 | 3 |
| 3 | &Arr+3 | 0x30 | 0x60 | 2 |
| 4 | &Arr+4 | 0x40 | 0xA0 | 1 |
| 5 | &Arr+5 | 0x50 | 0xF0 | 0 |

**Final Result:** `Sum = 0xF0 = 240 (decimal)`.

*(Award 1.5 marks each for the three AREA sections, 1.5 marks for loop logic, 1 mark for trace and final value.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — KTU Valuation 2024 Scheme**
> 1. **Missing `CODE` or `DATA` attribute:** Omitting the type keyword causes an assembler error `A1903E: Section type missing`. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Mismatched type-access pair:** Writing `CODE, READWRITE` or `DATA` without access attribute. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting `END` directive at file end:** Causes assembler warning. Loss: **0.5 mark**.
> 4. **Two `AREA` directives with the same name in the same file:** Causes a "Duplicate symbol" error. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing `ENTRY` with `AREA`:** Many students place `ENTRY` outside any `AREA` block. `ENTRY` must be **inside** the first `AREA` block. Loss: **0.5 mark**.
> 6. **Not aligning the vector table to 512 bytes** (`ALIGN=9`): Causes a **HardFault** at startup on Cortex-M devices — a frequent viva question. Loss: **2 marks** in Part B.
> 7. **Using `DCD` inside a `READONLY DATA` section without `=` sign in `LDR`:** Wrong load address. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE)**
> - **Definition:** `AREA` directive **declares a named section** in ARM assembly.
> - **Syntax:** `AREA name, attr1, attr2, ...`
> - **Mandatory Pair Rule:** `CODE` ⇔ `READONLY` and `DATA` ⇔ `READWRITE`.
> - **Section Types:** `CODE` (instructions), `DATA` (values), `COMMON` (shared block).
> - **Access Types:** `READONLY` (Flash), `READWRITE` (SRAM).
> - **Alignment:** Use `ALIGN=n` for 2ⁿ byte boundary (e.g., `ALIGN=9` for vector tables).
> - **At Least One AREA:** Every ARM `.s` file **must** contain **at least one** `AREA` directive.
> - **Multiple AREAs Allowed:** Different sections of code/data can coexist in the same file with unique names.
> - **Linker Cooperation:** `armlink` uses the `AREA` name to match sections with the **scatter file** for physical placement.
> - **C-Interworking Reserved Names:** `|.text|`, `|.data|`, `|.bss|` allow C/ASM integration.
> - **ENDP / END Use:** `END` marks the **end of source file**; `ENDP` marks the **end of a procedure** (in `PROC/ENDP` blocks).
> - **Pictorial Memory:** `CODE, READONLY` ⇒ **Flash**; `DATA, READWRITE` ⇒ **SRAM**.
> - **Frequent Viva Line:** *"AREA does not emit any machine code; it is a logical-only directive processed by the assembler and consumed by the linker."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ENTRY
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** ENTRY # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **ENTRY (in Advanced Microcontrollers)** refers to the controlled, hardware-assisted mechanism by which the CPU temporarily suspends the currently executing program flow and transfers control to a predefined address (the **Entry Point** or **Interrupt Vector Address**) in response to an asynchronous event such as a **Reset**, **Interrupt Request (IRQ)**, **Trap**, or **Exception**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of *Advanced Microcontrollers* (PECPT631), **ENTRY** is the procedural sequence that the processor core executes the moment a hardware event (external interrupt, timer overflow, peripheral flag, software interrupt `SWI`, or reset) is acknowledged. It encompasses the **Context Save Phase** (pushing PC, status registers, and other critical CPU state onto the stack), the **Vector Fetch Phase** (loading the new PC from the interrupt vector table), and the **Service Routine Activation** (beginning execution of the Interrupt Service Routine). The *entry sequence* is governed by the microcontroller's **Interrupt Controller Unit (ICU)**, the **Program Counter (PC)** auto-increment logic, and the **Stack Pointer (SP)**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Office Worker's Phone Call:** Imagine you are an office worker (the **CPU**) deeply focused on a report (the **Main Program**). Suddenly, your phone rings (the **Interrupt Request**). - **Step 1 (Detection):** You notice the phone ring — this is the **Interrupt Detection** stage. - **Step 2 (Pause & Note-taking):** You quickly grab a sticky note and write down: *"I was on page 17, paragraph 3, with the blue pen in hand."* — This is the **Context Save** (saving PC, Status Register onto the Stack). - **Step 3 (Answering):** You pick up the phone and the company's automated system routes you to the correct department (Accounts, Sales, HR) — this is the **Vector Fetch** (loading the address of the appropriate ISR from the vector table). - **Step 4 (Handling):** You talk to the person on the line (the **Interrupt Service Routine Execution**). - **Step 5 (Return):** You hang up, read your sticky note, and resume exactly where you left off — this is the **Return from Interrupt (RETI) / EXIT**. The **ENTRY** is precisely Steps 1, 2, and 3 — the *organized, structured handoff* from the main task to the interrupt handler. Without a proper entry sequence, the CPU would "forget" what it was doing, leading to system crashes. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!TIP] > The following standard KTU 2024 metrics apply universally to entry mechanisms in 8/16/32-bit advanced microcontrollers (8051 family, ARM Cortex-M, PIC, AVR): | Parameter | Standard Value / Behavior | |---|---| | **Minimum Interrupt Latency** | **4 to 12 CPU clock cycles** (architecture-dependent) | | **Context Save Cycles (8051)** | **2 machine cycles** (push PC + push PSW) | | **Context Save Cycles (ARM Cortex-M3/M4)** | **12 cycles** (hardware stacking of xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0) | | **Stack Growth Direction** | **Downward** (high → low address) on most modern MCUs | | **Vector Table Size (8051)** | **8 bytes × 5 sources = 40 bytes** (at 0x0000–0x0028) | | **Vector Table Size (Cortex-M)** | **4 bytes × 256 = 1024 bytes** (at 0x00000000) | --- ## 1.4 Core Syllabus Highlight > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3 ENTRY Focus Areas:** > 1. Reset Vector and Power-On Entry sequence > 2. Hardware Interrupt Entry (maskable + non-maskable) > 3. Software Interrupt (TRAP / SWI) Entry > 4. Context Saving (Stack operations during entry) > 5. Vector table architecture and address generation > 6. Interrupt Enable/Disable flags interaction with entry > 7. Priority resolution *before* entry is granted # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The ENTRY Sequence — Structured Logic The complete **ENTRY** mechanism in any advanced microcontroller can be broken down into **7 deterministic phases**. KTU examiners frequently test these phases as 7-mark sub-questions. ### Phase 1: Event Detection (Hardware Stage) - The **Interrupt Request (IRQ) flag** is set by the peripheral (timer, ADC, external pin, UART, etc.). - This flag is latched in a **peripheral-specific interrupt flag register** (e.g., `TIFR` in AVR, `IRCON` in 8051, `ISPR` in Cortex-M). - The signal propagates to the **Interrupt Controller** (e.g., 8051's IE/IP logic, ARM's NVIC). ### Phase 2: Masking & Priority Arbitration - The controller checks the **Global Interrupt Enable** (e.g., `EA` bit in IE register of 8051, `PRIMASK` in Cortex-M). - If a higher-priority interrupt is already being serviced, the new request is **held pending**. - If multiple simultaneous requests exist, the **Priority Resolver** picks the highest-priority one. ### Phase 3: Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA) Cycle - The CPU completes the current instruction (this is the **"instruction boundary guarantee"** — you cannot interrupt mid-instruction). - The CPU issues an internal **INTA** signal. - The current **Program Counter (PC)** is captured in a temporary register. ### Phase 4: Context Save (PUSH operations) - The **Program Counter** is pushed onto the **Stack** (decrement SP, store PC-high; decrement SP, store PC-low). - The **Processor Status Word (PSW)** is pushed (carry, zero, auxiliary carry flags, register bank select bits). - Some MCUs also push **Accumulator (A)** and **B registers** (8051 does this in extended mode). - **Stack Pointer (SP)** auto-decrements with each push. ### Phase 5: Vector Fetch & PC Reload - The interrupt controller places the **vector address** on the internal address bus. - The **PC** is loaded with the start address of the corresponding ISR. - For 8051: `PC ← vector_address` (e.g., `0x000B` for Timer 0 interrupt). - For Cortex-M: `PC ← (*(uint32_t*)0xE000ED08)` reads from the Vector Table Offset Register (VTOR). ### Phase 6: ISR Activation - The CPU begins fetching and executing instructions from the new PC location. - This is the actual "**Entry**" into user-defined ISR code. - Global interrupts may be automatically cleared (e.g., 8051 clears the `EA` bit on entry). ### Phase 7: Latency Calculation - **Interrupt Latency** = time from IRQ assertion to first ISR instruction. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Master these equations — they account for ~60% of the numerical marks in Module 3 ESE questions.** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Interrupt Latency (cycles)** | $T_{latency} = T_{inst} \cdot (N_{remaining} + N_{entry})$ | $T_{inst}$ = instruction cycle time, $N_{remaining}$ = cycles left in current instruction (worst case = full instruction), $N_{entry}$ = cycles to complete entry (4–12) | | 2 | **Interrupt Latency (time, worst case)** | $t_{latency,max} = (C_{instr} + C_{entry}) \times T_{clock}$ | $C_{instr}$ = longest instruction cycle count, $C_{entry}$ = entry overhead, $T_{clock}$ = clock period | | 3 | **Stack Depth (8051 standard mode)** | $D_{stack} = 2 \text{ bytes per entry}$ | PC pushed (2 bytes). PSW *not* automatically pushed unless using extended stack. | | 4 | **Stack Depth (8051 extended / Cortex-M)** | $D_{stack} = 8 \text{ to } 32 \text{ bytes per entry}$ | xPSR + PC + LR + R12–R0 = 8 registers × 4 bytes = 32 bytes (Cortex-M3) | | 5 | **Vector Address (8051)** | $V_{addr} = V_{base} + (k \cdot 8)$ | $V_{base}=0x0000$, $k$ = interrupt index. e.g., Ext0 → $0x0003$, T0 → $0x000B$ | | 6 | **Vector Address (Cortex-M)** | $V_{addr} = \text{VTOR} + (N \cdot 4)$ | $N$ = interrupt number, VTOR = Vector Table Offset Register | | 7 | **Effective Interrupt Rate** | $f_{eff} = \frac{f_{source}}{N_{pre} \cdot N_{post}}$ | Where $N_{pre}, N_{post}$ are pre/post-scaler factors for timer interrupts | | 8 | **Context Save Time (Cortex-M3)** | $t_{save} = 12 \cdot T_{clock}$ | Hardware auto-stacking of 8 words | | 9 | **Nesting Depth (8051, no nesting allowed by default)** | $L_{nest} = 1$ | Re-entrancy requires software simulation | | 10 | **Nesting Depth (Cortex-M with BASEPRI)** | $L_{nest} = 2^{N_{prio}}$ | $N_{prio}$ = number of priority bits (typically 3–8) | --- ## 2.3 Why ENTRY Matters — Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Role of ENTRY | |---|---| | **Automotive ECU (CAN Bus)** | Entry latency determines responsiveness to airbag-deployment interrupts (must be **< 10 µs**). | | **Motor Control (PWM + ADC)** | Precise ENTRY synchronization ensures ADC sampling aligns with PWM zero-crossing. | | **Medical Devices (Pacemakers)** | Deterministic ENTRY is a **safety-critical requirement** (IEC 62304). | | **IoT Edge Devices** | ENTRY from sleep-mode wake-up sources (GPIO, RTC) defines power-budget calculations. | | **RTOS Kernel Design** | The first instruction of every task is its "Entry Point" — analogous to the ISR entry. | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** In safety-critical real-time systems, **bounded interrupt latency** (guaranteed maximum entry time) is a contractual requirement. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this because modern embedded interviews almost always ask *"How long does your system take to react to an interrupt?"* # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Interrupt Latency (8051 Architecture, 11.0592 MHz Crystal) **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** An 8051 system is clocked with a crystal frequency $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$. Calculate the **worst-case interrupt latency** in microseconds, given that the longest instruction is `MUL AB` (4 machine cycles) and the ENTRY sequence requires 2 machine cycles. --- ### Step 1: Determine the Machine Cycle Frequency The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 to generate the machine cycle clock. $$f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12}$$ $$f_{mc} = 921600 \text{ Hz} = 0.9216 \text{ MHz}$$ --- ### Step 2: Determine the Machine Cycle Period $$T_{mc} = \frac{1}{f_{mc}} = \frac{1}{921600} \text{ seconds}$$ $$T_{mc} = 1.08507 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \text{ µs}$$ --- ### Step 3: Identify the Worst-Case Remaining Instruction The interrupt is only acknowledged at the **end of the current instruction**. In the worst case, the CPU has just *begun* a 4-machine-cycle instruction (`MUL AB`). $$N_{remaining, max} = 4 \text{ machine cycles}$$ --- ### Step 4: Add the ENTRY Sequence Machine Cycles The 8051 ENTRY sequence requires **2 machine cycles** to: - Machine Cycle 1: Push PC-low onto stack, SP decrements - Machine Cycle 2: Push PC-high onto stack, SP decrements, then fetch vector address $$N_{entry} = 2 \text{ machine cycles}$$ --- ### Step 5: Total Machine Cycles for Worst-Case Latency $$N_{total} = N_{remaining, max} + N_{entry}$$ $$N_{total} = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ machine cycles}$$ --- ### Step 6: Convert to Time $$t_{latency, max} = N_{total} \times T_{mc}$$ $$t_{latency, max} = 6 \times 1.085 \text{ µs}$$ $$\boxed{t_{latency, max} = 6.51 \text{ µs}}$$ --- ### Step 7: Interpretation > The CPU will execute the **first instruction of the ISR no later than 6.51 µs** after the interrupt flag is set. This is the *hard real-time bound* for this 8051 system. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Vector Address Calculation (Cortex-M3, Interrupt #21) **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** In an ARM Cortex-M3 microcontroller, the Vector Table Offset Register (VTOR) is loaded with `0x08000000` (start of Flash). Calculate the **vector address for interrupt number 21** (e.g., a USART1 receive interrupt). --- ### Step 1: Recall the Cortex-M Vector Table Structure Each vector entry occupies **4 bytes** (a 32-bit address, since the Cortex-M is a 32-bit core). $$\text{Vector Entry Width} = 4 \text{ bytes} = 32 \text{ bits}$$ --- ### Step 2: Apply the Vector Address Formula $$V_{addr} = \text{VTOR} + (N \times 4)$$ Where: - $\text{VTOR} = 0x08000000$ - $N = 21$ (interrupt number) --- ### Step 3: Compute the Offset $$\text{Offset} = 21 \times 4 = 84 \text{ bytes}$$ $$84_{10} = 0x54_{16}$$ --- ### Step 4: Compute the Final Vector Address $$V_{addr} = 0x08000000 + 0x00000054$$ $$\boxed{V_{addr} = 0x08000054}$$ --- ### Step 5: Interpretation > When USART1 asserts its interrupt, the hardware reads the 32-bit word at address `0x08000054`, interprets it as the ISR's start address, and loads it into the PC. The first instruction of the ISR is fetched from that new PC. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ENTRY Comparison Across Architectures > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiners love comparison tables. Memorize this for guaranteed 7 marks.** | Feature | **8051 (8-bit)** | **AVR (8-bit)** | **PIC18 (8-bit)** | **ARM Cortex-M3 (32-bit)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Vector Table Location** | Fixed at `0x0000` | Fixed at `0x0000` | Fixed at `0x0000` / `0x0008` | Configurable via **VTOR** | | **Vector Spacing** | 8 bytes | 2 bytes (RJMP) / 4 bytes (JMP) | 4 bytes | 4 bytes | | **Auto Context Save** | Partial (PC only) | None (software) | Partial (PC, WREG, BSR, STATUS) | **Full hardware** (8 words) | | **Entry Cycles** | 2 machine cycles | 4 cycles | 4 cycles | **12 cycles** | | **Nesting Support** | None (default) | Via sei/cli logic | Via IPEN + GIEL/GIEH | **Yes (BASEPRI)** | | **Latency (typ.)** | 6–10 µs | 4–7 cycles | 4–6 cycles | **12 cycles** | | **Stack Direction** | Up (increments) | Down (decrements) | Down | **Down** | --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3: Stack Pointer Arithmetic During ENTRY **Problem Statement:** An 8051 microcontroller has an initial SP = `0x07`. Two nested interrupt entries occur (Timer 0 followed by External Interrupt 1). Calculate the final SP value. --- ### Step 1: Identify ENTRY Stack Operations Each 8051 ENTRY pushes the 16-bit PC onto the stack. This requires **2 byte pushes**, and the 8051 stack grows **upward** (SP increments after each push). $$\text{Push Operation: } \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 1, \text{ then } \text{Stack[SP]} \leftarrow \text{data}$$ --- ### Step 2: First Entry (Timer 0) $$\text{SP}_{after\_T0} = 0x07 + 2 = 0x09$$ --- ### Step 3: Second Entry (External Interrupt 1, nested inside T0) $$\text{SP}_{after\_INT1} = 0x09 + 2 = 0x0B$$ --- ### Step 4: Final Answer $$\boxed{\text{SP}_{final} = 0x0B}$$ > The stack now contains 4 bytes (2 PC values) of return addresses. Upon `RETI`, SP decrements by 2 for each return. $$\text{SP after INT1 RETI} = 0x0B - 2 = 0x09$$ $$\text{SP after T0 RETI} = 0x09 - 2 = 0x07 \quad \text{(restored)}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ENTRY Sequence Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete ENTRY mechanism** as a state-transition flow, compliant with all Mermaid syntax safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs only, no reserved keywords, no inline formatting in labels). ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CPU Executing Main Program] --> B[IRQ Flag Set by Peripheral] B --> C{Global Interrupt Enable} C -- DISABLED --> A C -- ENABLED --> D[Priority Resolver Check] D --> E{Higher Priority IRQ Active} E -- YES --> F[Hold Request Pending] F --> A E -- NO --> G[Wait for Instruction Boundary] G --> H[Capture Current PC] H --> I[Push PC Low onto Stack] I --> J[Push PC High onto Stack] J --> K[Push PSW onto Stack optional] K --> L[Fetch Vector Address from Table] L --> M[Load PC with Vector Address] M --> N[Clear Global Enable Flag auto] N --> O[Begin ISR Execution] O --> P[ISR Body Runs] P --> Q[RETI Instruction Encountered] Q --> R[Pop PSW from Stack] R --> S[Pop PC High from Stack] S --> T[Pop PC Low from Stack] T --> U[Set Global Enable Flag] U --> A ``` --- ## 4.2 Vector Table Memory Map (8051 Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[Address 0x0000
RESET Entry] --> A1[Address 0x0003
External Interrupt 0] A1 --> A2[Address 0x000B
Timer 0 Overflow] A2 --> A3[Address 0x0013
External Interrupt 1] A3 --> A4[Address 0x001B
Timer 1 Overflow] A4 --> A5[Address 0x0023
Serial Port UART] A5 --> A6[Address 0x002B
Timer 2 Overflow 8052 only] A6 --> A7[Address 0x0030 onwards
User Code Starts] ``` > [!NOTE] > Each vector is **8 bytes wide** in the 8051, allowing an `LJMP` (3 bytes) or `AJMP` (2 bytes) instruction followed by padding. --- ## 4.3 Stack Memory Layout During Nested ENTRY (Cortex-M3) ```mermaid flowchart TB H[Higher Address] --> S0[SP plus 0
Top of Stack] S0 --> S1[SP plus 4
R0 of Outer ISR] S1 --> S2[SP plus 8
R1 of Outer ISR] S2 --> S3[SP plus 12
R2 of Outer ISR] S3 --> S4[SP plus 16
R3 of Outer ISR] S4 --> S5[SP plus 20
R12 of Outer ISR] S5 --> S6[SP plus 24
LR of Outer ISR] S6 --> S7[SP plus 28
PC of Outer ISR] S7 --> S8[SP plus 32
xPSR of Outer ISR] S8 --> S9[SP plus 36
Stacked R0 Inner ISR] S9 --> S10[SP plus 40
Stacked PC Inner ISR] S10 --> L[Lower Address
Current SP points here] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cortex-M3 hardware auto-stacks exactly 8 words (32 bytes) on ENTRY.** Software never has to push these — they are restored automatically on `EXC_RETURN`. --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram of Interrupt Controller Interacting with CPU ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Peripheral Timer] --> F1[IRQ Flag Register] P2[Peripheral UART] --> F1 P3[Peripheral ADC] --> F1 P4[External Pin INT0] --> F1 F1 --> ICU[Interrupt Controller Unit] ICU --> MSK[Mask and Enable Logic] MSK --> PRIO[Priority Resolver] PRIO --> INTA[INTA Generator] INTA --> CPU[CPU Core] CPU --> STK[Stack Pointer and RAM] CPU --> VEC[Vector Table in Flash] STK --> CPU VEC --> CPU ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]` **Define Interrupt Entry. List the typical phases involved in the interrupt entry sequence of an advanced microcontroller.** **Model Answer:** > **Interrupt Entry** is the hardware-controlled procedure by which the CPU suspends the currently executing main program, saves its critical context (Program Counter and status flags) onto the stack, fetches the address of the corresponding Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) from the vector table, and begins ISR execution. The typical phases of the entry sequence are: 1. **IRQ Detection** — peripheral sets the interrupt flag. 2. **Masking & Priority Check** — global enable flag and priority logic validate the request. 3. **Instruction Boundary Wait** — CPU finishes the current instruction. 4. **Context Save** — PC and PSW pushed onto the stack. 5. **Vector Fetch** — vector address loaded into PC. 6. **ISR Activation** — execution begins at the new PC. 7. **Global Flag Management** — hardware may auto-clear enable bits (e.g., 8051's `EA`). --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]` **What is an Interrupt Vector Table? Explain with reference to 8051 and ARM Cortex-M3 architectures.** **Model Answer:** > An **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** is a reserved region of memory that stores the starting addresses (or jump instructions) of all Interrupt Service Routines. Each interrupt source has a fixed slot in the table. - **8051:** Located at fixed address `0x0000`, with **5 vectors** (Reset, Ext0, T0, Ext1, T1, Serial). Each entry is **8 bytes** wide. After reset, the table occupies `0x0000` to `0x002B`. - **ARM Cortex-M3:** Vector table base is **configurable** via the **VTOR register**. Default location is `0x00000000`. Each entry is **4 bytes** wide (32-bit address). Up to **256 interrupt vectors** are supported. The IVT decouples the hardware's IRQ signal from the software's ISR location — the hardware only knows the *index*, the table provides the *address*. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete interrupt entry mechanism** in the 8051 microcontroller. Discuss the role of the **TCON**, **IE**, and **IP** registers during the entry sequence. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. The longest instruction in the user's main loop is `DIV AB` (**4 machine cycles**). Calculate the **worst-case interrupt latency** in microseconds. The 8051 entry sequence consumes **2 machine cycles**. Justify each step. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A: **Part (a) — 7 Marks** The 8051 interrupt entry mechanism involves coordinated action of three SFRs: **1. TCON (Timer/Counter Control Register, address 0x88):** - Holds the **IT0** and **IT1** bits (Interrupt Type: edge-triggered vs. level-triggered for External Interrupts). - Holds the **IE0** and **IE1** bits (External Interrupt Edge flags) that are auto-set by hardware and auto-cleared during entry. - Holds the **TR0** and **TR1** bits (timer run controls — not directly part of entry, but related to timer interrupts). **2. IE (Interrupt Enable Register, address 0xA8):** - `EA` (Enable All, bit 7) — **Global Master Switch.** Must be `1` for any interrupt to enter. - `ET0`, `ET1` (bits 1, 3) — enable Timer 0 and Timer 1 overflow interrupts. - `EX0`, `EX1` (bits 0, 2) — enable External Interrupts 0 and 1. - `ES` (bit 4) — enable Serial Port interrupt. - During ENTRY, the 8051 hardware **does not auto-clear `EA`** in the original 8051, but does in some derivatives (e.g., 8052). **3. IP (Interrupt Priority Register, address 0xB8):** - `PT0`, `PT1`, `PX0`, `PX1`, `PS` bits define whether each interrupt is **High Priority (1)** or **Low Priority (0)**. - A high-priority interrupt can **preempt** a low-priority ISR, but two same-priority interrupts are resolved by fixed polling order. **Entry Sequence Steps:** 1. Peripheral sets its flag in TCON or peripheral register. 2. IE checks if the specific enable bit and EA are set. 3. IP resolves simultaneous requests. 4. CPU finishes current instruction. 5. Hardware pushes current PC (2 bytes) onto stack. 6. PC loaded with vector address (e.g., `0x000B` for T0). 7. ISR executes from vector address. --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** - $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ - Longest instruction: `DIV AB` = 4 machine cycles - ENTRY sequence: 2 machine cycles **Step 1 — Machine Cycle Frequency:** $$f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12} = 921600 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2 — Machine Cycle Period:** $$T_{mc} = \frac{1}{f_{mc}} = \frac{1}{921600} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \text{ µs}$$ **Step 3 — Worst-Case Cycles:** $$N_{total} = N_{instr, max} + N_{entry} = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ cycles}$$ **Step 4 — Worst-Case Latency:** $$t_{latency, max} = N_{total} \times T_{mc} = 6 \times 1.085 \text{ µs}$$ $$\boxed{t_{latency, max} = 6.51 \text{ µs}}$$ **Justification:** This 6.51 µs is the *hard upper bound*. In practice, the average latency is lower (~3.25 µs) because on average the IRQ arrives halfway through the longest instruction. The 2 machine cycles for ENTRY account for the two-byte PC push to internal RAM stack. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]` **(a)** Compare the **interrupt entry mechanism of ARM Cortex-M3** with that of the **8051**, highlighting differences in context saving, vector table structure, and nesting support. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An ARM Cortex-M3 has VTOR = `0x08010000`. Calculate the **vector address for interrupts #5, #16, and #42**.
