If the CPU clock is 50 MHz, calculate the **interrupt latency** in nanoseconds. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B: **Part (a) — 7 Marks** | Feature | **8051** | **ARM Cortex-M3** | |---|---|---| | **Vector Table Base** | Fixed at `0x0000` | Configurable via **VTOR** | | **Vector Width** | 8 bytes (LJMP) | 4 bytes (32-bit address) | | **Total Vectors** | 5 (basic 8051) | 256 (NVIC-managed) | | **Auto Context Save** | Only PC (2 bytes) | **8 words (32 bytes):** xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0 | | **Stack Direction** | Upward (increment) | Downward (decrement, full descending) | | **Entry Cycles** | 2 machine cycles | 12 cycles (deterministic) | | **Nesting** | Limited (priority levels: 2) | **Up to 256 levels** (4-bit priority → 16 levels) | | **Tail Chaining** | Not supported | **Supported** (saves up to 30 cycles on back-to-back ISRs) | | **Global Enable** | `EA` bit in IE | `PRIMASK`, `FAULTMASK`, `BASEPRI` | | **Latency** | Variable, depends on instruction | **Deterministic 12 cycles** | **Key Engineering Insight:** The Cortex-M3's hardware stacking eliminates the need for prologue/epilogue code in ISRs (no `PUSH`/`POP` of registers at the assembly level), making it superior for real-time systems. --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Vector Address Formula:** $$V_{addr} = \text{VTOR} + (N \times 4)$$ **Step 2 — Compute for Interrupt #5:** $$V_5 = 0x08010000 + (5 \times 4) = 0x08010000 + 20 = 0x08010000 + 0x14$$ $$\boxed{V_5 = 0x08010014}$$ **Step 3 — Compute for Interrupt #16:** $$V_{16} = 0x08010000 + (16 \times 4) = 0x08010000 + 64 = 0x08010000 + 0x40$$ $$\boxed{V_{16} = 0x08010040}$$ **Step 4 — Compute for Interrupt #42:** $$V_{42} = 0x08010000 + (42 \times 4) = 0x08010000 + 168 = 0x08010000 + 0xA8$$ $$\boxed{V_{42} = 0x080100A8}$$ **Step 5 — Interrupt Latency:** The Cortex-M3 ENTRY takes **12 clock cycles** (deterministic). $$T_{clock} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{latency} = 12 \times 20 \text{ ns} = 240 \text{ ns}$$ $$\boxed{t_{latency} = 240 \text{ ns}}$$ This is **27× faster** than the 8051's 6.51 µs — a critical advantage for high-speed control loops. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 2–3 Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to divide oscillator by 12 for 8051.** Many students use $f_{osc}$ directly instead of $f_{mc}$. Always write $\frac{f_{osc}}{12}$ explicitly. > > 2. **Confusing vector spacing.** 8051 uses **8-byte** spacing; Cortex-M uses **4-byte** spacing. Writing 8051 vectors with 4-byte offsets loses 1 mark. > > 3. **Forgetting the "current instruction boundary" delay.** Interrupt latency is NOT just the entry time. You must add the cycles remaining in the *currently executing* instruction. > > 4. **Stack growth direction confusion.** 8051 stack grows **upward** (SP increments on push); ARM/AVR/PIC grow **downward** (SP decrements on push). Mixing these up means wrong SP arithmetic. > > 5. **Omitting units in the final answer.** "Latency = 6.51" without "µs" loses 0.5 mark. Always box the answer with units. > > 6. **Drawing PC push as 1 byte.** PC is 16-bit in 8051, so **2 bytes** are always pushed per entry. > > 7. **Claiming Cortex-M requires software PUSH of R0–R3.** This is a frequent textbook error. The hardware does it **automatically** in 12 cycles. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Your Exam** - [x] **ENTRY** = hardware-driven transfer of control from main code to ISR. - [x] **7 Entry Phases:** Detect → Mask → Priority → Boundary → Save → Vector → Activate. - [x] **8051 Vector Table:** Base `0x0000`, 5 sources, 8-byte slots. - [x] **Cortex-M Vector Table:** Base from VTOR (default `0x00000000`), 256 sources, 4-byte slots. - [x] **8051 ENTRY = 2 machine cycles.** Cortex-M ENTRY = 12 clock cycles. - [x] **Machine cycle formula:** $f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12}$ (8051 only). - [x] **Latency formula:** $t_{latency} = (C_{instr,max} + C_{entry}) \times T_{clock}$. - [x] **8051 stack grows UP** (SP increments). **Cortex-M stack grows DOWN** (SP decrements). - [x] **Cortex-M auto-stacks 8 words** (xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0) = **32 bytes**. - [x] **8051 entry pushes only PC** (2 bytes) unless extended mode is used. - [x] **Vector address formula:** $V_{addr} = \text{Base} + (N \times \text{slot width})$. - [x] **Interrupt always waits for the current instruction to complete** — "instruction boundary rule." - [x] **Global enable flags:** 8051 uses `EA` (IE.7); Cortex-M uses `PRIMASK` and `BASEPRI`. - [x] **RETI** = Return from Interrupt; restores PC from stack and re-enables interrupts. - [x] **Cortex-M `EXC_RETURN`** mechanism: a special LR value signals "return from exception" to the hardware. - [x] **Nesting:** 8051 supports 2 levels only (high/low priority); Cortex-M supports 16+ levels via BASEPRI. - [x] **Tail-chaining** is a Cortex-M3 optimization that reduces back-to-back ISR latency by 6–30 cycles. --- > **END OF MODULE 3 — ENTRY NOTES** > **Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework. Course Outcomes CO3, CO4 addressed. Bloom's Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze.**

topics.title: SPACE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SPACE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **ARM Cortex-M** advanced microcontrollers, the term **"SPACE"** (Address / Memory Space) refers to the **architecturally defined, unified 4 GigaByte (4 GB) linear address space** of the processor. It is the complete range of unique binary addresses (from `0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF`) that the **Load/Store unit (LSU)**, the **Bus Matrix**, and the **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** can reference in a single, unified addressing model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"The Space of an ARM Cortex-M microcontroller is a single, flat, linear **2³² byte address map (4 GB)** which is partitioned into standardized functional regions: Code, SRAM, Peripheral, External RAM, External Device, and System regions, each with strictly defined access attributes."*

The 4 GB addressable space is the direct consequence of the **32-bit program counter and 32-bit data pointers** (`R0`–`R12`, `SP`, `LR`, `PC`) of the ARMv7-M architecture.

> **Key Constant:** Total Addressable Space = **2³² Bytes = 4,294,967,296 Bytes = 4 GB**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine the microcontroller's memory as a **massive, single-floor warehouse** rather than a multi-storey building:

- 🏢 **Old-school 8051 model:** A "multi-storey" building where you had *Code memory* on one floor, *XDATA* on another, and *PDATA/SFR* on a third. Each floor had its own pointer rules and access instructions (`MOVC` vs `MOVX`).
- 🏬 **ARM Cortex-M "SPACE" model:** A **single, gigantic, one-floor warehouse of 4 billion uniquely numbered mailboxes** (each mailbox = 1 byte). Every byte — whether it holds a C instruction, a global variable, a peripheral register, or an external sensor value — lives on the **same floor**, with the **same door number (address)**. The CPU doesn't care "what kind" of memory it is at the address level; the *memory map* and the *bus matrix* decide what physically sits behind each door.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** The "Space" in ARM is a *flat* world — there is **no memory paging, no bank switching, and no separate address spaces** in the programmer's view. Everything from `0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF` is theoretically addressable in C with a single pointer.

---

## 1.3 Why Does the "Space" Matter in Engineering?

1. **Portability of C code** — A pointer to a peripheral register is structurally identical to a pointer to a RAM variable.
2. **Linker Script Design** — Engineers must know the *fixed* address of every region to place `.text`, `.data`, `.bss`, `.heap`, and `.stack` correctly.
3. **Bit-Banding** — A unique ARM feature that exploits the 4 GB flat space to give *atomic, single-instruction* bit manipulation for SRAM and peripherals.
4. **Memory Protection Unit (MPU)** — Divides the flat space into **8 programmable regions** with strict access rights for RTOS tasks.
5. **DMA & Bus Arbitration** — The bus matrix routes master accesses (CPU, DMA, Ethernet) to the correct physical bus (I-Code, D-Code, System) based on the address alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Favorite):** The 4 GB space is divided into **6 primary architectural regions**, each of exactly **512 MB (0x2000_0000 bytes)** in size, except the **System region** which is **512 MB** mapped at the top.

---

## 1.4 Prerequisites & Forward References

| Concept | Used In Section |
|---|---|
| 32-bit registers | §2.1 |
| Bus Matrix (AHB-Lite) | §2.3 |
| Endianness (Little-Endian default) | §3.1 |
| Bit-band alias math | §3.2 |
| MPU region configuration | §2.4 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The 6 Architectural Regions

The 4 GB linear space is **architecturally fixed** by ARM Holdings. Silicon vendors (ST, NXP, TI, Microchip) **cannot move these regions** — they can only decide *how much* of each region they actually implement as physical memory or devices.

The regions are organized from the **bottom (address 0)** upwards to the **top (address 0xFFFFFFFF)**.

### 2.1.1 The Six Standardized Memory Regions

| # | Region Name | Start Address | End Address | Default Access | Typical Use |
|:-:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Code (Flash)** | `0x0000_0000` | `0x1FFF_FFFF` | Normal, XN possible | Program code, vector table, constants |
| 2 | **SRAM** | `0x2000_0000` | `0x3FFF_FFFF` | Normal, RW | Static variables, stack, heap |
| 3 | **Peripheral** | `0x4000_0000` | `0x5FFF_FFFF` | Device, RW | On-chip peripheral registers |
| 4 | **External RAM** | `0x6000_0000` | `0x7FFF_FFFF` | Normal, Cacheable | External SRAM/SDRAM (off-chip) |
| 5 | **External Device** | `0x8000_0000` | `0x9FFF_FFFF` | Device, Shareable | External peripherals (FPGA, NAND) |
| 6 | **System** | `0xE000_0000` | `0xFFFF_FFFF` | varies | NVIC, SysTick, MPU, ID, vendor ROM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Region Size Formula:** Each primary region occupies exactly **2²⁹ = 512 MB** (0x2000_0000 bytes). There is a **"Private Peripheral Bus" (PPB)** hole between `0xA000_0000` and `0xDFFF_FFFF` that is architecturally *Reserved* and will cause a BusFault if accessed.

---

## 2.2 The Bus Matrix — How the Space is Physically Reached

The ARM Cortex-M uses a **multi-master AHB-Lite bus matrix**. Each region is reachable by specific buses:

| Bus | Width | Master | Targets |
|---|:-:|---|---|
| **I-Code (I-bus)** | 32-bit | CPU instruction fetch | Code region only |
| **D-Code (D-bus)** | 32-bit | CPU literal loads | Code & SRAM |
| **System (S-bus)** | 32/8/16-bit | CPU data + DMA | All regions |
| **PPB** | 32-bit | CPU only | System region (NVIC etc.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** The I-Code bus is *optimized for sequential instruction fetch*. The D-Code bus *cannot reach peripherals* in many implementations. Any `LDR` to a peripheral register must therefore go through the **System bus** — this is why peripheral reads sometimes incur a wait-state penalty even at 0 wait-state CPU clock.

---

## 2.3 Access Attributes — The "Personality" of Each Space

Every address in the space carries one of three **memory type attributes** (defined in the ARMv7-M Architecture Reference Manual):

1. **Normal** — Compiler/CPU may reorder, merge, or speculate accesses. Used for Code, SRAM, External RAM. *Cacheable, Bufferable.*
2. **Device** — No reordering, no merging, no speculation. Used for Peripheral registers and External Devices. *Bufferable, Non-cacheable.*
3. **Strongly Ordered** — Like Device, but additionally *all subsequent accesses* must wait until this one completes. Used for the System region (NVIC, MPU control).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule:** A peripheral register MUST be marked `volatile` in C and the compiler MUST place it in the Device region; otherwise the optimizer can legally *drop a write to a GPIO port* thinking it is redundant.

---

## 2.4 Bit-Band Region — A Special Sub-Space

Within the SRAM and Peripheral regions, ARM defines a **1 MB Bit-Band Region** at the start of each:

| Bit-Band Region | Alias Region | Expansion |
|:---|:---|:-:|
| `0x2000_0000` – `0x200F_FFFF` (1 MB SRAM) | `0x2200_0000` – `0x23FF_FFFF` (32 MB) | ×32 |
| `0x4000_0000` – `0x400F_FFFF` (1 MB Periph) | `0x4200_0000` – `0x43FF_FFFF` (32 MB) | ×32 |

**Why 32 MB?** Each bit in the 1 MB region (8 Mbits = 8,388,608 bits) gets a full **32-bit word** in the alias region (8,388,608 × 4 = 32 MB). This enables **atomic read-modify-write** at the bit level.

> **Real-world utility:** Writing 1 to a single bit in a peripheral register (e.g., setting `GPIOA->BSRR` bit 5) is non-atomic in normal memory — an ISR can intervene. Through the bit-band alias, the write becomes a single 32-bit aligned store, which the AHB-Lite protocol guarantees is atomic.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\text{Total Space} = 2^{32} \text{ Bytes} = 4\,\text{GB}$ | Total linear addressable space | Bytes |
| 2 | $\text{Region Size} = 2^{29} = 512\,\text{MB}$ | Size of each of 6 primary regions | Bytes |
| 3 | $N_{\text{regions}} \times 2^{29} = 2^{32} \Rightarrow 8 \times 512\,\text{MB}$ | Theoretical partition into 8 equal slices | Bytes |
| 4 | $\text{Alias} = A_{\text{base}} + (\text{byte\_offset} \times 32) + (\text{bit} \times 4)$ | Bit-band alias address (SRAM) | Bytes |
| 5 | $\text{Alias} = 0x42000000 + (\text{byte\_offset} \times 32) + (\text{bit} \times 4)$ | Bit-band alias address (Peripheral) | Bytes |
| 6 | $\text{Number of aliased words} = 1\,\text{MB} \times 8 = 8\,\text{M} = 2^{23}$ | Count of bit-band alias words | Words |
| 7 | $\text{Alias size} = 2^{23} \times 4 = 2^{25} = 32\,\text{MB}$ | Size of the alias region | Bytes |
| 8 | $\text{Address bits needed} = \lceil \log_2(\text{size in bytes}) \rceil$ | General address-width requirement | bits |
| 9 | $\text{MPU regions}_{\text{max}} = 8$ | Maximum programmable MPU regions | regions |
| 10 | $\text{MPU region size}_{\text{min}} = 32\,\text{Bytes}$ | Smallest MPU region (power of 2) | Bytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Note that the alias formula multiplies byte offset by **32** (not 4). This is the *bit-expansion factor* (1 byte = 8 bits, each aliased to a 4-byte word → 8 × 4 = 32).

---

## 2.6 Why This Concept is Critical in Industry

- **Safety-Critical (ISO 26262, DO-178C):** The MPU partitioning of the 4 GB space is the foundation of *Freedom From Interference (FFI)* in AUTOSAR.
- **IoT Firmware:** Firmware update bootloaders place new code in External RAM region (`0x6000_0000`) so that running code in Code region can validate it.
- **RTOS Porting:** FreeRTOS, Zephyr, and ThreadX all derive the kernel's `.text` placement from the Code region's start address and the task stack from the SRAM region's end address.
- **Debug Probes (J-Link, ST-Link):** Map the entire 4 GB space via the Debug Access Port (DAP) for memory reads during breakpoints.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Address Space Size

**Problem:** Show that a 32-bit address bus can address exactly 4 GB of unique byte locations.

**Given:** Address width $n = 32$ bits.

**Step 1 — Count unique binary combinations:**
Each address bit can be 0 or 1. With 32 independent bits, the number of unique binary addresses is:

$$N_{\text{unique}} = 2^{32}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to bytes:**
Each unique address refers to exactly **1 byte** of addressable memory:

$$N_{\text{bytes}} = 2^{32} = 4\,294\,967\,296 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to GigaBytes:**
Using $1 \text{ GB} = 2^{30} \text{ Bytes} = 1\,073\,741\,824 \text{ Bytes}$:

$$\text{Total Space} = \frac{2^{32}}{2^{30}} = 2^{2} = 4 \text{ GB}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is why ARMv7-M chips can have *up to* 4 GB physical memory, but in practice no Cortex-M chip has more than a few MB of actual SRAM — the *virtual space* is large, but the *physical space* is whatever the silicon vendor populates.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bit-Band Alias Address Calculation

**Problem:** Compute the bit-band alias address of **bit 5 of the byte at address `0x2000_1234`** (SRAM bit-band).

**Given:**
- Bit-band base: $A_{\text{base}} = 0x2200\_0000$
- Original byte address: $A_{\text{orig}} = 0x2000\_1234$
- Bit number: $b = 5$

**Step 1 — Calculate the byte offset from the start of the bit-band region:**
The bit-band region starts at `0x2000_0000`. The byte offset is the lower 20 bits of the original address (because the region is 1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes wide).

$$\text{byte\_offset} = A_{\text{orig}} - 0x20000000$$

Substituting:

$$\text{byte\_offset} = 0x20001234 - 0x20000000 = 0x00001234$$

**Step 2 — Apply the bit-band alias formula for SRAM:**

$$A_{\text{alias}} = 0x22000000 + (\text{byte\_offset} \times 32) + (b \times 4)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the multiplication (byte_offset × 32 = byte_offset × 2⁵ = left-shift by 5):**

$$\text{byte\_offset} \times 32 = 0x00001234 \times 0x20$$

Performing the multiplication in hex:

$$0x1234 \times 0x20 = 0x24680$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bit-contribution term (b × 4 = left-shift by 2):**

$$b \times 4 = 5 \times 4 = 20 = 0x14$$

**Step 5 — Sum all three components:**

$$A_{\text{alias}} = 0x22000000 + 0x00024680 + 0x00000014$$

Performing the hex addition:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{alias}} &= 0x22000000 \\
                 &\quad +\, 0x00024680 \\
                 &\quad +\, 0x00000014 \\
                 &= 0x22024694
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**
The alias address `0x22024694` should be a 4-byte aligned address (multiple of 4) within `0x2200_0000` – `0x23FF_FFFF`. Check alignment: $0x22024694 \bmod 4 = 0$. ✅ Aligned. Check range: $0x22024694 < 0x24000000$. ✅ In-range.

> **Result:** Writing a 32-bit value of `0x00000001` to address `0x22024694` will **atomically set bit 5** of the byte at `0x2000_1234` in SRAM.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Peripherals — Bit-Band Alias (Different Base)

**Problem:** Find the alias address of **bit 13 of peripheral register at `0x4000_5400`** (e.g., GPIOA's mode register, approximate).

**Step 1 — Select the correct base** (peripheral alias base):

$$A_{\text{base}} = 0x42000000$$

**Step 2 — Calculate byte offset:**

$$\text{byte\_offset} = 0x40005400 - 0x40000000 = 0x00005400$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 32 (left-shift by 5):**

$$0x5400 \times 0x20 = 0xA8000$$

**Step 4 — Add bit-contribution (b × 4 = 13 × 4 = 52 = 0x34):**

**Step 5 — Final sum:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{alias}} &= 0x42000000 + 0x000A8000 + 0x00000034 \\
                 &= 0x420A8034
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Verify alignment:** $0x420A8034 \bmod 4 = 0$. ✅

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Number of Bits in a Bit-Band Region

**Problem:** How many individual bits can be addressed through the SRAM bit-band alias?

**Step 1 — Size of bit-band region:**

$$S_{\text{BB}} = 1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Bits per byte:**

$$1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Total addressable bits:**

$$N_{\text{bits}} = S_{\text{BB}} \times 8 = 2^{20} \times 2^3 = 2^{23} = 8\,388\,608 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with alias size:**
Each bit needs one 32-bit word in the alias:

$$\text{Alias size} = 2^{23} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 2^{23} \times 2^2 = 2^{25} = 32 \text{ MB}$$

This matches the architectural specification. ✅

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Address Sub-Spaces

| Sub-Space | Address Range | Size | Type | Bit-Band? | Cacheable? | Shareable? | Atomic Bit-Ops? |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Code | `0x00000000`–`0x1FFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Normal | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| SRAM | `0x20000000`–`0x3FFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Normal | ✅ (1 MB) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (via alias) |
| Peripheral | `0x40000000`–`0x5FFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Device | ✅ (1 MB) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ (via alias) |
| External RAM | `0x60000000`–`0x7FFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Normal | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| External Device | `0x80000000`–`0x9FFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Device | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Reserved Hole | `0xA0000000`–`0xDFFFFFFF` | 1 GB | BusFault | — | — | — | — |
| System (PPB) | `0xE0000000`–`0xFFFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Strongly-Ordered | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (NVIC regs are word/byte) |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Computing Stack & Heap Boundaries

In a typical linker script, the SRAM region starts at `0x2000_0000` and the chip has 64 KB of SRAM ending at `0x2001_0000` (exclusive).

**Stack grows downward from the top of SRAM** (full-descending model, ARM ABI standard):

$$A_{\text{stack\_top}} = 0x20010000 \quad (\text{initial SP value})$$

**Heap grows upward from the end of `.bss`**, suppose `.bss` ends at `0x2000_8000`:

$$A_{\text{heap\_start}} = 0x20008000$$

**Free RAM available for stack + heap:**

$$S_{\text{avail}} = A_{\text{stack\_top}} - A_{\text{heap\_start}} = 0x20010000 - 0x20008000 = 0x8000 = 32\,768 \text{ bytes} = 32 \text{ KB}$$

> **Engineering Tip:** A common stack overflow is exactly 0 → 32 KB region collision. The MPU can guard the *upper 1 KB* of stack to catch overflows with a MemManage fault.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 4 GB ARM Cortex-M Address Space — Architectural Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["0xFFFFFFFF  TOP OF SPACE"]:::top
    A --> B["SYSTEM REGION  0xE0000000 to 0xFFFFFFFF  512 MB  NVIC SysTick MPU FPU VENDOR ROM"]
    B --> C["RESERVED HOLE  0xA0000000 to 0xDFFFFFFF  1 GB  BusFault on access"]
    C --> D["EXTERNAL DEVICE  0x80000000 to 0x9FFFFFFF  512 MB  Device typed"]
    D --> E["EXTERNAL RAM  0x60000000 to 0x7FFFFFFF  512 MB  Normal cacheable"]
    E --> F["PERIPHERAL  0x40000000 to 0x5FFFFFFF  512 MB  Device registers"]
    F --> G["SRAM  0x20000000 to 0x3FFFFFFF  512 MB  Volatile memory"]
    G --> H["CODE  0x00000000 to 0x1FFFFFFF  512 MB  Flash and vectors"]
    H --> I["0x00000000  BOTTOM OF SPACE"]:::bot

    classDef top fill:#1f2937,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef bot fill:#1f2937,stroke:#22c55e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading aid:** Top of the diagram is the **highest** address (`0xFFFFFFFF`); bottom is the **lowest** (`0x00000000`). The diagram is a vertical "slice" of the 32-bit space.

---

## 4.2 Bit-Band Alias Mechanism — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["BIT BAND REGION 1 MB"]
        A1["Byte 0x2000_0000  bit 0 to 7"]:::src
        A2["Byte 0x2000_0001  bit 0 to 7"]:::src
        A3["Byte 0x2000_FFFF  last byte"]:::src
    end

    subgraph MAP["ALIAS MAPPING  32x EXPANSION"]
        F["byte_offset  x  32  plus  bit  x  4"]:::map
    end

    subgraph DST["BIT BAND ALIAS  32 MB"]
        B0["0x2200_0000  aliased bit 0 of byte 0"]:::dst
        B1["0x2200_0004  aliased bit 1 of byte 0"]:::dst
        B2["0x2200_0008  aliased bit 2 of byte 0"]:::dst
        B3["0x23FF_FFFF  aliased bit 7 of last byte"]:::dst
    end

    A1 --> F
    A2 --> F
    A3 --> F
    F --> B0
    F --> B1
    F --> B2
    F --> B3

    classDef src fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0c1a3a
    classDef map fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#3a2400
    classDef dst fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#053d18
```

> **Reading aid:** Each *bit* in the source 1 MB region is expanded to a *32-bit aligned word* in the 32 MB alias. Writing `1` to the alias word sets that bit atomically; writing `0` clears it.

---

## 4.3 Bus Matrix Routing of the Space

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["ARM CORTEX M4 CORE"]:::cpu

    subgraph MASTERS["AHB LITE BUS MATRIX"]
        IC["I CODE BUS  instruction fetch only"]:::bus
        DC["D CODE BUS  literal and SRAM data"]:::bus
        SB["SYSTEM BUS  data plus DMA plus peripherals"]:::bus
        PB["PPB  private peripheral bus"]:::bus
    end

    subgraph TARGETS["ADDRESS SPACE TARGETS"]
        T1["CODE 0x0000_0000"]:::tgt
        T2["SRAM 0x2000_0000"]:::tgt
        T3["PERIPHERAL 0x4000_0000"]:::tgt
        T4["EXTERNAL 0x6000_0000 to 0x9FFF_FFFF"]:::tgt
        T5["SYSTEM 0xE000_0000"]:::tgt
    end

    CPU --> IC
    CPU --> DC
    CPU --> SB
    CPU --> PB
    DMA["DMA CONTROLLER"]:::cpu --> SB

    IC --> T1
    DC --> T1
    DC --> T2
    SB --> T1
    SB --> T2
    SB --> T3
    SB --> T4
    PB --> T5

    classDef cpu fill:#1e293b,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef bus fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#0c0a3a
    classDef tgt fill:#ecfccb,stroke:#4d7c0f,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a2e05
```

---

## 4.4 Stacking Model of the SRAM Sub-Space

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["0x2001_0000  HIGH SRAM  Stack Top  initial SP"]:::high
    A["STACK  grows downward"]:::stack
    B["FREE HEAP  grows upward"]:::heap
    C["DYNAMIC ALLOC  malloc free"]:::heap
    D["STATIC DATA  .data .bss globals"]:::data
    BOT["0x2000_0000  LOW SRAM  start of region"]:::low

    TOP --> A
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> BOT

    classDef high fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#3a0808
    classDef stack fill:#fecaca,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:1px,color:#3a0808
    classDef heap fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#059669,stroke-width:1px,color:#053d18
    classDef data fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:1px,color:#0c1a3a
    classDef low fill:#1e293b,stroke:#22c55e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading aid:** The **Full Descending** stack model is mandated by the **ARM ABI (AAPCS)** for Cortex-M. The stack pointer (`MSP` or `PSP`) is decremented *before* a push and *incremented* after a pop.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"Define the Address Space of an ARM Cortex-M microcontroller. Why is it called a 'linear' address space?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distributed as 1+2):**

The **Address Space** of an ARM Cortex-M microcontroller is the complete, unified set of all unique binary addresses — from `0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF` — that the CPU, DMA, and other bus masters can reference. The size of this space is $2^{32} = 4\,\text{GB}$.

It is called **linear** (or *flat*) because every address in this 4 GB range can be accessed using a **single, uniform pointer** in C. There is **no memory paging, no bank switching, and no separate code/data space distinction** at the architectural level — unlike the 8051, which had distinct address spaces for `code`, `xdata`, and `pdata`. **(2 marks for linearity reason.)**

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**"List the six primary memory regions of the ARM Cortex-M 4 GB address space with their start addresses."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark per region):**

| # | Region | Start Address |
|:-:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Code | `0x0000_0000` |
| 2 | SRAM | `0x2000_0000` |
| 3 | Peripheral | `0x4000_0000` |
| 4 | External RAM | `0x6000_0000` |
| 5 | External Device | `0x8000_0000` |
| 6 | System | `0xE000_0000` |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / 2024 Pattern]`
**(a)** Draw and explain the complete 4 GB memory map of the ARM Cortex-M processor. List the **access attributes** of each region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Bit-Band** and **Bit-Band Alias** mechanism. Compute the alias address of **bit 6 of the byte at `0x2000_00A3`** in SRAM. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution B-A (a) — Memory Map & Access Attributes**

The ARM Cortex-M has a **single, linear 4 GB address space** divided into **6 primary regions** of 512 MB each, plus a **1 GB reserved hole**:

| Region | Start – End | Size | Default Attribute |
|---|---|:-:|---|
| Code | `0x00000000` – `0x1FFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Normal, cacheable, XN optional |
| SRAM | `0x20000000` – `0x3FFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Normal, cacheable, RW |
| Peripheral | `0x40000000` – `0x5FFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Device, non-cacheable, RW |
| External RAM | `0x60000000` – `0x7FFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Normal, cacheable, shareable |
| External Device | `0x80000000` – `0x9FFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Device, shareable, non-cacheable |
| Reserved | `0xA0000000` – `0xDFFFFFFF` | 1 GB | BusFault on access |
| System (PPB) | `0xE0000000` – `0xFFFFFFFF` | 512 MB | Strongly-Ordered |

**Access Attribute Explanations (for full 7 marks):**

- **Normal:** Compiler/CPU may reorder, merge, or speculate loads/stores. Used for memory that tolerates such optimizations (Flash, SRAM).
- **Device:** No reordering, no merging, no speculation allowed. Each access must complete before the next one. Used for memory-mapped peripheral registers where write order matters.
- **Strongly-Ordered:** Like Device, but additionally all subsequent instructions must wait. Used for the PPB region containing the NVIC and system control.

> **Why this matters:** Mis-classifying a peripheral register as Normal-type memory allows the compiler/CPU to *drop a write*, leading to silent hardware bugs. The `volatile` keyword in C enforces compiler discipline, while the *memory type* enforces CPU/bus discipline. **(1 mark for the engineering significance.)**

**Diagrammatic representation** (any neat box diagram with the six regions labelled with start addresses and access types earns full marks).

---

#### **Solution B-A (b) — Bit-Band & Alias Address Computation**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Bit-banding is a feature of the ARM Cortex-M that maps **each individual bit** in two dedicated 1 MB regions (one in SRAM, one in Peripheral) to **a full 32-bit word** in a corresponding 32 MB **alias region**, enabling *single-instruction, atomic* bit manipulation.

**Diagram Description (1 mark):**

The 1 MB SRAM bit-band region (`0x2000_0000` – `0x200F_FFFF`) is expanded to a 32 MB alias at `0x2200_0000` – `0x23FF_FFFF`. Each of the $2^{20} \times 8 = 2^{23}$ bits in the source gets a 4-byte aligned word in the alias.

**General formula (1 mark):**
$$A_{\text{alias}} = A_{\text{base}} + (\text{byte\_offset} \times 32) + (b \times 4)$$

**Numerical computation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the base address.** For SRAM, the alias base is:

$$A_{\text{base}} = 0x22000000$$

**Step 2 — Compute the byte offset** of `0x2000_00A3` from the start of the bit-band region:

$$\text{byte\_offset} = 0x200000A3 - 0x20000000 = 0x000000A3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula** with bit number $b = 6$:

$$A_{\text{alias}} = 0x22000000 + (0xA3 \times 0x20) + (6 \times 4)$$

**Step 4 — Compute byte_offset × 32** (left-shift by 5):

$$0xA3 \times 0x20 = 0x1460$$

**Step 5 — Compute bit contribution:**

$$6 \times 4 = 24 = 0x18$$

**Step 6 — Final sum:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{alias}} &= 0x22000000 \\
                 &\quad + 0x00001460 \\
                 &\quad + 0x00000018 \\
                 &= 0x22001478
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Writing `0x00000001` to address `0x22001478` atomically sets bit 6 of the byte at `0x200000A3`; writing `0x00000000` atomically clears it.

**Verification (for self-check):** The address `0x22001478` is 4-byte aligned ($0x22001478 \bmod 4 = 0$) and lies in the valid SRAM alias range (`0x22000000` to `0x23FFFFFF`).

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Internal Choice]`
**(a)** Explain the **bus matrix architecture** of the ARM Cortex-M. How does the address space determine the path of an access? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the System region (`0xE000_0000` – `0xFFFF_FFFF`), describe the **NVIC address space layout** and the **MPU region configuration registers**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution B-B (a) — Bus Matrix & Address-Based Routing**

The ARM Cortex-M uses an **AHB-Lite multi-master bus matrix** with **four** main buses:

1. **I-Code bus** — 32-bit, dedicated to *instruction fetch* from the Code region. Optimized for sequential prefetch.
2. **D-Code bus** — 32-bit, used for *literal loads* and SRAM data access from the CPU. Faster than System bus for SRAM.
3. **System bus** — 32/8/16-bit, used for *all CPU data accesses* to peripherals, external memories, and the System region. Also shared with the DMA controller.
4. **Private Peripheral Bus (PPB)** — 32-bit, dedicated to the System region (`0xE000_0000` – `0xFFFF_FFFF`). Only the CPU can master it; DMA cannot.

**How the address space determines the path (3 marks):**

- If the address is in `0x0000_0000` – `0x1FFF_FFFF` and the access is an instruction fetch, it is routed to the **I-Code bus**.
- If the address is in `0x0000_0000` – `0x1FFF_FFFF` and the access is a *literal* (constant pool load), it goes to the **D-Code bus**.
- If the address is in `0x2000_0000` – `0x3FFF_FFFF`, SRAM access goes via **D-Code** for CPU, or **System bus** for DMA.
- If the address is in `0x4000_0000` – `0x5FFF_FFFF` (Peripheral), it goes via the **System bus** because the D-Code bus does not reach peripherals in most implementations.
- If the address is in `0xE000_0000` – `0xFFFF_FFFF` (System), the **PPB** is the only allowed bus.

> **Engineering insight (1 mark):** A peripheral register read (`volatile uint32_t *reg = (volatile uint32_t *)0x40000000; value = *reg;`) is therefore *always* slower than a SRAM read at the same CPU clock, because the System bus incurs arbitration and wait-state penalties. This is a common cause of "why is my GPIO toggle too slow?" complaints in introductory labs.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Any block diagram showing the 4 buses and their routing to memory regions earns full credit.

---

#### **Solution B-B (b) — System Region, NVIC & MPU Layout**

**System Region Layout (3 marks):**

The System region occupies the **top 512 MB** of the address space. Key architectural sub-regions:

| Sub-region | Address Range | Purpose |
|---|:---:|---|
| NVIC (ITM, FPB, DWT) | `0xE000_0000` – `0xE000_EFFF` | Interrupt & debug |
| TPIU | `0xE004_0000` – `0xE004_0FFF` | Trace port |
| ETM (Cortex-M3/M4) | `0xE004_1000` – `0xE004_1FFF` | Instruction trace |
| MPU | `0xE000_ED90` – `0xE000_EDB0` | Memory protection |
| FPU (Cortex-M4F) | `0xE000_EF30` – `0xE000_EF4F` | Floating-point unit |
| Vendor ROM / SCS | `0xE00F_F000` – `0xE00F_FFFF` | System Control Space |
| ID Space | `0xE000_FD00` – `0xE000_FFFF` | Device ID, etc. |
| ROM Table | `0xE00F_F000` – `0xE00F_FFFF` | Debug component ID |

**MPU Register Set (4 marks):**

The **Memory Protection Unit (MPU)** can divide the 4 GB space into **up to 8 programmable regions** for access control. Key registers:

| Register | Address | Function |
|---|:---:|---|
| `MPU_TYPE` | `0xE000ED90` | Indicates number of regions (8) |
| `MPU_CTRL` | `0xE000ED94` | Enable, default memory map enable |
| `MPU_RNR` | `0xE000ED98` | Region number (0–7) |
| `MPU_RBAR` | `0xE000ED9C` | Region base address & valid & region number |
| `MPU_RASR` | `0xE000EDA0` | Region attribute & size & enable |

**MPU Region Attributes (1 mark):** Each region can specify:
- **Size** (32 B to 4 GB, power of 2)
- **Access permissions** (no access, privileged RW, full RW, RO)
- **Memory type** (Normal, Device, Strongly-Ordered)
- **Shareability** (Non-shareable, shareable)
- **Cacheability** (Non-cacheable, write-through, write-back)

> **Use case (1 mark):** In an RTOS, the kernel can place the kernel's `.text` and `.data` in one MPU region with *privileged-only* access, isolate task stacks in separate regions to detect overflows, and leave the peripheral region with *device* type and *privileged RW, unprivileged RW* access.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "×32" in the bit-band alias formula.** Many students write `byte_offset × 4` instead of `byte_offset × 32`. The **×32** is the *bit expansion*; ×4 is only the *word size*. Marks deducted: 1–2 per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong alias base.** SRAM base is `0x22000000`; Peripheral base is `0x42000000`. Mixing them up yields a BusFault-address answer. Marks deducted: 1.
>
> 3. **Omitting the "Reserved Hole" between `0xA0000000` and `0xDFFFFFFF`.** The 4 GB space is **6 regions + 1 reserved hole**, not 6 contiguous regions. Marks deducted: 1.
>
> 4. **Confusing "linear" with "physical" memory.** The 4 GB is the *virtual/architectural* address space. The actual *physical* memory in a typical Cortex-M4 is 256 KB–1 MB Flash + 32–256 KB SRAM. Marks deducted: 1.
>
> 5. **Not specifying access attributes** in the memory map. KTU examiners want at least one of *Normal/Device/Strongly-Ordered* per region. Marks deducted: 0.5–1.
>
> 6. **Stating that the bit-band region is the entire SRAM/peripheral region.** It is only the **first 1 MB** (`0x2000_0000` – `0x200F_FFFF` and `0x4000_0000` – `0x400F_FFFF`). Marks deducted: 1.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TOPIC RECAP — "SPACE" in Advanced Microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M)**

- [x] **Space size:** $2^{32} = \mathbf{4 \text{ GB}}$ linear, flat, unified.
- [x] **Region count:** **6 primary regions** of **512 MB each** + **1 GB reserved hole** + **512 MB System** = 4 GB.
- [x] **Region start addresses** (memorize in order): `0x0000_0000` (Code), `0x2000_0000` (SRAM), `0x4000_0000` (Peripheral), `0x6000_0000` (Ext RAM), `0x8000_0000` (Ext Dev), `0xE000_0000` (System).
- [x] **Reserved hole:** `0xA0000000` – `0xDFFFFFFF` → BusFault.
- [x] **Access types:** **Normal** (Code, SRAM, Ext RAM), **Device** (Peripheral, Ext Dev), **Strongly-Ordered** (System).
- [x] **Bit-band base addresses:** SRAM alias = `0x22000000`, Peripheral alias = `0x42000000`.
- [x] **Bit-band alias formula (memorize):**
  $$A_{\text{alias}} = A_{\text{base}} + (\text{byte\_offset} \times 32) + (b \times 4)$$
- [x] **Bit-band expansion factor:** 1 MB region → 32 MB alias (factor 32×), 1 bit → 32-bit word (factor 4× per bit, 8 bits per byte, 8 × 4 = 32).
- [x] **Bus matrix:** 4 buses — **I-Code, D-Code, System, PPB**; the address alone determines routing.
- [x] **NVIC** lives in the **System region** starting at `0xE000_0000` (private peripheral bus only).
- [x] **MPU** supports up to **8 regions**, with sizes from **32 B** to **4 GB** in powers of 2.
- [x] **Stack model:** **Full-descending** (ARM AAPCS standard) — SP decrements before push, increments after pop.
- [x] **Little-endian** is the default byte ordering for ARM Cortex-M.
- [x] **`volatile`** keyword is **mandatory** for any pointer to a peripheral register or bit-band alias to prevent compiler reordering.
- [x] **Caveat for exam answers:** Always state whether you are addressing the *architectural* (4 GB virtual) or *implemented* (vendor-specific physical) memory size.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** EXPORT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the programming architecture of advanced microcontrollers (PIC18F, dsPIC33, ARM Cortex-M families), **EXPORT** is a *linker-visible symbol scope directive* used in **modular programming** to declare that a label, variable, or function defined within the current source module is to be made **publicly accessible** to other separately compiled source modules during the linking stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme — PECPT631):**
> In KTU's Advanced Microcontrollers syllabus, EXPORT is examined under the *Modular Programming, Multi-file Project Organization, and Linker Symbol Resolution* theme. Students are expected to know both the **assembler-level EXPORT/GLOBAL directive** (MPASM, MPASMX, ARM AArch32 assembler) and the **C-language `extern` storage class specifier** (XC8, XC16, ARM-GCC).

### 1.1.1 Definition across Two Implementation Layers

| Layer | Keyword | Compiler Family | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Assembly | `EXPORT` / `GLOBAL` | MPASMX, ARM ASM | Declares symbol visible to linker for cross-file reuse |
| C Language | `extern` | XC8, XC16, MPLAB XC32, ARM-GCC | Declares variable/function defined in another translation unit |

### 1.1.2 Formal Syntax

```assembly
; ASSEMBLY (MPASMX for PIC18F)
        GLOBAL  MyVariable      ; EXPORT the symbol "MyVariable"
        GLOBAL  ISR_Handler      ; EXPORT an interrupt service routine
```

```c
/* C LANGUAGE (XC8 / ARM-GCC) */
extern unsigned int SystemTick;   /* EXPORT-style declaration: defined elsewhere */
extern void UART_Init(void);     /* Function prototype from another .c file   */
```

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Blueprint Office

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Builder — Think of EXPORT as a "Public Notice Board"**
>
> Imagine a large engineering office where **each engineer works in a separate cubicle (a `.c` or `.asm` file)**. Each cubicle has:
>
> - A **private drawer** — tools, variables, scratch work that only the engineer inside uses (these are *local* symbols — never exported).
> - A **public notice board outside the cubicle** — names of tools and reports that other engineers are allowed to request.
>
> The act of **EXPORTING** a symbol is exactly like *pinning a name on that public board*. Other cubicles can now ask the linker "please fetch the report called `SystemTick`" and the linker knows where to find it.
>
> If a name is **not exported**, it remains in the private drawer. Even if another engineer asks for it, the linker responds with the classic error:
> `undefined symbol` or `multiple definition` — depending on whether they asked for it or accidentally re-defined it.

## 1.3 Why EXPORT Matters in KTU Context

Advanced microcontroller projects in KTU lab examinations (PIC-based line follower robots, ARM-based sensor hubs, dsPIC motor controllers) routinely exceed **500 lines of code**. A single monolithic `.c` file is unmaintainable. EXPORT is the **enabling mechanism** that:

1. Splits a project across multiple files (`main.c`, `uart.c`, `adc.c`, `isr.c`).
2. Allows header files (`*.h`) to declare symbols defined in exactly one `.c` file.
3. Prevents **multiple-definition errors** by enforcing a *single definition rule (SDR)*.
4. Enables **library creation** — pre-compiled `.a` archives expose only EXPORTed symbols.
5. Supports **interrupt vector sharing** — the ISR vector table EXPORTs handler names to the startup code.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Relevant to EXPORT

| Parameter | Typical Value (PIC18F / dsPIC / ARM) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum exported symbols per module** | Practically unbounded; linker limit ~ 65,535 | Set by object-file symbol table capacity |
| **Header guard depth** | 8–32 nested `#ifndef` | Compiler-specific |
| **Single Definition Rule (SDR)** | **Strictly enforced** in C99/C11 | ODR in C++ |
| **Default linkage (C, file-scope)** | `extern` (tentative definition) | Without `static` keyword |

---

## 1.5 Section 1 Recap — Mental Hooks

> [!TIP]
> Before moving on, lock these three mental hooks:
> 1. **EXPORT = Make visible to the linker.**
> 2. **EXPORT is the *declaration* half; the actual storage is the *definition* in exactly one place.**
> 3. **Without EXPORT, every function/variable would have to live in one giant file.**

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Compilation Pipeline — Where EXPORT Operates

To master EXPORT, you must understand the **five-stage translation pipeline** that transforms a multi-file microcontroller project into the final HEX/BIN firmware burned into program memory.

| Stage | Input | Tool | Output | EXPORT Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Preprocessing** | `.c` + `.h` | Preprocessor (`cpp`) | Expanded `.i` source | Header guards, macro expansion — *no EXPORT yet* |
| **2. Compilation** | `.i` / `.asm` | Compiler (XC8, ARM-GCC) | `.o` / `.obj` object file | **`EXPORT`/`GLOBAL` written into symbol table here** |
| **3. Assembly** | `.s` | Assembler | `.o` / `.obj` | EXPORT directive processed |
| **4. Linking** | multiple `.o` + `.a` | Linker (`xlk`, `arm-none-eabi-ld`) | `.elf` / `.axf` | **Linker resolves EXPORTed symbols against IMPORTs** |
| **5. Locating** | `.elf` | Locator (`xc8-ld`, `armlink`) | `.hex` / `.bin` | Assigns final addresses; sections placed in memory map |

## 2.2 The Two Halves of Cross-Module Communication

EXP is a *two-sided contract*. There must be **exactly one EXPORT (definition) and one or more IMPORTs (declarations)**.

### 2.2.1 The EXPORT Side (Definition)

- In **C**: A variable declared *without* `extern` and *with* an initializer is a *tentative definition* that becomes a full definition.
- In **Assembly**: `EXPORT label` followed by the label appearing in code/data sections.

### 2.2.2 The IMPORT Side (Declaration)

- In **C**: `extern` keyword — promises the compiler the symbol exists elsewhere; **does not allocate storage**.
- In **Assembly**: `IMPORT label` / `EXTERN label` — declares an external symbol the assembler must not allocate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> In KTU theory exams, students often confuse *declaration* with *definition*. Remember:
> - **Declaration** = introduces the name and type (may use `extern`).
> - **Definition** = introduces the name AND allocates storage (exactly **once** per project).

## 2.3 Linker Symbol Table Mechanics

When the compiler produces an object file, it constructs a **Symbol Table** with three core entries for each label:

| Symbol Property | Description | EXPORT-related Flag |
|---|---|---|
| **Name** | The identifier string | — |
| **Value** | Address offset within section | — |
| **Binding (Scope)** | LOCAL vs GLOBAL | `STB_GLOBAL` ← set by EXPORT |
| **Section** | `.text`, `.data`, `.bss`, etc. | — |
| **Type** | `STT_FUNC`, `STT_OBJECT`, `STT_NOTYPE` | — |

> **Binding values (per ELF specification):**
> - `STB_LOCAL`  → symbol is private to its object file (no EXPORT).
> - `STB_GLOBAL` → symbol is EXPORTed, visible to other object files.
> - `STB_WEAK`   → EXPORTed but may be overridden by a non-weak definition.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes every formula, rule, and numeric constraint a KTU student must memorize for EXPORT-related problems. There are no physical constants in the traditional engineering sense here — the "formulas" are **linker equations** and **declaration rules**.

| # | Rule / Equation / Symbol | Statement | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{N}_{\text{EXPORT}} \geq 1 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Symbol accessible across modules}$ | For any cross-module symbol, at least one EXPORT must exist | Multi-file project linking |
| 2 | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{DEF}_i \;\leq\; 1$ for each global symbol | **Single-Definition Rule (SDR)** — only one `.c` file may *define* a global variable | Prevents multiple-definition link errors |
| 3 | $\text{Decl}_{\text{extern}} \;\in\; [0, \infty)$ | Any number of `.c` files may declare with `extern` | Header file pattern |
| 4 | `static` keyword ⇒ LOCAL binding | `static int x;` makes `x` file-private — EXPORT suppressed | Encapsulation |
| 5 | `volatile` + `extern` | `extern volatile uint8_t flag;` | Shared variable between ISR and main loop |
| 6 | `const` data EXPORT | `extern const uint16_t LUT[];` defined in flash (.rodata) | Lookup tables in program memory |
| 7 | `__interrupt` + EXPORT | ARM/PIC interrupt handlers must be EXPORTed with `__interrupt` qualifier | ISR vector wiring |
| 8 | Linker section: `.text` | Code EXPORT lives here | Function placement |
| 9 | Linker section: `.data` / `.bss` | Initialized / zero-initialized EXPORTed variables | RAM placement |
| 10 | `STB_WEAK` override precedence | Strong EXPORT beats weak EXPORT | HAL/layered driver patterns |
| 11 | Header file expansion multiplier | $\text{Size}_{\text{final}} = \text{Size}_{\text{preprocessed}} \times (\text{inclusion count})$ | Sanity-check redefinition bugs |
| 12 | Address-resolution equation | $A_{\text{final}} = A_{\text{section-base}} + A_{\text{offset-in-section}}$ | Linker locator output |

> [!WARNING]
> **No pipes in tables**: Notice that all set-membership and absolute-value notations use `\vert` rather than the pipe character — this is mandatory to keep the KTU markdown renderer from breaking table cells.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of EXPORT in Real Designs

| Application Domain | Use of EXPORT |
|---|---|
| **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR)** | Each software component EXPORTs only its public API; internals remain LOCAL |
| **Bootloader + Application split** | Bootloader EXPORTs a jump-table signature; application IMPORTS it |
| **Real-Time Operating Systems (FreeRTOS)** | Task functions EXPORTed as `void vTask(void*)`; kernel IMPORTS them |
| **DSP Filter Libraries (dsPIC)** | Coefficient arrays EXPORTed as `extern const fract coeffs[N]` |
| **Communication Protocol Stacks** | `UART_Send()`, `SPI_Transfer()` EXPORTed from `comm.c`; used by `app.c` |

## 2.6 Section 2 Mental Anchor

> [!TIP]
> Hold this single sentence in working memory:
> **"EXPORT is the *advertisement* of a symbol; the linker is the *postman* who delivers it."**

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Building a Two-File Project

We now construct a complete, runnable two-file firmware for a **PIC18F4550** (the typical KTU lab target) and derive each linker step. The project splits an LED-blinking firmware into two translation units, with EXPORT used to expose a counter.

### 3.1.1 File Inventory

```
project/
├── main.c          ← contains main(), IMPORTS blink.c symbols
├── blink.c         ← contains tick counter and ISR, EXPORTS them
├── config.h        ← shared macros
├── p18f4550.gld    ← linker script
```

### 3.1.2 `config.h` — Shared Header (De-facto EXPORT)

```c
/* config.h — shared declarations across modules */
#ifndef CONFIG_H
#define CONFIG_H

#include <p18f4550.h>

#define FOSC 48000000UL          /* 48 MHz crystal — required for USB  */
#define FCY  (FOSC / 4UL)        /* Instruction cycle = 12 MHz         */

/* EXPORT declarations (no storage allocated here) */
extern volatile unsigned long sys_tick_ms;
extern void Blink_Init(void);
extern void Blink_Task(void);

#endif /* CONFIG_H */
```

> **Step-by-step commentary:**
> 1. Header guard `#ifndef CONFIG_H` prevents repeated inclusion.
> 2. `#include <p18f4550.h>` injects SFR definitions.
> 3. `extern volatile unsigned long sys_tick_ms;` — *declaration only*. No RAM is allocated by this line.
> 4. Function prototypes are *implicitly `extern`* in C — even without writing `extern`, they advertise themselves as cross-file callable.

---

### 3.1.3 `blink.c` — The EXPORT Side (Definition)

```c
/* blink.c — contains the real definitions */
#include "config.h"

/* === EXPORTED GLOBAL VARIABLE ===================================== */
/* volatile: shared between ISR and main — must never be optimized away */
volatile unsigned long sys_tick_ms = 0UL;   /* ← actual RAM allocation here */

/* === PRIVATE (LOCAL) HELPER ======================================= */
static unsigned char led_state = 0;          /* ← file-private, NOT exported */

/* === EXPORTED ISR ================================================= */
void __interrupt(high_priority) Timer0_ISR(void)
{
    if (INTCONbits.TMR0IF) {
        INTCONbits.TMR0IF = 0;          /* clear flag */
        TMR0L = 0;                       /* reload   */
        sys_tick_ms++;                   /* shared counter */
    }
}

/* === EXPORTED FUNCTIONS =========================================== */
void Blink_Init(void)
{
    T0CON = 0x07;            /* TMR0 off, 1:256 prescale */
    TMR0L = 0;
    INTCONbits.TMR0IF = 0;
    INTCONbits.TMR0IE = 1;
    T0CONbits.TMR0ON = 1;
    INTCONbits.GIEH = 1;
}

void Blink_Task(void)
{
    if ((sys_tick_ms % 500UL) == 0UL) {
        led_state ^= 1U;
        LATBbits.LATB0 = led_state;
    }
}
```

> **Derivation of the EXPORT mechanism here:**
> 1. `volatile unsigned long sys_tick_ms = 0UL;` → compiler allocates **4 bytes** in the `.data` section of `blink.o`. The symbol is recorded in `blink.o`'s symbol table with binding `STB_GLOBAL` and section `.data`.
> 2. `static unsigned char led_state` → recorded with binding `STB_LOCAL`. Linker will reject any external reference to `led_state`.
> 3. `__interrupt(high_priority) Timer0_ISR` → function address becomes the EXPORTed symbol, and a *vector patch-up directive* (provided by the startup `.c` file) places its address into the high-priority interrupt vector slot (0x0008 in PIC18F).

---

### 3.1.4 `main.c` — The IMPORT Side (Consumer)

```c
/* main.c — uses the EXPORTed symbols */
#include "config.h"

void main(void)
{
    TRISBbits.TRISB0 = 0;       /* RB0 as output — drives LED */
    Blink_Init();

    while (1) {
        /* uses IMPORTed globals */
        Blink_Task();
        /* could also read sys_tick_ms directly here */
    }
}
```

> **Step-by-step IMPORT commentary:**
> 1. `Blink_Init()` and `Blink_Task()` are called without prior definition in `main.c` — they are resolved via the prototypes in `config.h`.
> 2. At compile time, `main.o` records each call site as an **undefined reference** (`SHN_UNDEF` in ELF).
> 3. At link time, the linker scans all object files, finds the matching `STB_GLOBAL` symbols in `blink.o`, and patches the call sites with the absolute addresses.

---

### 3.1.5 Algebraic Derivation of Link-Time Address Resolution

Let us derive the final address of `Blink_Init` in program memory.

$$
A_{\text{final}}(\text{Blink\_Init}) \;=\; A_{\text{section-base}}(\text{.text}) \;+\; A_{\text{offset-in-section}}(\text{Blink\_Init})
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
A_{\text{section-base}}(\text{.text}) \;=\; 0x0000_{\text{hex}} \quad \text{(PIC18F places .text at reset vector)}
$$

$$
A_{\text{offset-in-section}}(\text{Blink\_Init}) \;=\; \text{byte-position within linked .text segment}
$$

$$
A_{\text{offset-in-section}} \;=\; \text{size}(\text{startup\_code}) \;+\; \text{size}(\text{main}) \;+\; \sum_{i} \text{size}(\text{other funcs})
$$

For our minimal project:

$$
A_{\text{offset-in-section}}(\text{Blink\_Init}) \;=\; 32_{\text{bytes (startup)}} \;+\; 24_{\text{bytes (main)}} \;+\; 0 \;=\; 56_{\text{bytes}} \;=\; 0x38_{\text{hex}}
$$

$$
\therefore\; A_{\text{final}}(\text{Blink\_Init}) \;=\; 0x0000 + 0x0038 \;=\; 0x0038_{\text{hex}}
$$

The linker emits this into the **HEX file** record `:020000040000FA`, followed by the actual bytes of `Blink_Init` at offset 0x0038.

> **Verification:** When `main.o` referenced `Blink_Init`, it carried an unresolved reloc of type `R_PIC18_CALLOFFSET`. The linker's relocation algorithm:
> 1. Inserts the final 16-bit absolute address `0x0038` into the `CALL` opcode's operand field.
> 2. Adjusts for the PIC18F's *program-memory word size* (2 bytes per instruction word).

---

### 3.1.6 Verification Matrix — Symbol States Across Pipeline

| Symbol | File | Stage | Type | Binding | Section | Bytes Allocated? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `sys_tick_ms` | `blink.c` | Pre-Compile | `volatile unsigned long` | — | — | No |
| `sys_tick_ms` | `blink.c` | Post-Compile | `STT_OBJECT` | `STB_GLOBAL` ← **EXPORT** | `.data` | **Yes (4 B in RAM)** |
| `sys_tick_ms` | `main.c` | Post-Compile | `STT_OBJECT` | (none — local ref) | — | No |
| `sys_tick_ms` | After Link | Linked ELF | Defined | `STB_GLOBAL` | `.data` (addr 0x0200) | 4 B |
| `led_state` | `blink.c` | Post-Compile | `STT_OBJECT` | `STB_LOCAL` | `.bss` | 1 B (private) |
| `Blink_Init` | `blink.c` | Post-Compile | `STT_FUNC` | `STB_GLOBAL` ← **EXPORT** | `.text` | Code |
| `Blink_Init` | `main.c` | Post-Compile | undefined | — | `SHN_UNDEF` | None (relocation entry) |
| `Blink_Init` | After Link | Linked ELF | Defined | `STB_GLOBAL` | `.text` | Resolved to 0x0038 |

---

## 3.2 Derivative Example — C-Language `extern` Storage Class

The same idea, expressed purely in C without an assembly EXPORT directive:

```c
/* sensor.c — provider module */
int  raw_adc_value;             /* tentative def → becomes global EXPORT */
void Sensor_Read(void)          /* function — implicitly EXPORTed       */
{
    raw_adc_value = ADRES;
}
```

```c
/* display.c — consumer module */
#include "sensor.h"             /* contains: extern int raw_adc_value; */
                                /*          extern void Sensor_Read(void); */

void Display_Update(void)
{
    printf("ADC = %d\n", raw_adc_value);  /* uses IMPORTed symbol */
    Sensor_Read();
}
```

> **Derivation of the symbol's lifetime:**
> 1. **Tentative definition** in `sensor.c` → the compiler emits it as a *common symbol* (`SHN_COMMON` in older ELF, `STB_GLOBAL` in modern).
> 2. At link time, the linker collapses all common references into **one** RAM location — satisfying SDR.
> 3. If another file accidentally defines `int raw_adc_value = 5;`, the linker raises `multiple definition of raw_adc_value` — proving SDR enforcement.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common EXPORT Errors & Fixes

| # | Error Message | Root Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `undefined reference to 'X'` | No EXPORT found; symbol not defined anywhere | Add definition or check spelling |
| 2 | `multiple definition of 'X'` | EXPORT in two `.c` files | Remove `extern` from one definition; add `static` |
| 3 | `redefinition of 'X'` (in same file) | Duplicate definition in one TU | Remove duplicate |
| 4 | `symbol 'X' has wrong type` | `extern int x;` vs `char x;` | Match types in header |
| 5 | Linker warning: `type mismatch` | EXPORT function but IMPORT as data | Match declaration kind |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Multi-File Compilation Pipeline with EXPORT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[main.c] -->|Compile| B[main.o]
    C[blink.c] -->|Compile| D[blink.o]
    E[config.h] -.->|Included by| A
    E -.->|Included by| C
    F[uart.c] -->|Compile| G[uart.o]
    H[lib_peripheral.a] -->|Already compiled| I[uart_lib.o]

    B --> J{Linker xlk / arm-none-eabi-ld}
    D --> J
    G --> J
    I --> J

    J -->|Symbol resolution| K[Symbol Table merge]
    K -->|EXPORTed STB_GLOBAL matched with IMPORTs| L[Address Patch]
    L -->|Relocations applied| M[ELF .axf file]
    M -->|Locate sections| N[Memory map .map]
    M -->|Convert to Intel HEX| O[firmware.hex]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style C fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style F fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style H fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style J fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
    style K fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
    style L fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
    style M fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C
    style O fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
```

> **Reading guide:** Each `.c` file is compiled independently into a `.o` object. Only at the linker stage does EXPORT/IMPORT resolution occur. The linker produces an ELF, which is then *located* into the microcontroller's memory map and converted to HEX for the programmer.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Symbol Scope Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Variable or Function defined]) --> Q1{Is 'static' keyword present?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> LOCAL[LOCAL binding<br/>STB_LOCAL<br/>File-private<br/>NOT exported]
    Q1 -- No  --> Q2{Is 'extern' keyword present?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> DECL[Declaration only<br/>No storage<br/>IMPORT-side]
    Q2 -- No  --> DEF[Definition<br/>STB_GLOBAL<br/>EXPORT-side<br/>Storage allocated]
    LOCAL --> End1([Encapsulation achieved])
    DECL --> End2([Header file pattern])
    DEF --> End3([Cross-module API])

    style Start fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style LOCAL fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style DECL fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
    style DEF fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style End1 fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F
    style End2 fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F
    style End3 fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — EXPORT ↔ IMPORT Handshake

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Author as Programmer
    participant H as Header config.h
    participant B as blink.c
    participant M as main.c
    participant L as Linker

    Author->>H: Write 'extern void Blink_Init(void);'
    Note over H: DECLARATION only<br/>no storage allocated
    Author->>B: Write 'void Blink_Init(void){ ... }'
    Note over B: DEFINITION + EXPORT<br/>symbol in .text section
    Author->>M: Call Blink_Init();
    Note over M: COMPILER marks call site<br/>as undefined reference
    M->>L: Ship main.o with UNDEF reloc
    B->>L: Ship blink.o with STB_GLOBAL symbol
    L->>L: Match name Blink_Init in both
    L->>M: Patch CALL opcode with final address
    L->>L: Emit ELF + map file
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Linker Memory-Map Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FlashProgramMemory["FLASH Program Memory (e.g., PIC18F4550 32 KB)"]
        direction TB
        ResetVec["0x0000 — Reset Vector"]
        HighVec["0x0008 — High-ISR Vector (Timer0_ISR EXPORTed)"]
        LowVec["0x0018 — Low-ISR Vector"]
        Startup["0x002A — startup code"]
        Main["0x0040 — main()"]
        BlinkInit["0x0078 — Blink_Init() (EXPORTed)"]
        BlinkTask["0x00C0 — Blink_Task() (EXPORTed)"]
        Lib["0x0200 — Library functions"]
    end

    subgraph RAMData["RAM Data Memory (2 KB)"]
        direction TB
        DataSec["0x000 — .data: sys_tick_ms (4 B) EXPORTed"]
        BssSec["0x100 — .bss: led_state (1 B) LOCAL"]
        Stack["0x7FF — stack grows down"]
    end

    ResetVec -.- HighVec
    HighVec -.- LowVec
    LowVec -.- Startup
    Startup -.- Main
    Main -.- BlinkInit
    BlinkInit -.- BlinkTask
    BlinkTask -.- Lib
    DataSec -.- BssSec
    BssSec -.- Stack
```

> **Reading guide:** The linker places every EXPORTed symbol into a specific section. Functions go to flash; EXPORTed variables go to `.data` (initialized) or `.bss` (zero-initialized). LOCAL symbols like `led_state` still consume RAM but are unreachable from other files — encapsulation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> Explain the role of the `EXPORT` directive in modular programming of advanced microcontrollers. How does it differ from a function declaration in a header file?

**Model Answer (3 marks — must cover 3 distinct points):**

1. The `EXPORT` directive (or its C equivalent `extern`) is a **linker-level visibility declaration**. It tells the assembler/compiler that a particular label — typically a function name, ISR handler, or global variable — should be placed in the **object file's symbol table with GLOBAL binding (`STB_GLOBAL`)**, making it resolvable by other object files during linking. **(1 mark)**

2. It is the *advertising* half of a two-sided cross-module contract. The other half is `IMPORT`/`EXTERN`, which is a *request* for a symbol defined elsewhere. Without EXPORT, the linker treats the symbol as file-private (LOCAL) and rejects any external reference with an `undefined reference` error. **(1 mark)**

3. The header-file function declaration (`void UART_Init(void);`) is a **C-language type-safe declaration** that the preprocessor pastes into every including `.c` file. While it serves the same purpose — letting other files *call* a function — the `EXPORT` directive is the lower-level, assembler/linker mechanism that the C declaration ultimately relies on. In C, declarations without `static` are *implicitly EXPORTed*. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

> What is the Single Definition Rule (SDR) in C? Show, with a small example, what happens if it is violated across two files in a PIC18 project.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Single Definition Rule states that within an *entire C program* (all linked translation units combined), any given external (EXPORTed) identifier may be **defined at most once**. It may be *declared* any number of times across different files using `extern`, but the *definition* — the line that actually allocates storage — must appear in **exactly one** `.c` file.

**Violation Example (2 marks):**

```c
/* file1.c */
int counter = 0;           /* definition — allocates 2 B in .data */

/* file2.c */
int counter = 0;           /* duplicate definition — LINK ERROR */
```

**Linker output:**
```
Error[1107]: redefinition of 'counter'
```

**Fix:** Move the actual definition to only one file, and declare `extern int counter;` in every other file (typically via a shared header).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> With a neat diagram, explain the multi-file compilation pipeline of an advanced microcontroller project (using PIC18F as an example). Highlight the exact stage at which the EXPORT directive is processed and where it is consumed.

**Model Answer:**

**Stage-by-stage description (5 marks):**

1. **Preprocessing stage:** The preprocessor (`cpp0`) reads each `.c` file, expands `#include`, `#define`, and conditional directives. Header files (`.h`) are pasted into the source. EXPORT-related content (i.e., `extern` declarations) is copied into every including translation unit.

2. **Compilation stage:** Each preprocessed `.i` source is fed to the compiler (XC8). It produces **assembly code** (`.s`), in which each EXPORTed C function/variable is emitted with an `EXPORT` directive (or `GLOBAL` in MPASMX). E.g.,
   ```assembly
   GLOBAL _Blink_Init
   _Blink_Init:
       ...
   ```

3. **Assembly stage:** The assembler (`mpasmx` / `xc8-as`) converts `.s` into a relocatable object file `.o`. The EXPORT directive causes the assembler to record the symbol in the **object file's symbol table** with `STB_GLOBAL` binding.

4. **Linking stage:** The linker (`xlk`) ingests all `.o` files plus any libraries (`.a`). For each EXPORTed global symbol, it scans other object files for matching unresolved references (`SHN_UNDEF`) and patches the call sites with the final address (relocation).

5. **Locating / Conversion:** The locator assigns the linked sections to physical memory regions (per the `.gld` script) and produces an **Intel HEX** file for the device programmer.

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1) — explain each block in 2 minutes (2 marks).**

> **Where EXPORT is processed:** Stage 2 (compiler emits `EXPORT`) and Stage 3 (assembler records in symbol table).
> **Where EXPORT is consumed:** Stage 4 (linker matches it against IMPORTs).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> A PIC18F4550 project contains three files: `main.c`, `led.c`, `uart.c`. The variable `unsigned int system_tick` is defined in `led.c`, incremented by a Timer0 ISR in `led.c`, and read by `uart.c` to format a debug string every second. Write the complete contents of all three files plus a shared header, clearly marking which statements constitute EXPORT and IMPORT.

**Model Solution:**

**`shared.h` (1.5 marks)**
```c
#ifndef SHARED_H
#define SHARED_H
#include <p18f4550.h>

/* IMPORT declarations visible to all .c files */
extern volatile unsigned int  system_tick;
extern void Led_Init(void);
extern void Led_Task(void);
extern void Uart_Init(void);
extern void Uart_Task(void);
#endif
```

**`led.c` — EXPORT side (2.5 marks)**
```c
#include "shared.h"

volatile unsigned int system_tick = 0;     /* EXPORT (definition) */

void __interrupt(high_priority) Timer0_ISR(void)
{
    if (INTCONbits.TMR0IF) {
        INTCONbits.TMR0IF = 0;
        TMR0L = 0;
        system_tick++;                       /* touches EXPORTed variable */
    }
}

void Led_Init(void) { /* configure RB0 */ }
void Led_Task(void) { /* blink logic   */ }
```

**`uart.c` — IMPORT consumer (1.5 marks)**
```c
#include "shared.h"

void Uart_Init(void) { /* 9600 baud @ 48 MHz */ }

void Uart_Task(void)
{
    static unsigned int last_tick = 0;
    if ((system_tick - last_tick) >= 1000U) {
        last_tick = system_tick;
        printf("t=%u ms\r\n", system_tick); /* uses IMPORTed var */
    }
}
```

**`main.c` (1.5 marks)**
```c
#include "shared.h"
void main(void)
{
    Led_Init();
    Uart_Init();
    while (1) {
        Led_Task();
        Uart_Task();
    }
}
```

**Summary table for the examiner (for clarity — half mark):**

| Statement | EXPORT or IMPORT? |
|---|---|
| `volatile unsigned int system_tick = 0;` in `led.c` | **EXPORT** (definition) |
| `extern volatile unsigned int system_tick;` in `shared.h` | **IMPORT** (declaration) |
| `extern void Led_Init(void);` in `shared.h` | **IMPORT** (declaration) |
| `void Led_Init(void){...}` in `led.c` | **EXPORT** (definition) |
| `system_tick++` in ISR | Consumer of IMPORTed global |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> Compare and contrast the assembler directives `EXPORT`, `GLOBAL`, `IMPORT`, `EXTERN`, and `WEAK` as used in MPASMX (PIC18F) and ARM AArch32 assembly. Provide a side-by-side table and explain when each is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

| Directive | MPASMX (PIC) | ARM ASM | Purpose | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `EXPORT` | ✓ (alias) | ✗ | Make symbol visible to linker | Default cross-file visibility |
| `GLOBAL` | ✓ (canonical) | ✓ (`EXPORT` equivalent) | Same as EXPORT | **MPASMX preferred keyword** |
| `IMPORT` | ✓ (alias) | ✓ (`EXTERN` equivalent) | Reference external symbol | Consumer-side declaration |
| `EXTERN` | ✓ (canonical) | ✓ (canonical) | Same as IMPORT | **ARM canonical keyword** |
| `WEAK` | ✗ | ✓ (`.weak`) | Mark symbol as overridable | HAL stub functions |
| `EXTERN` (C extern) | via C | via C | C-language storage-class specifier | Header-file pattern |

**Explanation (5 marks):**

1. **EXPORT/GLOBAL** are *synonymous* in MPASMX — both insert `STB_GLOBAL` into the symbol table. In ARM AArch32 assembly, the directive is named `EXPORT`. KTU students must remember that the *semantics* are identical even though the *names* differ.

2. **IMPORT/EXTERN** are also *synonymous*. They mark an external symbol as *required* — the assembler emits an `SHN_UNDEF` relocation entry. ARM's preferred keyword is `EXTERN`.

3. **WEAK** is unique to ARM (and supported in XC8 with attribute declarations). A weak EXPORT may be overridden by a non-weak definition in another file. Use cases: HAL function stubs, default ISR handlers, library placeholders.

4. **Preferred usage:** Use `GLOBAL` in PIC MPASMX code; use `EXPORT` in ARM assembly; use `EXTERN` in ARM assembly when *importing*; use `extern` (lowercase) in C headers.

5. **Linker error contrast:**
   - Missing IMPORT: `undefined reference to symbol`
   - Missing EXPORT: `symbol X has no GLOBAL binding — treating as LOCAL`

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> Explain the linker output (.map file) of the multi-file project in Question A part (b). Identify which lines correspond to EXPORTed symbols, what their final addresses are, and how the linker resolved the cross-file call from `Uart_Task()` to `system_tick`.

**Model Solution:**

The MAP file is divided into several sections:

**Section 1 — Archive member included (1 mark):**
```
Archive member included to satisfy reference by file (symbol)

c:\pic\lib\clib.lib(doprnt.obj)
                              main.o (___printf)
```

**Section 2 — Linker-defined symbols (1 mark):**
```
__pc_stack        = 0x003800
__startup         = 0x000000
```

**Section 3 — Section Map (2 marks):**
```
SECTION                  START        END          LENGTH
.text                    0x00000000   0x000003FF   0x400
.data                    0x00000200   0x00000203   0x4     ← system_tick lives here
.bss                     0x00000300   0x00000301   0x1
```

**Section 4 — Symbol Map (3 marks):**
```
Symbol                  Address         Section
Blink_Init              0x00000038      .text     ← EXPORTed, resolved
Blink_Task              0x000000C0      .text     ← EXPORTed, resolved
Timer0_ISR              0x00000008      .text     ← EXPORTed, placed at vector
main                    0x00000040      .text
system_tick             0x00000200      .data     ← EXPORTed, RAM address
led_state               0x00000300      .bss      ← LOCAL, not visible to others
__smallconst            0x00080000      .const
```

**Resolution narrative (for full 3 marks on narrative):**
1. During compile, `uart.c` referenced `system_tick` and emitted an `SHN_UNDEF` relocation entry of type `R_PIC18_LO16`.
2. The linker found the matching `STB_GLOBAL` definition in `led.o`'s `.data` section.
3. The locator computed `A_final = 0x0200` and patched the `MOVFF` opcode in `Uart_Task` with this address.
4. The HEX file records the runtime address `0x0200` so the CPU accesses RAM correctly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing EXPORT with `#include`** — `#include` is textual copying; EXPORT is symbol visibility. They work together but are not the same.
> 2. **Forgetting `volatile`** on EXPORTed variables shared with ISRs — the compiler may optimize away reads, producing *spurious behavior*. Examiner will deduct **2 marks** for missing `volatile` in ISR-shared variable definitions.
> 3. **Using `static` and `extern` together** — semantically contradictory. `static` ⇒ LOCAL; `extern` ⇒ GLOBAL. Compile-time error in strict mode.
> 4. **Omitting header guards** (`#ifndef`) — leads to multiple-declaration errors when the header is included indirectly. Examiner expects guards in **every** header file.
> 5. **Defining variables in a header file** instead of declaring with `extern` — causes *multiple-definition linker errors* when the header is included by multiple `.c` files. This is the **single most common mistake** flagged by KTU examiners.
> 6. **Spelling the directive as `EXPORT` vs `GLOBAL`** — both work in MPASMX but ARM uses `EXPORT` exclusively. Examiners expect the correct keyword for the target architecture.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — EXPORT in Modular Microcontroller Programming**
>
> - **Definition:** EXPORT is the linker-level mechanism that makes a symbol (function or variable) visible across separately compiled object files.
> - **Assembly keyword:** `EXPORT` (ARM) / `GLOBAL` (MPASMX canonical). Consumer-side: `IMPORT` / `EXTERN`.
> - **C keyword:** `extern` declares (no storage); definition without `extern` allocates storage.
> - **Single Definition Rule:** Exactly **one** definition per global symbol across the entire program; **any number** of declarations allowed.
> - **Static Suppresses EXPORT:** `static int x;` is LOCAL — invisible to other TUs.
> - **Volatile is Mandatory** for ISR-shared EXPORTed variables; otherwise optimizer caches stale values.
> - **Header guards (`#ifndef … #define … #endif`)** are required in every shared header to prevent re-declaration.
> - **Pipeline position:** EXPORT is emitted by the compiler in stage 2, recorded by the assembler in stage 3, and consumed by the linker in stage 4.
> - **Symbol binding values:** `STB_LOCAL` (private), `STB_GLOBAL` (EXPORTed), `STB_WEAK` (overridable).
> - **ELF sections touched:** `.text` (functions), `.data` (initialized globals), `.bss` (zero-init globals), `.rodata` (const data).
> - **WEAK EXPORT** enables HAL stub override patterns; strong EXPORT always wins over weak.
> - **Common linker errors:** `undefined reference` (missing EXPORT), `multiple definition` (duplicate EXPORT), `type mismatch` (signature drift).
> - **KTU exam weightage (typical):** 2–3 marks in Part A as a short question; 7–14 marks in Part B under modular programming or linker questions.
> - **Lab relevance:** KTU lab viva often asks *"Why did you put `extern` in the header but not `static`?"* — answer with the SDR and the encapsulation idea.
> - **Architectures tested:** PIC18F (MPASMX), dsPIC33 (XC16), ARM Cortex-M0/M4 (ARM-GCC). Know both assembly directive and C `extern` variants for all three.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ALIGN
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ALIGN

# 📘 KTU 2024 SCHEME — ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
## Module 3: ALIGN Directive in ARM Assembly Language

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **ALIGN** directive in ARM assembly language is an **assembler directive** (not a processor instruction) that instructs the assembler to advance the **location counter** until it reaches an address that is a multiple of a specified power-of-two boundary. In the context of the ARM Cortex-M architecture (LM3S / TM4C / Tiva / STM32 platforms), proper data and code alignment is critical for:

- **Word-aligned (4-byte)** access of 32-bit data
- **Halfword-aligned (2-byte)** access of 16-bit data
- Avoiding **bus faults** on Cortex-M3/M4 when accessing unaligned addresses in certain C structures
- Optimizing **fetch cycles** of the Thumb-2 instruction pipeline

The canonical ARM syntax is:

```assembly
ALIGN   n                       ; Align to 2^n byte boundary
ALIGN   expr, max_bytes_skipped ; Align with padding restriction
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (PECPT631 Module 3):** The ALIGN directive is grouped under *ARM Assembly Language Programming* and is essential when writing startup files, vector tables, and linker scripts for LPC2148 / LPC1768 / Cortex-M3 boards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Shelf" Metaphor

Imagine a librarian placing books on shelves:

| Real World | Microcontroller World |
|------------|----------------------|
| 📚 Books of different sizes | Data types (byte, halfword, word) |
| 🪜 Standard shelf heights | Memory address boundaries (2, 4, 8 bytes) |
| 🧱 A brick used as spacer | **Padding bytes inserted by ALIGN** |
| 📏 Books aligned to shelf edge | Data aligned to address boundary |

If you place a **4-byte integer (word)** at address `0x20000001` instead of `0x20000000`, the CPU must perform **two memory fetch cycles** instead of one — analogous to a librarian having to fetch a book from two different shelves because it straddles the divider.

### 1.3 Key Constants & Boundary Terms

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Boundaries Used in ARM Cortex-M**
> - **Byte boundary** — every address (1 byte = 8 bits)
> - **Halfword boundary** — addresses divisible by **2** (16-bit alignment)
> - **Word boundary** — addresses divisible by **4** (32-bit alignment)
> - **Doubleword boundary** — addresses divisible by **8** (64-bit alignment, used in Cortex-M4 FPU)
> - The number **0x00000000** is always aligned to every boundary

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the ALIGN Directive

The ALIGN directive operates on the assembler's **location counter (LC)**, which tracks the current memory address being assembled. The execution logic is:

**Step 1 — Evaluate Target Boundary**
- The assembler reads the parameter `n` (a power of 2)
