**Differentiate between Little-Endian and Big-Endian byte ordering. State which one ARM Cortex-M uses by default.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Little-Endian | Big-Endian |
|---|---|---|
| Byte order (lowest address) | **Least Significant Byte (LSB)** | **Most Significant Byte (MSB)** |
| Example: `0x12345678` at `0x1000` | `0x78` @ `0x1000`, `0x56` @ `0x1001`, `0x34` @ `0x1002`, `0x12` @ `0x1003` | `0x12` @ `0x1000`, `0x56` @ `0x1001`, `0x34` @ `0x1002`, `0x78` @ `0x1003` |
| Used by | **ARM Cortex-M (locked)** | Network byte order, some DSPs |

**ARM Cortex-M default**: **Little-Endian**, locked at reset; the `AIRCR.ENDIANNESS` bit is read-only and always `0`. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

**What is the Bit-Band region in ARM Cortex-M? Give the formula for the alias address of a bit in the SRAM bit-band region.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **bit-band** mechanism maps each bit in two dedicated 1-MB regions (SRAM at `0x2000_0000` and Peripherals at `0x4000_0000`) to a full **32-bit word** in a corresponding 32-MB **alias** region. This allows **atomic bit-level read/write** via a single load or store instruction.

- **Alias address formula**:

$$
A_{\text{alias}} = \texttt{0x2200\_0000} + 32 \cdot \delta_{\text{byte}} + 4 \cdot b
$$

where `δ_byte` = offset of target byte from `0x2000_0000`, and `b` = bit number `0..7`. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **ARM Cortex-M data path** and describe how DATA flows between registers, ALU, and memory via the **Load/Store architecture**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **bit-band aliasing** in ARM Cortex-M. Compute the **alias address** to clear bit 7 of the byte located at SRAM address `0x2000_00C4`. Write the corresponding assembly snippet. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The **ARM Cortex-M data path** is organized as a 3-stage pipeline (Fetch → Decode → Execute) with a separate **Load/Store Unit (LSU)** that handles all memory transactions.

1. **Register File**: The processor contains **16 general-purpose 32-bit registers** (`R0–R12`), plus `R13 (SP)`, `R14 (LR)`, `R15 (PC)`. Operands are read from these registers; the ALU cannot operate directly on memory.
2. **Barrel Shifter**: Pre-shifts the second operand (by 0–31 positions) within a single cycle, eliminating separate shift instructions.
3. **ALU**: Performs arithmetic, logic, and comparison operations, writing the result back to the register file in the writeback phase.
4. **Load/Store Unit (LSU)**: Computes effective addresses using addressing modes (pre-indexed, post-indexed, PC-relative literal) and drives the **AHB-Lite bus matrix**.
5. **AHB-Lite Bus Matrix**: Routes DATA transactions to **SRAM** (`0x2000_0000`), **Flash** (`0x0000_0000`), **Peripherals** (`0x4000_0000`), or **System** (`0xE000_0000`).
6. **MPU Check**: Each transaction is checked against the Memory Protection Unit (if enabled) for **privilege and access permissions**.

**Flow of DATA in a typical `ADD R0, R1, R2` followed by `STR R0, [R3]`:**

- Fetch reads the instruction from Flash.
- Decode identifies the register operands.
- Execute: ALU reads `R1` and `R2`, computes the sum, writes the result to `R0`.
- The next instruction `STR R0, [R3]` causes the LSU to drive address `R3` on the AHB-Lite bus and write `R0` to SRAM.

> **Why Load/Store?** It simplifies the pipeline (no memory operands in ALU stage), improves clock frequency, and allows single-cycle deterministic execution. **(7 marks — 1 mark each for the six path components + 1 mark for flow integration)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Bit-band aliasing concept**:
In ARM Cortex-M, two 1-MB regions have a 32-MB **bit-band alias** that maps each individual bit to a 32-bit word. A normal `STR` to an alias address performs an **atomic bit set/clear** on the corresponding real bit.

- SRAM bit-band base: `0x2000_0000`
- SRAM alias base: `0x2200_0000`

**Step 2 — Compute byte offset**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\text{byte}} &= \texttt{0x2000\_00C4} - \texttt{0x2000\_0000} \\
&= \texttt{0xC4} \\
&= 196_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply alias formula for bit 7**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{alias}} &= \texttt{0x2200\_0000} + (196 \times 32) + (7 \times 4) \\
&= \texttt{0x2200\_0000} + 6272 + 28 \\
&= \texttt{0x2200\_0000} + \texttt{0x188C} \\
&= \texttt{0x2200\_188C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Assembly snippet to CLEAR bit 7** (write `0` to the alias word):

```
LDR R1, =0x2200188C      ; load alias address
MOV R0, #0                ; value 0 means clear bit
STR R0, [R1]              ; atomic clear of bit 7 of byte at 0x2000_00C4
```

To **SET** the bit, the same instruction with `MOV R0, #1` would suffice. **(7 marks — 1 mark for theory, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for code, 1 mark for explanation of atomicity)**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **ARM Cortex-M unified memory map** with the address ranges for Code, SRAM, Peripheral, External, and System regions. Describe how DATA is stored in **Little-Endian** format with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **stack memory organization** in ARM Cortex-M. Differentiate between **MSP** and **PSP**, and describe the `PUSH`/`POP` mechanism for saving and restoring DATA during interrupt handling. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The **ARM Cortex-M unified memory map** defines a **single 4-GB linear address space** (`0x0000_0000` to `0xFFFF_FFFF`) divided into functional regions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{0x0000\_0000} - \texttt{0x1FFF\_FFFF} &\rightarrow \text{Code (Flash)} \\
\texttt{0x2000\_0000} - \texttt{0x3FFF\_FFFF} &\rightarrow \text{SRAM} \\
\texttt{0x4000\_0000} - \texttt{0x5FFF\_FFFF} &\rightarrow \text{Peripherals} \\
\texttt{0x6000\_0000} - \texttt{0x9FFF\_FFFF} &\rightarrow \text{External RAM / Device} \\
\texttt{0xA000\_0000} - \texttt{0xDFFF\_FFFF} &\rightarrow \text{External Device} \\
\texttt{0xE000\_0000} - \texttt{0xFFFF\_FFFF} &\rightarrow \text{System (NVIC, SysTick, MPU)}
\end{aligned}
$$

- The **Code region** stores program instructions and read-only DATA constants.
- The **SRAM region** stores read/write DATA — variables, stack, and heap.
- The **Peripheral region** contains **memory-mapped registers** — each register is a fixed DATA address.
- The **System region** holds private CPU peripherals such as **NVIC**, **SysTick**, **MPU**, and **SCB**.

**Little-Endian storage example**: Store `0x12345678` at `0x2000_0000`:

| Address | Byte stored |
|---|---|
| `0x2000_0000` | `0x78` (LSB) |
| `0x2000_0001` | `0x56` |
| `0x2000_0002` | `0x34` |
| `0x2000_0003` | `0x12` (MSB) |

This is critical for **portability**: identical C code produces identical DATA layouts across all Cortex-M vendors. **(7 marks — 3 marks for map, 2 marks for regions, 2 marks for endianness example)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The **Cortex-M stack** is **full-descending** — the stack pointer starts high and moves to **lower addresses** as DATA is pushed.

**MSP vs PSP**:
- **`MSP` (Main Stack Pointer)**: Default after reset; used by the kernel, exception handlers, and bare-metal code.
- **`PSP` (Process Stack Pointer)**: Used by **RTOS application tasks**; allows isolation between kernel and task stacks.
- The **CONTROL[1]** register selects which SP is active in Thread mode (`0` = MSP, `1` = PSP).

**PUSH/POP mechanism during an interrupt**:

1. CPU automatically **stacks** eight registers: `R0-R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR` — a fixed 32-byte frame at the **active SP**.
2. ISR executes and uses the **MSP** for its own locals.
3. ISR returns via `BX LR` (where `LR = 0xFFFF_FFF9` for thread mode, `0xFFFF_FFFD` for handler mode).
4. The CPU automatically **unstacks** the eight registers, restoring DATA context.

**Custom register saving** in C extension:

```
push {r4-r7, lr}    ; save non-banked DATA registers
; ... ISR body ...
pop  {r4-r7, pc}    ; restore and return (PC <- LR)
```

This **deterministic stacking** makes Cortex-M ideal for hard real-time systems where worst-case interrupt latency must be predictable. **(7 marks — 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks MSP/PSP, 3 marks PUSH/POP ISR flow)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Endianness confusion** (–1 mark): Forgetting that ARM Cortex-M is **little-endian LSB-first**, not big-endian.
> 2. **Bit-band formula mistakes** (–2 marks): Off-by-one in the `$4 \cdot b$` term or forgetting the base `0x2200_0000` for SRAM alias (use `0x4200_0000` for peripheral alias — common slip).
> 3. **Data type suffix confusion** (–1 mark): Writing `LDRB` instead of `LDRSB` for signed extension. The `S` prefix matters!
> 4. **MSP/PSP swap** (–1 mark): In RTOS designs, writing to `MSP` when `PSP` is active silently corrupts task stacks — students must mention `CONTROL` register.
> 5. **Alignment violation** (–2 marks): Forgetting that halfword access must be on a 2-byte boundary; word access on a 4-byte boundary. Misaligned access causes a **HardFault** on many Cortex-M devices.
> 6. **No mention of AAPCS** (–1 mark): KTU frequently asks about the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard** — register roles `R0-R3` (args), `R4-R11` (callee-saved) must be stated explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **DATA in Cortex-M** = typed, addressable, endian-ordered, alignment-sensitive objects moving through a Load/Store architecture.
- ✅ **Register file** has **16 × 32-bit registers** — `R0-R12` general, `R13=SP`, `R14=LR`, `R15=PC`.
- ✅ **Memory map** is unified 4 GB; Code `0x0000_0000`, SRAM `0x2000_0000`, Peripheral `0x4000_0000`, System `0xE000_0000`.
- ✅ **Little-Endian** is locked; **LSB at the lowest address**.
- ✅ **Data types**: `B` (8-bit), `H` (16-bit), `W` (32-bit); `S` prefix denotes signed extension.
- ✅ **Bit-band aliasing** maps 1 MB → 32 MB; formula uses factor ×32 for byte and ×4 for bit.
- ✅ **Stack is full-descending**; **MSP** for kernel, **PSP** for tasks; selected via `CONTROL[1]`.
- ✅ **PUSH/POP** is the primary mechanism for saving/restoring DATA context in ISRs.
- ✅ **No memory-to-memory ALU ops** — all arithmetic requires registers.
- ✅ **Alignment**: word = 4-byte, halfword = 2-byte; misalignment → **HardFault**.
- ✅ **Two's complement** for signed integers; negation = `~x + 1`.
- ✅ **Load/Store addressing modes**: immediate, register, scaled, pre-indexed (`!`), post-indexed.
- ✅ **Endianness + bit-band + MPU** form the three pillars of robust DATA handling in embedded C.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple ALP Programs on Arithmetic and Logical Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simple ALP Programs on Arithmetic and Logical Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assembly Language Program (ALP):** A low-level programming representation of machine code instructions using mnemonic opcodes (e.g., `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `ORR`) and symbolic labels that directly correspond to the native instruction set of the target microcontroller (ARM7TDMI / LPC2148 in the context of KTU PECPT631).

In the context of **PECPT631 – Advanced Microcontrollers**, *Simple ALP Programs on Arithmetic and Logical Operations* refers to the set of fundamental assembly programs that exercise the **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** of the ARM7TDMI-S core. These programs manipulate data stored in **General Purpose Registers (R0–R12)**, use the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** for condition flags, and typically employ **load/store architecture** where memory is accessed only through `LDR`/`STR` instructions.

### Categories of Operations Covered

| Category | Sub-Operations | Key Mnemonics |
|----------|----------------|---------------|
| **Arithmetic** | Add, Sub, Mul, MLA | `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `MLA` |
| **Logical** | AND, OR, XOR, Bit Clear | `AND`, `ORR`, `EOR`, `BIC` |
| **Shift/Rotate** | Logical/Arithmetic Shift, Rotate | `LSL`, `LSR`, `ASR`, `ROR` |
| **Comparison** | Flag-setting tests | `CMP`, `TST`, `TEQ` |
| **Multiply-Accumulate** | DSP-style | `MLA`, `MUL` |
| **Barrel Shifter** | Operand2 pre-processing | Embedded in data-processing |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Calculator's Workshop" 🧮

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a microcontroller's CPU as a **highly skilled carpenter's workshop**.
> - **Registers (R0–R12)** are the **workbenches** where the carpenter places raw wood.
> - **The ALU** is the **table saw / chisel** that cuts, shapes, or joins the wood.
> - **CPSR Flags (N, Z, C, V)** are the **quality-check labels** stuck on the finished piece — *Normal?, Zero?, Carry? (overflow?)*
> - **Mnemonics (`ADD`, `AND`, `LSL`)** are the **verbal commands** you shout to the carpenter.
> - **ALP** is the **instruction sheet** the carpenter reads line-by-line.

When you write `ADD R0, R1, R2`, you are essentially saying:
> *"Take the wood from workbench **R1** and the wood from workbench **R2**, glue/join them together using the joining tool, and place the result on workbench **R0**. Then update the quality labels."*

This mental model makes it easy to remember that **ARM is a load/store RISC architecture** — you must first fetch data into registers (place wood on workbenches) before any arithmetic/logical operation can occur.

---

## 1.3 The ARM7TDMI Execution Pipeline (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ARM7TDMI uses a **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch → Decode → Execute). Arithmetic and logical instructions occupy only the **Execute stage**, making them single-cycle on the ALU (not counting operand2 shifter cycles, which are folded into the execute stage for free).

Standard metric for ARM7TDMI:
- **Clock Speed:** up to **60 MHz** (LPC2148 typical)
- **Instruction Width:** **32 bits** (ARM mode) or **16 bits** (Thumb mode)
- **Register File:** **37 × 32-bit registers** (31 general-purpose + 6 status)
- **Barrel Shifter Latency:** **0 cycles** (operand2 is pre-processed in the decode/shift stage — this is ARM's unique advantage)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an ARM Data-Processing Instruction

Every arithmetic/logical instruction in ARM has the **same canonical format**:

```
[COND]  [Opcode]  [Rn], [Rd], [Operand2]
   |        |         |      |       |
   |        |         |      |       └── Operand2: register + optional shift
   |        |         |      └── Destination register (R0–R12, R14)
   |        |         └── First source register (Rn)
   |        └── Mnemonic (ADD, SUB, AND, etc.)
   └── Condition code (EQ, NE, LT, GT, …) — optional
```

### 2.1.1 Operand2 — The "Free Lunch" Barrel Shifter

> [!NOTE]
> **The Operand2 field is the secret weapon of ARM.** It allows a register to be **shifted or rotated by an immediate value or another register *for free***, before being used in the operation. This means `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #4` is a **single instruction, single cycle** — equivalent to `R0 = R1 + (R2 << 4)`.

The 12-bit Operand2 field contains:
- **8-bit immediate value** (zero-extended to 32 bits) OR
- **4-bit shift amount** applied to an **Rm register**

---

## 2.2 Complete Instruction Set — Arithmetic

| Mnemonic | Operation | CPSR Flags Updated? | Syntax |
|----------|-----------|:-------------------:|--------|
| `ADD` | Rd = Rn + Operand2 | ✅ Yes (default) | `ADD{S} Rd, Rn, Op2` |
| `ADC` | Rd = Rn + Operand2 + C | ✅ Yes | `ADC{S} Rd, Rn, Op2` |
| `SUB` | Rd = Rn − Operand2 | ✅ Yes | `SUB{S} Rd, Rn, Op2` |
| `SBC` | Rd = Rn − Operand2 − (1−C) + 1 | ✅ Yes | `SBC{S} Rd, Rn, Op2` |
| `RSB` | Rd = Operand2 − Rn | ✅ Yes | `RSB{S} Rd, Rn, Op2` |
| `RSC` | Rd = Operand2 − Rn − (1−C) + 1 | ✅ Yes | `RSC{S} Rd, Rn, Op2` |
| `MUL` | Rd = Rm × Rs (32-bit result) | ✅ Yes | `MUL{S} Rd, Rm, Rs` |
| `MLA` | Rd = (Rm × Rs) + Rn | ✅ Yes | `MLA{S} Rd, Rm, Rs, Rn` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **`{S}` suffix** controls flag updates. Without `S`, CPSR is untouched. With `S`, the **N, Z, C, V flags** are updated based on the result.
> The condition codes are written as 2-letter suffixes: `ADDEQ`, `ADDNE`, `ADDGT`, `ADDLT`, etc.

---

## 2.3 Complete Instruction Set — Logical & Comparison

| Mnemonic | Operation | CPSR Updated? | Truth-Table Hint |
|----------|-----------|:-------------:|-------------------|
| `AND` | Rd = Rn & Op2 | ✅ (with S) | `1·1=1, else 0` |
| `ORR` | Rd = Rn \| Op2 | ✅ (with S) | `0·0=0, else 1` |
| `EOR` | Rd = Rn ^ Op2 | ✅ (with S) | Same bits → 0, diff → 1 |
| `BIC` | Rd = Rn & ~Op2 | ✅ (with S) | **Bit Clear**: clears bits set in Op2 |
| `MVN` | Rd = ~Op2 | ✅ (with S) | Logical NOT |
| `CMP` | Op1 − Op2, **result discarded** | ✅ Yes | Sets flags only |
| `TST` | Op1 & Op2 | ✅ Yes | AND test |
| `TEQ` | Op1 ^ Op2 | ✅ Yes | XOR test |

---

## 2.4 Shift / Rotate Operations (Barrel Shifter)

| Mnemonic | Operation | Use Case |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| `LSL` | Logical Shift Left (fill with 0) | Multiply by 2ⁿ |
| `LSR` | Logical Shift Right (fill with 0) | Divide unsigned by 2ⁿ |
| `ASR` | Arithmetic Shift Right (sign-extend) | Divide signed by 2ⁿ |
| `ROR` | Rotate Right (bit-wrap) | Circular buffer / checksum |
| `RRX` | Rotate Right Extended by C | 33-bit rotate including C flag |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Must-Memorize Equations)

| # | Concept | Formula | Flag Behavior |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Carry (Unsigned Overflow)** | $C = \text{carry out from bit 31}$ | $C = 1$ if addition produced unsigned overflow |
| 2 | **Overflow (Signed)** | $V = (C_{31}^{\text{sub}} \oplus C_{30}^{\text{sub}})$ for SUB | $V = 1$ if signed result is invalid |
| 3 | **Zero** | $Z = 1 \iff \text{Result} = 0$ | Indicates equality / empty register |
| 4 | **Negative** | $N = \text{bit 31 of result}$ | Indicates signed-negative result |
| 5 | **Multiplication scaling** | $\text{MUL} \to 32\text{-bit truncated result}$ | High 32 bits are **discarded** (use `UMULL` for 64-bit) |
| 6 | **Baud/Time** | $t = \dfrac{N_{\text{cycles}}}{f_{\text{CLK}}}$ | For delay loops |
| 7 | **Delay loop value** | $N_{\text{cycles}} = (L+1) \cdot C_{i}$ | Where $L$ is loop counter, $C_i$ is cycles/iteration |
| 8 | **2's complement negation** | $-x = \tilde{x} + 1 = \text{MVN}+ \text{ADD 1}$ | Used by `RSB` |
| 9 | **Mask to extract bits** | $\text{Field} = (x \ \& \ m) \gg s$ | Use `AND` then `LSR` |
| 10 | **Barrel shifter equivalent** | $x \ll n \equiv x \cdot 2^{n}$ | $x \gg n \equiv \lfloor x / 2^{n} \rfloor$ (unsigned) |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BCD arithmetic in financial meters** — Addition routines that correct after each nibble operation using the `C` flag (DAA equivalent via `ADD` + conditional `SUB 0x06`).
- **Checksum / CRC computation** — `EOR` and `ROR` loops over message bytes for serial protocols.
- **DSP filters** — `MLA` (multiply-accumulate) is the heart of every FIR filter; one cycle per tap.
- **Bit manipulation in peripheral registers** — `BIC` to clear specific bits, `ORR` to set bits when configuring GPIO, UART, or timers.
- **Sign/zero extension** — `MOV` with `LSL #24` packs/unpacks data efficiently.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step ALP Programs with Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading convention:** All programs assume ARM7TDMI (LPC2148). Data is stored in word-aligned memory. We use **little-endian** byte ordering (ARM default). Register conventions: `R0`–`R3` are scratch / argument registers; `R4`–`R11` are local variables; `R13=SP`, `R14=LR`, `R15=PC`.

---

## 3.1 Program 1 — Addition of Two 32-bit Numbers

### Problem
Add two 32-bit numbers stored in memory locations `0x40000000` and `0x40000004`, store the 64-bit result at `0x40000008` (low word) and `0x4000000C` (high word, for future carry).

### Algorithm
1. Load first operand → R0
2. Load second operand → R1
3. ADD with `S` suffix → capture carry in CPSR
4. Store low word at result location
5. Use `ADC` trick or `MOVCS` to capture carry → store at high word

### Source Code

```armasm
        AREA    Addition, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =SRC1      ; R0 ← address of first number
        LDR     R1, [R0]       ; R1 ← SRC1 value
        LDR     R0, =SRC2
        LDR     R2, [R0]       ; R2 ← SRC2 value
        ADDS    R3, R1, R2     ; R3 = R1+R2, update flags (C set on overflow)
        LDR     R0, =RESULT
        STR     R3, [R0]       ; store low 32 bits
        MOVCS   R4, #1         ; if Carry set → high word = 1
        MOVCC   R4, #0         ; else high word = 0
        STR     R4, [R0, #4]   ; store high 32 bits

STOP    B       STOP

        AREA    DataArea, DATA, READWRITE
SRC1    DCD     0xFFFFFFFE     ; 4294967294
SRC2    DCD     0x00000005     ; 5
RESULT  DCD     0, 0           ; low word, high word
        END
```

### Step-by-Step Trace (Analytical Matrix)

| Step | Instruction | R1 (hex) | R2 (hex) | R3 (hex) | C flag | Z flag | Result (decimal) |
|:----:|-------------|:--------:|:--------:|:--------:|:------:|:------:|:----------------:|
| 1 | `LDR R1, [R0]` | 0xFFFFFFFE | – | – | – | – | 4 294 967 294 |
| 2 | `LDR R2, [R0]` | 0xFFFFFFFE | 0x00000005 | – | – | – | 5 |
| 3 | `ADDS R3, R1, R2` | – | – | 0x00000003 | **1** | 0 | 4 294 967 299 |
| 4 | `STR R3, [R0]` | – | – | – | – | – | low=0x00000003 |
| 5 | `MOVCS R4, #1` | – | – | – | – | – | high=0x00000001 |

### Mathematical Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SRC1} + \text{SRC2} &= 0xFFFFFFFE + 0x00000005 \\
&= (2^{32} - 2) + 5 \\
&= 2^{32} + 3 \\
&= 4\,294\,967\,299_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

In 64-bit representation: low word = **0x00000003**, high word = **0x00000001** ✔

---

## 3.2 Program 2 — Subtraction with Borrow (Multi-precision)

### Problem
Subtract a 64-bit number (Y) from a 64-bit number (X). Use `SBC` for chained borrow propagation.

### Concept Recap — Borrow vs. Carry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In ARM, the **C flag represents "NOT borrow"** for subtraction.
> - `C = 1` → no borrow occurred (result ≥ 0 in unsigned sense)
> - `C = 0` → borrow occurred (result underflowed)
> The mnemonic `SBC` (Subtract with Carry) automatically does: `Rd = Rn − Op2 − (1 − C)`

### Source Code

```armasm
        AREA    Subtraction64, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =X
        LDMIA   R0, {R1, R2}    ; R1 = X_low, R2 = X_high
        LDR     R0, =Y
        LDMIA   R0, {R3, R4}    ; R3 = Y_low, R4 = Y_high
        SUBS    R5, R1, R3      ; diff_low = X_low − Y_low, set flags
        SBC     R6, R2, R4      ; diff_high = X_high − Y_high − borrow
        LDR     R0, =DIFF
        STMIA   R0, {R5, R6}
STOP    B       STOP

        AREA    DataArea, DATA, READWRITE
X       DCD     0x00000003, 0x00000002   ; X = 0x200000003
Y       DCD     0xFFFFFFFF, 0x00000001   ; Y = 0x1FFFFFFFF
DIFF    DCD     0, 0
        END
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

| Step | Instruction | R5 (low diff) | R6 (high diff) | C after op |
|:----:|-------------|:-------------:|:--------------:|:----------:|
| 1 | `SUBS R5, R1, R3` | 0x00000004 | – | **1** (no borrow) |
| 2 | `SBC R6, R2, R4` | – | 0x00000000 | – |

### Mathematical Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
X - Y &= 0x200000003 - 0x1FFFFFFFF \\
&= (2 \cdot 2^{32} + 3) - (2^{32} - 1) \\
&= 2^{32} + 4 \\
&= 0x1\,0000\,0004_{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

Stored as: low = **0x00000004**, high = **0x00000001** ✔

---

## 3.3 Program 3 — Multiplication of Two 32-bit Numbers (64-bit Result)

### Problem
Multiply two unsigned 32-bit numbers and store the **full 64-bit result** using `UMULL`.

### Source Code

```armasm
        AREA    Multiply64, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =A
        LDR     R1, [R0]        ; R1 = A
        LDR     R0, =B
        LDR     R2, [R0]        ; R2 = B
        UMULL   R3, R4, R1, R2  ; R4:R3 = R1 × R2 (unsigned 64-bit)
        LDR     R0, =PROD
        STR     R3, [R0]        ; low word
        STR     R4, [R0, #4]    ; high word
STOP    B       STOP

        AREA    DataArea, DATA, READWRITE
A       DCD     0x00010000      ; 65 536
B       DCD     0x00010000      ; 65 536
PROD    DCD     0, 0
        END
```

### Trace

$$
A \times B = 65{,}536 \times 65{,}536 = 2^{16} \times 2^{16} = 2^{32} = 0x1\,0000\,0000
$$

Stored: low = **0x00000000**, high = **0x00000001** ✔

> [!NOTE]
> `UMALL` / `SMULL` are the **only** ways to get the full 64-bit product. The plain `MUL` instruction **discards the upper 32 bits**, which is a common exam pitfall.

---

## 3.4 Program 4 — Division by Repeated Subtraction (Logic Building)

Since ARM7TDMI has **no native division instruction**, division is emulated.

```armasm
        AREA    Division, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =NUM
        LDR     R1, [R0]        ; dividend
        LDR     R0, =DEN
        LDR     R2, [R0]        ; divisor
        MOV     R3, #0          ; quotient = 0

LOOP    CMP     R1, R2
        BLT     DONE            ; if dividend < divisor, exit
        SUB     R1, R1, R2      ; dividend -= divisor
        ADD     R3, R3, #1      ; quotient++
        B       LOOP

DONE    STR     R3, =QUOT       ; store quotient
        STR     R1, =REM        ; store remainder
STOP    B       STOP

        AREA    DataArea, DATA, READWRITE
NUM     DCD     17
DEN     DCD     5
QUOT    DCD     0
REM     DCD     0
        END
```

For input `17 ÷ 5`: **Quotient = 3, Remainder = 2**.

---

## 3.5 Program 5 — Logical AND/OR/XOR on Memory Data

```armasm
        AREA    Logical, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =OP1
        LDR     R1, [R0]        ; R1 = 0xF0F0F0F0
        LDR     R0, =OP2
        LDR     R2, [R0]        ; R2 = 0x0F0F0F0F

        AND     R3, R1, R2      ; R3 = 0x00000000
        ORR     R4, R1, R2      ; R4 = 0xFFFFFFFF
        EOR     R5, R1, R2      ; R5 = 0xFFFFFFFF (NOT of AND)

        LDR     R0, =RES
        STMIA   R0, {R3-R5}
STOP    B       STOP

        AREA    DataArea, DATA, READWRITE
OP1     DCD     0xF0F0F0F0
OP2     DCD     0x0F0F0F0F
RES     DCD     0, 0, 0
        END
```

### Truth-table verification for `AND`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0xF0F0F0F0 \ \& \ 0x0F0F0F0F &= \begin{pmatrix} 1111\ 0000 \\ \text{AND} \\ 0000\ 1111 \end{pmatrix}_{\text{per nibble}} \\
&= 0000\ 0000\ \text{(repeated 8 times)} \\
&= 0x00000000 \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 Program 6 — Bit Manipulation (Set, Clear, Toggle using BIC/ORR/EOR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most exam-frequent ALP pattern for KTU** — manipulating individual bits of a peripheral register (e.g., enabling a UART, toggling an LED pin).

**Goal:** Set bit 7, clear bit 3, toggle bit 0 of register value `0x55`.

```armasm
        AREA    BitManip, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =VAL
        LDR     R1, [R0]        ; R1 = 0x55 = 0101 0101

        ; --- Set bit 7 (OR with mask) ---
        ORR     R2, R1, #(1<<7) ; R2 = 0xD5 = 1101 0101

        ; --- Clear bit 3 (BIC with mask) ---
        BIC     R3, R2, #(1<<3) ; R3 = 0xD5 & ~0x08 = 1101 0101 & 1111 0111 = 0xD5 (already 0)

        ; --- Toggle bit 0 (EOR with mask) ---
        EOR     R4, R3, #(1<<0) ; R4 = 0xD4 = 1101 0100

        LDR     R0, =OUT
        STMIA   R0, {R2-R4}
STOP    B       STOP

        AREA    DataArea, DATA, READWRITE
VAL     DCD     0x55
OUT     DCD     0, 0, 0
        END
```

| Operation | Original | Mask | New Value |
|-----------|:--------:|:----:|:---------:|
| Set bit 7 | 0101 0101 | 1000 0000 | **1101 0101** (0xD5) |
| Clear bit 3 | 1101 0101 | 0000 1000 | **1101 0101** (0xD5) |
| Toggle bit 0 | 1101 0101 | 0000 0001 | **1101 0100** (0xD4) |

---

## 3.7 Program 7 — BCD Addition (Packed BCD)

### Concept
Each byte stores two decimal digits (00–99). After binary addition, if any nibble > 9 or carry out, add `0x06` to that nibble (Decimal Adjust).

```armasm
        AREA    BCD_Add, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =BCD1
        LDR     R1, [R0]        ; 0x45 (BCD for 45)
        LDR     R0, =BCD2
        LDR     R2, [R0]        ; 0x38 (BCD for 38)
        ADD     R3, R1, R2      ; R3 = 0x7D

        ; --- Decimal Adjust Upper Nibble ---
        CMP     R3, #0x99
        ADDGT   R3, R3, #0x60   ; if > 99, add 0x60 (low byte) + 0x60? — handled byte-wise

        ; --- Decimal Adjust Lower Nibble ---
        AND     R4, R3, #0x0F
        CMP     R4, #0x09
        ADDGT   R3, R3, #0x06   ; adjust low nibble

        ; --- Adjust High Nibble ---
        AND     R4, R3, #0xF0
        CMP     R4, #0x90
        ADDGT   R3, R3, #0x60   ; adjust high nibble

        STR     R3, =RESULT
STOP    B       STOP

BCD1    DCD     0x45
BCD2    DCD     0x38
RESULT  DCD     0
        END
```

### Verification
$$
45 + 38 = 83_{10} = 0x83 \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A more rigorous implementation must check the **C flag** from the lower-nibble addition (using `ADDS`) and conditionally add `0x60` to the upper nibble — this is the exam-favorite variation.

---

## 3.8 Program 8 — Logical Shift Left by N (Multiplication by 2ⁿ)

```armasm
        AREA    ShiftDemo, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =VAL
        LDR     R1, [R0]        ; R1 = 0x00000007
        MOV     R2, R1, LSL #4  ; R2 = 7 × 16 = 0x70 = 112
        STR     R2, =OUT
STOP    B       STOP

VAL     DCD     0x07
OUT     DCD     0
        END
$$

### Mathematical Proof

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSL \#4 of } 0x07 &= 0x07 \times 2^{4} \\
&= 7 \times 16 \\
&= 112 = 0x70 \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.9 Program 9 — Finding the Number of 1s in a Word (Population Count)

```armasm
        AREA    PopCount, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =NUM
        LDR     R1, [R0]        ; R1 = 0xF0F0A0C1
        MOV     R2, #0          ; count = 0
        MOV     R3, #32         ; loop = 32

LOOP    TST     R1, #1          ; test lowest bit
        ADDNE   R2, R2, #1      ; if set, count++
        LSR     R1, R1, #1      ; shift right
        SUBS    R3, R3, #1
        BNE     LOOP

        STR     R2, =CNT
STOP    B       STOP

NUM     DCD     0xF0F0A0C1
CNT     DCD     0
        END
```

For `0xF0F0A0C1` = `1111 0000 1111 0000 1010 0000 1100 0001`, the count of 1s = **12**.

---

## 3.10 Program 10 — Greatest of Three Numbers (using CMP + conditional moves)

```armasm
        AREA    MaxOf3, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =A
        LDMIA   R0, {R1, R2, R3}  ; R1=A, R2=B, R3=C

        CMP     R1, R2
        MOVGT   R4, R1             ; if A > B → R4 = A
        MOVLE   R4, R2             ; else R4 = B

        CMP     R4, R3
        MOVGT   R4, R4             ; if (A or B) > C, keep
        MOVLE   R4, R3             ; else R4 = C

        STR     R4, =MAX
STOP    B       STOP

A       DCD     25
B       DCD     42
C       DCD     17
MAX     DCD     0
        END
```

**Expected output:** MAX = **42**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM Data-Processing Execution Flow (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Instruction Fetch<br/>32-bit opcode from memory] --> B[Decode Stage<br/>Identify opcode, Rn, Rd, Operand2]
    B --> C{Operand2 is<br/>shifted register?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Barrel Shifter<br/>applied in Decode stage<br/>0 extra cycles]
    C -- No --> E[Pass Operand2 raw]
    D --> F[Execute Stage<br/>ALU operation]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Result written<br/>to Rd register]
    F --> H[CPSR flags<br/>updated if S suffix]
    G --> I[Next instruction]
    H --> I
```

## 4.2 CPSR Flag Update Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Data-processing executes] --> Q1{S suffix<br/>present?}
    Q1 -- No --> NoUp[Flags unchanged]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Opcode type?}
    Q2 --> CMP_TST[Comparison<br/>CMP TST TEQ]
    Q2 --> Arith[Arithmetic<br/>ADD SUB RSB etc.]
    Q2 --> Logic[Logical<br/>AND ORR EOR BIC MVN]
    Arith --> SetAll[Update N, Z, C, V]
    Logic --> SetNZCV[Update N, Z, C<br/>C = shifter carry out]
    CMP_TST --> SetFlags[Update N, Z, C, V per result]
```

## 4.3 Program Flow Diagram — Typical ALP with LDR/STR Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: __main] --> P2[Base address of array → R0]
    P2 --> P3[Loop count N → R4]
    P3 --> P4[Initialize sum R5 = 0]
    P4 --> P5[Load next element → R1<br/>LDR R1, R0, #4]
    P5 --> P6[Accumulate<br/>ADD R5, R5, R1]
    P6 --> P7[Decrement counter<br/>SUBS R4, R4, #1]
    P7 --> P8{Loop counter<br/>= 0?}
    P8 -- No --> P5
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Store final sum<br/>STR R5, =RESULT]
    P9 --> P10[STOP: B STOP]
```

## 4.4 Operand2 Barrel Shifter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Rm[Source Register Rm] --> Shift[4-bit Shift amount<br/>or 8-bit immediate]
    Shift --> Sel{Shift type<br/>select}
    Sel -->|LSL| LSL[Logical Shift Left]
    Sel -->|LSR| LSR[Logical Shift Right]
    Sel -->|ASR| ASR[Arithmetic Shift Right]
    Sel -->|ROR| ROR[Rotate Right]
    Sel -->|RRX| RRX[Rotate Right via Carry]
    LSL --> ALU[ALU Operand2 Input]
    LSR --> ALU
    ASR --> ALU
    ROR --> ALU
    RRX --> ALU
    ALU --> Result[Operation result Rd]
```

## 4.5 Carry/Borrow Propagation in 64-bit Subtraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low word X_low<br/>0x00000003] --> SUB1[SUBS R5, X_low, Y_low]
    B[Low word Y_low<br/>0xFFFFFFFF] --> SUB1
    SUB1 --> Carry{C flag<br/>after SUBS?}
    Carry -- C = 0<br/>borrow taken --> SBC1[SBC R6, X_high, Y_high<br/>− 1]
    Carry -- C = 1<br/>no borrow --> SBC1
    C[High word X_high<br/>0x00000002] --> SBC1
    D[High word Y_high<br/>0x00000001] --> SBC1
    SBC1 --> DiffLow[R5 = diff_low]
    SBC1 --> DiffHigh[R6 = diff_high]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the significance of the `S` suffix in ARM data-processing instructions with an example.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
The **`{S}` suffix** in ARM assembly controls whether the instruction updates the **CPSR (Current Program Status Register) condition flags** (N, Z, C, V).
- **Without `S`** → CPSR flags are **not modified** (preserves flags for a subsequent conditional branch).
- **With `S`** → Flags are updated based on the result of the operation.

**Example:**
```
ADDS R0, R1, R2   ; R0 = R1 + R2, and N, Z, C, V flags updated
ADD  R0, R1, R2   ; R0 = R1 + R2, flags unchanged
```
This is critical when followed by a **conditional branch** like `BEQ` (Branch if Equal), which depends on the Z flag set by the `ADDS` form. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the difference between `MUL` and `UMULL` instructions in ARM7TDMI?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature | `MUL` | `UMULL` |
|---------|:-----:|:-------:|
| Result size | 32-bit (lower half only) | 64-bit |
| Mnemonic | Multiply | Unsigned Multiply Long |
| Syntax | `MUL Rd, Rm, Rs` | `UMULL RdLo, RdHi, Rm, Rs` |
| Use case | Small products, DSP taps | Full-precision 64-bit product |

`MUL` **discards the upper 32 bits**, so for any product $\geq 2^{32}$ the result is truncated. `UMULL` stores the full 64-bit product across two registers, requiring **2–4 extra cycles** and **restricting Rd ≠ Rm** to avoid hazards. **(3 marks)**

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE Module 3 pattern, **answer ANY ONE** from the following two choices.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU Model Question Paper – Module 3]**

**(a)** Write an ARM7 ALP to **add two 64-bit numbers** stored in memory and store the 64-bit result. Explain the **role of `ADCS`** in handling the carry chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an ARM7 ALP to **clear bits 4, 5, and 7** and **set bits 0 and 2** of a 32-bit number stored at memory location `0x40000000`, without modifying any other bits. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Solution to A(a) — 64-bit Addition with Carry Chain

```armasm
        AREA    Add64, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =X
        LDMIA   R0, {R1, R2}     ; R1=X_low, R2=X_high
        LDR     R0, =Y
        LDMIA   R0, {R3, R4}     ; R3=Y_low, R4=Y_high

        ADDS    R5, R1, R3       ; sum_low = X_low + Y_low, set flags
        ADCS    R6, R2, R4       ; sum_high = X_high + Y_high + carry

        LDR     R0, =SUM
        STMIA   R0, {R5, R6}
STOP    B       STOP

        AREA    DataArea, DATA, READWRITE
X       DCD     0xFFFFFFFF, 0x00000000   ; X = 0x0_FFFFFFFF
Y       DCD     0x00000001, 0x00000000   ; Y = 0x0_00000001
SUM     DCD     0, 0
        END
```

**Trace:**
| Step | R1/R3 (low) | R2/R4 (high) | R5 (sum_low) | R6 (sum_high) | C |
|:----:|:-----------:|:------------:|:------------:|:-------------:|:-:|
| ADDS R5, R1, R3 | FFFFFFFF + 1 | – | 0x00000000 | – | **1** |
| ADCS R6, R2, R4 | – | 0 + 0 + 1 | – | 0x00000001 | 0 |

**Role of `ADCS`:** The first `ADDS` generates a **Carry flag = 1** (since FFFFFFFF + 1 overflows 32 bits). The `ADCS` (Add with Carry, update flags) **adds that carry into the upper-word addition** in the same instruction, producing the correct 64-bit result. Without `ADCS`, the upper word would be off-by-one in overflow cases. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Solution to A(b) — Selective Bit Manipulation

**Approach:** Use `BIC` (Bit Clear) and `ORR` (Logical OR) with appropriate masks.

**Required masks:**
- Clear bits 4, 5, 7 → mask = `~(1<<4 \cup 1<<5 \cup 1<<7)` = `~0b10110000` = `0xFFFFFF4F`
- Set bits 0, 2 → mask = `0b00000101` = `0x05`

```armasm
        AREA    BitManip, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =VAL
        LDR     R1, [R0]         ; R1 = original value

        LDR     R2, =0xFFFFFF4F  ; Clear mask
        BIC     R3, R1, R2       ; R3 = R1 & ~mask → bits 4,5,7 cleared

        ORR     R4, R3, #0x05    ; R4 = R3 | 0x05 → bits 0,2 set

        STR     R4, =RESULT
STOP    B       STOP

VAL     DCD     0x000000FF       ; example input
RESULT  DCD     0
        END
```

**Worked example** for input `0xFF`:
1. After `BIC`: `0xFF & 0x4F` = `0x4F` (bits 4, 5, 7 cleared).
2. After `ORR 0x05`: `0x4F | 0x05` = `0x4F` (bits 0, 2 already set).

Final value: `0x4F` = `0100 1111` ✔ **(7 marks)**

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **barrel shifter** in ARM7TDMI. Write an ALP to compute **`Y = 5X + 12`** using a **single ARM instruction** exploiting the barrel shifter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an ARM7 ALP to find the **number of 1-bits (population count)** in a 32-bit word stored in memory. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Solution to B(a) — Barrel Shifter & Constant Coefficient

**Theory (3 marks):** The **barrel shifter** is a hardware unit that performs any shift or rotate on a 32-bit value in **a single clock cycle, for free**, as part of the instruction's execute stage. It processes the **12-bit Operand2 field** of every data-processing instruction before the value enters the ALU. Supported operations: `LSL`, `LSR`, `ASR`, `ROR`, `RRX`. This eliminates the need for explicit shift instructions and enables efficient **constant-coefficient multiplication** by sums of powers of two.

**ALP (4 marks):** $Y = 5X + 12 = (X \ll 2) + X + 12$

```armasm
        AREA    FiveX12, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =X
        LDR     R1, [R0]         ; R1 = X
        ADD     R2, R1, R1, LSL #2  ; R2 = X + (X << 2) = 5X
        ADD     R3, R2, #12      ; R3 = 5X + 12
        STR     R3, =Y
STOP    B       STOP

X       DCD     7
Y       DCD     0
        END
```

**Verification:** X = 7 → 5(7) + 12 = 47 = 0x2F ✔ **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Solution to B(b) — Population Count

```armasm
        AREA    PopCount, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =NUM
        LDR     R1, [R0]         ; R1 = input word
        MOV     R2, #0           ; count = 0
        MOV     R3, #32          ; loop counter

LOOP    TST     R1, #1           ; test bit 0
        ADDNE   R2, R2, #1       ; if bit 0 set → count++
        LSR     R1, R1, #1       ; shift right by 1
        SUBS    R3, R3, #1
        BNE     LOOP             ; loop until 32 bits processed

        STR     R2, =COUNT
STOP    B       STOP

NUM     DCD     0xF0F0A0C1
COUNT   DCD     0
        END
```

For `0xF0F0A0C1` = binary `1111 0000 1111 0000 1010 0000 1100 0001`, total 1s = **12**. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose-Marks Traps**
> 1. **Forgetting the `S` suffix** before a conditional branch — the branch condition never becomes true, and the program "skips" logic silently. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. **Using `MUL` for results exceeding 32 bits** — silent truncation of the upper word. Always use `UMULL`/`SMULL` for full-precision products. *(−2 marks)*
> 3. **Confusing Carry and Borrow** in `SUBS` — the C flag inverts meaning for subtraction. Use `SBC` (not `SUB`) for the upper word of multi-precision subtraction. *(−3 marks)*
> 4. **Using `B = STOP` without `STOP B STOP`** — infinite branch loop forgotten, program may "fall through" into the data area and corrupt memory. *(−1 mark)*
> 5. **Wrong area declaration** — forgetting `AREA DataArea, DATA, READWRITE` causes linker errors. Always declare code/data areas. *(−1 mark)*
> 6. **Register conflict in `MUL`** — Rd must not equal Rm; doing so produces **undefined results** on ARM7 (architecture constraint). *(−2 marks)*

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ ARM is a **load/store RISC** architecture — only `LDR`/`STR` access memory.
- ✅ Data-processing format: `[COND] OPCODE{S} Rd, Rn, Operand2`.
- ✅ **Operand2 = register + shift** OR **8-bit immediate** — runs through **barrel shifter for free**.
- ✅ `S` suffix is **mandatory** when result must influence a subsequent conditional branch.
- ✅ **CPSR flags** (N, Z, C, V) are updated only by `S`-suffixed instructions or by `CMP`/`TST`/`TEQ`.
- ✅ `MUL` gives **32-bit** result; `UMULL`/`SMULL` give **64-bit** across two registers.
- ✅ `ADC` / `SBC` are the **carry-chain** workhorses for multi-precision arithmetic.
- ✅ `BIC Rd, Rn, mask` is the **canonical clear-bits** pattern (e.g., for clearing GPIO or interrupt flags).
- ✅ `ORR` to **set** bits, `EOR` to **toggle**, `BIC` to **clear** — without touching other bits.
- ✅ **Logical shifts** = unsigned; `ASR` preserves sign for signed division by 2ⁿ.
- ✅ **No native DIV** in ARM7TDMI — implement via **repeated subtraction** or shift-subtract algorithms.
- ✅ **C flag = "NOT borrow"** for subtraction; `C=1` means **no borrow** occurred.
- ✅ **Barrel shifter advantage**: constant multiplications like 5X = `X + (X<<2)` cost only **1 cycle**.
- ✅ **Pipeline caveat**: data-processing instructions write Rd in the **execute stage**, but the next instruction's **decode reads it early** — forward path is automatic, but for `MUL`/`LDR` use, an **interlock** of 1 cycle may be inserted.
- ✅ In `TST R1, #1`, the `#1` is the **Operand2 immediate**, expanded by the barrel shifter before ANDing — useful for **bit-testing without modifying R1**.
- ✅ `MVN` performs **bitwise NOT** in one cycle (equivalent to `EOR Rx, Rx, #0xFFFFFFFF` but faster).
- ✅ **Memory alignment**: `LDR` from a word address requires **4-byte alignment**; misaligned access on ARM7TDMI (without the `-S` variant) generates a **data abort** exception.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factorial
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factorial

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Microcontrollers (PECPT631)**, the **Factorial** program is a classical **computational algorithm** implemented using assembly language instructions of a target microcontroller (typically the **Intel 8051** family or **ARM Cortex-M** in KTU labs). 

Mathematically, the factorial of a non-negative integer $n$ is defined as:

$$n! = \prod_{k=1}^{n} k = 1 \times 2 \times 3 \times \cdots \times n$$

For a microcontroller, the factorial of $n$ is computed by repeatedly multiplying successive integers from **1 to n**, storing the result in a register/memory location. The result grows rapidly in size, so the program must handle **register overflow (OV flag in 8051)** carefully.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Factorial computation is part of the Assembly Language Programming (ALP) module. Students must demonstrate the ability to write, debug, and trace an assembly program using loops, indirect addressing, and conditional jumps.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **mechanical calculator** (the microcontroller) that can only perform one multiplication at a time. To compute $5!$:
- You press **MULTIPLY × 1 = 5**
- Then **MULTIPLY × 2 = 10**
- Then **MULTIPLY × 3 = 30**
- Then **MULTIPLY × 4 = 120**
- Then **MULTIPLY × 5 = 600**

The microcontroller does exactly this: it iterates through numbers, multiplying them into an **accumulator** until it reaches $n$. The "running total" is kept in a register (e.g., `A` or `R0`), and the "counter" (current multiplier) is kept in another register (e.g., `R1` or `R2`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Factorial growth rate:** Even for modest inputs, factorials become enormous. $8! = 40320$, which already exceeds the 8-bit range of a typical microcontroller register (max **255**). This is why KTU problems usually restrict $n \leq 5$ for 8-bit 8051, or use 16-bit registers for larger values.

## 1.3 Physical/Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Value/Range |
|---|---|
| Maximum 8-bit unsigned value | **255 (0xFF)** |
| Maximum 16-bit unsigned value | **65,535 (0xFFFF)** |
| 8-bit overflow threshold for factorial | $n = 6$ (since $6! = 720$) |
| Register overflow flag | **OV (PSW.2)** in 8051 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic Breakdown

The factorial program on a microcontroller follows this structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Initialize the result register** to **1** (since $0! = 1! = 1$). This is the multiplicative identity.
- **Step 2 — Initialize the counter register** to the input value $n$ (or start from 1 and count up).
- **Step 3 — Loop Body:** Multiply the result register by the counter.
- **Step 4 — Decrement the counter** (or increment, depending on loop direction).
- **Step 5 — Branch Condition:** If counter has not reached the terminal value, **jump back to Step 3**. Otherwise, exit loop.
- **Step 6 — Store the result** in memory (internal RAM or external data memory).
- **Step 7 — Check Overflow Flag (OV):** If result exceeds register width, handle gracefully (set an error flag, halt, or use 16-bit result storage).

## 2.2 Register Allocation Strategy (8051 Convention)

| Register | Purpose | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| `A` (Accumulator) | Holds running product | Only register capable of `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` |
| `B` | Multiplier / Holds counter temporarily | Pairs with A for multiplication |
| `R0` or `R1` | Loop counter / pointer to data memory | Used for indirect addressing `@R0` |
| `R2` or `R3` | Secondary counter or input storage | General-purpose scratchpad |
| `DPTR` | Data pointer for table lookups | 16-bit addressing |
| `PSW` (Program Status Word) | Contains OV, CY, AC, P flags | Used to detect overflow |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Factorial definition | $n! = n \times (n-1)!$ | Recursive form |
| Factorial product form | $n! = \prod_{k=1}^{n} k$ | Iterative form |
| Base case | $0! = 1! = 1$ | Initialize accumulator to **1** |
| 8051 Multiply instruction | `MUL AB` | Result: B:A (16-bit), `A` = low byte, `B` = high byte |
| OV flag after `MUL AB` | `OV = 1` if $B \neq 0$ | Indicates 8-bit overflow |
| Loop decrement | `DJNZ Rn, label` | Decrement and Jump if Not Zero |
| Indirect addressing | `MOV A, @R0` | Accesses RAM at address in `R0` |
| 16-bit storage | Use two consecutive memory bytes | High byte first (big-endian) or low byte first |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Factorial programs are foundational test cases for validating any processor's instruction set. They exercise the **arithmetic logic unit (ALU)**, **control unit**, **loop handling**, and **flag management** — all in one compact program. Compiler benchmarks and processor validation suites routinely use factorial to measure execution speed.

## 2.4 Why "How" and "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why initialize to 1, not 0?** Because multiplying any number by 0 yields 0, which would make $n! = 0$ for all $n$. The identity element for multiplication is **1**, not 0.
- **Why use `MUL AB` and not repeated addition?** Because `MUL AB` executes in a single machine cycle (12 clock cycles in original 8051) and uses dedicated hardware — far faster and more accurate than software loops.
- **Why check OV after multiplication?** In 8-bit 8051, multiplication produces a 16-bit result in `B:A`. If the result exceeds 255, the OV flag is set, signaling that the high byte (B) holds significant data and the programmer must handle it.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of Factorial Growth

Starting from the recursive definition:

$$n! = n \times (n-1)!$$

We expand iteratively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n! &= n \times (n-1)! \\
&= n \times (n-1) \times (n-2)! \\
&= n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \cdots \times 1 \times 0! \\
&= \prod_{k=1}^{n} k \quad \text{(closed form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Trace of 8051 Assembly Program for $n = 5$

**Program: Compute 5! and store result in internal RAM at address 30H.**

```
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     A, #01H         ; A = 1 (initial result)
        MOV     R2, #05H        ; R2 = 5 (counter, will decrement)
LOOP:   MOV     B, R2           ; B = current counter value
        MUL     AB              ; A = A * B (result in B:A)
        JNC     NO_OVERFLOW     ; If no carry (8-bit OK), continue
        SETB    PSW.2           ; Else set OV flag
NO_OVERFLOW:
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP        ; Decrement R2, if not zero, loop
        MOV     30H, A          ; Store low byte at 30H
        MOV     31H, B          ; Store high byte at 31H
        END
```

### Detailed Execution Trace

| Iteration | R2 (counter) | A (running) | B | A × R2 (decimal) | New A (hex) | New B | OV? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Start | 05H | 01H | xx | — | 01H | xx | 0 |
| 1 | 04H | 01H | 05H | $1 \times 5 = 5$ | 05H | 00H | 0 |
| 2 | 03H | 05H | 04H | $5 \times 4 = 20$ | 14H | 00H | 0 |
| 3 | 02H | 14H | 03H | $20 \times 3 = 60$ | 3CH | 00H | 0 |
| 4 | 01H | 3CH | 02H | $60 \times 2 = 120$ | 78H | 00H | 0 |
| 5 | 00H | 78H | 01H | $120 \times 1 = 120$ | 78H | 00H | 0 |
| Exit | — | 78H | 00H | — | 78H | 00H | — |

### Final Result Verification

$$5! = 120 = 78_{16}$$

The result stored at internal RAM address `30H` is `78H` (low byte), and `31H` is `00H` (high byte, since 120 fits in 8 bits). ✓

## 3.3 Tracing $n = 8$ (Demonstrates Overflow Handling)

| Iteration | R2 | A (before) | A (after) | B (after) | Running $n!$ | 8-bit fit? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 08H | 01H | 08H | 00H | 8 | ✓ |
| 2 | 07H | 08H | 38H | 00H | 56 | ✓ |
| 3 | 06H | 38H | D0H | 00H | 336 → $0 \times 150$ | ✗ (low=50H, high=01H) |
| 4 | 05H | 50H | F4H | 01H | 1680 | ✗ |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| End | 00H | 00H | 00H | 9EH | 40320 = $0 \times 9D80$ | ✗ |

$$
\begin{aligned}
8! &= 40320 \\
&= 0 \times 9D80 \\
&= \underbrace{0 \times 9D}_{\text{high byte (B)}} \;\underbrace{0 \times 80}_{\text{low byte (A)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This trace illustrates **why 16-bit result handling is mandatory** for $n \geq 6$ in 8-bit microcontrollers.

## 3.4 Algorithm Conversion Logic (Step-by-Step Text)

- The instruction `MOV A, #01H` loads the **multiplicative identity** into the accumulator, ensuring that the first multiplication yields the counter itself.
- `MOV R2, #05H` sets up the **loop counter**; `DJNZ` is a hardware-optimized instruction that combines decrement and conditional branch in **2 machine cycles**.
- `MOV B, R2` is necessary because **`MUL AB` requires the multiplier in B**. We cannot multiply A directly by R2.
- The `MUL AB` instruction **automatically sets the OV flag** when $B \neq 0$ after multiplication, providing hardware-level overflow detection without any extra code.
- After loop exit, both `A` (low byte) and `B` (high byte) are stored in **consecutive memory locations** (30H and 31H) to preserve the complete 16-bit result.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Program Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["START: Initialize Program"] --> node2["Load A with 01H"]
    node2 --> node3["Load R2 with input N"]
    node3 --> node4{"Check N equals 0 or 1"}
    node4 -->|YES| node5["Store 01H as Result"]
    node4 -->|NO| node6["Copy R2 into B register"]
    node6 --> node7["Execute MUL AB"]
    node7 --> node8{"Check OV flag"}
    node8 -->|OV=1| node9["Set Overflow Error Flag"]
    node8 -->|OV=0| nodeA["Decrement R2 via DJNZ"]
    node9 --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeB{"R2 equals 0"}
    nodeB -->|NO| node6
    nodeB -->|YES| nodeC["Store A at RAM 30H"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Store B at RAM 31H"]
    node5 --> nodeE["END Program"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
```

## 4.2 Register Interaction Diagram (8051 Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Accumulator A"] -->|"holds running product"| nodeMUL["MUL AB Engine"]
    nodeB["Register B"] -->|"holds current multiplier"| nodeMUL
    nodeMUL -->|"low byte result"| nodeA
    nodeMUL -->|"high byte result"| nodeB
    nodeMUL -->|"overflow detection"| nodeOV["PSW OV Flag"]
    nodeR["Register R2"] -->|"loop counter"| nodeDJNZ["DJNZ Engine"]
    nodeDJNZ -->|"decrement and branch"| nodeR
    nodeA -->|"MOV 30H, A"| nodeRAM1["Internal RAM 30H"]
    nodeB -->|"MOV 31H, B"| nodeRAM2["Internal RAM 31H"]
```

## 4.3 Memory Map After Execution (Example: $n = 5$)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InternalRAM["8051 Internal RAM (Lower 128 bytes)"]
        addr30["Address 30H: 78H (low byte of 5!)"]
        addr31["Address 31H: 00H (high byte of 5!)"]
        addr32["Address 32H: 00H (reserved)"]
        addr33["Address 33H: 05H (original input N)"]
    end
    addr30 --- addr31 --- addr32 --- addr33
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define factorial. Explain why the accumulator is initialized to 1 (and not 0) when computing factorial in assembly language.**

**Model Answer:**

The factorial of a non-negative integer $n$, denoted $n!$, is the product of all positive integers from 1 to $n$:

$$n! = 1 \times 2 \times 3 \times \cdots \times n$$

The accumulator is initialized to **1** because 1 is the **multiplicative identity element**. If initialized to 0, every multiplication would yield 0 (since $n \times 0 = 0$), making the result always zero regardless of input. The base case $0! = 1! = 1$ also confirms that the smallest valid factorial result is **1**, not 0. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of the OV flag after the `MUL AB` instruction in an 8051 factorial program?**

**Model Answer:**

The `OV (overflow) flag` in the Program Status Word (PSW) is **automatically set by hardware** when the result of `MUL AB` exceeds 8 bits, i.e., when register `B ≠ 0` after the multiplication. In factorial programs, since the result grows rapidly (e.g., $6! = 720 > 255$), the OV flag signals that the result is now **16-bit** and stored across both `B` (high byte) and `A` (low byte). The programmer must then handle both registers when storing the final result. *(3 marks)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to compute the factorial of a number stored in internal RAM location `20H`. Store the 16-bit result in locations `30H` (low byte) and `31H` (high byte). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace the program execution step-by-step for input $n = 4$, showing the contents of registers A, B, and R2 at each iteration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Assembly Program:**

```
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     A, #01H         ; A = 1 (result accumulator)
        MOV     R2, 20H         ; R2 = input number N
        CJNE    R2, #00H, SKIP  ; If N ≠ 0, skip
        SJMP    STORE           ; Else N = 0, result = 1
SKIP:   CJNE    R2, #01H, LOOP  ; If N ≠ 1, go to loop
        SJMP    STORE           ; Else N = 1, result = 1
LOOP:   MOV     B, R2           ; B = counter
        MUL     AB              ; A = A * B (B:A = 16-bit result)
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP        ; Decrement R2, loop if not zero
STORE:  MOV     30H, A          ; Store low byte
        MOV     31H, B          ; Store high byte
        END
```

#### Step-by-Step Trace for $n = 4$:

| Step | Instruction | A | B | R2 | Comments |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV A, #01H` | 01H | xx | xx | Initialize result |
| 2 | `MOV R2, 20H` | 01H | xx | 04H | Load input N |
| 3 | `MOV B, R2` | 01H | 04H | 04H | Prepare multiplier |
| 4 | `MUL AB` | 04H | 00H | 04H | $1 \times 4 = 4$ |
| 5 | `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | 04H | 00H | 03H | R2 ≠ 0, continue |
| 6 | `MOV B, R2` | 04H | 03H | 03H | — |
| 7 | `MUL AB` | 0CH | 00H | 03H | $4 \times 3 = 12$ |
| 8 | `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | 0CH | 00H | 02H | Continue |
| 9 | `MOV B, R2` | 0CH | 02H | 02H | — |
| 10 | `MUL AB` | 18H | 00H | 02H | $12 \times 2 = 24$ |
| 11 | `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | 18H | 00H | 01H | Continue |
| 12 | `MOV B, R2` | 18H | 01H | 01H | — |
| 13 | `MUL AB` | 18H | 00H | 01H | $24 \times 1 = 24$ |
| 14 | `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | 18H | 00H | 00H | R2 = 0, exit |
| 15 | `MOV 30H, A` | 18H | 00H | 00H | RAM[30H] = 18H |
| 16 | `MOV 31H, B` | 18H | 00H | 00H | RAM[31H] = 00H |

**Final Result:** $4! = 24 = 0 \times 0018$. RAM[30H] = `18H`, RAM[31H] = `00H`. ✓

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the limitations of computing factorial using an 8-bit 8051 microcontroller. What is the maximum value of $n$ for which the factorial fits in a single 8-bit register? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an 8051 assembly program that computes $n!$ for $n \leq 7$ using **16-bit result storage** in two consecutive RAM locations. Include proper overflow handling. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Limitations of 8-bit Factorial Computation:**

- The 8051's `MUL AB` instruction produces a 16-bit result stored in `B:A`. However, the programmer must manually manage both registers.
- The largest 8-bit unsigned value is **255 (0xFF)**.
- Computing factorials sequentially:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1! &= 1 \\
2! &= 2 \\
3! &= 6 \\
4! &= 24 \\
5! &= 120 \\
6! &= 720 \quad (> 255, \text{ overflow}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

- Therefore, the **maximum value of $n$** that fits in a single 8-bit register without overflow is **$n = 5$**.
- Beyond $n = 5$, the programmer must use 16-bit arithmetic, multiple-precision libraries, or external memory to store the result.

**(b) 16-bit Factorial Program with Overflow Handling:**

```
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #2000H    ; Point to input location
        MOVX    A, @DPTR        ; Load N from external memory
        MOV     R2, A           ; R2 = N
        MOV     A, #01H         ; A = low byte of result = 1
        MOV     B, #00H         ; B = high byte of result = 0
LOOP:   MOV     30H, A          ; Save current low byte
        MOV     31H, B          ; Save current high byte
        MOV     A, R2           ; A = counter
        MOV     B, #00H         ; B = 0 (counter is 8-bit)
        ; Now need: result = result * counter
        ; Use repeated addition or external sub-routine
        ; Simplified: assume result in 31H:30H, multiply by R2
        CALL    MUL16           ; 16-bit multiply sub-routine
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP
        MOV     40H, A          ; Store final low byte
        MOV     41H, B          ; Store final high byte
        END
```

*(Note: Full 16-bit multiply requires a sub-routine. The above demonstrates the structure.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to initialize the accumulator to **1** (students often use 0). This causes the entire result to be 0. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using `MOV A, R2` directly before `MUL AB` without first moving R2 into B. The 8051's `MUL AB` **only** multiplies A by B. **Loss: 3 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Ignoring the OV flag and storing only register A as the result. For $n \geq 6$, this gives a **truncated 8-bit answer**. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Using `DEC R2` followed by a separate `JNZ` instead of the efficient `DJNZ R2, LOOP` (which combines both). Examiners may not deduct marks but it shows poor understanding.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not handling the base cases $n = 0$ and $n = 1$ separately. If the loop is `DJNZ R2, LOOP` and R2 starts at 0, the loop body never executes, which is actually correct for $0!$, but students often write incorrect conditional logic.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Factorial definition:** $n! = \prod_{k=1}^{n} k$, with base case $0! = 1! = 1$.
- ✅ **Always initialize the accumulator to 1**, never 0 — this is the multiplicative identity.
- ✅ **8051 multiplication:** Use `MUL AB`. Result is **16-bit** in `B:A`. A = low byte, B = high byte.
- ✅ **OV flag** is set automatically when $B \neq 0$ after `MUL AB`, indicating 8-bit overflow.
- ✅ **Maximum $n$ for 8-bit factorial** without overflow: **$n = 5$** (since $5! = 120 < 255$, but $6! = 720 > 255$).
- ✅ **Loop instruction:** Prefer `DJNZ Rn, label` over manual `DEC` + `JNZ` — it is faster and idiomatic.
- ✅ **16-bit result storage:** Use two consecutive RAM locations (e.g., 30H and 31H), with high byte at the higher address (big-endian convention).
- ✅ **Register usage:** A = running product, B = multiplier, R0–R7 = loop counters or pointers.
- ✅ **Indirect addressing:** `MOVX A, @DPTR` for external memory, `MOV A, @R0` for internal RAM.
- ✅ **Trace carefully** in exams — examiners award marks for **register-by-register step tables**.
- ✅ **Engineering relevance:** Factorial programs validate ALU design, loop control, and flag handling — essential skills for any embedded systems engineer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: String Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** String Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PIC18 Advanced Microcontrollers**, a **String** is defined as a contiguous sequence of **8-bit ASCII characters** stored in sequential memory locations, terminated by a **NULL character (0x00)**. A **String Operation** refers to a set of hardware-assisted and software-implemented procedures used to manipulate, access, transfer, and process these character sequences between the **General Purpose Registers (GPR)**, **Data Memory (RAM)**, and the **Program Memory (Flash ROM)** of the microcontroller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** String operations in PIC18 are intrinsically linked with **Table Read (TBLRD)** and **Table Write (TBLWT)** instructions because string constants are typically stored in Program Memory to conserve scarce RAM resources (typically only **3,968 bytes** of GPR in PIC18F4550).

The PIC18 architecture provides dedicated hardware features for string manipulation:
- **TBLRD\*** and **TBLRD\*+** (Table Read with Post-Increment)
- **TBLWT\*** and **TBLWT\*+** (Table Write with Post-Increment)
- **TBLPTR** (21-bit Table Pointer) split into **TBLPTRL**, **TBLPTRH**, **TBLPTRU**
- **TABLAT** (8-bit Table Latch register) as the data gateway
- **FSR0, FSR1, FSR2** (File Select Registers) as RAM pointers

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **library building** (Program Memory) filled with reference books (strings), and a small **backpack** (RAM) where you can only carry a few pages at a time:

- **Program Memory (ROM)** = The library's permanent bookshelf — fast to read, but you cannot easily scribble notes directly.
- **Data Memory (RAM)** = Your backpack — fast to write/edit, but very limited space.
- **Table Read (TBLRD)** = Photocopying a page from the library book into your hands (TABLAT register).
- **Table Write (TBLWT)** = Saving notes back into the library archive (latch buffer).
- **String** = A sentence in the book — you must read it character-by-character until you hit the "period" (NULL terminator).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Takeaway:** A string operation is essentially the controlled "shuttling" of characters one byte at a time between permanent storage (ROM) and working memory (RAM) using specialized pointer registers, with the NULL byte `0x00` acting as the universal "end-of-message" marker.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| NULL Terminator | **0x00** | Marks end of string |
| Tablat Register | **8-bit** | Gateway for program memory data |
| TBLPTR Width | **21-bit** | Can address up to 2 MB program memory |
| ASCII Range (Printable) | **0x20 to 0x7E** | Valid display characters |
| Carriage Return | **0x0D** | Used in terminal commands |
| Line Feed | **0x0A** | New line character |
| PIC18F4550 Program Memory | **32 KB (0x8000 bytes)** | Typical string storage limit |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental String Operations in PIC18

The PIC18 instruction set does not contain dedicated "string instructions" like x86 processors (MOVSB, CMPSB). Instead, strings are manipulated using a combination of:

1. **Pointer-based addressing** (using FSRx registers for RAM)
2. **Table operations** (using TBLPTR/TABLAT for ROM)
3. **Conditional branching** (checking for NULL terminator)

#### 2.1.1 Categories of String Operations

**A. String Reading (ROM → RAM)**
- Used to copy constants like LCD messages, fixed text strings from Flash to RAM.

**B. String Writing (RAM → ROM)**
- Used during runtime for storing dynamic data into program memory (requires special sequence).

**C. String Copy (RAM → RAM)**
- Pure data manipulation between RAM buffers using indirect addressing.

**D. String Compare**
- Character-by-character equality check; sets flags based on result.

**E. String Length Calculation**
- Iterates through characters until NULL is detected.

### 2.2 The PIC18 String Access Architecture

The fundamental challenge is the **Harvard Architecture** of PIC18, which physically separates Program Memory and Data Memory into distinct address spaces with separate buses. A bridge mechanism is required:

```
[Program Memory] ←→ [TBLPTR 21-bit] ←→ [TABLAT 8-bit] ←→ [WREG] ←→ [Data Memory]
```

### 2.3 Key String-Related Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation | Bytes | Cycles | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| `TBLRD*` | Table Read | 2 | 2 | Read ROM[TBLPTR] → TABLAT |
| `TBLRD*+` | Table Read + Post-Inc | 2 | 2 | Read then TBLPTR++ |
| `TBLRD*-` | Table Read + Post-Dec | 2 | 2 | Read then TBLPTR-- |
| `TBLRD+*` | Table Read + Pre-Inc | 2 | 2 | TBLPTR++ then Read |
| `TBLWT*` | Table Write | 2 | 2 | Write TABLAT → Holding Latch |
| `TBLWT*+` | Table Write + Post-Inc | 2 | 2 | Write then TBLPTR++ |
| `MOVFF` | Move F to F | 4 | 2 | Useful for FSR-based string ops |
| `MOVLW` | Move Literal to W | 2 | 2 | Load ASCII value |
| `TSTFSZ` | Test F, Skip if Zero | 2 | 1-3 | NULL detection |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Effective Program Memory Address | $A_{eff} = (TBLPTRU \ll 16) \lor (TBLPTRH \ll 8) \lor TBLPTRL$ | 21-bit linear address |
| Null Terminated String Length | $L = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} [s_i \neq 0]$ | Counts non-NULL bytes |
| String Compare Result | $R = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (s_i \oplus t_i)$ | Zero indicates equality |
| RAM Pointer Logic | $FSR_{eff} = FSRxH \ll 8 + FSRxL$ | 12-bit address space |
| Tablat Transfer Time | $t_{tab} = 2 \times T_{cy}$ | Two instruction cycles |
| ASCII to Hex Digit | $D = c - 0\text{x}30$ (for '0'-'9'), $D = c - 0\text{x}37$ (for 'A'-'F') | Conversion utility |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** String operations form the backbone of **Human-Machine Interfaces (HMI)** in embedded systems — LCD displays, UART serial communication, command parsing for IoT devices, and EEPROM data logging all rely on efficient string manipulation. A poorly implemented string routine can cause **buffer overflow**, **stack corruption**, or **infinite loops** in safety-critical applications like medical devices and automotive ECUs.

### 2.5 Step-by-Step Operational Logic for ROM-to-RAM String Copy

1. **Initialize** TBLPTR to point to the start of the source string in ROM.
2. **Initialize** an FSR register (e.g., FSR0L/FSR0H) to point to the destination buffer in RAM.
3. **Execute** `TBLRD*+` to read one byte from ROM into TABLAT and increment TBLPTR.
4. **Move** TABLAT to the RAM location pointed by FSR0 using `MOVFF TABLAT, POSTINC0`.
5. **Test** for NULL character (0x00) using `TSTFSZ` or compare logic.
6. **Branch** back to step 3 if not NULL; otherwise terminate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: String Length Calculation Algorithm

Let $S$ be a string stored in RAM starting at address pointed by FSR0. We want to compute the length $L$ of string $S$ before the NULL terminator.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$L = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } S[0] = 0 \text{x}00 \\ 1 + L(S[1 \ldots n]) & \text{if } S[0] \neq 0 \text{x}00 \end{cases}$$

**Step-by-step Assembly Implementation:**

```assembly
StringLength:
    LFSR    FSR0, STRING_ADDR    ; Load FSR0 with start address
    CLRF    COUNT, 0             ; Clear length counter (L = 0)
LOOP:
    MOVF    POSTINC0, 0, 0       ; W = *FSR0, FSR0++
    BZ      DONE                  ; If W == 0 (NULL), branch to DONE
    INCF    COUNT, 0, 0          ; L = L + 1
    BRA     LOOP                  ; Repeat
DONE:
    RETURN                       ; Result in COUNT register
```

**Analytical Verification:**

For string "HELLO\0" stored at addresses 0x100-0x105:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1:} & \quad S[0] = 'H' = 0\text{x}48 \neq 0, \quad L = 0 + 1 = 1 \\
\text{Iteration 2:} & \quad S[1] = 'E' = 0\text{x}45 \neq 0, \quad L = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
\text{Iteration 3:} & \quad S[2] = 'L' = 0\text{x}4C \neq 0, \quad L = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
\text{Iteration 4:} & \quad S[3] = 'L' = 0\text{x}4C \neq 0, \quad L = 3 + 1 = 4 \\
\text{Iteration 5:} & \quad S[4] = 'O' = 0\text{x}4F \neq 0, \quad L = 4 + 1 = 5 \\
\text{Iteration 6:} & \quad S[5] = 0\text{x}00 = NULL, \quad \text{BRANCH to DONE} \\
\therefore L & = 5 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: ROM-to-RAM String Copy with TBLPTR

**Problem Statement:** Copy the string `"KTU2024"` stored at Program Memory address 0x1000 to RAM buffer starting at 0x500.

**Setup Conditions:**
- Source string in ROM: stored as `DB 'K','T','U','2','0','2','4', 0` at address 0x1000.
- Destination in RAM: Buffer starting at 0x500.
- Use FSR0 as destination pointer.

**TBLPTR Initialization (21-bit address 0x01000):**

$$\begin{aligned}
TBLPTRU & = 0\text{x}00 \quad \text{(bits 20-16, MSB of 0x01000)} \\
TBLPTRH & = 0\text{x}10 \quad \text{(bits 15-8 of 0x01000)} \\
TBLPTRL & = 0\text{x}00 \quad \text{(bits 7-0 of 0x01000)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Assembly Code with Step-by-Step Logic:**

```assembly
ROMtoRAMCopy:
    MOVLW   UPPER(STRING_ROM)    ; Load upper byte
    MOVWF   TBLPTRU, 1           ; TBLPTRU = 0x00
    MOVLW   HIGH(STRING_ROM)     ; Load high byte
    MOVWF   TBLPTRH, 1           ; TBLPTRH = 0x10
    MOVLW   LOW(STRING_ROM)      ; Load low byte
    MOVWF   TBLPTRL, 1           ; TBLPTRL = 0x00
    
    LFSR    FSR0, 0x500          ; FSR0 = 0x0500 (RAM buffer)
    
LOOP_COPY:
    TBLRD*+                       ; TABLAT = ROM[TBLPTR], TBLPTR++
    MOVFF   TABLAT, POSTINC0     ; *FSR0 = TABLAT, FSR0++
    MOVLW   0x00                 ; W = 0x00
    CPFSEQ  TABLAT                ; Compare TABLAT with WREG, skip if equal
    BRA     LOOP_COPY             ; Not NULL, continue
    RETURN                        ; NULL found, exit
```

**Cycle-by-Cycle Execution Trace:**

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Cycle} & \textbf{TBLPTR (21-bit)} & \textbf{TABLAT (8-bit)} & \textbf{FSR0 (12-bit)} & \textbf{Action} & \textbf{NULL?} \\
\hline
\text{Init} & \text{0x01000} & \text{--} & \text{0x500} & \text{Setup} & \text{--} \\
1 & \text{0x01001} & \text{'K'=0x4B} & \text{0x501} & \text{Copy 'K'} & \text{No} \\
2 & \text{0x01002} & \text{'T'=0x54} & \text{0x502} & \text{Copy 'T'} & \text{No} \\
3 & \text{0x01003} & \text{'U'=0x55} & \text{0x503} & \text{Copy 'U'} & \text{No} \\
4 & \text{0x01004} & \text{'2'=0x32} & \text{0x504} & \text{Copy '2'} & \text{No} \\
5 & \text{0x01005} & \text{'0'=0x30} & \text{0x505} & \text{Copy '0'} & \text{No} \\
6 & \text{0x01006} & \text{'2'=0x32} & \text{0x506} & \text{Copy '2'} & \text{No} \\
7 & \text{0x01007} & \text{'4'=0x34} & \text{0x507} & \text{Copy '4'} & \text{No} \\
8 & \text{0x01008} & \text{0x00} & \text{0x508} & \text{Copy NULL} & \text{Yes} \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

**Final Result:** RAM buffer at 0x500 contains `K, T, U, 2, 0, 2, 4, 0x00` (length = 7 characters + terminator = 8 bytes).

### 3.3 C Language Implementation (XC8 Compiler)

In **MPLAB XC8**, string operations are abstracted using standard C library functions, but with `rom` qualifiers for program memory:

```c
#include <xc.h>
#include <string.h>

const rom char myString[] = "KTU2024";  // Stored in Program Memory
unsigned char ramBuffer[20];            // Stored in RAM

void copyString(void) {
    strcpypgm2ram(ramBuffer, myString); // ROM -> RAM copy
}
```

The compiler translates `strcpypgm2ram` into the exact TBLRD-based assembly shown in Section 3.2.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of String Access Methods

| Method | Speed | Memory Type | Code Size | Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| `TBLRD` + RAM Buffer | Medium | ROM + RAM | Large | Large constants, LCD messages |
| Direct ROM Access | Slow (each byte = TBLRD) | ROM Only | Small | Single-character checks |
| RAM-Only Strings | Fast | RAM | Small | User input, dynamic data |
| `const rom char[]` (XC8) | Auto-optimized | ROM | Auto | Standard C development |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: PIC18 String Read Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PM["Program Memory ROM"]
        S1["String Byte 0: 'K'"]
        S2["String Byte 1: 'T'"]
        S3["String Byte 2: 'U'"]
        S4["NULL: 0x00"]
    end
    
    TBLPTR["TBLPTR 21-bit Pointer"]
    TABLAT["TABLAT 8-bit Latch"]
    WREG["WREG Working Register"]
    FSR0["FSR0 RAM Pointer"]
    DM["Data Memory RAM Buffer"]
    
    S1 --> TBLPTR
    TBLPTR --> TABLAT
    TABLAT --> WREG
    WREG --> FSR0
    FSR0 --> DM
    DM -.-> S2

    style TABLAT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000

```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Generic String Operation Algorithm

```mermaid

    A(["Start: Initialize Pointers"]) --> B["Set TBLPTR = Source ROM Address"]
    B --> C["Set FSR0 = Destination RAM Address"]
    C --> D["Execute TBLRD Post-Increment"]

    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G(["End: String Copy Complete"])

```
