## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Explain how strings are stored and accessed in the Program Memory of a PIC18 microcontroller. Mention the role of the TABLAT register.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In PIC18 microcontrollers, strings are stored in Program Memory as a sequence of ASCII characters followed by a NULL terminator (0x00). They are accessed using a 21-bit Table Pointer (`TBLPTR`), which is split into three 8-bit registers: `TBLPTRU` (upper), `TBLPTRH` (high), and `TBLPTRL` (low). The `TABLAT` register acts as an **8-bit data latch** that temporarily holds the byte read from program memory. To read a string, the `TBLRD*+` instruction is executed, which transfers the byte from the address pointed by `TBLPTR` into `TABLAT`, then auto-increments the pointer. The byte in `TABLAT` is then moved to RAM using `MOVFF` instruction for further processing. This mechanism bridges the Harvard architecture's separate program and data memories, enabling efficient retrieval of constant strings like LCD messages and serial commands without consuming scarce RAM resources. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the significance of the NULL terminator (0x00) in string operations? What happens if it is missing?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The NULL terminator (0x00) is a sentinel character that marks the logical end of a string in C and embedded systems. In PIC18 string operations, NULL serves as the loop termination condition when copying or comparing strings — algorithms iterate byte-by-byte until 0x00 is detected, then stop. If the NULL terminator is **missing or corrupted**, the string routine will continue reading past the actual string boundary, leading to **(i)** buffer overflow into adjacent memory variables, **(ii)** reading of unintended data or code, **(iii)** infinite loops that hang the microcontroller, and **(iv)** display of garbage characters on LCD or UART output. For safety-critical embedded systems, this can cause system crashes or unpredictable behavior. Hence, every string constant stored in PIC18 ROM must be explicitly terminated with a 0x00 byte. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks - Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Option 1*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** With neat diagrams and assembly code, explain how a string is copied from Program Memory to Data Memory in PIC18. What registers are involved?

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Draw the block diagram showing string transfer from ROM to RAM and explain the function of TBLPTR and TABLAT registers.

**Model Solution:**

The PIC18 microcontroller uses a Harvard architecture, requiring a special mechanism to transfer data between Program Memory (ROM) and Data Memory (RAM). The block diagram shows:

```
[ROM Address 0x1000: 'K' 'T' 'U' 0x00]
            ↓
[TBLPTR 21-bit pointer points here]
            ↓ (TBLRD*+ instruction)
[TABLAT 8-bit latch holds current byte]
            ↓ (MOVFF TABLAT, POSTINC0)
[RAM Buffer at FSR0 address 0x500]
```

**Register Functions:**
- **TBLPTR (21-bit):** Composed of TBLPTRU (bits 20-16), TBLPTRH (bits 15-8), and TBLPTRL (bits 7-0). It holds the address of the byte in program memory to be accessed.
- **TABLAT (8-bit):** Acts as a gateway register. Data from ROM is first loaded into TABLAT, then transferred to WREG, then to RAM via FSR0.
- **FSR0 (16-bit):** Indirect pointer that addresses the destination RAM location, auto-incremented after each write.

The TABLAT register is essential because the PIC18 cannot directly read program memory into RAM in a single instruction — it serves as the bridge across the Harvard architecture gap. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Write and explain the PIC18 assembly code to copy the string "HELLO" from ROM address 0x2000 to RAM buffer at 0x600.

**Model Solution:**

```assembly
; Program Memory address 0x2000 contains: 'H','E','L','L','O',0x00

STRING_ROM    EQU    0x2000
DEST_RAM      EQU    0x600

StringCopy:
    ; Step 1: Initialize TBLPTR to source ROM address (21-bit)
    MOVLW   UPPER(STRING_ROM)    ; W = 0x00 (TBLPTRU part)
    MOVWF   TBLPTRU, 1
    MOVLW   HIGH(STRING_ROM)     ; W = 0x20 (TBLPTRH part)
    MOVWF   TBLPTRH, 1
    MOVLW   LOW(STRING_ROM)      ; W = 0x00 (TBLPTRL part)
    MOVWF   TBLPTRL, 1
    
    ; Step 2: Initialize FSR0 to destination RAM address
    LFSR    FSR0, DEST_RAM       ; FSR0 = 0x600
    
LOOP:
    ; Step 3: Read byte from ROM and increment TBLPTR
    TBLRD*+                       ; TABLAT = ROM[TBLPTR], TBLPTR++
    
    ; Step 4: Copy TABLAT to RAM at FSR0, then increment FSR0
    MOVFF   TABLAT, POSTINC0     ; *FSR0 = TABLAT, FSR0++
    
    ; Step 5: Check for NULL terminator
    MOVLW   0x00
    CPFSEQ  TABLAT                ; Skip if TABLAT == 0
    BRA     LOOP                  ; Not NULL, continue loop
    
    RETURN                        ; NULL detected, exit
```

**Explanation:**
- **Lines 7-12:** Three-byte address loaded into TBLPTR because PIC18 has 21-bit program memory addressing.
- **Line 15:** `LFSR` loads FSR0 with 0x600 in a single 2-word instruction.
- **Line 19:** `TBLRD*+` performs two operations atomically: reads byte to TABLAT and increments TBLPTR for next iteration.
- **Line 22:** `POSTINC0` writes TABLAT to RAM at FSR0 and increments FSR0 automatically.
- **Lines 25-27:** The `CPFSEQ` instruction compares TABLAT with WREG (0x00). If equal (NULL found), execution skips the next instruction (BRA), effectively exiting the loop.
- **Execution Trace:** The string "HELLO" (5 bytes + NULL) is transferred from ROM 0x2000-0x2005 to RAM 0x600-0x605 in **5 iterations × 4 cycles = 20 instruction cycles** (approx 5 µs at 16 MHz clock). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Option 2*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Explain the PIC18 instruction set features for string manipulation. Compare ROM-based and RAM-based string storage with examples.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the various TBLRD instruction variants and explain how TBLPTRU, TBLPTRH, TBLPTRL registers work together.

**Model Solution:**

The PIC18 instruction set provides **four variants** of Table Read operations for accessing program memory bytes:

| Instruction | Operation | Pointer Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `TBLRD*` | Read ROM[TBLPTR] → TABLAT | No change to TBLPTR |
| `TBLRD*+` | Read ROM[TBLPTR] → TABLAT, then TBLPTR++ | Post-increment (best for sequential reads) |
| `TBLRD*-` | Read ROM[TBLPTR] → TABLAT, then TBLPTR-- | Post-decrement (reverse traversal) |
| `TBLRD+*` | TBLPTR++ first, then Read ROM[TBLPTR] → TABLAT | Pre-increment |

**TBLPTR Register Operation:**
The PIC18 program memory is organized as 21-bit linear address space, capable of addressing up to **2 MB** of flash memory. Since no single 8-bit register can hold this address, the pointer is split into three SFRs:

$$\text{TBLPTR}_{21-bit} = [\text{TBLPTRU}: \text{TBLPTRH}: \text{TBLPTRL}]$$

**Initialization Example for Address 0x012ABC:**
- `TBLPTRU = 0x01` (bits 20-16, contains upper address bits)
- `TBLPTRH = 0x2A` (bits 15-8, contains middle address bits)
- `TBLPTRL = 0xBC` (bits 7-0, contains lower address bits)

**Why this matters:** Each TBLRD instruction fetches one byte in **2 instruction cycles** (4 clock cycles). For accessing long strings, this is slower than RAM access (1 cycle), but is the **only** way to read constant data stored in flash memory without manually loading it into RAM at compile time. The post-increment variant `TBLRD*+` is most commonly used because string characters are stored sequentially in ROM. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare ROM-based vs RAM-based string storage in PIC18 with C code examples. Discuss the role of the `const rom` qualifier.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | ROM-Based String | RAM-Based String |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Declaration | `const rom char msg[] = "HELLO";` | `char buffer[10] = "HELLO";` |
| Memory Location | Program Memory (Flash) | Data Memory (RAM) |
| Mutability | Read-only (cannot modify at runtime) | Read/Write (modifiable at runtime) |
| Access Method | `TBLRD*+` instructions | Direct FSR addressing |
| Access Speed | Slower (2 cycles per byte) | Faster (1 cycle per byte) |
| Capacity | Large (32 KB+ available) | Limited (~2 KB total GPR) |
| Persistence | Retained when powered off | Lost on power cycle |
| Typical Use | LCD messages, fixed menus, lookup tables | User input buffers, dynamic data |

**Example Code in XC8:**

```c
#include <xc.h>

// ROM-based string (compile-time constant)
const rom char greeting[] = "WELCOME TO KTU";
unsigned char length;

// RAM-based string (runtime data)
unsigned char inputBuffer[20];

void main(void) {
    // Length of ROM string using library function
    length = strlenpgm(greeting);  // Returns 14
    
    // Copy ROM string to RAM buffer for manipulation
    strcpypgm2ram(inputBuffer, greeting);
    
    // Now inputBuffer in RAM can be modified
    inputBuffer[0] = 'H';  // Change 'W' to 'H'
    
    while(1);
}
```

**Role of `const rom` qualifier:**
- **`const`**: Tells the compiler the data must not be modified at runtime, allowing aggressive optimization (compiler may place it in a protected section).
- **`rom`**: Explicitly directs the compiler to allocate the string in **Program Memory** rather than Data Memory. Without this qualifier, the string would consume precious RAM.
- **Library functions like `strlenpgm()` and `strcpypgm2ram()`** are specially designed to handle ROM-based strings, internally generating the TBLRD instructions.

**Engineering Trade-off:** In battery-powered IoT devices, RAM strings are temporary scratchpads for incoming UART data, while ROM strings hold permanent configuration and display messages. A balanced embedded system uses ROM for ~80% of strings (constants) and RAM for ~20% (variable data). **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the NULL terminator** (0x00): String loops will read past buffer boundaries, causing system hang — **deduct 2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong TBLPTR initialization**: Using only 16-bit addressing instead of 21-bit (missing TBLPTRU) for addresses > 0xFFFF — **deduct 1-2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing `TBLRD` with `MOVFF`**: Students often write `MOVFF ROM_ADDR, RAM_BUF` which is INVALID in Harvard architecture — **deduct 2 marks**.
> 4. **Not incrementing FSR0/TBLPTR**: Forgetting post-increment causes infinite loops reading the same byte — **deduct 2 marks**.
> 5. **Mixing up `CPFSEQ` (skip if equal) vs `CPFSGT` (skip if greater)**: Wrong condition causes early or late termination — **deduct 1 mark**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **String Definition:** A NULL-terminated (0x00) sequence of ASCII characters stored sequentially in memory.
- **Harvard Architecture Challenge:** PIC18 has separate Program and Data memory spaces, requiring special TBLRD/TBLWT instructions to bridge them.
- **TBLPTR (21-bit):** Composed of TBLPTRU + TBLPTRH + TBLPTRL; addresses up to 2 MB of program memory.
- **TABLAT (8-bit):** The mandatory gateway register for all ROM data transfers.
- **TBLRD\*+ is the most-used variant** for sequential string reading (post-increment saves a separate INCF instruction).
- **NULL terminator (0x00) is critical** — it marks string end and prevents buffer overflow.
- **ROM strings are accessed slower (2 cycles)** but conserve RAM; RAM strings are faster (1 cycle) but consume scarce memory.
- **XC8 C Compiler:** Use `const rom char[]` for ROM strings, `char[]` for RAM strings; library functions `strlenpgm`, `strcpypgm2ram` automate TBLRD code generation.
- **FSR0, FSR1, FSR2** are the three indirect RAM pointers used for destination buffers in string operations.
- **String Length Algorithm:** Iterate byte-by-byte, incrementing counter, until NULL (0x00) is detected.
- **String Compare Algorithm:** XOR corresponding bytes; if XOR result is zero AND NULL reached in both, strings are equal.
- **Common Pitfall:** Always increment TBLPTR/FSR0 — failing to do so causes infinite loops.
- **KTU Favourite Questions:** "Explain ROM-to-RAM string copy with assembly code" and "Compare ROM vs RAM strings" appear frequently in Part B (14-mark) section.
- **Cycle Count Formula:** $t_{string\_copy} = (L+1) \times t_{TBLRD} + (L+1) \times t_{MOVFF}$ where L is string length.
- **Maximum Practical String Length:** Limited by RAM buffer size (typically 16-64 bytes for PIC18F4550 due to 2 KB total RAM).
- **Watch out for PCLATH/PCLATU** when using CALL or GOTO to string-handling subroutines placed above 0xFFFF boundary.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sorting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Microcontrollers (PECPT631)**, **Sorting** is defined as the algorithmic process of re-arranging a finite sequence of data elements stored in microcontroller memory (typically RAM, Flash buffers, or peripheral data registers such as ADC result registers) into a prescribed monotonic order — either **ascending (non-decreasing)** or **descending (non-increasing)** — based on a defined total ordering relation (numeric, lexicographic, or custom comparator).

Mathematically, given an input sequence $S = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots, a_n\}$ stored in array `arr[0..n-1]`, a sorting algorithm produces a permutation $S' = \{a'_1, a'_2, \dots, a'_n\}$ such that:

$$a'_1 \leq a'_2 \leq a'_3 \leq \dots \leq a'_n \quad (\text{Ascending Order})$$

$$a'_1 \geq a'_2 \geq a'_3 \geq \dots \geq a'_n \quad (\text{Descending Order})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Sorting is studied under embedded algorithm design, where constraints such as **limited RAM (typically 4 KB – 256 KB)**, **deterministic execution time (hard real-time systems)**, and **fixed instruction cycles per clock** govern the choice of sorting algorithm. Algorithms are evaluated on **comparisons, swaps, stack depth, and in-place memory footprint**.

### 1.2 Classification of Sorting Algorithms (Foundation Block)

Sorting algorithms are classified along four orthogonal axes in microcontroller-driven embedded design:

1. **Comparison-based vs Non-Comparison-based** — Whether the algorithm uses relational operators (`<`, `>`) or exploits numeric properties (e.g., digit positions, count of elements).
2. **In-place vs Out-of-place** — In-place algorithms use **$O(1)$ auxiliary memory** (critical for low-RAM MCUs like MSP430 or ATmega328); out-of-place algorithms require **$O(n)$ or more** extra RAM.
3. **Stable vs Unstable** — Stable sorts preserve the relative order of equal-keyed records (essential when sorting structs like `{timestamp, sensor_value}` where temporal order matters).
4. **Adaptive vs Non-adaptive** — Adaptive algorithms exploit existing partial order (e.g., nearly sorted ADC buffers) to run faster.

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded Context:** When sorting sensor arrays (e.g., median filtering of 5 ADC samples to reject outliers), the choice between Bubble, Insertion, and Quick sort is dictated by **stack size**, **interrupt latency budget**, and **code memory footprint (Flash)**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Card Player's Hand:**

Imagine you are holding a hand of **10 playing cards** face-up on a table. You want to arrange them in increasing rank order (2, 3, 4, …, 10, J, Q, K, A). Several strategies can achieve this:

- **Bubble Sort Strategy:** Repeatedly scan the row; if two adjacent cards are out of order, swap them. Each pass "bubbles" the largest unsorted card to the right end. Like bubbles rising in water, the biggest item drifts to the end.
- **Selection Sort Strategy:** Scan the entire hand, locate the smallest card, and place it at the leftmost position. Repeat for the next position, and so on. You are *selecting* the minimum each iteration.
- **Insertion Sort Strategy:** Take cards one at a time from the table and *insert* each into its correct position in the already-sorted left portion of your hand (shifting larger cards right). Exactly how you would naturally sort a hand of cards.
- **Quick Sort Strategy:** Pick a "pivot" card (say, the 7). Partition all cards into "less than 7" and "greater than 7" piles. Recursively sort each pile.
- **Merge Sort Strategy:** Split the hand into single cards (trivially sorted), then repeatedly *merge* adjacent sorted piles to form larger sorted piles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** On a microcontroller, these strategies translate directly to **nested loops with conditional swaps (Bubble/Selection/Insertion)** or **recursive function calls consuming stack frames (Quick/Merge)**. The stack frame concern is what makes Insertion Sort extremely popular in embedded interrupt service routines (ISRs) and boot-time firmware initialization.

### 1.4 Importance of Sorting in Microcontroller-Based Systems

| **Embedded Application** | **Sorting Use-Case** | **Algorithm Preference** |
|---|---|---|
| Digital filtering (median filter) | Find median of $N$ ADC samples | Selection or partial Quick Sort |
| Real-time OS task scheduling | Sort tasks by deadline | Counting / Radix Sort |
| Data logging (sorted timestamps) | Sort event log buffer | Insertion Sort (nearly-sorted) |
| Display rendering | Sort pixel Y-coordinates | Counting Sort (bounded range) |
| Network packet processing | Sort packets by priority | Heap Sort (in-place, $O(n \log n)$) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Analysis of Major Sorting Algorithms

#### 2.1.1 Bubble Sort — "The Repeated Adjacent Swap"

**Mechanism:** Iteratively traverse the array; for each adjacent pair $(arr[i], arr[i+1])$, swap if out of order. Each complete pass guarantees that the largest unsorted element "bubbles" to its final correct position at the end.

**Logic Steps (Embedded View):**
1. Initialize `swapped = 0` at start of each pass (acts as a flag in a general-purpose register, e.g., `R4` on ARM Cortex-M).
2. Outer loop counter $i$ from $0$ to $n-2$ (decreasing bound after each pass).
3. Inner loop counter $j$ from $0$ to $n-i-2$.
4. Compare $arr[j]$ and $arr[j+1]$ using conditional branch (`CMP` + `BGT` on ARM).
5. If out of order, execute swap using a temporary register.
6. Set `swapped = 1`. Early-exit optimization: if no swap occurred, array is sorted.

#### 2.1.2 Selection Sort — "The Minimum Finder"

**Mechanism:** For each position $i$ from $0$ to $n-2$, find the index $k$ of the minimum element in $arr[i..n-1]$ and swap $arr[i]$ with $arr[k]$.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Outer loop index $i$ from $0$ to $n-2$.
2. Initialize `min_idx = i`.
3. Inner loop index $j$ from $i+1$ to $n-1$.
4. If $arr[j] < arr[min\_idx]$, update `min_idx = j`.
5. After inner loop, swap $arr[i]$ and $arr[min\_idx]$ if `min_idx != i`.

#### 2.1.3 Insertion Sort — "The Card Sorter"

**Mechanism:** Maintain a sorted sub-array $arr[0..i-1]$. For each new element $key = arr[i]$, shift all sorted elements greater than $key$ one position to the right, and insert $key$ into the gap.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Outer loop $i$ from $1$ to $n-1$.
2. Store $key = arr[i]$ in a temporary variable.
3. Inner loop shifts elements: while $j \geq 0$ AND $arr[j] > key$, set $arr[j+1] = arr[j]$, decrement $j$.
4. Place $key$ at $arr[j+1]$.
5. Repeat until array is fully traversed.

#### 2.1.4 Quick Sort — "The Divide-and-Conquer Pivot"

**Mechanism:** Select a pivot element. Partition the array such that elements $\leq$ pivot are on the left, elements $>$ pivot are on the right. Recursively sort the two partitions.

**Logic Steps (Hoare / Lomuto Partition):**
1. Choose pivot (commonly `arr[high]` for Lomuto, or median-of-three for robustness).
2. **Partition (Lomuto):** Maintain index $i$ of "smaller-element boundary". Traverse $j$ from `low` to `high-1`. If $arr[j] \leq pivot$, increment $i$ and swap $arr[i]$, $arr[j]$.
3. Place pivot at correct position by swapping $arr[i+1]$ and $arr[high]$.
4. Recursively call Quick Sort on `arr[low..i]` and `arr[i+2..high]`.

#### 2.1.5 Merge Sort — "The Recursive Merger"

**Mechanism:** Recursively split the array in half until sub-arrays are size 1. Merge adjacent sorted sub-arrays into a single sorted array using a temporary buffer.

**Logic Steps:**
1. **Divide:** $mid = (low + high) / 2$. Recurse on left half $arr[low..mid]$ and right half $arr[mid+1..high]$.
2. **Conquer (Merge):** Use two pointers $i$ (left sub-array) and $j$ (right sub-array). Compare $L[i]$ and $R[j]$, append the smaller to a temporary buffer, advance the corresponding pointer.

#### 2.1.6 Heap Sort — "The In-Place $O(n \log n)$"

**Mechanism:** Build a max-heap from the array. Repeatedly extract the maximum (root), place it at the end of the array, and re-heapify the reduced heap.

**Logic Steps:**
1. **Build Max-Heap:** For $i = n/2 - 1$ down to $0$, call `heapify(arr, n, i)`.
2. **Heapify:** Compare node with its children `2i+1` and `2i+2`; swap with the largest child if violated; recurse on the affected child.
3. **Extraction Loop:** Swap $arr[0]$ and $arr[n-1]$. Reduce heap size by 1. Re-heapify root.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following table is **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU ESE questions on algorithmic complexity analysis. Memorize the time-space columns and stability entries.

| **Algorithm** | **Best Case $T_b$** | **Average Case $T_a$** | **Worst Case $T_w$** | **Auxiliary Space $S$** | **Stable?** | **In-place?** | **Adaptive?** | **Method** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bubble Sort | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | Comparison, Exchange |
| Selection Sort | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | No (in-place variant) | **Yes** | No | Comparison, Selection |
| Insertion Sort | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | Comparison, Insertion |
| Quick Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\log n)$ | No | **Yes** (Lomuto) | No | Comparison, Partition (D&C) |
| Merge Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | **Yes** | No | No | Comparison, Merge (D&C) |
| Heap Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(1)$ | No | **Yes** | No | Comparison, Heap-based Selection |
| Counting Sort | $O(n + k)$ | $O(n + k)$ | $O(n + k)$ | $O(k)$ | **Yes** | No | No | Non-comparison (indexing) |
| Radix Sort | $O(d \cdot (n + k))$ | $O(d \cdot (n + k))$ | $O(d \cdot (n + k))$ | $O(n + k)$ | **Yes** | No | No | Non-comparison (digit-wise) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $n$ = number of elements, $k$ = range of input values (for Counting Sort), and $d$ = number of digits (for Radix Sort). For microcontroller inputs where $k$ is bounded (e.g., 10-bit ADC $\Rightarrow k = 1024$), **Counting Sort** often outperforms $O(n \log n)$ comparison sorts.

### 2.3 Mathematical Foundations — Why $O(n^2)$ and $O(n \log n)$?

#### 2.3.1 Lower Bound for Comparison-Based Sorting (Information-Theoretic)

Any comparison-based sorting algorithm must perform **at least $\lceil \log_2(n!) \rceil$ comparisons** in the worst case, because there are $n!$ possible permutations of $n$ elements, and each comparison yields a binary outcome (yes/no).

Using **Stirling's approximation**: $\log_2(n!) \approx n \log_2(n) - 1.4427n + O(\log n)$

Hence the **tightest lower bound** is:

$$T(n) = \Omega(n \log n)$$

This proves that **Merge Sort, Heap Sort, and (average-case) Quick Sort are asymptotically optimal** among comparison-based sorts.

#### 2.3.2 Derivation of $O(n^2)$ for Simple Sorts (Bubble, Selection, Insertion)

The number of comparisons in Bubble Sort / Selection Sort / worst-case Insertion Sort is:

$$C(n) = (n-1) + (n-2) + (n-3) + \dots + 2 + 1 = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{n^2 - n}{2}$$

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^2 - n}{2n^2} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C(n) = \Theta(n^2)$$

#### 2.3.3 Recurrence Relations for Divide-and-Conquer Sorts

**Merge Sort Recurrence:**

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \Theta(1) & \text{if } n = 1 \\ 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n) & \text{if } n > 1 \end{cases}$$

By the **Master Theorem** with $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$:

$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n = f(n) \quad \Rightarrow \quad T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

**Quick Sort Recurrence (Average Case):**

$$T(n) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left[ T(k-1) + T(n-k) \right] + \Theta(n)$$

Solving this recurrence yields $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

**Quick Sort Recurrence (Worst Case, e.g., already-sorted input with bad pivot):**

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n) \quad \Rightarrow \quad T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$$

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace of Bubble Sort

**Problem:** Sort the array $A = \{5, 1, 4, 2, 8\}$ in ascending order using Bubble Sort. Show all passes, comparisons, and swaps.

**Initial State:** $A = [5, 1, 4, 2, 8]$, $n = 5$

#### Pass 1 ($i = 0$, unsorted range: indices $0 \rightarrow 4$)
- **Compare** $A[0] = 5$ and $A[1] = 1$: $5 > 1$ → **SWAP** → $A = [1, 5, 4, 2, 8]$
- **Compare** $A[1] = 5$ and $A[2] = 4$: $5 > 4$ → **SWAP** → $A = [1, 4, 5, 2, 8]$
- **Compare** $A[2] = 5$ and $A[3] = 2$: $5 > 2$ → **SWAP** → $A = [1, 4, 2, 5, 8]$
- **Compare** $A[3] = 5$ and $A[4] = 8$: $5 \leq 8$ → **NO SWAP** → $A = [1, 4, 2, 5, 8]$

**End of Pass 1:** $A = [1, 4, 2, 5, 8]$. Element $8$ is now in its final position.

#### Pass 2 ($i = 1$, unsorted range: indices $0 \rightarrow 3$)
- **Compare** $A[0] = 1$ and $A[1] = 4$: $1 \leq 4$ → **NO SWAP**
- **Compare** $A[1] = 4$ and $A[2] = 2$: $4 > 2$ → **SWAP** → $A = [1, 2, 4, 5, 8]$
- **Compare** $A[2] = 4$ and $A[3] = 5$: $4 \leq 5$ → **NO SWAP**

**End of Pass 2:** $A = [1, 2, 4, 5, 8]$. Element $5$ is now in its final position.

#### Pass 3 ($i = 2$, unsorted range: indices $0 \rightarrow 2$)
- **Compare** $A[0] = 1$ and $A[1] = 2$: $1 \leq 2$ → **NO SWAP**
- **Compare** $A[1] = 2$ and $A[2] = 4$: $2 \leq 4$ → **NO SWAP**

**End of Pass 3:** $A = [1, 2, 4, 5, 8]$. No swaps occurred, so algorithm terminates early.

#### Final Sorted Array
$$A_{\text{sorted}} = [1, 2, 4, 5, 8]$$

#### Total Operations Count
- **Comparisons:** $4 + 3 + 2 = 9$ comparisons
- **Swaps:** $3 + 1 + 0 = 4$ swaps
- **Formula Check:** $C(n) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = \dfrac{5 \cdot 4}{2} = 10$ maximum comparisons (we did 9 due to early termination in Pass 3).

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace of Insertion Sort

**Problem:** Sort the array $A = \{12, 11, 13, 5, 6\}$ in ascending order using Insertion Sort.

**Initial State:** $A = [12, 11, 13, 5, 6]$, $n = 5$

#### Iteration 1 ($i = 1$, $key = A[1] = 11$)
- Sorted sub-array so far: $[12]$.
- Compare $key = 11$ with $A[0] = 12$: $12 > 11$ → shift $A[0]$ to position $1$ → $A = [12, 12, 13, 5, 6]$
- Insert $key = 11$ at position $0$ → $A = [11, 12, 13, 5, 6]$

#### Iteration 2 ($i = 2$, $key = A[2] = 13$)
- Sorted sub-array so far: $[11, 12]$.
- Compare $key = 13$ with $A[1] = 12$: $12 \leq 13$ → **NO SHIFT**.
- Insert $key = 13$ at position $2$ → $A = [11, 12, 13, 5, 6]$

#### Iteration 3 ($i = 3$, $key = A[3] = 5$)
- Sorted sub-array so far: $[11, 12, 13]$.
- Compare $key = 5$ with $A[2] = 13$: $13 > 5$ → shift $13$ → $A = [11, 12, 13, 13, 6]$
- Compare $key = 5$ with $A[1] = 12$: $12 > 5$ → shift $12$ → $A = [11, 12, 12, 13, 6]$
- Compare $key = 5$ with $A[0] = 11$: $11 > 5$ → shift $11$ → $A = [11, 11, 12, 13, 6]$
- Insert $key = 5$ at position $0$ → $A = [5, 11, 12, 13, 6]$

#### Iteration 4 ($i = 4$, $key = A[4] = 6$)
- Sorted sub-array so far: $[5, 11, 12, 13]$.
- Compare $key = 6$ with $A[3] = 13$: $13 > 6$ → shift $13$ → $A = [5, 11, 12, 13, 13]$
- Compare $key = 6$ with $A[2] = 12$: $12 > 6$ → shift $12$ → $A = [5, 11, 12, 12, 13]$
- Compare $key = 6$ with $A[1] = 11$: $11 > 6$ → shift $11$ → $A = [5, 11, 11, 12, 13]$
- Compare $key = 6$ with $A[0] = 5$: $5 \leq 6$ → **STOP**.
- Insert $key = 6$ at position $1$ → $A = [5, 6, 11, 12, 13]$

#### Final Sorted Array
$$A_{\text{sorted}} = [5, 6, 11, 12, 13]$$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Time Complexity for Bubble Sort (Worst Case)

We derive the worst-case number of **comparisons** and **swaps** for Bubble Sort on an array of size $n$ where the input is in **strictly decreasing order** (the worst configuration).

**Setup:** In the worst case, the $i$-th pass performs $(n - 1 - i)$ comparisons and $(n - 1 - i)$ swaps.

**Step 1 — Total Comparisons in Worst Case:**

$$C_w(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n - 1 - i)$$

$$C_w(n) = (n-1) + (n-2) + (n-3) + \dots + 1$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the index:** Let $k = n - 1 - i$. When $i = 0$, $k = n-1$. When $i = n-2$, $k = 1$. Hence:

$$C_w(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k$$

**Step 3 — Apply the arithmetic series formula:**

$$C_w(n) = \frac{(n-1) \cdot n}{2} = \frac{n^2 - n}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic Big-O Notation:**

$$C_w(n) = \frac{n^2}{2} - \frac{n}{2}$$

Since for large $n$, the dominant term is $\dfrac{n^2}{2}$, and Big-O drops constant factors:

$$C_w(n) = O(n^2)$$

**Step 5 — Total Swaps in Worst Case:** Since the array is reverse-sorted, every comparison results in a swap, so the number of swaps equals the number of comparisons:

$$S_w(n) = C_w(n) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = O(n^2)$$

**Step 6 — Total Time:** Each comparison and each swap take constant time $c_1$ and $c_2$ respectively. Total time $T(n) = c_1 \cdot C_w(n) + c_2 \cdot S_w(n) = O(n^2)$.

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation of Time Complexity for Merge Sort (Recurrence Tree)

We derive $T(n) = O(n \log n)$ for Merge Sort using the **recursion tree method** applied to:

$$T(n) = 2T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + cn$$

where $cn$ represents the linear-time merge step.

**Step 1 — Root Level (Level 0):** The root represents the original problem of size $n$. Work at this level = $cn$.

**Step 2 — Level 1:** The problem splits into 2 sub-problems, each of size $n/2$. Work per sub-problem = $c \cdot n/2$. Total work at Level 1:

$$W_1 = 2 \cdot c \cdot \frac{n}{2} = cn$$

**Step 3 — Level 2:** 4 sub-problems, each of size $n/4$. Total work:

$$W_2 = 4 \cdot c \cdot \frac{n}{4} = cn$$

**Step 4 — Inductive Pattern:** At each level $j$ (for $0 \leq j \leq \log_2 n - 1$), there are $2^j$ sub-problems each of size $n / 2^j$. Total work at level $j$:

$$W_j = 2^j \cdot c \cdot \frac{n}{2^j} = cn$$

**Step 5 — Number of Levels:** The recursion bottoms out at level $L = \log_2 n$ where sub-problems have size $1$. At this leaf level, work per call is $c \cdot 1 = c$. Total leaf work:

$$W_{\text{leaves}} = n \cdot c = cn$$

**Step 6 — Summing Across All Levels:** Total work is the sum of work at all levels:

$$T(n) = \sum_{j=0}^{\log_2 n} W_j = \sum_{j=0}^{\log_2 n} cn = cn \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)$$

**Step 7 — Big-O Simplification:**

$$T(n) = cn \log_2 n + cn = O(n \log n)$$

**This confirms the Master Theorem result.**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Suitability Scoring for Microcontroller Use

| **Criterion** | **Bubble** | **Selection** | **Insertion** | **Quick** | **Merge** | **Heap** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Code Size (Flash footprint)** | Very Low | Very Low | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Stack Depth Required** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ avg, $O(n)$ worst | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| **Determinism (real-time safe)** | Poor (data-dependent) | Yes | Yes | Poor (worst-case) | Yes | Yes |
| **Stability** | Yes | No | Yes | No | Yes | No |
| **Best for $n < 16$ (ISR-friendly)** | Excellent | Excellent | **Excellent** | Good | Poor | Good |
| **Best for $n > 1000$ (large buffers)** | Poor | Poor | Poor | **Excellent** | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Cache Friendliness (Cortex-M)** | High | Medium | High | Low | Low | Medium |
| **Worst-Case Latency Bound** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | **$O(n \log n)$** | **$O(n \log n)$** |

### 4.2 Bubble Sort — Detailed Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Initialize swapped = 0]
    B --> C[Outer loop i = 0 to n minus 2]
    C --> D[Inner loop j = 0 to n minus i minus 2]
    D --> E{arr j greater than arr j plus 1}
    E -- YES --> F[Swap arr j and arr j plus 1]
    E -- NO --> G[Continue inner loop]
    F --> H[Set swapped = 1]
    G --> D
    H --> D
    D --> I{Inner loop done}
    I -- NO --> D
    I -- YES --> J{swapped equals 0}
    J -- YES --> K([ARRAY IS SORTED, EXIT])
    J -- NO --> C
    C --> L{Outer loop done}
    L -- NO --> C
    L -- YES --> K
```

### 4.3 Quick Sort — Partitioning and Recursion Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([QuickSort arr low high]) --> B{low less than high}
    B -- NO --> C([RETURN])
    B -- YES --> D[Call Partition arr low high]
    D --> E[Receive pivot index pi]
    E --> F[Recursive call QuickSort arr low pi minus 1]
    F --> G[Recursive call QuickSort arr pi plus 1 high]
    G --> C
    
    D --> D1[Set pivot = arr high]
    D1 --> D2[Set i = low minus 1]
    D2 --> D3[Loop j = low to high minus 1]
    D3 --> D4{arr j less than or equal to pivot}
    D4 -- YES --> D5[i = i + 1]
    D5 --> D6[Swap arr i and arr j]
    D4 -- NO --> D3
    D6 --> D3
    D3 --> D7[Swap arr i plus 1 and arr high]
    D7 --> D8[Return i plus 1]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*Define sorting. List any four comparison-based sorting algorithms and state their worst-case time complexities.*

**Model Answer:**

**Sorting** is the process of re-arranging the elements of a finite sequence into a monotonic order (ascending or descending) based on a defined ordering relation. Formally, given a sequence $S = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n\}$, a sorting algorithm produces a permutation $S'$ such that the elements are non-decreasing (or non-increasing).

**Four comparison-based sorting algorithms and their worst-case time complexities:**

| **Algorithm** | **Worst-Case Time Complexity** |
|---|---|
| Bubble Sort | $O(n^2)$ |
| Selection Sort | $O(n^2)$ |
| Insertion Sort | $O(n^2)$ |
| Quick Sort | $O(n^2)$ |
| Merge Sort | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Heap Sort | $O(n \log n)$ |

*(Any four with correct complexities accepted; 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for table.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Distinguish between stable and unstable sorting algorithms. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Property** | **Stable Sort** | **Unstable Sort** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Preserves the relative order of records with equal keys. | May change the relative order of records with equal keys. |
| **Example** | Bubble Sort, Insertion Sort, Merge Sort, Counting Sort | Selection Sort, Quick Sort, Heap Sort, Shell Sort |

**Engineering Significance:** Stable sorts are required in microcontroller applications where records are structs (e.g., `{timestamp, sensor_value}`) and secondary ordering must be preserved. For example, when sorting 10-bit ADC samples with attached sample-index metadata, only stable sorts guarantee the chronological order is maintained among equal-valued readings.

*(2 marks for distinction + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the Bubble Sort algorithm with a suitable example. Derive its worst-case time complexity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare Insertion Sort and Selection Sort with respect to their mechanism, time complexity (best, average, worst), stability, and adaptability. State one application scenario for each in embedded systems. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Algorithm Description:**

Bubble Sort repeatedly traverses the array, comparing adjacent pairs and swapping them if they are out of order. Each complete pass guarantees that the largest unsorted element "bubbles up" to the end of the unsorted region. The algorithm terminates when a complete pass produces no swaps, indicating the array is sorted.

**Worked Example — Sort $[5, 1, 4, 2, 8]$:**

| **Pass** | **Comparisons** | **Array State After Pass** |
|---|---|---|
| Initial | — | $[5, 1, 4, 2, 8]$ |
| Pass 1 | 4 | $[1, 4, 2, 5, 8]$ |
| Pass 2 | 3 | $[1, 2, 4, 5, 8]$ |
| Pass 3 | 2 (no swap) | $[1, 2, 4, 5, 8]$ — Terminated early |

**Derivation of Worst-Case Time Complexity:**

In the worst case (reverse-sorted input), the $i$-th pass performs $(n - 1 - i)$ comparisons and the same number of swaps. Total comparisons:

$$C_w(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n - 1 - i) = (n-1) + (n-2) + \dots + 1 = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{n^2 - n}{2}$$

Since each comparison and swap is $O(1)$ time, total time is:

$$T_w(n) = O\left(\frac{n^2 - n}{2}\right) = O(n^2)$$

**Auxiliary Space:** $O(1)$ (in-place, only one temp variable for swap).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| **Criterion** | **Insertion Sort** | **Selection Sort** |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Inserts each new element into its correct position in the sorted left sub-array by shifting larger elements right. | Selects the minimum element from the unsorted region and swaps it to the current position. |
| **Best Case** | $O(n)$ (already sorted; only one comparison per pass) | $O(n^2)$ (must scan even if already sorted) |
| **Average Case** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| **Worst Case** | $O(n^2)$ (reverse sorted) | $O(n^2)$ (always) |
| **Stability** | Stable | Unstable (in standard implementation) |
| **Adaptivity** | Adaptive (runs faster on nearly-sorted input) | Non-adaptive |
| **Swaps** | Up to $O(n^2)$ | Exactly $O(n)$ swaps |
| **Embedded Application** | **Median-of-5 filter in ADC interrupt service routine** (small, nearly-sorted window; low stack usage) | **Top-K selection in sorted priority queue** (min number of swaps useful for flash wear reduction) |

**Key Inference:** Insertion Sort is preferred when input is **nearly sorted** (e.g., real-time sensor streams with small perturbations). Selection Sort is preferred when **write operations are expensive** (e.g., EEPROM writes on limited-cycle memory), as it makes exactly $n-1$ swaps.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the Quick Sort algorithm using the Lomuto partition scheme. Trace the algorithm on the input array $[10, 7, 8, 9, 1, 5]$ and show all recursive calls and partitions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the recurrence relation for Quick Sort. Solve it for the average case to show $T(n) = O(n \log n)$. Explain why Quick Sort is preferred in general-purpose sorting but may not be suitable for hard real-time systems. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Lomuto Partition Scheme:**

```
Partition(arr, low, high):
    pivot = arr[high]              // Choose last element as pivot
    i = low - 1                    // Index of smaller-element boundary
    for j = low to high - 1:
        if arr[j] <= pivot:
            i = i + 1
            swap arr[i] and arr[j]
    swap arr[i+1] and arr[high]    // Place pivot at correct position
    return i + 1                   // Pivot index
```

**Trace on $[10, 7, 8, 9, 1, 5]$ (low=0, high=5):**

**Initial Call:** `QuickSort(arr, 0, 5)`
- Pivot = $arr[5] = 5$
- $i = -1$
- $j = 0$: $10 > 5$ → no action
- $j = 1$: $7 > 5$ → no action
- $j = 2$: $8 > 5$ → no action
- $j = 3$: $9 > 5$ → no action
- $j = 4$: $1 \leq 5$ → $i = 0$, swap $arr[0]$ and $arr[4]$ → $arr = [1, 7, 8, 9, 10, 5]$
- Final swap: swap $arr[1]$ and $arr[5]$ → $arr = [1, 5, 8, 9, 10, 7]$
- **Return $i+1 = 1$** (pivot position)

**Recursive Call 1:** `QuickSort(arr, 0, 0)` → returns immediately (single element).

**Recursive Call 2:** `QuickSort(arr, 2, 5)`
- Pivot = $arr[5] = 7$
- $i = 1$
- $j = 2$: $8 > 7$ → no action
- $j = 3$: $9 > 7$ → no action
- $j = 4$: $10 > 7$ → no action
- Final swap: swap $arr[2]$ and $arr[5]$ → $arr = [1, 5, 7, 9, 10, 8]$
- **Return $i+1 = 2$**

**Recursive Call 3:** `QuickSort(arr, 2, 2)` → returns.

**Recursive Call 4:** `QuickSort(arr, 3, 5)`
- Pivot = $arr[5] = 8$
- $i = 2$
- $j = 3$: $9 > 8$ → no action
- $j = 4$: $10 > 8$ → no action
- Final swap: swap $arr[3]$ and $arr[5]$ → $arr = [1, 5, 7, 8, 10, 9]$
- **Return $i+1 = 3$**

**Recursive Call 5:** `QuickSort(arr, 3, 3)` → returns.

**Recursive Call 6:** `QuickSort(arr, 4, 5)`
- Pivot = $arr[5] = 9$
- $i = 3$
- $j = 4$: $10 > 9$ → no action
- Final swap: swap $arr[4]$ and $arr[5]$ → $arr = [1, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10]$
- **Return $i+1 = 4$**

**Final Sorted Array:** $arr = [1, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10]$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Recurrence Relation for Quick Sort:**

$$T(n) = T(k) + T(n - k - 1) + \Theta(n)$$

where $k$ is the size of the left partition and $\Theta(n)$ is the partition work.

**Average Case Recurrence** (assuming uniform random pivot placement, expected $k = (n-1)/2$):

$$T(n) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \left[ T(k) + T(n - k - 1) \right] + cn$$

$$T(n) = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} T(k) + cn$$

**Solving by Substitution (Guess $T(n) \leq an \log n$):**

Multiply both sides by $n$:

$$nT(n) = 2 \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} T(k) + cn^2$$

Subtract $(n-1)T(n-1) = 2 \sum_{k=0}^{n-2} T(k) + c(n-1)^2$:

$$nT(n) - (n-1)T(n-1) = 2T(n-1) + 2cn - c$$

$$nT(n) = (n+1)T(n-1) + 2cn - c$$

Divide by $n(n+1)$:

$$\frac{T(n)}{n+1} = \frac{T(n-1)}{n} + \frac{2c}{n+1} - \frac{c}{n(n+1)}$$

Telescoping gives:

$$\frac{T(n)}{n+1} = \frac{T(0)}{1} + 2c \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k+1} - O(1) = 2c \ln n + O(1)$$

$$T(n) = 2c (n+1) \ln n + O(n) = O(n \log n)$$

**Why Quick Sort is preferred in general-purpose sorting:**
- Excellent cache locality due to in-place partitioning.
- Average-case $O(n \log n)$ is the theoretical optimum.
- Low auxiliary memory ($O(\log n)$ stack frames).
- Highly optimized in standard libraries (e.g., `qsort` in C, `Arrays.sort` in Java for primitive types).

**Why Quick Sort may not be suitable for hard real-time systems:**
- **Unbounded worst-case latency:** $O(n^2)$ if input is sorted/reverse-sorted with bad pivot choice (e.g., always choose first or last element).
- **Stack overflow risk:** Pathological input can cause $O(n)$ stack depth, exceeding MCU stack limits.
- **Non-deterministic execution time:** Worst-case execution time (WCET) cannot be statically bounded without randomized pivots.
- **Cache thrashing** in deeply recursive calls on memory-constrained Cortex-M devices.

For hard real-time systems, **Heap Sort or Merge Sort** is preferred due to deterministic $O(n \log n)$ worst-case.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> **1. Confusing Best/Average/Worst Case:** Many students incorrectly write "$O(n \log n)$" as the worst-case for Quick Sort. **Deduct 1 mark** if worst-case is stated as $O(n \log n)$.
> 
> **2. Forgetting Stability:** When asked "is Selection Sort stable?", the answer is **No** in the standard implementation (because of the $arr[i] \leftrightarrow arr[min\_idx]$ swap that can jump over equal elements). Many students wrongly mark it as stable. **Deduct 1 mark.**
> 
> **3. Skipping the "Why":** In derivation questions, simply stating "$O(n^2)$" without showing the arithmetic series summation $\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k = n(n-1)/2$ **loses 2-3 marks**.
> 
> **4. Misapplying Master Theorem:** The recurrence $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n^2$ gives $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$, NOT $\Theta(n^{\log_2 3})$. Many students miscalculate. **Always compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$.**
> 
> **5. Embedded Context Missing:** When discussing sorting for microcontrollers, students often ignore RAM/stack/WCET concerns. **Always mention at least one embedded constraint** (RAM, determinism, stack depth) for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Sorting** = re-arranging elements into monotonic order; classified as **comparison-based** or **non-comparison-based**.
- [x] **In-place** algorithms use $O(1)$ auxiliary memory; critical for low-RAM MCUs.
- [x] **Stable** sorts preserve order of equal keys; **Unstable** sorts may not.
- [x] **Bubble Sort:** $O(n^2)$ worst/average, $O(n)$ best (with early-exit), stable, in-place, adaptive.
- [x] **Selection Sort:** $O(n^2)$ in all cases, unstable (standard impl.), in-place, non-adaptive, exactly $n-1$ swaps.
- [x] **Insertion Sort:** $O(n^2)$ worst/average, $O(n)$ best, stable, in-place, **adaptive** (best for nearly-sorted).
- [x] **Quick Sort:** $O(n \log n)$ average, $O(n^2)$ worst, unstable, in-place, **unpredictable stack depth**.
- [x] **Merge Sort:** $O(n \log n)$ in all cases, stable, **NOT in-place** ($O(n)$ auxiliary), deterministic.
- [x] **Heap Sort:** $O(n \log n)$ in all cases, unstable, in-place, deterministic, $O(1)$ auxiliary.
- [x] **Comparison-based lower bound:** $\Omega(n \log n)$ — proven via information theory / Stirling's approximation.
- [x] **Counting Sort / Radix Sort** can break the $O(n \log n)$ bound by using **non-comparison** techniques — $O(n + k)$ or $O(d \cdot (n + k))$.
- [x] **Comparison count for $O(n^2)$ sorts:** $C(n) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ — derivable from arithmetic series.
- [x] **Master Theorem cases** for divide-and-conquer sorts:
  - Case 1: $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$
  - Case 2: $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$
  - Case 3: $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$
- [x] **Embedded design rule:** For $n < 16$, prefer **Insertion Sort**; for $n > 1000$ with deterministic WCET, prefer **Heap Sort** or **Merge Sort**.
- [x] **Randomized Quick Sort** (random pivot selection) reduces worst-case probability to negligible $\dfrac{1}{n!}$, but does not eliminate it.
- [x] **Median-of-three pivot selection** mitigates the sorted-input pathological case in Quick Sort.
- [x] **In ISR (Interrupt Service Routine):** Avoid recursive sorts; use iterative Insertion Sort or pre-sorted buffer with binary insertion.
- [x] **Memory-mapped I/O registers** must never be sorted directly — always copy to a RAM buffer before sorting.
- [x] **FLASH vs RAM trade-off:** On Harvard-architecture MCUs (e.g., MSP430), code in Flash executes with wait-states; minimize code size of sort routine.
- [x] **Final Mantra for KTU Exam:** "If asked for an example of stable sort → Bubble / Insertion / Merge / Counting. If asked for unstable → Selection / Quick / Heap / Shell. If asked for $O(n \log n)$ worst-case → Merge / Heap. If asked for in-place $O(n \log n)$ worst-case → **Heap Sort only**."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Searching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Searching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Microcontrollers (PECPT631)**, *Searching* refers to the algorithmic procedure used to locate a specific target data element (key) within a structured data set — typically an array, a lookup table, or a peripheral register map — executed by the firmware running on the embedded processor. The operation returns either the **index/address** of the matched element or a sentinel value (commonly `-1`) indicating *absence*.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) Outcome-Based Education framework**, searching algorithms are assessed under *Course Outcome CO3: "Design and implement efficient firmware algorithms for resource-constrained embedded environments."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT631 — Module 3):**
> Searching algorithms form the backbone of embedded lookup routines, sensor-data filtering, command interpreters, and real-time control loops. Mastery of **time-space trade-offs** is mandatory for board examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in front of a **stack of 1,000 identical, sealed envelopes**, and one contains a letter addressed to you. You have two strategies:

1. **Brute Force (Linear Search):** Open every envelope one by one, from top to bottom. In the worst case, you open all 1,000 envelopes.
2. **Clever Approach (Binary Search):** If the envelopes are *alphabetically sorted by sender's surname*, you open the *middle* envelope first. If your target surname comes *before* it alphabetically, you discard the entire right half; otherwise, discard the left half. Repeat. You will find your envelope in roughly **log₂(1000) ≈ 10** openings.

The second strategy is exponentially faster, but it **requires the data to be pre-sorted** — a critical trade-off engineers must evaluate in microcontroller environments where RAM is precious (often only **4 KB to 64 KB** on devices like STM32 or PIC families).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Time Embedded Context:**
> In an automotive ECU running on a 32-bit ARM Cortex-M3 @ 72 MHz, executing a linear search over 256 bytes of calibration data consumes roughly **3.5 µs**, while binary search over the same set consumes only **0.4 µs** — a 9× speed-up that determines whether a control loop meets its **1 ms deadline**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics

The two governing metrics, both of which are **bold** in KTU valuation keys, are:

- **Time Complexity (T(n)):** Asymptotic number of primitive operations executed as a function of input size *n*.
- **Space Complexity (S(n)):** Auxiliary memory consumed beyond the input array itself.

Boundary conditions typically assumed by KTU examiners:

- **n → ∞** asymptotic behaviour (Big-O notation).
- **Array indexing base:** `0` (C-language convention used in Keil µVision / MPLAB X IDE).
- **Sorted array prerequisite:** Required only for divide-and-conquer searches.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Searching Algorithms

Search algorithms are classified along three orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Category A | Category B |
|:-----|:-----------|:-----------|
| **Data Order** | Works on *unsorted* data | Requires *sorted* data |
| **Access Pattern** | Sequential (one-by-one) | Random (indexed jumps) |
| **Auxiliary Memory** | In-place (O(1) extra) | Needs buffer (O(log n) extra) |

Applying these axes yields the canonical taxonomy:

1. **Linear Search (Sequential Search)** — *unsorted, sequential, in-place.*
2. **Binary Search (Half-Interval Search)** — *sorted, random-access, in-place (iterative) or O(log n) stack (recursive).*
3. **Jump Search (Block Search)** — *sorted, sequential-with-blocks, in-place.*
4. **Interpolation Search** — *sorted + uniformly distributed, position-estimated, in-place.*
5. **Exponential Search** — *sorted, unbounded size, O(1) iterative / O(log n) recursive.*

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Algorithm

### 2.2.1 Linear Search — The Universal Fallback

**Why it exists:** It makes *zero assumptions* about data ordering. In embedded firmware, sensor buffers, ring queues, and unsorted log records are routinely scanned linearly.

**How it works:** Compare the target *key* with each element $A[0], A[1], \ldots, A[n-1]$ sequentially. On match, return the index. If the loop completes without a match, return $-1$.

### 2.2.2 Binary Search — The Divide-and-Conquer Powerhouse

**Why it exists:** When data is sorted and stored in a contiguous array (e.g., a calibration LUT in flash memory), binary search achieves logarithmic time — the fastest possible for comparison-based search.

**How it works:** Maintain two pointers, `low` and `high`. At each iteration:
- Compute $mid = low + \lfloor (high - low) / 2 \rfloor$ (the **overflow-safe** form required in 8/16-bit microcontrollers).
- Compare $A[mid]$ with the key.
- Discard the half that cannot contain the key.
- Repeat until either $A[mid] == key$ or $low > high$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mid-Calculation Pitfall (Valuation Hot-Spot):**
> Examiners **deduct 1 mark** if students write $mid = (low + high) / 2$ instead of the overflow-safe form $mid = low + (high - low) / 2$. On an 8-bit AVR where `int` is 16 bits, the naive form causes subtle wrap-around bugs when both pointers are near 32767.

### 2.2.3 Jump Search — A Useful Middle Ground

**Why it exists:** On microcontrollers with **slow random-access memory** (e.g., external SPI flash), jumping ahead in fixed blocks of size $\sqrt{n}$ is faster than binary search because it exploits *sequential prefetch*.

**How it works:**
1. Jump ahead in blocks of size $m = \lfloor \sqrt{n} \rfloor$ until $A[min(j, n-1)] \geq key$.
2. Perform a linear search backwards within the identified block of size $m$.

### 2.2.4 Interpolation Search — The Statistical Specialist

**Why it exists:** When sorted data is *uniformly distributed* (typical for ADC samples, temperature tables, PWM duty cycles), interpolation search estimates the likely position of the key directly, achieving $O(\log \log n)$ average time.

**How it works:** Position estimate is given by:

$$pos = low + \frac{(key - A[low]) \cdot (high - low)}{A[high] - A[low]}$$

If $A[pos] == key$, return *pos*. Otherwise, narrow the search range and repeat.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — Memorize for KTU Board Exams (Dec 2024 / May 2025 sessions):**

| Algorithm | Pre-condition | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Auxiliary Space |
|:----------|:--------------|:---------:|:------------:|:----------:|:---------------:|
| Linear Search | None | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Binary Search (Iterative) | Sorted array | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Binary Search (Recursive) | Sorted array | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ stack |
| Jump Search | Sorted array | $O(1)$ | $O(\sqrt{n})$ | $O(\sqrt{n})$ | $O(1)$ |
| Interpolation Search | Sorted + uniform | $O(1)$ | $O(\log \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Exponential Search | Sorted, unbounded | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ iter / $O(\log n)$ rec |

**Optimal Block Size for Jump Search:**

$$m_{opt} = \lfloor \sqrt{n} \rfloor$$

**Optimality Lower Bound (Comparison-Based Search):**

$$T(n) = \Omega(\log n)$$

This is the *information-theoretic lower bound* — no comparison-based search can beat $\log_2(n+1)$ comparisons in the worst case.

**Recurrence Relation for Binary Search:**

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1$$

Solving via the Master Theorem (Case 2, $a=1, b=2, f(n)=n^0$):

$$T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$$

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Engineering Domain | Search Algorithm Used | Justification |
|:-------------------|:---------------------|:--------------|
| **Automotive ECU calibration tables** | Binary Search | Sorted 2-D LUT stored in flash |
| **RFID tag identification** | Linear Search | Small tag ID sets (n < 50) |
| **TCP/IP routing table lookup** | Hash / Binary Search | Sorted prefix table |
| **Sensor noise filtering (median filter)** | Linear Selection | Small sliding window |
| **GPS coordinate lookup** | Interpolation Search | Uniformly distributed latitude/longitude |
| **Flash file system (FAT)** | Linear + Cluster chain | Small directory entries |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Linear Search Time Complexity

### 3.1.1 Formal Algorithm Statement

Given an array $A[0 \ldots n-1]$ and a target key $k$, define:

$$\text{LinearSearch}(A, n, k) = \begin{cases} i & \text{if } \exists\, i \in [0, n-1] \text{ such that } A[i] = k \\ -1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 3.1.2 Counting Primitive Operations

Let $C(n)$ denote the total number of comparisons. For each index $i$, exactly **one comparison** $A[i] == k$ is performed. The number of iterations before termination follows a geometric distribution.

**Best Case (k at A[0]):**

$$C_{best}(n) = 1$$

**Worst Case (k absent or at A[n-1]):**

$$C_{worst}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1 = n$$

**Average Case (uniform random position of k):**

If $k$ is equally likely to be at any position $i \in \{0, 1, \ldots, n-1\}$ or absent, with probability $1/(n+1)$ for each outcome:

$$C_{avg}(n) = \frac{1}{n+1} \left( \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (i+1) + n \right)$$

Expanding the inner sum:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (i+1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

Substituting back:

$$C_{avg}(n) = \frac{1}{n+1} \left( \frac{n(n+1)}{2} + n \right)$$

Simplifying:

$$C_{avg}(n) = \frac{1}{n+1} \cdot \frac{n(n+1) + 2n}{2} = \frac{1}{n+1} \cdot \frac{n^2 + 3n}{2}$$

$$C_{avg}(n) = \frac{n^2 + 3n}{2(n+1)} = \frac{n(n+3)}{2(n+1)}$$

Asymptotic simplification as $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n(n+3)}{2(n+1)} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^2 + 3n}{2n + 2} = \frac{n}{2} = O(n)$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Binary Search via Master Theorem

### 3.2.1 Recurrence Setup

At each step, binary search divides the problem of size $n$ into exactly **one** sub-problem of size $n/2$, and performs a constant amount of work (one comparison and pointer arithmetic):

$$T(n) = 1 \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(1)$$

Formally, with $T(1) = 1$:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1$$

### 3.2.2 Master Theorem Application

The Master Theorem applies to recurrences of the form:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

For binary search: $a = 1$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = 1 = n^0$.

Compute the critical exponent:

$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 1} = n^0 = 1$$

Since $f(n) = 1 = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, we are in **Case 2** of the Master Theorem, which gives:

$$T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log n\right) = \Theta(\log n)$$

### 3.2.3 Closed-Form by Recursion Tree (Verification)

Expanding $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ level by level:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1$$

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 1 + 1 = T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2$$

After $k$ levels of expansion:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + k$$

The recursion bottoms out when $n / 2^k = 1$, i.e., when $k = \log_2 n$. Substituting:

$$T(n) = T(1) + \log_2 n = 1 + \log_2 n$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(\log n)}$$

### 3.2.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** Given the sorted array $A = [3, 7, 11, 14, 19, 22, 28, 35, 41, 50]$, find the index of key $k = 22$ using binary search. Show all iterations.

**Solution Trace:**

| Iteration | low | high | mid | A[mid] | Comparison | Action |
|:---------:|:---:|:----:|:---:|:------:|:----------:|:-------|
| 1 | 0 | 9 | 4 | 19 | 22 > 19 | low = mid+1 = 5 |
| 2 | 5 | 9 | 7 | 35 | 22 < 35 | high = mid−1 = 6 |
| 3 | 5 | 6 | 5 | 22 | 22 == 22 | **FOUND at index 5** |

**Verification:** Exactly **3 iterations** consumed. Predicted bound: $\lceil \log_2(10) \rceil = 4$. The element was found before exhausting the upper bound, confirming best-case proximity.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection Decision Tree

The decision logic an embedded firmware engineer follows in selecting a search algorithm is captured below as a structured flowchart of conditions:

```
START
  │
  ▼
[Is the dataset pre-sorted?]
  │
  ├── NO ──────────────► Linear Search (n < 500) or Hash Table (n ≥ 500)
  │
  └── YES
        │
        ▼
      [Is n known and bounded?]
        │
        ├── NO ──────────► Exponential Search
        │
        └── YES
              │
              ▼
            [Is data uniformly distributed?]
              │
              ├── YES ──────► Interpolation Search (if RAM allows floating point)
              │
              └── NO
                    │
                    ▼
                  [Is random-access memory fast?]
                    │
                    ├── YES ─► Binary Search
                    │
                    └── NO ───► Jump Search (√n block size)
END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (often quoted in KTU viva-voce):**
> Use **Linear Search** when $n \leq 64$. Use **Binary Search** when $n > 64$ and data is sorted. Beyond $n = 100{,}000$ stored in external memory, prefer **Hash-based** lookups despite their $O(n)$ worst case.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linear Search — Mermaid Control Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the complete state-machine flow of an iterative linear search, including the sentinel-exit condition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode1([START]) --> initI["i = 0"]
    initI --> condCheck{"i less than n AND A of i not equal to key"}
    condCheck -- "TRUE: continue scan" --> incI["i = i + 1"]
    incI --> condCheck
    condCheck -- "FALSE: exit loop" --> keyFound{"A of i equals key?"}
    keyFound -- "YES: matched" --> returnIndex["RETURN i"]
    keyFound -- "NO: sentinel reached" --> returnNeg["RETURN -1"]
    returnIndex --> stopNode1([END])
    returnNeg --> stopNode1([END])

    style startNode1 fill:#1f6feb,color:#ffffff
    style stopNode1 fill:#d73a49,color:#ffffff
    style returnIndex fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff
    style returnNeg fill:#d73a49,color:#ffffff
    style condCheck fill:#fff3cd,color:#000000
    style keyFound fill:#fff3cd,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Binary Search — Mermaid Divide-and-Conquer Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode2([START]) --> initBounds["low = 0, high = n - 1"]
    initBounds --> loopCheck{"low less than or equal to high?"}
    loopCheck -- "FALSE: range empty" --> returnNotFound["RETURN -1"]
    loopCheck -- "TRUE: divide" --> calcMid["mid = low + floor of high minus low over 2"]
    calcMid --> compareKey{"A of mid compared to key?"}
    compareKey -- "EQUAL" --> returnMid["RETURN mid"]
    compareKey -- "key less than A of mid" --> goLeft["high = mid - 1"]
    compareKey -- "key greater than A of mid" --> goRight["low = mid + 1"]
    goLeft --> loopCheck
    goRight --> loopCheck
    returnMid --> stopNode2([END])
    returnNotFound --> stopNode2([END])

    style startNode2 fill:#1f6feb,color:#ffffff
    style stopNode2 fill:#d73a49,color:#ffffff
    style returnMid fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff
    style returnNotFound fill:#d73a49,color:#ffffff
    style compareKey fill:#fff3cd,color:#000000
    style loopCheck fill:#fff3cd,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Searching Module Inside a Microcontroller Firmware

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CORE["Cortex-M3 Core @ 72 MHz"]
        cpuA["CPU Pipeline"]
        aluA["ALU Unit"]
        regA["Register Bank R0-R12"]
    end

    subgraph FW["Firmware Layer"]
        appTaskA["Application Task Scheduler"]
        searchEngineA["Generic Search Engine API"]
        linearModA["Linear Search Module"]
        binaryModA["Binary Search Module"]
        jumpModA["Jump Search Module"]
    end

    subgraph DATA["Data Storage Layer"]
        sramA["Internal SRAM 20 KB"]
        flashA["Internal Flash 128 KB LUT"]
        eepromA["EEPROM 4 KB"]
    end

    cpuA --> appTaskA
    appTaskA --> searchEngineA
    searchEngineA --> linearModA
    searchEngineA --> binaryModA
    searchEngineA --> jumpModA
    linearModA --> sramA
    binaryModA --> flashA
    jumpModA --> eepromA
    regA -.-> linearModA
    regA -.-> binaryModA
    regA -.-> jumpModA
    aluA -.-> searchEngineA

    style CORE fill:#e3f2fd,color:#000000
    style FW fill:#fff8e1,color:#000000
    style DATA fill:#f3e5f5,color:#000000
    style searchEngineA fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Search Algorithm Trade-off Radar

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef goodNode fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff
    classDef mediumNode fill:#ffc107,color:#000000
    classDef poorNode fill:#d73a49,color:#ffffff

    linA["Linear: Speed POOR, Simplicity GOOD"]
    binA["Binary: Speed GOOD, Simplicity MEDIUM"]
    jmpA["Jump: Speed MEDIUM, Simplicity MEDIUM"]
    interpA["Interpolation: Speed GOOD, Simplicity POOR"]

    linA --- linASpeed((O of n)):::poorNode
    binA --- binASpeed((O of log n)):::goodNode
    jmpA --- jmpASpeed((O of sqrt n)):::mediumNode
    interpA --- interpASpeed((O of log log n avg)):::goodNode

    style linA fill:#ffcccc,color:#000000
```
