| Case 5 | $P_{\text{active}} = 0$ (any) | $P_{\text{new}} = \text{NMI}$ | **NMI preempts** (negative priority always wins) |
| Case 6 | $P_{\text{active}} = \text{anything}$ | $P_{\text{new}} = \text{Reset}$ | **Reset always preempts** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tail-Chaining** is a Cortex-M optimization: when one handler completes and another of equal/lower priority is pending, the processor **skips the unstacking-restacking cycle**, saving up to **12 clock cycles**.

## 3.4 Mathematical Justification of "Lower Number = Higher Priority"

The NVIC priority comparator performs the operation:

$$
\text{Winner} = \arg\min_{i} (P_i)
$$

This is justified because:
1. Hardware comparator naturally finds the minimum of unsigned integers in a single cycle.
2. Signed comparison is reserved for negative-priority system exceptions (Reset, NMI, HardFault).
3. The "lower is better" convention allows **easy demotion of priorities** by simple addition of constants.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NVIC Priority Resolution Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Exception Triggered] --> B{Active Handler?}
    B -- No --> C[Service Immediately]
    B -- Yes --> D{New Preempt < Active Preempt?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Stack Active Context]
    E --> F[Service New Exception]
    D -- No --> G{New Preempt == Active Preempt?}
    G -- No --> H[Mark as Pending]
    G -- Yes --> I{New Sub < Active Sub?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Queue for Tail-Chain]
    I -- No --> K{New Sub == Active Sub?}
    K -- Yes --> L{New Vector < Active Vector?}
    K -- No --> H
    L -- Yes --> J
    L -- No --> H
    F --> M[Execute Handler]
    C --> M
    J --> N[After Current Handler Ends]
    N --> M
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of NVIC Priority Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT SIGNALS]
        A1[IRQ1 Request]
        A2[IRQ2 Request]
        A3[IRQ3 Request]
        A4[NMI Request]
        A5[Reset Request]
    end
    subgraph IPR[INTERRUPT PRIORITY REGISTERS]
        B1[IPR0]
        B2[IPR1]
        B3[IPRn]
    end
    subgraph PRIGROUP_CFG[PRIGROUP CONFIGURATION]
        C1[AIRCR Register]
        C2[Preemption Splitter]
        C3[Sub Priority Splitter]
    end
    subgraph ARBITER[NVIC ARBITER]
        D1[Priority Comparator]
        D2[Vector Table Indexer]
        D3[Tail Chain Logic]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT TO CORE]
        E1[CPU Vector Fetch]
        E2[Stack Push Engine]
        E3[Active Priority Register]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> D1
    A5 --> D1
    B1 --> D1
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    D3 --> E2
    D1 --> E3
```

## 4.3 Priority Bit Splitting Visualization (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[8-bit IPRn Byte] --> B{8-N Unimplemented Bits}
    B -- LSB region --> C[Read As Zero Region]
    A --> D{N Bits Implemented Region}
    D --> E{Preemption Priority Field}
    D --> F{Sub Priority Field}
    E --> G[MSBs of Implemented Region]
    F --> H[LSBs of Implemented Region]
    G --> I[Determines Nesting Depth]
    H --> J[Determines Tie Breaking Order]
```

## 4.4 Exception Priority Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Highest Logical Priority] --> A1[Reset -3]
    A1 --> A2[NMI -2]
    A2 --> A3[HardFault -1]
    A3 --> B[Configurable Exception Priorities]
    B --> B1[Priority 0 Highest Configurable]
    B1 --> B2[Priority 1]
    B2 --> B3[Priority 2]
    B3 --> B4[Priority ...]
    B4 --> B5[Priority 0xFF Lowest Configurable]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Part A — 3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q:** What is the significance of priority grouping in the ARM Cortex-M NVIC? How is it configured?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Priority grouping determines **how the implemented priority bits in each IPRn register are split** between preemption priority and sub-priority fields.

- It is configured using the **PRIGROUP field (bits [10:8])** of the **Application Interrupt and Reset Control Register (AIRCR)**.
- The PRIGROUP value ranges from **0 to 7**, specifying how many of the LSBs of the implemented priority region are assigned to sub-priority; the remaining MSBs become preemption priority.
- **Significance:** Allows the system designer to balance between deep interrupt nesting (more preemption bits) and fine-grained ordering among equal-preemption exceptions (more sub-priority bits).

**[Mark Allocation: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 (Part A — 3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q:** Why do Reset, NMI, and HardFault have fixed priorities in Cortex-M? List their priority values.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Reset, NMI, and HardFault are **system-critical exceptions** that must always be serviceable regardless of application-level priority settings.

- Their priorities are **fixed at negative values** so that they always preempt any configurable exception.
- **Priority values:**
  - **Reset:** −3 (highest priority in the system)
  - **NMI:** −2 (Non-Maskable Interrupt)
  - **HardFault:** −1 (catches all fault conditions that cannot be handled by configurable fault handlers)

**(1 Mark for reason + 1 Mark for significance + 1 Mark for values)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | Module 4 Internal Choice A]**

**Q (a)** Explain the structure of Interrupt Priority Registers (IPRn) in the NVIC. How are unused priority bits handled, and what is the effect of PRIGROUP on the priority field? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part a):

**1. Structure of IPRn:**
- Each IPRn register is **8 bits wide** and controls the priority of one exception (some exceptions may share a register).
- The register is byte-addressable; for a 32-bit aligned IPRn base, offsets of 0, 1, 2, 3 bytes cover four exceptions.
- Only the **upper N bits** (where N is implementation-defined, typically **3 to 8**) are implemented for actual priority storage. **(2 Marks)**

**2. Handling of Unused Bits:**
- The lower $(8 − N)$ bits are **Read-As-Zero (RAZ)** and **Write-Ignored (WI)** — known as "sticky" bits.
- This means writing a value to these bits has no effect, and reading returns 0.
- For example, if only 4 bits are implemented, writing `0x5A` actually stores only `0x50` (upper nibble preserved, lower nibble ignored). **(2 Marks)**

**3. Effect of PRIGROUP:**
- The PRIGROUP field (bits [10:8] of AIRCR) splits the N implemented bits into two logical sub-fields.
- The **MSB region** becomes the preemption priority field.
- The **LSB region** (within the implemented bits) becomes the sub-priority field.
- PRIGROUP value $G$ determines sub-priority bit count as $\max(0, G − (8 − N))$. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Diagram Representation:**

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Implemented | Implemented | Implemented | Implemented | RAZ/WI | RAZ/WI | RAZ/WI | RAZ/WI |

(For N = 4 bits implemented in upper nibble)

---

**Q (b)** With N = 4 implemented priority bits and PRIGROUP = 0b100 (decimal 4), three exceptions with the following priority bytes are pending: IRQ_A = 0x40, IRQ_B = 0x60, IRQ_C = 0x50. Determine the order of service and justify each step. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part b):

**Step 1: Calculate Sub-Priority Bits**

$$
\text{Sub-bits} = \max(0, \, G - (8 - N)) = \max(0, \, 4 - 4) = 0
$$

So **all 4 implemented bits are preemption priority**, and **sub-priority bits = 0**.

**Step 2: Extract Upper Nibble (Implemented Region)**

- IRQ_A: `0x40` → upper nibble = `0100` → $P_A = 4$
- IRQ_B: `0x60` → upper nibble = `0110` → $P_B = 6$
- IRQ_C: `0x50` → upper nibble = `0101` → $P_C = 5$

**Step 3: Apply Priority Resolution**

Since there are no sub-priority bits, ties are broken by vector number. Here all preemption values are distinct.

**Order (lowest value first):**

$$
\boxed{\text{IRQ\_A} \;(4) \;\rightarrow\; \text{IRQ\_C} \;(5) \;\rightarrow\; \text{IRQ\_B} \;(6)}
$$

**(Each step: 2 Marks; Final order with justification: 1 Mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 | Module 4 Internal Choice B]**

**Q (a)** Describe the three types of exceptions in Cortex-M with their priority handling. Compare fixed-priority and configurable-priority exceptions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part a):

**1. Three Categories of Exceptions:**

**(i) System Exceptions (Fixed Negative Priority):**
- Reset (−3), NMI (−2), HardFault (−1) — Fixed, cannot be changed, cannot be masked by BASEPRI/PRIMASK (except Reset).
- Always preempt any other exception. **(2 Marks)**

**(ii) Configurable Fault Handlers:**
- MemManage, BusFault, UsageFault — Have configurable priorities but can be **escalated to HardFault** if their handler is not enabled or if they occur while being serviced.
- Default behavior: HardFault-all (locked to HardFault priority). **(2 Marks)**

**(iii) External Interrupts (IRQs):**
- Peripheral-driven interrupts (EXTI, TIM, USART, etc.) — Fully configurable priorities via IPRn registers. **(1 Mark)**

**2. Comparison Table:** **(2 Marks)**

| Aspect | Fixed Priority | Configurable Priority |
|--------|----------------|-----------------------|
| Values | −3, −2, −1 | 0 to max implemented |
| Modifiable | No | Yes (via IPRn) |
| Preemption capability | Always preempt configurable | Based on numeric comparison |
| Examples | Reset, NMI, HardFault | TIM2, USART1, EXTI0 |

---

**Q (b)** A real-time system requires: Motor control interrupt (IRQ 44) must always preempt Communication interrupt (IRQ 37). Both must preempt ADC interrupt (IRQ 18). Communication and ADC must never preempt each other. Design the priority scheme assuming N = 4 bits implemented, and write the IPRn values using PRIGROUP = 0b011. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part b):

**Step 1: Design Priority Requirements**

- Motor control: Highest preemption → $P_{\text{preempt}} = 0$
- Communication: Middle preemption → $P_{\text{preempt}} = 1$
- ADC: Lowest preemption → $P_{\text{preempt}} = 2$

**Step 2: Verify No Cross-Preemption**

Since preemption values are distinct (0, 1, 2), Motor > Communication > ADC in preemption, satisfying the requirement. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 3: Apply PRIGROUP = 0b011 (decimal 3)**

$$
\text{Sub-bits} = \max(0, \, 3 - (8 - 4)) = \max(0, 3 - 4) = 0
$$

**Note:** With PRIGROUP = 0b011 and N = 4, we get **0 sub-priority bits**. All 4 bits become preemption. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 4: Compute IPRn Byte Values**

Using the formula:

$$
V = (P_{\text{preempt}} \ll \text{Sub-bits}) \;\vert\; P_{\text{sub}}
$$

Since sub-bits = 0:

$$
V = P_{\text{preempt}} \ll 4
$$

- **Motor (IRQ 44):** $V = 0 \ll 4 = 0x00$
- **Communication (IRQ 37):** $V = 1 \ll 4 = 0x10$
- **ADC (IRQ 18):** $V = 2 \ll 4 = 0x20$

**Final Assignment Table:**

| Interrupt | IPRn Value | Preemption Priority | Sub-Priority |
|-----------|------------|---------------------|--------------|
| Motor Control | **0x00** | 0 | N/A |
| Communication | **0x10** | 1 | N/A |
| ADC | **0x20** | 2 | N/A |

**(Each step: 1 Mark; Final IPRn values with justification: 2 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write "higher numerical value = higher priority." This is **WRONG**. In Cortex-M, **lower numerical value = higher logical priority**. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that unimplemented bits are RAZ/WI. Writing a value with non-zero lower nibble in IPRn and not mentioning the masking effect loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing vector number with priority. Vector number is used **only as a final tie-breaker** when both preemption and sub-priority are equal.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Not showing the PRIGROUP bit-split calculation explicitly. Always write out: $\text{sub-bits} = \max(0, G - (8-N))$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ⭐ **Golden Rule:** Lower numerical priority value = Higher logical priority.
- ⭐ **Fixed Priorities:** Reset (−3) > NMI (−2) > HardFault (−1).
- ⭐ **IPRn:** 8-bit byte per exception; only upper N bits implemented; lower $(8 − N)$ bits are RAZ/WI.
- ⭐ **PRIGROUP:** Bits [10:8] of AIRCR; controls split between preemption and sub-priority.
- ⭐ **Resolution Order:** Compare preemption → Compare sub-priority → Compare vector number.
- ⭐ **Tail-Chaining:** Saves up to 12 cycles when chaining equal-priority exceptions.
- ⭐ **Preemption Condition:** New $P_{\text{preempt}}$ < Active $P_{\text{preempt}}$ (strict).
- ⭐ **Sub-Priority Bits Formula:** $\max(0, G - (8 - N))$.
- ⭐ **STM32 Default:** Often 4 bits implemented, PRIGROUP often set to 0b011 in HAL.
- ⭐ **Real-Time Rule:** Higher urgency interrupts must be assigned numerically lower priority values.
- ⭐ **Escalation:** If MemManage/BusFault/UsageFault handlers are disabled, faults escalate to HardFault.
- ⭐ **NVIC Registers:** IPRn, ISER/ICER (enable), ISPR/ICPR (set/clear pending), IABR (active bit).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Link Register Offsets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Link Register Offsets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> In the **ARM Cortex-M** architecture, the **Link Register (LR, R14)** is a dedicated core register used to store the **return address** of a subroutine call and to hold a special **EXC_RETURN** value during exception processing. A **Link Register Offset** refers to the *fixed numerical displacement* (most commonly **+4 bytes**, but sometimes **+8 bytes** in pipelined/exception scenarios) added by the hardware to the current **Program Counter (PC)** to compute the correct return address pushed onto the stack or written into LR when a **Branch-and-Link (BL)**, exception entry, or interrupt tail-chain event occurs.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are reading a book and you come across a footnote that says "see Chapter 5, Page 102." To return to the exact sentence you were reading in Chapter 3, you would normally use your finger as a bookmark. The **Link Register (LR)** in ARM is that "electronic bookmark."

Now, suppose the footnote itself is two lines long. When you mark your current spot (PC) to jump to the footnote (BL instruction), the ARM processor does **not** simply bookmark the *current* word; it bookmarks the word **+4 bytes ahead** — the *next* instruction. This **+4 byte offset** exists because the ARM Cortex-M3/M4 uses a **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch → Decode → Execute), and by the time the BL instruction actually executes, the PC has already advanced 4 bytes beyond it. If the processor did not compensate with the offset, control would return to the middle of the BL instruction itself — a catastrophic error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The **Link Register Offset** is mandatory knowledge for questions on **exception entry/exit sequencing, NVIC behavior, tail-chaining, late-arrival interrupts, and stack frame unwinding** in the ARM Cortex-M3/M4. Examiner frequently tests whether a student understands *why* the offset is **+4** and not **+2** or **0**.

### Physical Constants / Standard Metrics
- **Pipeline Depth (Cortex-M3/M4):** **3-stages** → justifies the **+4 byte** offset.
- **Instruction Width:** **Fixed 32-bit (4 bytes)** in ARM Thumb-2.
- **Stack Push Width on Exception:** **8 words (32 bytes)** in standard handler mode (Cortex-M3/M4 with floating point disabled: **8 words = 32 bytes**).
- **EXC_RETURN Bit Pattern Mask:** `0xFFFFFFF0` (top 28 bits indicate return mode).


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Role of the Link Register (LR / R14)
- Holds the **return address** for subroutine calls made via `BL` or `BLX`.
- Holds a special token called **EXC_RETURN** during exception processing.
- Acts as the *fastest possible return path* — no memory access required to read the return address.

### 2.2 Why an Offset is Needed — The Pipelining Problem
The ARM Cortex-M3/M4 uses a **3-stage instruction pipeline**:

| Pipeline Stage | Function | Address Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Stage 1 | Fetch | PC = address of instruction being fetched |
| Stage 2 | Decode | PC = address of instruction being decoded |
| Stage 3 | Execute | PC = address of instruction being executed |

When the `BL` instruction reaches the **Execute stage**, the PC has already moved **8 bytes ahead** (Fetch of next 2 instructions). ARM's design convention is that LR must contain the address of the **next instruction after the BL**, not the BL itself. The hardware therefore forces:
$$\text{LR} \leftarrow \text{PC} + \text{offset}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Formula / Behavior | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `BL label` (Branch-and-Link) | $\text{LR} = \text{PC} + 4$ | PC here = address of BL + 4 (already in Execute stage) |
| Standard Exception Entry | $\text{Stack}[\text{PC slot}] = \text{Next\_Inst\_Addr} = \text{Faulting\_Inst\_Addr} + 4$ | Offset = **+4** bytes |
| Exception Entry from **BL/BLX** | $\text{Stack}[\text{PC slot}] = \text{BL} + 4$ (the LR value) | LR is the return address pushed |
| **Tail-Chained** Interrupt | $\text{Stack}[\text{PC slot}] = 0xFFFFFFFF$ | No return; new handler proceeds directly |
| **Late-Arrival** Interrupt | First stacking uses **+4**; second exception abandons first | Pre-emption optimization |
| `POP {PC}` on Return | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{Stack}[n]$ | Recovered value depends on prior offset |
| `BX LR` | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{LR}$ | Restores PC; if LR = EXC_RETURN, exits handler |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** The offset is **always in bytes**, and because every Thumb instruction is **2 bytes (16-bit)** or **4 bytes (32-bit)**, a "+4 offset" typically points to the *next sequential instruction* in the vast majority of cases.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **RTOS Context Switching:** The offset ensures the scheduler resumes the correct thread instruction after pre-emption.
- **Debugger Stack Unwinding:** Tools like Keil µVision and IAR Embedded Workbench rely on the offset to correctly reconstruct the call graph from the stacked PC.
- **Fault Analysis:** HardFault handlers examine the stacked PC + offset to localize the offending instruction.
- **Interrupt Latency Optimization:** Tail-chaining (using offsets cleverly) reduces interrupt latency from ~12 cycles to **6 cycles**.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the +4 Offset for the BL Instruction

Let the address of the `BL subroutine` instruction be `A`. The 3-stage pipeline at the cycle of execution contains:

- **Fetch Stage (F):** Instruction at address `A + 4`
- **Decode Stage (D):** Instruction at address `A + 2` (typically — depends on instruction width)
- **Execute Stage (E):** Instruction at address `A` (i.e., the `BL` itself)

The Program Counter register physically exposed by the architecture always points to the instruction **currently being fetched + 4** (an ARM convention). Therefore, when `BL` is in the Execute stage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{hardware}} &= A + 4 \quad \text{(since PC reads as "current fetch address + 4")} \\
\text{LR}_{\text{new}} &= \text{PC}_{\text{hardware}} \\
&= A + 4
\end{aligned}
$$

The ARM hardware then performs the branch by writing:

$$
\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{subroutine address}
$$

Upon returning from the subroutine (via `BX LR` or `POP {PC}`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC} &\leftarrow \text{LR} \\
\text{PC} &= A + 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Since `A + 4` is precisely the address of the **instruction after** the `BL`, control flow resumes correctly.

> **Conversion Logic Explained:** The "+4" is not arbitrary — it is the architectural guarantee that **PC reads as (address of currently-executing-instruction) + 4** in ARM mode. Without this contract, every branch instruction would have to subtract 2 or 4 manually, bloating the ISA.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Offset for Exception Entry

When an exception is accepted by the NVIC, the processor must save **8 registers** on the current stack:

$$
\text{Push order: } xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0
$$

The crucial question is: **What value of PC is pushed?**

Let `I` be the address of the instruction that was interrupted (or the instruction whose fault triggered the exception). The processor pushes:

$$
\text{Stack}[\text{PC slot}] = I + 4
$$

The derivation is identical to the BL case:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \text{address of interrupted instruction} \\
\text{At exception entry, PC}_{\text{hardware}} &= I + 4 \\
\text{Stacked PC} &= I + 4
\end{aligned}
$$

When the handler returns using `POP {PC}` or a special return instruction, control resumes at `I + 4`, which is the **next instruction** after the interrupted one — exactly the desired behavior.

### 3.3 Special Case: Exception Entry from Inside a `BL` Sequence

Suppose the main code executes:
```
0x0800_0100:   BL  Foo     ; LR = 0x0800_0104
0x0800_0104:   MOV R0, #1  ; <-- this is where PC points after BL
```

If `Foo` triggers an interrupt while executing, the stacked PC will be **not 0x0800_0104**, but rather the address inside `Foo` + 4. However, the LR register is still loaded with `0x0800_0104` (from the BL before entering Foo). The hardware therefore pushes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stack}[\text{PC}] &= \text{Address inside Foo} + 4 \\
\text{LR} &\leftarrow \text{EXC\_RETURN value} \quad (\text{e.g., } \texttt{0xFFFFFFF1})
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why **`EXC_RETURN` is loaded into LR** during exception processing — the actual return address is on the **stack**, not in LR. LR becomes a *token* that tells the hardware "use the stacked value of PC when exiting."

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Offset Behavior Summary

| Scenario | PC Value Pushed to Stack | LR Value After Entry | Return Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal `BL` call | N/A (LR holds return) | $A + 4$ | `BX LR` or `POP {PC}` |
| Exception from mainline | $I + 4$ | `EXC_RETURN` token | Automatic on POP/POP |
| Exception from inside `BL` | Address-in-subroutine + 4 | `EXC_RETURN` token | Stack-stored PC used |
| Tail-chained exception | `0xFFFFFFFF` (no return) | New EXC_RETURN | Proceeds to new handler |
| Late-arrival exception | Original PC + 4 | New EXC_RETURN | Original handler aborted |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An ARM Cortex-M4 processor is executing a `BL subroutine` instruction located at address `0x0800_1234`. The `subroutine` begins at `0x0800_2000`. The subroutine triggers an interrupt whose handler starts at `0x0800_3000`. Calculate:
- (i) The value written into LR by the `BL` instruction.
- (ii) The value pushed onto the stack as PC at the moment the interrupt is accepted.
- (iii) The value of EXC_RETURN (assuming Handler mode, MSP usage, basic return).

**Solution:**

**(i) Value in LR after BL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LR} &= \text{Address of BL} + 4 \\
&= \texttt{0x0800\_1234} + \texttt{0x4} \\
&= \texttt{0x0800\_1238}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) PC pushed onto stack at interrupt entry:**

The processor is currently inside `subroutine` at some address, say `0x0800_2008`. The stacked PC will be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stack[PC]} &= \texttt{0x0800\_2008} + \texttt{0x4} \\
&= \texttt{0x0800\_200C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) EXC_RETURN value:**

For Handler mode using MSP, returning to basic (no FPU):

$$
\text{EXC\_RETURN} = \texttt{0xFFFFFFF1}
$$

Interpretation of bit fields:
- Bits [31:4] = `0xFFFFFFF` → **valid EXC_RETURN marker**
- Bit [3] = `0` → not sleeping
- Bit [2] = `0` → basic stack frame (no extended FPU)
- Bit [1] = `0` → return to **Handler** mode
- Bit [0] = `1` → use **MSP** as stack


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Link Register Behavior During Normal Subroutine Call

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Main: PC = 0x0800_0100"] -->|"BL Foo"| nodeB["Execute BL: PC = 0x0800_0104"]
    nodeB -->|"LR = PC + 4"| nodeC["LR = 0x0800_0104"]
    nodeB -->|"Branch"| nodeD["PC jumps to Foo: 0x0800_2000"]
    nodeD -->|"Subroutine runs"| nodeE["Execute subroutine body"]
    nodeE -->|"BX LR or POP PC"| nodeF["PC = LR = 0x0800_0104"]
    nodeF -->|"Resume mainline after BL"| nodeA
```

### 4.2 Exception Entry — Stack Frame and LR Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX["Interrupted Instruction at I"] -->|"NVIC accepts exception"| nodeY["Stacking Phase Begins"]
    nodeY -->|"Push xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0"| nodeZ["8 words pushed to stack"]
    nodeZ -->|"Stacked PC = I + 4"| nodeP["Stack contains return address"]
    nodeZ -->|"LR loaded with EXC_RETURN token"| nodeQ["LR = 0xFFFFFFF1 or similar"]
    nodeQ -->|"Handler executes"| nodeR["Interrupt Service Routine runs"]
    nodeR -->|"On Return: POP PC"| nodeS["PC = I + 4, normal flow resumes"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["Execution continues at instruction after I"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Offset Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSrc["Instruction Memory"] -->|"Current Instruction I"| nodeFetch["Fetch Stage"]
    nodeFetch -->|"Next Fetch PC = I + 4"| nodeDec["Decode Stage"]
    nodeDec -->|"PC = I + 4 visible"| nodeEx["Execute Stage"]
    nodeEx -->|"BL or Exception occurs"| nodeCalc["Offset Calculation Unit"]
    nodeCalc -->|"Return Address = PC + 4"| nodeDest["Destination: LR or Stack Slot"]
    nodeDest -->|"Saved for later POP or BX LR"| nodeRet["Return Path"]
```

### 4.4 Offset Comparison: Normal vs Tail-Chained

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Exception 1 active, stacking with PC + 4"] -->|"New IRQ arrives"| nodeB{"New Exception Higher Priority"}
    nodeB -->|"Yes: Pre-empt"| nodeC["Original stacked PC kept, switch to new handler"]
    nodeB -->|"No: Tail-chain"| nodeD["Skip stacking, PC slot = 0xFFFFFFFF, jump to new handler"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Stack PC still has +4 offset"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["New handler returns directly to original interrupted code"]
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Link Register (LR) in ARM Cortex-M and state its dual role during normal execution versus exception processing.**

**Model Answer:**
The Link Register (R14) is a dedicated 32-bit core register in the ARM Cortex-M architecture. During normal execution, it stores the **return address** automatically written by the `BL` (Branch-and-Link) or `BLX` instruction, enabling fast subroutine returns via `BX LR`. During exception processing, LR is loaded with a special **EXC_RETURN** value (e.g., `0xFFFFFFF1`) that encodes information about which stack pointer to use, return mode, and stack frame format. The actual return address is then retrieved from the **stacked PC** on the process stack.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is the link register offset always +4 bytes in ARM Cortex-M3/M4 processors? Justify with reference to the pipeline.**

**Model Answer:**
The offset is **+4 bytes** because the ARM Cortex-M3/M4 uses a **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch, Decode, Execute). By architectural convention, the PC register always points to the address of the **current instruction + 4** (i.e., two instruction slots ahead). When `BL` executes, the hardware computes LR = PC + 4 so that upon return (`BX LR`), execution resumes at the instruction **immediately after** the BL — not at the BL itself. Without this +4 offset, the processor would re-execute the BL, causing an infinite loop.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose marks on this topic because they: (1) confuse **LR value** with **stacked PC value** during exceptions; (2) forget that LR holds **EXC_RETURN** (not the return address) inside a handler; (3) assume the offset is +2 or +8 — it is **+4** for standard Cortex-M; (4) misunderstand **tail-chaining** and wrongly claim that no PC is pushed at all (actually `0xFFFFFFFF` is pushed).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the behavior of the Link Register when a `BL` instruction is executed. Show how the return address is computed and used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain what happens to the Link Register and the stacked PC value during exception entry in ARM Cortex-M4. Discuss the significance of the EXC_RETURN value with its bit fields. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BL Instruction and LR Behavior (7 marks):**

The `BL subroutine` instruction performs two operations atomically:
1. Saves the return address into LR.
2. Branches PC to the subroutine entry.

Since Cortex-M4 has a 3-stage pipeline, when `BL` is in the Execute stage, PC has already advanced 4 bytes beyond the BL's address. Hence:

$$
\text{LR} = \text{Address of next instruction} = \text{Address of BL} + 4
$$

```
Example:
0x0800_0100:   BL   0x0800_0200     ; LR <- 0x0800_0104
0x0800_0104:   MOV  R0, #1          ; <- return point
0x0800_0108:   ...
0x0800_0200:   ...subroutine body...
0x0800_0280:   BX   LR              ; PC <- 0x0800_0104, normal flow
```

Upon `BX LR`, PC = 0x0800_0104, and execution continues correctly with `MOV R0, #1`. The **+4 offset** ensures no instruction is skipped or repeated.

**(b) Exception Entry and EXC_RETURN (7 marks):**

When an exception is accepted by the NVIC, the processor performs an **8-word hardware stack push** in the order:

$$
xPSR \rightarrow PC \rightarrow LR \rightarrow R12 \rightarrow R3 \rightarrow R2 \rightarrow R1 \rightarrow R0
$$

The stacked PC is set to:
$$
\text{Stack}[PC] = \text{Address of next instruction to execute} = \text{Interrupted address} + 4
$$

The actual LR register is loaded with a special token **EXC_RETURN**, e.g., `0xFFFFFFF1`. Bit decoding:

| Bits | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| [31:4] | `0xFFFFFFF` | Valid EXC_RETURN marker |
| [3] | 0 | Not in sleep mode |
| [2] | 0 | Basic stack frame (no FPU extended state) |
| [1] | 0 | Return to Handler mode |
| [0] | 1 | Use MSP as stack pointer |

When the handler executes a return (e.g., via `POP {PC}` or special hardware return), the EXC_RETURN value is detected, the stacked PC is popped into PC, and normal execution resumes at `Interrupted_Address + 4`.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **tail-chaining** in ARM Cortex-M interrupts. How does the Link Register offset behave in this scenario? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A nested interrupt arrives during the execution of a `BL` instruction's subroutine. Trace the complete path of the LR value and the stacked PC, clearly showing the offsets involved at each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Tail-Chaining and LR Offset (7 marks):**

Tail-chaining is an **NVIC optimization** that minimizes interrupt latency when one exception completes and another is pending. Instead of fully unstacking (POP) the first handler's context and then stacking again for the second handler, the processor **skips both** and proceeds directly to the second handler.

**Link Register Offset Behavior:**

- During the first handler, the stacked PC contains the normal return address (offset +4).
- When tail-chaining occurs, the processor **does not push a new frame**. Instead, the LR is set to a fresh **EXC_RETURN** for the second exception, and the **stacked PC slot is overwritten with `0xFFFFFFFF`** — a sentinel meaning *"do not return to me, I am not a real interrupted instruction."*

The second handler runs immediately. On its return via `POP {PC}`, it encounters `0xFFFFFFFF`, recognizes this as a tail-chain token, and the NVIC continues unstacking the **first** exception's actual stacked PC (with the original +4 offset) to resume the original code.

**(b) Nested Interrupt During BL Subroutine (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Main code executes BL:**
- Address of BL = `0x0800_0500`
- Hardware sets `LR = 0x0800_0504` (PC + 4 offset)
- PC jumps to subroutine entry at `0x0800_0800`

**Step 2 — Subroutine runs:**
- PC is now `0x0800_0810` (executing some instruction in the subroutine)
- LR still holds `0x0800_0504`

**Step 3 — Higher-priority interrupt fires:**
- NVIC accepts the exception
- Stacking phase begins: **8 words pushed onto MSP**
- Stacked PC = `0x0800_0810 + 0x4 = 0x0800_0814` (+4 offset applied)
- LR is now overwritten with **EXC_RETURN** value `0xFFFFFFF1`

**Step 4 — Interrupt Handler runs:**
- LR contains `EXC_RETURN` (not the return address!)
- The actual return address to resume the subroutine is at `Stack[PC]` = `0x0800_0814`

**Step 5 — Handler returns:**
- `POP {PC}` causes PC ← `0x0800_0814`
- Subroutine resumes at `0x0800_0814`

**Step 6 — Subroutine completes:**
- Executes `BX LR` → PC ← LR? **No!** LR is now `EXC_RETURN`, not the return address.
- The programmer must use `POP {PC}` with the original LR value still on the subroutine's own stack frame (if any), or the compiler-generated epilogue handles this.

This illustrates the **critical difference** between LR (which holds either return address OR EXC_RETURN) and the stacked PC (which holds the true +4-offset return address during exceptions).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **LR (R14)** has two distinct roles: (1) subroutine return address holder during `BL`, (2) EXC_RETURN token holder during exception processing.
- ✅ **Offset value is +4 bytes** because of the 3-stage pipeline and Thumb-2 32-bit instruction width.
- ✅ During exception entry, **8 registers are stacked**: xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0.
- ✅ **Stacked PC** contains the true return address (interrupted instruction + 4), **not** the LR value.
- ✅ **EXC_RETURN** has the bit pattern `0xFFFFFFF_xxx0` where lower bits encode stack pointer choice, return mode, and frame type.
- ✅ **Tail-chaining** uses `0xFFFFFFFF` as the stacked PC sentinel to indicate "no real return address."
- ✅ For standard Cortex-M3/M4, the offset is **+4**, **not** +2 or +8 (unlike older ARM7/ARM9 which used +8 due to deeper pipelines).
- ✅ `BX LR` and `POP {PC}` both perform the same PC-load operation, but the **source** (LR vs Stack) determines the offset path.
- ✅ Fault analysis uses `Stack[PC] − 4` to locate the offending instruction in HardFault handlers.
- ✅ In RTOS context switches, the saved context follows the same +4 offset rule for PC restoration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupts - Assigning Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interrupts - Assigning Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **PIC18F family of Advanced Microcontrollers** (the core architecture of KTU's PECPT631 syllabus), **"Assigning Interrupts"** refers to the systematic process of mapping each peripheral or external interrupt source to one of two priority levels — **High Priority** or **Low Priority** — by programming the dedicated **Interrupt Priority Registers (IPR1, IPR2, IPR3)** and enabling the priority-leveling engine via the **IPEN bit (RCON<7>)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT631 – Module 4):**
> The phrase "Assigning Interrupts" specifically covers the hardware mechanism by which the PIC18F core supports a **two-level prioritized interrupt architecture**, allowing a high-priority interrupt to preempt an ongoing low-priority Interrupt Service Routine (ISR), but not vice versa. This is a **core examinable topic** and frequently appears in Part B questions of KTU ESE.

The assignment process involves three sub-actions:
1. **Enabling** priority levels globally (IPEN = 1).
2. **Assigning** each interrupt source to a priority level (via IPRx bits).
3. **Enabling** the corresponding peripheral interrupt (via PIEx bits) and the global high/low enable bits (GIEH / GIEL).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Triage Model

Imagine a **hospital emergency ward**:

- **Patients (Interrupt Requests)** keep arriving at the reception desk (the microcontroller).
- The **Triage Nurse (IPEN bit)** decides whether the ward operates in a **single-priority** mode (first-come-first-served) or in a **two-tier** mode (critical vs. non-critical).
- The **Priority Tags (IPRx bits)** on each patient file determine if they go to the **Red Zone (High Priority – 0x0008 vector)** or the **Green Zone (Low Priority – 0x0018 vector)**.
- The **Senior Doctor (GIEH)** only attends to Red Zone patients, while the **Junior Doctor (GIEL)** handles Green Zone cases. The Senior Doctor can **interrupt** the Junior Doctor mid-treatment, but the reverse is forbidden.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** "Assigning an interrupt" simply means writing a `1` (High) or `0` (Low) into the corresponding bit of the **IPRx register** after the **IPEN** bit has been set. This single configuration controls which vector (0x0008 or 0x0018) the CPU will jump to when that specific interrupt fires.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundaries (Bolded for Recall)

| Parameter | Value / Boundary | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **High Priority Vector Address** | **0x0008** | Reset vector is 0x0000; next is High ISR |
| **Low Priority Vector Address** | **0x0018** | Second interrupt vector for low-priority ISRs |
| **Number of Priority Levels** | **2 (when IPEN = 1)** | Backward-compat mode has only 1 level |
| **IPR Register Width** | **8 bits each** | Three registers: IPR1, IPR2, IPR3 |
| **Status Register Priority Field** | **STATUS<7:5> = IPL<2:0>** | Reflects current CPU execution priority |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Mode Architecture: Why "Assignment" Exists

The PIC18F core was designed to retain **backward compatibility** with the older PIC16F instruction set while adding modern features. Hence, the interrupt system operates in **two distinct modes**, dictated entirely by the **IPEN bit of the RCON register**:

### Mode A — Compatibility Mode (IPEN = 0)
- Only **ONE** interrupt vector exists at **0x0008**.
- All interrupts are treated as **equal priority**.
- The `GIE` bit (INTCON<7>) globally enables/disables **all** interrupts.
- No preemption is possible.

### Mode B — Priority Mode (IPEN = 1) — *The "Assignment" Mode*
- **TWO** interrupt vectors exist: **0x0008 (High)** and **0x0018 (Low)**.
- Each peripheral interrupt can be **assigned** to either vector.
- **High-priority ISRs can preempt Low-priority ISRs**, but NOT the reverse.
- The `GIEH` bit (INTCON<7>) enables High-priority interrupts globally.
- The `GIEL` bit (INTCON<6>) enables Low-priority interrupts globally.
- The CPU's current priority is reflected in `IPL<2:0>` (STATUS<7:5>).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why IPEN must be set FIRST:**
> Without IPEN = 1, the IPRx register bits are **completely ignored** by the hardware. The assignment of priorities is a **no-op** in compatibility mode. KTU examiners frequently test this prerequisite relationship.

## 2.2 The Assignment Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The complete process of **assigning an interrupt** follows this ordered logic:

1. **Step 1 — Activate the Priority Engine:**
   Set the **IPEN bit (RCON<7>) = 1** to enable two-level priority.
2. **Step 2 — Assign Priority via IPRx:**
   For each peripheral, write to the corresponding bit in **IPR1, IPR2, or IPR3**:
   - Bit = **1** → High Priority (will use 0x0008 vector)
   - Bit = **0** → Low Priority (will use 0x0018 vector)
3. **Step 3 — Enable the Peripheral Interrupt:**
   Set the corresponding bit in **PIE1, PIE2, or PIE3** to enable that peripheral's interrupt request flag.
4. **Step 4 — Set the Appropriate Global Enable:**
   - To allow **High Priority** interrupts: Set **GIEH (INTCON<7>) = 1**
   - To allow **Low Priority** interrupts: Set **GIEL (INTCON<6>) = 1**
5. **Step 5 — Peripheral Event Occurs:**
   The peripheral sets its **PIR (Peripheral Interrupt Request) flag bit**; the core checks the **IPR bit** to decide which vector to jump to.

## 2.3 Mapping of IPRx Bits to Peripheral Sources

Below is the **canonical PIC18F Interrupt Assignment Map**. KTU examiners expect students to be familiar with at least the first two registers.

### IPR1 — Interrupt Priority Register 1
| Bit Position | Peripheral | Default Priority (if IPEN=1) |
|---|---|---|
| Bit 7 | **CCP1** (Capture/Compare/PWM) | Low |
| Bit 6 | **TMR2** (Timer2 to PR2 match) | Low |
| Bit 5 | **TMR1** (Timer1 overflow) | Low |
| Bit 4 | **RC1** (EUSART Receive) | Low |
| Bit 3 | **TX1** (EUSART Transmit) | Low |
| Bit 2 | **SSP1** (MSSP – I²C/SPI) | Low |
| Bit 1 | **CCP2** | Low |
| Bit 0 | **AD** (ADC conversion complete) | Low |

### IPR2 — Interrupt Priority Register 2
| Bit Position | Peripheral | Default Priority (if IPEN=1) |
|---|---|---|
| Bit 7 | **OSCFail** (Oscillator Failure) | High |
| Bit 6 | **CM2** (Comparator 2) | Low |
| Bit 5 | **CM1** (Comparator 1) | Low |
| Bit 4 | **EE** (EEPROM/Flash write complete) | High |
| Bit 3 | **BCL** (Bus Collision – MSSP) | Low |
| Bit 2 | **LVD** (Low-Voltage Detect) | High |
| Bit 1 | **TMR3** (Timer3 overflow) | Low |
| Bit 0 | **CCP3 / ECCP** | Low |

### IPR3 — Interrupt Priority Register 3
| Bit Position | Peripheral | Default Priority (if IPEN=1) |
|---|---|---|
| Bit 7 | **IRX** (IrDA Receiver) | Low |
| Bit 6 | **WAK** (USB Wake-up) | Low |
| Bit 5 | **ERR** (USB Error) | Low |
| Bit 4 | **USB** (USB Transaction) | Low |
| Bit 3 | **BTLF** (Bus Toggle Error) | Low |
| Bit 2 | **RB Port Change** | High |
| Bit 1 | **CCP5** | Low |
| Bit 0 | **CCP4** | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** On **device reset**, all IPRx bits are cleared to **0** (Low Priority). To make any interrupt high-priority, the user code **must explicitly** write `1` to the corresponding IPRx bit. This is the literal "assignment" action.

## 2.4 The IPL<2:0> Mechanism (STATUS<7:5>)

The **Interrupt Priority Level (IPL) bits** in the **STATUS register** act as a **hardware lockout** mechanism:

- When a **High Priority ISR** is executing, the hardware **automatically** sets IPL = 111 (binary), blocking any nested High-priority interrupt.
- The CPU also **refuses** to enter another High-priority ISR while IPL = 111.
- Low-priority ISRs **can still interrupt** a High-priority ISR? — **NO**. A High-priority ISR is non-preemptible.
- A High-priority ISR **can preempt** a Low-priority ISR — the hardware clears IPL<2:0> when the High ISR completes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Context Saving Rule:**
> - **High-priority ISRs**: Context (WREG, BSR, STATUS) is **automatically saved** on the shadow registers by hardware.
> - **Low-priority ISRs**: **Manual** context saving is required in software by the user.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbolic / Bit-Level Representation | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Priority Enable | $\text{IPEN} = \text{RCON}\langle 7 \rangle$ | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | High Vector Address | $\vec{V}_{H} = 0\text{x}0008$ | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | Low Vector Address | $\vec{V}_{L} = 0\text{x}0018$ | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | Global High Enable | $\text{GIEH} = \text{INTCON}\langle 7 \rangle$ | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | Global Low Enable | $\text{GIEL} = \text{INTCON}\langle 6 \rangle$ | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | Current CPU Priority | $\text{IPL}\langle 2\!:\!0 \rangle = \text{STATUS}\langle 7\!:\!5 \rangle$ | ★★★★ |
| 7 | Assignment Bit Value | $\text{IPR}x\langle n \rangle = 1 \Rightarrow \text{High} \quad \vert \quad = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Low}$ | ★★★★★ |
| 8 | Preemption Condition | $\text{High} \rightarrow \text{Low ISR} = \text{Allowed} \quad \vert \quad \text{Low} \rightarrow \text{High ISR} = \text{Denied}$ | ★★★★ |
| 9 | Nesting Lockout | $\text{IPL} = 111_{b} \Rightarrow \text{No nested High ISR}$ | ★★★ |
| 10 | Context Save | $\text{High ISR} = \text{Hardware} \quad \vert \quad \text{Low ISR} = \text{Software}$ | ★★★★ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (Anti-lock Braking Systems):** Wheel-speed sensor overflow (TMR1) is assigned **High Priority** to immediately trigger ABS action, while OBD-II diagnostic logging is assigned **Low Priority**.
- **Industrial Motor Control:** Overcurrent fault interrupts from comparators are assigned **High Priority**; RPM measurement via CCP is **Low Priority**.
- **Medical Devices (Pacemakers, Insulin Pumps):** Heart-beat/timer-expiry interrupts are **High Priority**, while LCD update tasks are **Low Priority**.
- **Smart Energy Meters:** Power-fail detection (LVD) is **High Priority** to flush EEPROM before brownout, whereas calibration timer ticks are **Low Priority**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Interrupt Assignment

The complete hardware decision tree executed by the PIC18F core upon receiving an interrupt request is laid out below. Each row represents one **decision gate** the hardware walks through before vectoring the CPU to the ISR.

| Gate # | Hardware Check | Condition for PASS | Effect on FAIL | Logical Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Master Oscillator / Reset Complete | $\overline{\text{POR}} = 1$ | CPU in Reset; ignored | $G_1$ |
| 2 | Global Interrupt Enable (GIEH $\vert$ GIEL) | At least one = 1 | No CPU action | $G_2$ |
| 3 | Peripheral Flag Set (PIRx) | $\text{PIR}x\langle n \rangle = 1$ | No request to service | $G_3$ |
| 4 | Peripheral Enable (PIEx) | $\text{PIE}x\langle n \rangle = 1$ | Request not unmasked | $G_4$ |
| 5 | IPEN Bit Status | $\text{IPEN} = 1$ | Falls back to single-vector mode (0x0008) | $G_5$ |
| 6 | IPRx Bit Value | $\text{IPR}x\langle n \rangle \in \{0,1\}$ | Determines target vector | $G_6$ |
| 7 | Current CPU Priority | $\text{IPL}\langle 2\!:\!0 \rangle \le \text{Requested Priority}$ | Request suppressed | $G_7$ |

The **overall acceptance criterion** for a CPU vectoring event is therefore:

$$
A = G_1 \;\land\; G_2 \;\land\; G_3 \;\land\; G_4 \;\land\; G_5 \;\land\; G_7
$$

And the **vector selection rule** $G_6$ is given by:

$$
\vec{V} = \begin{cases} 0\text{x}0008, & \text{if } \text{IPR}x\langle n \rangle = 1 \quad (\text{High Priority}) \\[4pt] 0\text{x}0018, & \text{if } \text{IPR}x\langle n \rangle = 0 \quad (\text{Low Priority}) \end{cases}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation: Why IPL2:0 = 111 During a High ISR

Let us derive the value of the **IPL<2:0>** field during the execution of a High Priority ISR.

**Step 1 — Interrupt request latched:**
When peripheral $k$ fires and $\text{IPR}x\langle k \rangle = 1$, the core checks the current priority level. Define the current level as $L_c = \text{IPL}\langle 2\!:\!0 \rangle$ (an integer from 0 to 7).

$$
L_c = \text{DECODE}(\text{STATUS}\langle 7\!:\!5 \rangle)
$$

**Step 2 — Priority comparison:**
The requested priority is a binary value $L_r$ where:

$$
L_r = \begin{cases} 7, & \text{if High Priority request} \\ 4, & \text{if Low Priority request} \end{cases}
$$

The hardware enters the ISR only if:

$$
L_c < L_r
$$

**Step 3 — High Priority ISR begins:**
At the moment the CPU begins executing the High Priority ISR (jumps to 0x0008), the hardware **automatically forces**:

$$
\text{IPL}\langle 2\!:\!0 \rangle \;\leftarrow\; 111_{b} = 7_{d}
$$

This is so that **no other High Priority interrupt can nest** inside the current High ISR. The STATUS register's R/W nature is preserved, but on entry the bits are loaded with 111.

**Step 4 — RETFIE instruction:**
When the ISR executes the `RETFIE` (Return From Interrupt) instruction, the hardware automatically restores the previous priority by popping the saved value of STATUS, so:

$$
\text{IPL}\langle 2\!:\!0 \rangle \;\leftarrow\; L_{c,\text{pre-ISR}}
$$

**Step 5 — Completion of cycle:**
If the previous context was a Low Priority ISR or main code (where $L_c \le 3$), the CPU resumes at that level. If a new High Priority interrupt was latched in the meantime and $L_c$ has dropped, it will be serviced next.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Insight:** During a High ISR, the encoded integer priority is **7** (binary 111). During a Low ISR, IPL<2:0> holds the value **4** (binary 100) due to the GIEL bit. Main-line code runs with IPL = **0** (binary 000). This explains the strict "High > Low > Main" ordering.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Assignment Scenario

> **Problem:** A PIC18F4550 system must service (i) an **ADC end-of-conversion** interrupt (AD) every 50 µs and (ii) a **Timer1 overflow** interrupt (TMR1) every 65.5 ms. The Timer1 ISR is non-critical (used for a slow LCD refresh), while the ADC ISR controls a closed-loop motor and must preempt the LCD code.
>
> **Required:** Write the C-like pseudo-code to "assign" these two interrupts to their correct priority levels.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Enable priority levels globally:**

$$
\text{RCON}\langle \text{IPEN} \rangle \;\leftarrow\; 1
$$

**Step 2 — Examine the IPR1 bit positions:**
From the IPR1 table: Bit 0 = AD, Bit 5 = TMR1.

**Step 3 — Assign ADC as HIGH priority:**

$$
\text{IPR1}\langle 0 \rangle \;\leftarrow\; 1 \quad (\text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{High})
$$

**Step 4 — Assign TMR1 as LOW priority (or leave as default 0):**

$$
\text{IPR1}\langle 5 \rangle \;\leftarrow\; 0 \quad (\text{TMR1} \rightarrow \text{Low})
$$

**Step 5 — Enable the peripheral interrupts:**

$$
\text{PIE1}\langle 0 \rangle \;\leftarrow\; 1 \quad (\text{Enable ADC}) \qquad
\text{PIE1}\langle 5 \rangle \;\leftarrow\; 1 \quad (\text{Enable TMR1})
$$

**Step 6 — Enable both global flags:**

$$
\text{INTCON}\langle \text{GIEH} \rangle \;\leftarrow\; 1 \qquad
\text{INTCON}\langle \text{GIEL} \rangle \;\leftarrow\; 1
$$

**Resulting pseudo-code in XC8:**

```c
RCONbits.IPEN = 1;        // Enable priority mode
IPR1bits.ADCIP = 1;       // Assign ADC to HIGH priority
IPR1bits.TMR1IP = 0;      // Assign TMR1 to LOW priority
PIE1bits.ADIE = 1;        // Enable ADC interrupt
PIE1bits.TMR1IE = 1;      // Enable TMR1 interrupt
INTCONbits.GIEH = 1;      // Enable HIGH priority globally
INTCONbits.GIEL = 1;      // Enable LOW priority globally
```

**Verification:** When ADC fires, CPU goes to **0x0008** (can preempt TMR1's ISR at 0x0018). When TMR1 fires, CPU goes to **0x0018** (cannot interrupt the ADC ISR).

## 3.4 Common Pitfall Matrix

| # | Mistake | Consequence | Correction |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forgetting to set `IPEN = 1` | Priority assignment is ignored | Always set IPEN first |
| 2 | Setting only GIEH but not GIEL | Low-priority ISRs never fire | Both must be set to enable low-priority |
| 3 | Writing to PIRx flag to "clear" it manually using `= 1` | Actually triggers the interrupt | PIR flags must be cleared with `= 0` |
| 4 | Using `goto` inside ISR | Skips `RETFIE`; CPU locks up | Always use `RETFIE` to exit |
| 5 | Long delays in a High ISR | Misses time-critical events | Keep High ISR short; defer to low-priority |
| 6 | Not saving WREG/BSR/STATUS in Low ISR | Corrupts main context on return | Use `#pragma interruptlow` or manual push/pop |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Interrupt Assignment Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete data flow and decision logic from an **interrupt source** (peripheral) to the **final ISR execution** in the PIC18F core.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Interrupt Event Occurs]) --> flagSet[Set PIRx flag bit n = 1]
    flagSet --> maskCheck{PIEx bit n = 1?}
    maskCheck -->|No| endA([No action])
    maskCheck -->|Yes| ipenCheck{RCON IPEN = 1?}
    ipenCheck -->|No| legacy[Single vector 0x0008 GIE controls all]
    ipenCheck -->|Yes| iprCheck{IPRx bit n value}
    iprCheck -->|1| hiPath[Classify as HIGH priority]
    iprCheck -->|0| loPath[Classify as LOW priority]
    hiPath --> giehChk{INTCON GIEH = 1?}
    loPath --> gielChk{INTCON GIEL = 1?}
    giehChk -->|No| endA
    gielChk -->|No| endA
    giehChk -->|Yes| iplHiCheck{IPL 2 0 less than 7?}
    gielChk -->|Yes| iplLoCheck{IPL 2 0 less than 4?}
    iplHiCheck -->|Yes| vecHi[Jump to vector 0x0008]
    iplHiCheck -->|No| endA
    iplLoCheck -->|Yes| vecLo[Jump to vector 0x0018]
    iplLoCheck -->|No| endA
    vecHi --> shadowSave[Hardware saves WREG BSR STATUS]
    shadowSave --> hiISR[Execute HIGH priority ISR]
    hiISR --> retfieH[RETFIE restores STATUS PC]
    vecLo --> swSave[Software saves WREG BSR STATUS]
    swSave --> loISR[Execute LOW priority ISR]
    loISR --> retfieL[RETFIE restores STATUS PC]
    retfieH --> resumeHi[Resume previous context]
    retfieL --> resumeLo[Resume previous context]
```

## 4.2 Interrupt Priority Register (IPRx) Internal Structure

The diagram below illustrates the **bit-to-peripheral mapping** within a single IPRx register, showing how each bit is the literal "assignment switch" between high and low priority.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bit7A[Bit 7] --> p7[Peripheral 7]
    bit6A[Bit 6] --> p6[Peripheral 6]
    bit5A[Bit 5] --> p5[Peripheral 5]
    bit4A[Bit 4] --> p4[Peripheral 4]
    bit3A[Bit 3] --> p3[Peripheral 3]
    bit2A[Bit 2] --> p2[Peripheral 2]
    bit1A[Bit 1] --> p1[Peripheral 1]
    bit0A[Bit 0] --> p0[Peripheral 0]
    p7 --> dec7{Value}
    p6 --> dec6{Value}
    p5 --> dec5{Value}
    p4 --> dec4{Value}
    p3 --> dec3{Value}
    p2 --> dec2{Value}
    p1 --> dec1{Value}
    p0 --> dec0{Value}
    dec7 -->|1| hiRes7[High Priority]
    dec7 -->|0| loRes7[Low Priority]
    dec0 -->|1| hiRes0[High Priority]
    dec0 -->|0| loRes0[Low Priority]
    hiRes7 --> vecHi2[Vector 0x0008]
    loRes7 --> vecLo2[Vector 0x0018]
    hiRes0 --> vecHi2
    loRes0 --> vecLo2
```

## 4.3 Priority Mode vs. Compatibility Mode — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ResetState
    ResetState --> CompatMode: IPEN = 0
    ResetState --> PriorityMode: IPEN = 1
    CompatMode --> PriorityMode: User sets IPEN = 1
    PriorityMode --> CompatMode: User clears IPEN = 0
    CompatMode --> CompatISR: Only one ISR vector at 0x0008
    PriorityMode --> HighISR: IPRx bit = 1 and GIEH = 1
    PriorityMode --> LowISR: IPRx bit = 0 and GIEL = 1
    HighISR --> PriorityMode: RETFIE
    LowISR --> PriorityMode: RETFIE
    CompatISR --> CompatMode: RETFIE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid Diagrams:**
> - Each box (e.g., `flagSet`, `vecHi`, `loISR`) is a **functional block** in the interrupt pipeline.
> - Diamonds (e.g., `ipenCheck`, `giehChk`) are **decision gates** controlled by specific SFR bits.
> - The two rightmost sinks `vecHi` and `vecLo` are the **two distinct ISR vectors** at 0x0008 and 0x0018 respectively.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is meant by "assigning an interrupt" in the PIC18F microcontroller? Name the registers involved.**

**Model Answer:**
Assigning an interrupt in the PIC18F architecture is the process of configuring each peripheral interrupt source to a **High Priority** or **Low Priority** level. This is achieved by:
1. Setting the **IPEN bit (RCON<7>)** to enable priority levels.
2. Writing to the **Interrupt Priority Registers — IPR1, IPR2, and IPR3** — where a bit value of `1` assigns the peripheral to High Priority and `0` to Low Priority.

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU pattern):**
- Definition of "assignment" — **1 mark**
- IPEN role — **1 mark**
- IPR1/IPR2/IPR3 naming — **1 mark**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between High Priority and Low Priority interrupts in PIC18F.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | High Priority | Low Priority |
|---|---|---|
| Vector Address | **0x0008** | **0x0018** |
| Global Enable Bit | GIEH (INTCON<7>) | GIEL (INTCON<6>) |
| Can Preempt Low ISR? | **Yes** | No |
| Context Saving | **Automatic** (hardware) | **Manual** (software) |
| Default on Reset | None — user must set | All (IPRx cleared to 0) |

**Marks Distribution:**
- Vector addresses — **1 mark**
- Preemption + Context saving — **2 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has **internal choice**. Two alternatives are provided below — answer **either** Option A **or** Option B.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain the PIC18F interrupt priority scheme. How are interrupts assigned to high or low priority? Discuss the role of IPEN, IPRx, GIEH, and GIEL with relevant register bits. (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the PIC18F Interrupt Priority Scheme (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The PIC18F core supports a **two-level prioritized interrupt architecture** when the **IPEN bit (RCON<7>)** is set to 1. This produces **two distinct vector locations** in program memory:

- **High Priority Vector → 0x0008**
- **Low Priority Vector → 0x0018**

**Key features:**
- A High Priority ISR can **interrupt (preempt)** an ongoing Low Priority ISR.
- A Low Priority ISR **cannot** interrupt a High Priority ISR.
- The CPU's current priority is hardware-tracked in **STATUS<7:5> = IPL<2:0>**.

**Hardware Block Diagram (Descriptive):**

```
[Peripheral Event] → [Set PIRx flag] → [Mask with PIEx] → [Check IPEN]
                                                          ↓
                                          ┌───────────────┴────────────────┐
                                          |                                |
                                  IPEN = 0                          IPEN = 1
                                  Single Mode                  Check IPRx bit
                                          |                                |
                                  Vector 0x0008                  ┌────────┴────────┐
                                  (GIE global)               IPR=1            IPR=0
                                                              |                |
                                                         Vector 0x0008   Vector 0x0018
                                                         (GIEH enable)   (GIEL enable)
```

**Marks:** Diagram 3 + Features 4 = **7 marks**

#### (b) Assignment Procedure & Role of Key Bits (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

To "assign" an interrupt to a priority level, the following five-step procedure must be executed:

1. **Step 1 — Enable priority mode:**
   Set `RCONbits.IPEN = 1` (IPEN bit in RCON register, bit 7).

2. **Step 2 — Assign priority via IPRx:**
   For peripheral $n$ in bank $x$:
   - `IPRxbits.n = 1` → **High Priority** (uses vector 0x0008)
   - `IPRxbits.n = 0` → **Low Priority** (uses vector 0x0018)

3. **Step 3 — Enable peripheral interrupt:**
   Set `PIExbits.n = 1`.

4. **Step 4 — Enable global flags:**
   - `INTCONbits.GIEH = 1` (enables High Priority interrupts)
   - `INTCONbits.GIEL = 1` (enables Low Priority interrupts)

5. **Step 5 — Context save protocol:**
   - High ISR: **Hardware auto-save** of WREG, BSR, STATUS.
   - Low ISR: **Software save** required.

**Example Pseudo-Code:**

```c
RCONbits.IPEN  = 1;        // Activate priority engine
IPR1bits.ADIP  = 1;        // Assign ADC interrupt to HIGH
IPR1bits.TMR1IP = 0;       // Assign TMR1 interrupt to LOW
PIE1bits.ADIE  = 1;        // Unmask ADC
PIE1bits.TMR1IE = 1;       // Unmask TMR1
INTCONbits.GIEH = 1;       // Enable High globally
INTCONbits.GIEL = 1;       // Enable Low globally
```

**Marks:** Step-by-step procedure 4 + Code example 2 + Role clarification 1 = **7 marks**

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Draw the block diagram of the PIC18F interrupt controller and explain the function of the IPEN, GIEH, GIEL, and IPL<2:0> bits. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Describe the context saving mechanism for High Priority and Low Priority ISRs. Why is software saving mandatory for Low Priority ISRs? (7 Marks)**

#### (a) Block Diagram & Function of Control Bits (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (Schematic Representation):**

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|                  PIC18F Interrupt Controller             |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                          |
|   [RCON.IPEN] --+--> Mode Select                         |
|                 |     |                                   |
|                 |     +-- IPEN=0 --> Single Mode (0x0008) |
|                 |     +-- IPEN=1 --> Priority Mode        |
|                 |                                         |
|   [INTCON.GIEH] ----> Enable High Priority Globally      |
|   [INTCON.GIEL] ----> Enable Low Priority Globally       |
|                                                          |
|   [IPR1, IPR2, IPR3] --> Assign Each Peripheral          |
|        |                       (1=High, 0=Low)            |
|        v                                                 |
|   [Per-Source Routing]                                   |
|        |                                                 |
|        +-- High --> Vector 0x0008                        |
|        +-- Low  --> Vector 0x0018                        |
|                                                          |
|   [STATUS.IPL<2:0>] <--- Current CPU Priority Level      |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Bit-by-Bit Function:**

- **IPEN (RCON<7>):** Master switch. When `1`, two-level priority is active. When `0`, the controller reverts to PIC16F-style single-vector mode.
- **GIEH (INTCON<7>):** Acts as the **Global Enable for High Priority** interrupts. Must be `1` for any High ISR to be recognized.
- **GIEL (INTCON<6>):** Acts as the **Global Enable for Low Priority** interrupts. Must be `1` for any Low ISR to be recognized.
- **IPL<2:0> (STATUS<7:5>):** A **read-only** field that reflects the current CPU priority level. Values range from 0 (main code) to 7 (active High ISR). Used by hardware to **lockout nested interrupts** of equal or lower priority.

**Marks:** Diagram 3 + Bit functions 4 = **7 marks**

#### (b) Context Saving Mechanism (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**High Priority ISR — Hardware Context Save:**
- When a High Priority interrupt is accepted, the PIC18F hardware **automatically** pushes **WREG, BSR, and STATUS** into dedicated **shadow registers** (non-memory-mapped internal registers).
- The user ISR body is therefore free to use WREG, BSR, and STATUS without any prologue.
- On `RETFIE`, hardware **automatically restores** the saved values in a single cycle.

**Low Priority ISR — Software Context Save:**
- The PIC18F hardware does **NOT** save context for Low Priority ISRs.
- This is because the hardware has only **one set of shadow registers**, reserved exclusively for the High Priority path (to ensure minimum latency for critical events).
- The user **must manually** save WREG, BSR, and STATUS at the entry of every Low ISR:

```c
void interrupt_low_priority low_isr(void) {
    // Manual context save
    unsigned char saved_WREG = WREG;
    unsigned char saved_BSR  = BSR;
    unsigned char saved_STATUS = STATUS;

    // ... ISR body ...

    // Manual context restore
    STATUS = saved_STATUS;
    BSR    = saved_BSR;
    WREG   = saved_WREG;
}
```

**Why is Software Save Mandatory for Low ISR?**
- The hardware's single set of shadow registers is "owned" by the High Priority path.
- If a Low ISR executes and a High ISR fires mid-way, the High ISR would overwrite the shadow registers, **corrupting** the Low ISR's return context.
- Therefore, to keep the shadow registers free for High ISR preemption, Low ISRs must use the **main memory stack** for context storage, which the **software must manage explicitly**.

**Marks:** Hardware mechanism 2 + Software mechanism 2 + Reason for mandatory software save 3 = **7 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes — KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern:**
> 1. **Forgetting IPEN = 1:** Writing IPRx assignments without first setting IPEN. Examiner deducts **2 marks** for this omission because the entire assignment becomes a no-op.
> 2. **Wrong vector address:** Mixing up 0x0008 and 0x0018. Examiner deducts **1–2 marks** per instance.
> 3. **Confusing GIE/GIEH/GIEL:** Using the old PIC16F GIE bit (which becomes GIEH in PIC18F) and missing the GIEL bit. Examiner deducts **1 mark** for each confusion.
> 4. **Not drawing the block diagram:** A neat block diagram of the interrupt controller is worth **3 marks** in a 14-mark question. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss.
> 5. **Missing context save explanation:** Many students write the ISR code but forget to mention **who** saves the context (hardware vs. software). Examiner allocates **2–3 marks** for this distinction alone.
> 6. **Single combined answer for Part B (a) and (b):** Not separating the two sub-parts (a) and (b) clearly leads to examiner confusion and partial mark cuts. Always use clear headings.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 / "Assigning Interrupts"**

- ⭐ **"Assigning" = writing to IPRx bits** after IPEN is set.
- ⭐ **IPEN = RCON<7>**; must be `1` for priority mode to function.
- ⭐ **High Vector = 0x0008; Low Vector = 0x0018** (memorize both).
- ⭐ **GIEH (INTCON<7>) → enables High Priority; GIEL (INTCON<6>) → enables Low Priority.**
- ⭐ **IPRx bit = 1 → High; IPRx bit = 0 → Low** (default after reset is Low for all).
- ⭐ **IPL<2:0> (STATUS<7:5>)** reflects current CPU priority (0 = main, 4 = Low ISR, 7 = High ISR).
- ⭐ **High ISR auto-saves context; Low ISR requires software context save.**
- ⭐ **High can preempt Low; Low cannot preempt High.**
- ⭐ **Three priority registers: IPR1, IPR2, IPR3 — each 8 bits wide.**
- ⭐ **PIE registers ENABLE the interrupt; PIR registers FLAG the interrupt; IPR registers PRIORITIZE the interrupt.**
- ⭐ **Without IPEN, the PIC18F behaves like a PIC16F with one vector at 0x0004 / 0x0008.**
- ⭐ **RETFIE instruction is mandatory to exit any ISR.**
- ⭐ **KTU most-asked sub-topic:** "Differentiate between High and Low Priority ISRs" — appears almost every semester.
- ⭐ **Engineering application:** Use High Priority for safety-critical, time-bound events; Low Priority for background, slow, or cosmetic tasks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupt Latency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Interrupt Latency # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interrupt Latency** is formally defined as the **time interval measured from the instant an external interrupt request (IRQ) signal arrives at the microcontroller's interrupt input pin (or is generated internally by a peripheral) until the very first instruction of the corresponding Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) begins execution on the CPU core.** In formal real-time systems literature aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome (CO4)** on real-time performance benchmarking, interrupt latency is expressed mathematically as: $$t_{latency} = t_{ISR\_start} - t_{IRQ\_arrival}$$ Where: - **$t_{IRQ\_arrival}$** is the timestamp when the interrupt event physically manifests at the hardware boundary. - **$t_{ISR\_start}$** is the timestamp when the CPU's **Program Counter (PC)** is loaded with the address of the first instruction of the ISR (the interrupt vector fetch complete cycle). The standard unit of measurement is **microseconds (µs)** for most modern 32-bit microcontrollers, although high-speed ARM Cortex-M cores may exhibit latencies in **nanoseconds (ns)**. ### 1.1.1 Sub-Categories of Interrupt Latency > [!NOTE] > KTU Examiners frequently test the distinction between these three latency types. Memorize the boxed terms. 1. **Worst-Case Interrupt Latency (WCIL)** — The *maximum possible delay* under any operational condition, including pipeline flush, wait states, higher-priority interrupts in service, and memory access delays. This is the **critical parameter for hard real-time systems**. 2. **Best-Case Interrupt Latency (BCIL)** — The *minimum possible delay*, occurring when the core is idle, the pipeline is flush, no higher-priority interrupts are active, and flash memory has zero wait states. 3. **Average Interrupt Latency (AIL)** — The *statistical mean* of latency measured over a large number of interrupt events under typical operating conditions. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you walk into a hospital Emergency Room (ER) with a critical injury. | Hospital ER Element | Microcontroller Equivalent | |---|---| | Patient (you) arriving at ER reception | External Interrupt Request (IRQ) signal arriving at the MCU pin | | Reception desk verifying your case file | Interrupt controller (e.g., ARM NVIC) recognizing and prioritizing the IRQ | | Doctor finishing with the previous patient | CPU completing the current instruction (pipeline drain) | | Doctor picking up your chart and walking to you | Vector fetch — PC loaded with ISR address from the vector table | | Doctor begins treatment (first injection/stitch) | **First instruction of the ISR executes** | The **Interrupt Latency** is precisely the **total time elapsed from when you entered the ER doors until the doctor actually begins treating you**. Just as a hospital must guarantee a maximum waiting time (WCIL) for critical patients (stroke, cardiac arrest), a hard real-time embedded system **must guarantee a deterministic maximum interrupt latency**, or the system fails its safety contract. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold) > [!NOTE] > **Key Benchmark Values for ARM Cortex-M4 (STM32F407 @ 168 MHz):** > - **Zero-latency ISR entry** is achievable when the core is not already servicing a higher-priority interrupt. > **Typical WCIL = 12 clock cycles ≈ 71.4 ns @ 168 MHz.** > - For 8051 (legacy 8-bit), typical WCIL is **3 µs to 7 µs** depending on instruction in progress. > - For ARM7TDMI (older architecture), WCIL is approximately **20 to 30 clock cycles** due to explicit pipeline refill and ARM/Thumb mode switching. > - For PIC18F (8-bit Microchip), WCIL is typically **2 to 4 instruction cycles** plus vector fetch latency. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Anatomy of an Interrupt — Timeline Breakdown To rigorously derive interrupt latency, we must dissect the *entire* interrupt handling chain into discrete atomic events. Each event contributes an additive delay component. ### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Chronological Analysis **Step 1 — Event Detection (Hardware Latency)** The peripheral generating the interrupt (e.g., a GPIO edge detector, Timer overflow, ADC End-of-Conversion) sets its interrupt flag. The flag is routed to the interrupt controller. - For external interrupts, there is an additional **synchronizer delay** of 1–2 clock cycles to avoid metastability from asynchronous external signals. - For internal peripherals, propagation is usually **1 clock cycle**. **Step 2 — Interrupt Controller Arbitration** The Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC) evaluates the new IRQ's priority against the currently active priority mask (BASEPRI / PRIMASK registers in ARM Cortex-M). - If the new IRQ has *higher or equal priority* and preemption is allowed, the NVIC asserts a **pending request to the CPU**. - Decision latency: **2 clock cycles** in ARM Cortex-M3/M4/M7. **Step 3 — Instruction Completion (Pipeline Drain)** The CPU **cannot abandon** the currently executing instruction mid-way. It must wait for the instruction to complete (including any memory access wait states). - Worst-case instruction: a 32-bit multiply or division on the Cortex-M takes **1 clock cycle**, but a flash memory read with **5 wait states** takes 6 cycles. - This is the **largest variable contributor** to interrupt latency. **Step 4 — Vector Fetch (ISR Address Acquisition)** The CPU reads the ISR entry address from the **Vector Table** located at address `0x00000000` (or remapped to `0x08000000` in flash for STM32). - Vector table entries are **32-bit addresses** stored contiguously. - Flash memory access typically requires 1–5 wait states. - On Cortex-M, vector fetch takes **2 bus cycles** (with zero wait states). **Step 5 — Stack Push (Context Save)** The hardware automatically pushes key registers onto the stack: - **Cortex-M3/M4**: Pushes **R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, PSR** (8 registers = 32 bytes). - Stack push takes **2 bus cycles** for aligned 32-bit pushes. **Step 6 — PC Update & ISR Entry** The PC is updated with the vector address. The **first instruction of the ISR now executes** — this marks the **end of latency measurement**. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) derivations.** | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value (Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz) | Engineering Context | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $t_{latency} = t_{synch} + t_{arb} + t_{inst} + t_{fetch} + t_{stack}$ | Total interrupt latency | 12 cycles ≈ 71.4 ns | Universal decomposition | | 2 | $t_{synch} = N_{synch} \cdot T_{clk}$ | Synchronizer delay ($N_{synch} \in \{1, 2\}$) | 2 cycles ≈ 11.9 ns | External IRQ only | | 3 | $t_{arb} = 2 \cdot T_{clk}$ | NVIC arbitration | 2 cycles ≈ 11.9 ns | Cortex-M3/M4 only | | 4 | $t_{inst} = (C_{inst} + W) \cdot T_{clk}$ | Instruction completion (W = wait states) | 1–10 cycles | Variable, dominant term | | 5 | $t_{fetch} = 2 \cdot T_{clk}$ | Vector table read | 2 cycles ≈ 11.9 ns | Constant on Cortex-M | | 6 | $t_{stack} = 2 \cdot N_{regs} \cdot T_{clk}$ | Hardware stack push (8 regs on Cortex-M4) | 4 cycles ≈ 23.8 ns | Architecture-dependent | | 7 | $f_{clk} = 1 / T_{clk}$ | CPU clock frequency | 168 MHz → $T_{clk} = 5.95$ ns | STM32F4 series | | 8 | $t_{BCIL} = t_{latency,min}$ | Best-case (idle core, 0 WS flash) | 12 cycles = **71.4 ns** | Benchmark floor | | 9 | $t_{WCIL} = t_{latency,max}$ | Worst-case (max WS, busy core, low priority) | ~50 cycles = **297.5 ns** | Hard real-time guarantee | | 10 | $t_{AIL} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{latency,i}$ | Statistical mean latency | ~20 cycles = **119 ns** | Empirical measurement | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The discipline of interrupt latency analysis underpins **certifiable safety-critical systems** governed by international standards: - **ISO 26262** (Automotive Functional Safety) — mandates documented WCIL for every ASIL-rated ECU. - **DO-178C** (Aviation Software) — requires deterministic interrupt response in flight control systems. - **IEC 61508** (Industrial Process Control) — SIL-3 and SIL-4 systems cannot tolerate unbounded latency. - **IEC 62304** (Medical Device Software) — Class C devices (pacemakers, infusion pumps) must guarantee WCIL bounds. **Industrial Application Examples:** 1. **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) ECU** — A wheel speed sensor interrupt must be serviced within **100 µs** to compute slip ratio and actuate valves; otherwise, wheel lock occurs. 2. **UAV Flight Controller (Pixhawk / ArduPilot)** — IMU sensor fusion interrupts must execute within **1 ms** to maintain stable PID loops at 1 kHz. 3. **Pacemaker** — A cardiac arrhythmia detection interrupt must trigger pacing within **10 ms**. 4. **Industrial CNC Machine** — Encoder quadrature interrupts determine spindle position accuracy; sub-microsecond latency is critical for closed-loop machining. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Worst-Case Interrupt Latency for Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz **Given Data:** - CPU Clock Frequency: **$f_{clk} = 168$ MHz** - Flash wait states: **$W = 5$** (full speed @ 168 MHz, 3.3V) - Instruction in progress: **32-bit multiply** ($C_{inst} = 1$ cycle core, but instruction fetch from flash = 5 WS) ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1:** Compute the fundamental clock period. $$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}$$ $$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{168 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$T_{clk} = 5.952 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \approx 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 2:** Compute synchronizer latency (assume external IRQ pin with 2-stage synchronizer). $$t_{synch} = N_{synch} \cdot T_{clk}$$ $$t_{synch} = 2 \cdot 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{synch} = 11.904 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 3:** Compute NVIC arbitration latency. $$t_{arb} = 2 \cdot T_{clk}$$ $$t_{arb} = 2 \cdot 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{arb} = 11.904 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 4:** Compute worst-case instruction completion latency. The currently executing instruction must finish. For a multiply that must also fetch operands from flash with 5 wait states, the effective cycles include the core cycle plus memory wait states. $$t_{inst} = (C_{inst} + W_{operand}) \cdot T_{clk}$$ $$t_{inst} = (1 + 5) \cdot 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{inst} = 6 \cdot 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{inst} = 35.714 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 5:** Compute vector fetch latency. The vector table entry (32-bit ISR address) is read from flash. With 5 wait states, this becomes: $$t_{fetch} = (1 + W) \cdot T_{clk}$$ $$t_{fetch} = (1 + 5) \cdot 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{fetch} = 6 \cdot 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{fetch} = 35.714 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 6:** Compute automatic stack push latency. Cortex-M4 pushes 8 registers × 32 bits = 32 bytes. With flash-based stack (worst case, though stack is usually in SRAM), but assuming SRAM (0 wait states): $$t_{stack} = 2 \cdot N_{regs} \cdot T_{clk}$$ $$t_{stack} = 2 \cdot 8 \cdot 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{stack} = 16 \cdot 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{stack} = 95.238 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 7:** Total Worst-Case Interrupt Latency (WCIL). $$t_{WCIL} = t_{synch} + t_{arb} + t_{inst} + t_{fetch} + t_{stack}$$ $$t_{WCIL} = 11.904 + 11.904 + 35.714 + 35.714 + 95.238$$ $$t_{WCIL} = 190.474 \text{ ns} \approx 190.5 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 8:** Convert to clock cycles. $$\text{Cycles}_{WCIL} = \frac{t_{WCIL}}{T_{clk}} = \frac{190.5}{5.952}$$ $$\text{Cycles}_{WCIL} \approx 32 \text{ cycles}$$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **stack pushing dominates** (50% of WCIL). The ISR's prologue — typically `push {r4-r11, lr}` — adds *additional* cycles beyond the hardware's automatic push.
