An ISR with extensive register saving can easily push WCIL above 100 cycles. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Comparing Best-Case vs Worst-Case for ARM7TDMI (Legacy Architecture) **Why we study this:** KTU examiners often pose comparative questions between **legacy ARM** and **modern Cortex-M** architectures. **ARM7TDMI Operating Conditions:** - Clock: **60 MHz** → $T_{clk} = 16.67$ ns - Architecture: Von Neumann, 3-stage pipeline (Fetch / Decode / Execute) - Mode switching required: ARM ↔ Thumb may add cycles **Step 1:** Best-case latency when no mode switch and pipeline is flush: $$t_{BCIL} = 7 \cdot T_{clk} = 7 \cdot 16.67 = 116.7 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 2:** Worst-case includes pipeline flush (3 stages) + Thumb-to-ARM switch + vector fetch from slow memory: $$t_{WCIL} = (3_{pipe} + 5_{mode} + 20_{fetch}) \cdot T_{clk}$$ $$t_{WCIL} = 28 \cdot 16.67 = 466.76 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 3:** Comparative ratio: $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{t_{WCIL, ARM7}}{t_{WCIL, CortexM4}} = \frac{466.76}{190.5} = 2.45$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The Cortex-M architecture reduces WCIL by approximately **2.45×** compared to the legacy ARM7TDMI due to the integrated NVIC, hardware stack frame, and absence of explicit mode-switching penalties. This is a **guaranteed KTU-style comparison question**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Latency Sources Across Microcontroller Families | Latency Source | 8051 (12 MHz) | PIC18F (64 MHz) | ARM7TDMI (60 MHz) | Cortex-M4 (168 MHz) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Sync / Detection | 2 µs | 31.25 ns | 16.67 ns | 11.9 ns | | Arbitration | Not applicable (priority encoder, ~1 cycle) | 15.6 ns | 16.67 ns | 11.9 ns | | Instruction Completion | up to 4 µs (MUL/DIV) | 62.5 ns | up to 5 cycles = 83.4 ns | 35.7 ns | | Vector Fetch | 4 µs (int # jump) | 125 ns | 83.4 ns | 35.7 ns | | Stack Push | Software (ISR prologue) | Software | Software / HW partial | 95.2 ns (HW) | | **Typical WCIL** | **5–10 µs** | **500 ns – 1 µs** | **~470 ns** | **~190 ns** | | **Best-Case Latency** | **3 µs** | **125 ns** | **117 ns** | **71 ns** | --- ## 3.4 Mathematical Proof of Pipeline Drain Penalty Consider a generic RISC pipeline with $k$ stages. An interrupt arriving during the *Execute* stage of stage $k$ requires all subsequent stages to flush before vector fetch can begin. $$t_{pipe\_drain} = (k - i) \cdot T_{clk}$$ Where $i$ is the stage index where the interrupt was detected (1 ≤ $i$ ≤ $k$). For $i = 1$ (interrupt detected in Fetch stage): $$t_{pipe\_drain, min} = (k - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$$ For $i = k$ (interrupt detected in Execute stage — the latest possible moment): $$t_{pipe\_drain, max} = 0$$ **Key Engineering Rule:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Maximum possible pipeline drain delay = $(k-1) \cdot T_{clk}$**. For a 5-stage pipeline at 168 MHz, this is $4 \cdot 5.952$ ns = **23.8 ns** of jitter purely from pipeline drain. This is **unavoidable** in any pipelined architecture. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Interrupt Latency Timeline The following diagram renders the **chronological flow of an interrupt event** and identifies each latency contributor along the timeline. ```mermaid flowchart LR eventA["External Event

Mention the difference between worst-case and best-case interrupt latency.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Interrupt latency is the time interval between the arrival of an interrupt request signal at the microcontroller and the start of execution of the first instruction of the corresponding Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). It is formally expressed as: $$t_{latency} = t_{ISR\_start} - t_{IRQ\_arrival}$$ **Difference (1.5 marks):** | Parameter | Best-Case Latency (BCIL) | Worst-Case Latency (WCIL) | |---|---|---| | Definition | Minimum possible latency under ideal conditions | Maximum possible latency under all conditions | | Condition | CPU idle, 0 wait states, high priority, pipeline flush | CPU busy, max wait states, low priority, deep pipeline | | Typical (Cortex-M4 @168 MHz) | ~12 cycles = 71.4 ns | ~32 cycles = 190.5 ns | | Use | Benchmark floor for hardware capability | Real-time guarantee for safety-critical design | --- ### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Q: List any four factors that affect interrupt latency in an ARM Cortex-M4 based system.** > **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 marks each):** 1. **Flash memory wait states** — Higher wait states increase the time to fetch both the vector table entry and the first ISR instructions. 2. **CPU clock frequency** — Higher clock frequency directly reduces all latency components since $T_{clk} = 1/f_{clk}$ scales all terms. 3. **Number of registers saved in the ISR prologue** — More `push` instructions in the ISR prologue extend the time before the *useful* ISR work begins. 4. **NVIC priority settings (BASEPRI / PRIMASK)** — Lower priority IRQs wait longer if higher priority interrupts are being serviced; aggressive `__disable_irq()` critical sections can cause indefinite blocking. *(Alternative valid factors: pipeline depth, vector table location in SRAM vs Flash, cache presence, DMA bus conflicts.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 ### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** **Question:** With a neat timing diagram, explain the concept of interrupt latency in an ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller. Derive the expression for worst-case interrupt latency and compute its value for a system running at 168 MHz with 5 flash wait states. List any four techniques to reduce interrupt latency. #### Part (a) — Concept & Timing Diagram [7 marks] > **Model Answer:** **Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):** Interrupt latency is the delay between the assertion of an interrupt request (IRQ) and the execution of the first instruction of the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). In the ARM Cortex-M4 architecture, interrupt handling is managed by the **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)**, which performs priority arbitration, vector fetch, and automatic hardware stack pushing without software intervention. **Timing Diagram (2 marks):** ``` | | | | | | IRQ|__________| | | | | \ | | | | \____________| | | | t_synch t_arb t_inst t_fetch t_stack t_user 2 cyc 2 cyc 6 cyc 6 cyc 16 cyc (ISR) ←────────── t_WCIL = 32 cycles ──────→ ``` **Description (2 marks):** The five latency contributors are: 1. **$t_{synch}$** — Peripheral synchronization (2 cycles) 2. **$t_{arb}$** — NVIC priority arbitration (2 cycles) 3. **$t_{inst}$** — Pipeline drain / current instruction completion (variable, 1+ cycles) 4. **$t_{fetch}$** — Vector table read (2 cycles with 0 WS, 6 cycles with 5 WS) 5. **$t_{stack}$** — Hardware push of R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR (16 cycles for 8 registers) --- #### Part (b) — Derivation, Computation & Reduction Techniques [7 marks] > **Model Answer:** **Derivation (3 marks):** $$t_{WCIL} = t_{synch} + t_{arb} + t_{inst,max} + t_{fetch,max} + t_{stack,max}$$ $$t_{WCIL} = N_{synch} \cdot T_{clk} + 2 \cdot T_{clk} + (C_{inst,max} + W) \cdot T_{clk} + (1 + W) \cdot T_{clk} + 2 N_{regs} \cdot T_{clk}$$ **Numerical Computation (2 marks):** Given $f_{clk} = 168$ MHz → $T_{clk} = 5.952$ ns, $W = 5$, $N_{regs} = 8$: $$t_{WCIL} = [2 + 2 + (1+5) + (1+5) + 2(8)] \cdot 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{WCIL} = [2 + 2 + 6 + 6 + 16] \cdot 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{WCIL} = 32 \cdot 5.952 \text{ ns}$$ $$\boxed{t_{WCIL} \approx 190.5 \text{ ns}}$$ **Reduction Techniques (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Place the vector table in SRAM** (set `VTOR` to a SRAM address) — eliminates flash wait states during vector fetch. 2. **Use the instruction cache (ICACHE)** on Cortex-M7 — turns multi-cycle flash fetches into single-cycle cache hits. 3. **Use `__attribute__((naked))` ISRs** — eliminates compiler-generated prologue/epilogue, saving 10–20 cycles. 4. **Minimize critical sections** — `__disable_irq()` blocks *all* interrupts indefinitely; use `BASEPRI` to mask only low-priority interrupts. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 ### **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]** **Question:** Compare the interrupt latency characteristics of three architectures: 8051, PIC18F, and ARM Cortex-M4. For an automotive ABS sensor application requiring WCIL ≤ 5 µs, determine which architecture is most suitable and justify with calculations. Discuss the role of the NVIC in minimizing interrupt latency on Cortex-M4. #### Part (a) — Comparative Analysis [7 marks] > **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Parameter | 8051 (12 MHz) | PIC18F (64 MHz) | ARM Cortex-M4 (168 MHz) | |---|---|---|---| | $T_{clk}$ | 83.33 ns | 15.625 ns | 5.952 ns | | Best-Case Latency | ~3 µs | ~125 ns | ~71.4 ns | | Worst-Case Latency | 5–10 µs | 0.5–1 µs | ~190.5 ns | | Interrupt Controller | Polled / software-driven | Hardware priority encoder | **NVIC (integrated)** | | Vector Table | Fixed at 0x0003 / 0x000B | Fixed at 0x0008 / 0x0018 | **Relocatable (VTOR)** | | Stack Save | Software | Software | **Automatic hardware** | | Determinism | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** | **Conclusion for ABS Application (2 marks):** The **ARM Cortex-M4** is the most suitable choice because its worst-case latency (~190.5 ns) is approximately **26× faster** than the 8051's WCIL and well below the 5 µs safety threshold required for ABS sensors. The 8051's WCIL of 5–10 µs is borderline unsafe; any cache miss or higher-priority interrupt can breach the deadline. The PIC18F is acceptable but offers only a 5–10× margin against the deadline, whereas Cortex-M4 offers a 26× margin. --- #### Part (b) — Role of NVIC & Calculation [7 marks] > **Model Answer:** **Role of NVIC (4 marks):** The **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** is a hardware unit tightly integrated with the Cortex-M4 core that minimizes interrupt latency through five architectural innovations: 1. **Hardware Vector Fetch (No Software Jumps):** The NVIC directly reads the 32-bit ISR address from the vector table in a single bus cycle — eliminating the multi-cycle "jump table" mechanism used by 8051. 2. **Automatic Stack Frame Push:** On exception entry, the NVIC *automatically* pushes 8 registers (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) onto the stack in hardware — this saves 10–20 instruction cycles compared to software prologue. 3. **Preemption by Hardware Priority Comparison:** The NVIC continuously compares the incoming IRQ priority with the active interrupt's priority. Higher-priority interrupts preempt in hardware with no software arbitration code — reducing priority inversion. 4. **Tail-Chaining Optimization:** When one ISR completes and another is pending, the NVIC skips the full stack pop/push cycle and chains directly to the next ISR — saving **6 cycles per back-to-back interrupt**. 5. **Late-Arriving Interrupt Optimization:** If a higher-priority IRQ arrives during the stack-push phase of a lower-priority ISR, the NVIC services the higher-priority one immediately without re-pushing the stack. **Calculation for ABS (3 marks):** **Given:** Required WCIL ≤ 5 µs = 5000 ns. **Check 8051:** $t_{WCIL} = 10 \text{ µs} = 10000 \text{ ns} > 5000 \text{ ns}$ ❌ **Fails the deadline.** **Check PIC18F:** $t_{WCIL} = 1 \text{ µs} = 1000 \text{ ns} < 5000 \text{ ns}$ ✅ Passes with 5× margin. **Check Cortex-M4:** $t_{WCIL} = 190.5 \text{ ns} < 5000 \text{ ns}$ ✅ Passes with **26× margin**. **Safety Margin Formula:** $$M_{safety} = \frac{t_{deadline}}{t_{WCIL}} = \frac{5000}{190.5} \approx 26.25$$ A safety margin of ≥ 10 is recommended for ISO 26262 ASIL-D systems. Cortex-M4 provides this with headroom for further degradation (temperature, voltage, aging). --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deduction Traps in Interrupt Latency Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert clock frequency to clock period** — Writing $T_{clk} = 168$ MHz instead of $T_{clk} = 1/168$ MHz = 5.952 ns will mark the entire numerical answer **wrong**. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly. > > 2. **Confusing Interrupt Latency with Interrupt Response Time** — *Latency* is hardware-only (until first ISR instruction). *Response Time* includes the *full ISR execution* until the system reacts. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this interchange. > > 3. **Omitting the wait state term in $t_{fetch}$** — Students often compute vector fetch as 2 cycles regardless of flash WS. With 5 WS, it is 6 cycles. Failing to include $W$ loses 1 mark. > > 4. **Stating "ARM has zero interrupt latency"** — No architecture has zero latency. The minimum is the pipeline drain + vector fetch + stack push = ~12 cycles on Cortex-M4. > > 5. **Forgetting the hardware stack push** — On Cortex-M, the stack push happens in *hardware*. Students who omit $t_{stack}$ from the WCIL formula undercount by ~50%. --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Interrupt Latency:** - **Definition:** $t_{latency} = t_{ISR\_start} - t_{IRQ\_arrival}$. Measure *until the first ISR instruction*, not until ISR completion. - **Three Types:** BCIL (best-case, ideal), AIL (statistical average), **WCIL (worst-case, used for real-time guarantees)** — WCIL is the most important for hard real-time. - **Five Decomposable Components:** Synchronizer ($t_{synch}$) + Arbiter ($t_{arb}$) + Instruction Completion ($t_{inst}$) + Vector Fetch ($t_{fetch}$) + Stack Push ($t_{stack}$). - **Cortex-M4 NVIC Magic Numbers:** Arbitration = **2 cycles**, Stack push = **8 registers in 2 cycles each = 16 cycles**, Vector fetch = **2 cycles (0 WS) or up to 6 cycles (5 WS)**. - **Key Formula:** $$t_{WCIL} = [N_{synch} + 2 + (C_{inst} + W) + (1 + W) + 2 N_{regs}] \cdot T_{clk}$$ - **Latency Scaling Rule:** Interrupt latency scales **inversely with clock frequency** and **linearly with flash wait states**. - **Architecture Comparison Heuristic:** 8051 ≫ PIC18F > ARM7TDMI > Cortex-M4 (in WCIL, higher is worse). - **Five NVIC Optimizations:** Vectored fetch, HW stack push, preemption, **tail-chaining**, late-arriving interrupt handling. - **Four Reduction Techniques:** (1) Vector table in SRAM, (2) ICACHE for ISR code, (3) Naked ISR attribute, (4) BASEPRI instead of `__disable_irq()`. - **Real-Time Standards:** ISO 26262 (automotive), DO-178C (aviation), IEC 61508 (industrial), IEC 62304 (medical) — all mandate WCIL documentation. - **Common Pitfall — Pipeline Drain:** Maximum pipeline drain penalty = $(k-1) \cdot T_{clk}$ where $k$ is the number of pipeline stages. **Unavoidable jitter** in any pipelined CPU. - **The "26× Safety Margin Rule of Thumb":** For ASIL-D systems, the WCIL should be at least **10× lower** than the system deadline. The Cortex-M4 ABS example delivers 26× margin, comfortably above this threshold. - **"First Useful Instruction" Distinction:** Hardware stack push ends at $t_{stack}$, but the ISR's *useful work* only begins after the software prologue (`push {r4-r11, lr}`) completes — an additional **5–15 cycles** typically. - **Master Equation for Exam:** Always show $T_{clk} = 1/f_{clk}$ conversion explicitly; examiners allocate 0.5 marks just for this step.

topics.title: IRQ and FIQ Exceptions with Example Code for Enabling and Disabling IRQ and FIQ Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IRQ and FIQ Exceptions with Example Code for Enabling and Disabling IRQ and FIQ Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — IRQ and FIQ Exceptions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Canonical):**
> In the ARM7TDMI / LPC2148 architecture (the canonical target of PECPT631), an **exception** is any event that causes the processor to temporarily suspend the currently executing instruction stream and transfer control to a dedicated *exception handler*. **IRQ (Interrupt Request)** and **FIQ (Fast Interrupt Request)** are the two *asynchronous external hardware* exceptions. They are gated (masked) by the **I-bit** and **F-bit** of the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** respectively.

### 1.1 The CPSR — The Heart of Exception Gating

The CPSR is a 32-bit status register whose upper byte contains the *program status flags and control bits*. The bits of immediate interest are:

| Bit Position | Name | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Bit 7** | **I** | **1 = IRQ masked (disabled); 0 = IRQ enabled** |
| **Bit 6** | **F** | **1 = FIQ masked (disabled); 0 = FIQ enabled** |
| Bit 5 | T | Thumb state (0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb) |
| Bits 4–0 | Mode | Processor mode encoding (User=10000, IRQ=10010, FIQ=10001, Supervisor=10011) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule of ARM Exception Logic:** *"I-bit set = IRQ OFF; I-bit clear = IRQ ON."* The opposite of a desktop PC's intuition. Always remember: **1 = blocked, 0 = permitted**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two Receptionists at a Hotel

Imagine a five-star hotel lobby (your **CPU**) handling guests (**instructions**):

- **The Main Reception Desk (IRQ):** Handles ordinary guest check-ins (timer ticks, UART, GPIO, ADC). Polished, courteous, but has to **log every visitor in a register first** before directing them. This bookkeeping = saving context → that's why IRQ is "slower."
- **The VIP Fast-Track Counter (FIQ):** Reserved exclusively for **V.I.P guests** (critical, time-strict hardware signals like DMA completion or emergency power-fail). The counter is **physically located at the lobby's far end**, with its own private bank of 7 extra registers (banked `r8_fiq–r14_fiq`), so it does **not need to save context** for the most-used registers. → Hence **F-as-t I-nterrupt Q-ueue**.

The two "switches" that toggle these desks ON/OFF are the **I-bit and F-bit in CPSR**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Exception Vector Table (LPC2148 / ARM7TDMI)

When an enabled IRQ/FIQ fires, the ARM core forces PC ← fixed vector address:

$$
\boxed{\text{Vector}_{\text{IRQ}} = 0x00000018 \qquad \text{Vector}_{\text{FIQ}} = 0x0000001C}
$$

The full ARM exception vector table (Board-exam favourite):

| Exception | Vector Address | Mode Entered | Priority |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Reset | **0x00000000** | Supervisor (SVC) | 1 (highest) |
| Undefined Instruction | 0x00000004 | Undefined | 6 |
| Software Interrupt (SWI) | 0x00000008 | Supervisor | 6 |
| Prefetch Abort | 0x0000000C | Abort | 5 |
| Data Abort | 0x00000010 | Abort | 4 |
| **IRQ** | **0x00000018** | **IRQ** | **3** |
| **FIQ** | **0x0000001C** | **FIQ** | **2 (lowest of externals)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-intuitive priority fact (often asked):** *Reset* has the highest priority, but within the IRQ/FIQ pair, **FIQ has higher priority than IRQ** and **FIQ can preempt an IRQ handler**. This is because FIQ is "last in the vector table" and the ARM core design intentionally allows FIQ to be re-entrant without saving `r8–r12`.

---

## 2.2 How Enabling / Disabling Maps to Bit Manipulation

The two instructions we use are:

$$
\boxed{\text{MRS}\ \ Rd,\ \text{CPSR}} \quad \text{(Move CPSR → General register — READ)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MSR}\ \ \text{CPSR\_c},\ Rm} \quad \text{(Move General register → CPSR control field — WRITE)}
$$

The `_c` suffix restricts the write to the **control byte** only (flags I, F, T, Mode), so we never accidentally clobber condition flags `N, Z, C, V`.

The bitmasks for I and F:

$$
\boxed{\text{I-bit mask} = 0x80 = 1000\ 0000_2 \qquad \text{F-bit mask} = 0x40 = 0100\ 0000_2}
$$

Combined IRQ + FIQ disable mask = `0xC0`.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Mask Cheat-Sheet

| Operation | Bit Action | Logic / Mnemonic | Hex Mask |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Enable IRQ** | Clear I-bit | `BIC Rd, Rd, #0x80` | **0x80** |
| **Disable IRQ** | Set I-bit | `ORR Rd, Rd, #0x80` | **0x80** |
| **Enable FIQ** | Clear F-bit | `BIC Rd, Rd, #0x40` | **0x40** |
| **Disable FIQ** | Set F-bit | `ORR Rd, Rd, #0x40` | **0x40** |
| **Disable BOTH** | Set I + F bits | `ORR Rd, Rd, #0xC0` | **0xC0** |
| **Enable BOTH** | Clear I + F bits | `BIC Rd, Rd, #0xC0` | **0xC0** |
| Read CPSR | — | `MRS Rd, CPSR` | — |
| Write CPSR (control only) | — | `MSR CPSR_c, Rd` | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **`BIC` = Bit Clear (AND with complement). `ORR` = Bit Set (Logical OR).** Both are ARM data-processing primitives that operate on a *32-bit immediate with barrel-shifter rotation*. This is what the **barrel shifter** in the ARM datapath is fundamentally used for in interrupt programming.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In any embedded product — **automotive ABS controllers, medical infusion pumps, drone flight controllers** — IRQ/FIQ discipline ensures:
- **Determinism:** Critical motor-control loops run on FIQ with deterministic latency.
- **Atomicity:** Critical sections (e.g., updating a shared buffer pointer) are protected by disabling IRQ (`I=1`).
- **Low Power:** The Wait-for-Interrupt (`WFI`) instruction pairs with masked exceptions for idle low-power states.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Assembly Code Walkthroughs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Prerequisite — Mode Check:**
> Direct CPSR modification via `MSR` is **forbidden in User Mode** (it would breach OS protection). The CPSR write must be done in a **privileged mode** (typically Supervisor or IRQ context). In bare-metal LPC2148 firmware (typical for KTU labs), startup code enters **Supervisor mode**, so direct MSR works throughout the program.

---

## 3.1 Example Code 1 — Enabling IRQ (The "Open the Door" Pattern)

**Goal:** Allow IRQ exceptions from peripheral hardware (Timer, UART, EINT) to reach the CPU.

```armasm
ENABLE_IRQ
        MRS     r0, CPSR             ; r0 = current CPSR snapshot
        BIC     r0, r0, #0x80       ; r0 = r0 AND NOT(0x80) → clear I-bit
        MSR     CPSR_c, r0           ; write modified byte back to CPSR_c
        MOV     pc, lr               ; return if called as subroutine
```

### Step-by-step derivation of the bit operation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Suppose CPSR} &= \ldots 1\underbrace{0}_{T}\underbrace{1}_{F}\underbrace{1}_{I}\underbrace{0000}_{\text{mode}} \\
\text{That is } r_0 &= \ldots 1101\ 0000_2 \\
\\
\text{BIC logic: } BIC &= Rd\ \text{AND NOT}\ \text{Operand2} \\
\text{NOT}(0x80) &= \text{NOT}(1000\ 0000_2) = 0111\ 1111_2 = 0x7F \\
\\
r_0 \leftarrow r_0\ \text{AND}\ 0x7F &= \ldots 1101\ 0000_2\ \text{AND}\ 0111\ 1111_2 \\
&= \ldots 0101\ 0000_2 \\
\\
\text{Result: I-bit flipped from } \mathbf{1 \rightarrow 0} \Rightarrow \text{IRQ NOW ENABLED}
\end{aligned}
$$

The mode bits `10000` (Supervisor) are **untouched** — we remain in the same privileged mode. No other state is corrupted.

---

## 3.2 Example Code 2 — Disabling IRQ (The "Close the Door" Pattern)

```armasm
DISABLE_IRQ
        MRS     r0, CPSR             ; r0 ← CPSR
        ORR     r0, r0, #0x80       ; set I-bit (1 = mask IRQ)
        MSR     CPSR_c, r0           ; update control byte only
        MOV     pc, lr
```

### Step-by-step derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before: } r_0 &= \ldots 0\underbrace{0}_{T}\underbrace{0}_{F}\underbrace{0}_{I}\underbrace{10100}_{\text{User}} \\
\text{ORR } r_0, r_0, \#0x80 &: \quad 0x80 = 1000\ 0000_2 \\
r_0 &\leftarrow r_0\ \text{OR}\ 0x80 \\
&= \ldots 1000\ 0000_2\ \text{bitwise-OR} \ldots 0000\ 0000_2 \\
\text{(with mode = 10000 SVC)} &= \ldots 1101\ 0000_2 \\
\\
\text{Result: I-bit set to } \mathbf{1} \Rightarrow \text{IRQ MASKED} \\
\text{Mode preserved as Supervisor (SVC) so MSR is legal}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Example Code 3 — Enabling FIQ

```armasm
ENABLE_FIQ
        MRS     r0, CPSR
        BIC     r0, r0, #0x40       ; clear F-bit
        MSR     CPSR_c, r0
        MOV     pc, lr
```

### Analytical Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{F-bit mask } 0x40 &= 0100\ 0000_2 \\
\text{NOT}(0x40) &= 1011\ 1111_2 = 0xBF \\
\\
r_0 &\leftarrow r_0\ \text{AND}\ 0xBF \quad \Rightarrow \text{F-bit: } \mathbf{1 \rightarrow 0} \\
\text{I-bit, T-bit, Mode bits: all unchanged.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Example Code 4 — Disabling FIQ

```armasm
DISABLE_FIQ
        MRS     r0, CPSR
        ORR     r0, r0, #0x40       ; set F-bit
        MSR     CPSR_c, r0
        MOV     pc, lr
```

---

## 3.5 Example Code 5 — Disabling Both IRQ and FIQ (Atomic Section Guard)

```armasm
ENTER_CRITICAL
        MRS     r0, CPSR
        ORR     r0, r0, #0xC0       ; 0xC0 = 1100 0000 → set I and F together
        MSR     CPSR_c, r0
        MOV     pc, lr
```

### Why use 0xC0?

$$
\begin{aligned}
0xC0 &= 1100\ 0000_2 \\
&= 0x80\ \text{(I-bit)} \ \vert\ 0x40\ \text{(F-bit)} \\
\text{Single ORR instruction} &\Rightarrow \text{atomic — no IRQ can sneak between I and F set}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 Example Code 6 — Nested IRQ Handler Skeleton (Full Pattern)

This pattern is **the most-likely 14-mark question** for KTU Module 4. It demonstrates:
- Saving context in IRQ mode.
- Re-enabling IRQ inside the handler to allow higher-priority IRQs to nest.
- Restoring CPSR on exit using a special form of `SUBS` + banked `SPSR`.

```armasm
        AREA    IRQ_Handler, CODE, READONLY
        EXPORT  IRQ_Handler
        IMPORT  C_IRQ_Handler

IRQ_Handler
        SUB     lr, lr, #4          ; LR_IRQ = return addr + 4; correct it
        STMFD   sp!, {r0-r12, lr}   ; save working regs + LR_IRQ on IRQ stack
        ; --- Optional: re-enable IRQ to allow nesting of higher-priority IRQs ---
        MRS     r2, CPSR
        BIC     r2, r2, #0x80
        MSR     CPSR_c, r2
        ; --- Call user C handler ---
        BL      C_IRQ_Handler
        ; --- Restore CPSR from SPSR_IRQ and return ---
        LDMFD   sp!, {r0-r12, lr}^  ; ^ restores user mode regs from banked
        NOP                          ; pipeline flush
        SUBS    pc, lr, #4           ; atomically restore CPSR and return
```

### Analytical Matrix — What each instruction accomplishes

| Instruction | Effect | Why Needed |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `SUB lr, lr, #4` | Adjusts return address | ARM pipeline advances PC by +8; need to back-step by 4 for IRQ return |
| `STMFD sp!, {r0-r12, lr}` | Pushes 14 registers | Preserve caller's context |
| `MRS r2, CPSR` | Read current I/F bits | To modify |
| `BIC r2, r2, #0x80` | Clear I-bit | Allow nesting |
| `MSR CPSR_c, r2` | Write back control byte | Commit the I-bit change |
| `LDMFD …^` | Load with User-mode registers | The `^` suffix forces user-mode banked restore |
| `SUBS pc, lr, #4` | Atomically restores SPSR → CPSR and returns | The `S` suffix makes the LR-to-PC move also copy SPSR_irq → CPSR |

> [!NOTE]
> The `^` (User-mode restore) and `S` (SPSR restore) suffixes are ARM-specific. They appear in **no other architecture** and are an examiner favourite.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPSR Control Byte — Bit-Level Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CPSR_Control_Byte
        A[Bit 7: I - IRQ disable] --> B[Bit 6: F - FIQ disable]
        B --> C[Bit 5: T - Thumb state]
        C --> D[Bit 4 - Mode MSB]
        D --> E[Bit 3 - Mode]
        E --> F[Bit 2 - Mode]
        F --> G[Bit 1 - Mode]
        G --> H[Bit 0 - Mode LSB]
    end

    classDef irqBit fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef fiqBit fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class A irqBit
    class B fiqBit
```

---

## 4.2 IRQ/FIQ Enable-Disable Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Start: Code Section] --> B{Mode is Privileged?}
    B -- No --> Z[MSR Trap - Privileged Instruction Fault]
    B -- Yes --> C[MRS r0, CPSR]
    C --> D{Action?}
    D -- Enable IRQ --> E[BIC r0, r0, #0x80]
    D -- Disable IRQ --> F[ORR r0, r0, #0x80]
    D -- Enable FIQ --> G[BIC r0, r0, #0x40]
    D -- Disable FIQ --> H[ORR r0, r0, #0x40]
    D -- Disable Both --> I[ORR r0, r0, #0xC0]
    E --> J[MSR CPSR_c, r0]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Return to Caller]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IRQ/FIQ Path in LPC2148

```mermaid
graph LR
    DEV1[Timer 0 - Periodic] -->|IRQ req| VIC[Vectored Interrupt Controller]
    DEV2[UART0 - Rx Data] -->|IRQ req| VIC
    DEV3[GPIO EINT0 - Button] -->|FIQ req| PIN[Fast GPIO Logic]
    DEV4[DMA - Completion] -->|FIQ req| PIN
    VIC -->|Combined IRQ Line| ARM_CORE[ARM7TDMI Core]
    PIN -->|Direct FIQ Line| ARM_CORE
    ARM_CORE -->|Check I-bit| CPSR[(CPSR.I and CPSR.F)]
    CPSR -->|If I=0| IRQ_VEC[IRQ Vector 0x00000018]
    CPSR -->|If F=0| FIQ_VEC[FIQ Vector 0x0000001C]
    CPSR -->|If I=1 OR F=1| MASKED[Exception Masked - Held Pending]
    IRQ_VEC --> ISR1[IRQ_Handler - Slow Path]
    FIQ_VEC --> ISR2[FIQ_Handler - Fast Path with Banked r8-r14]
```

---

## 4.4 The `MRS / MSR` Read-Modify-Write State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UserApp
    UserApp --> ReadCPSR: MRS r0, CPSR
    ReadCPSR --> ModifyBits: BIC or ORR with 0x80 / 0x40 / 0xC0
    ModifyBits --> WriteCPSR: MSR CPSR_c, r0
    WriteCPSR --> UserApp: Effect Applied
    UserApp --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of the I-bit and F-bit of the CPSR register in the ARM7 processor with respect to IRQ and FIQ exceptions.*

**Model Answer:**
The CPSR (Current Program Status Register) of the ARM7 processor contains two control bits that mask asynchronous exceptions:
- **I-bit (bit 7):** When **set to 1**, the IRQ (Interrupt Request) line is **masked/blocked**, preventing the processor from accepting any IRQ. When **cleared to 0**, IRQ requests are accepted and force the PC to vector `0x00000018`.
- **F-bit (bit 6):** When **set to 1**, the FIQ (Fast Interrupt Request) line is **masked/blocked**. When **cleared to 0**, FIQs are accepted at vector `0x0000001C`.
Both bits can be modified only in **privileged modes** (not in User mode) using `MSR CPSR_c, Rn` after reading with `MRS`. *(3 marks distributed: 1 for I-bit, 1 for F-bit, 1 for privileged mode constraint.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Differentiate between IRQ and FIQ exceptions in ARM7TDMI.*

**Model Answer (tabular):**

| Parameter | IRQ | FIQ |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Vector address | `0x00000018` | `0x0000001C` |
| Mask bit | CPSR.**I** (bit 7) | CPSR.**F** (bit 6) |
| Priority | Lower than FIQ | Higher than IRQ |
| Banked registers | Only `r13_irq`, `r14_irq`, `SPSR_irq` | Plus `r8_fiq–r12_fiq` (extra 5) |
| Use case | General peripherals (UART, Timer, ADC) | Time-critical (DMA, motor control, power-fail) |
| Tail-chaining | Possible with `SUBS pc, lr, #4` | Naturally fast — minimal context save |

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Enable/Disable Assembly Code Focus*
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Write the ARM7 assembly code routines to **enable and disable IRQ exceptions**, explaining each instruction. State the prerequisite mode for executing these routines.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Write the assembly code to **disable both IRQ and FIQ simultaneously**, and explain why a single ORR with `0xC0` is preferred over two separate `MSR` writes.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Enabling and Disabling IRQ:**

**Prerequisite:** CPU must be in a **privileged mode** (Supervisor/System) because `MSR CPSR_c` is a privileged operation. In bare-metal LPC2148 firmware, startup `B __main` leaves CPU in Supervisor mode.

```armasm
;---------- ENABLE IRQ ----------
ENABLE_IRQ
        MRS     r0, CPSR        ; r0 ← copy of CPSR (1)
        BIC     r0, r0, #0x80   ; clear bit 7 → I=0 (2)
        MSR     CPSR_c, r0      ; write control byte back (3)
        MOV     pc, lr          ; return to caller (4)

;---------- DISABLE IRQ ----------
DISABLE_IRQ
        MRS     r0, CPSR
        ORR     r0, r0, #0x80   ; set bit 7 → I=1
        MSR     CPSR_c, r0
        MOV     pc, lr
```

**Line-by-line justification:**
1. `MRS r0, CPSR` — *Read* the entire CPSR into general register `r0` without affecting flags.
2. `BIC r0, r0, #0x80` — BIC = **B**it **C**lear. `r0 ← r0 AND NOT(0x80)`. Mask `0x80` = `1000 0000` targets only bit 7 (I-bit). All other bits including mode are preserved.
3. `MSR CPSR_c, r0` — The `_c` qualifier limits the write to the **control field byte** only, protecting condition flags `N Z C V` from corruption.
4. `MOV pc, lr` — Conventional return from leaf subroutine.

**(b) Disabling both with 0xC0:**

```armasm
ENTER_CRITICAL
        MRS     r0, CPSR
        ORR     r0, r0, #0xC0   ; 0xC0 = 1100 0000 → set I (bit7) and F (bit6)
        MSR     CPSR_c, r0
        MOV     pc, lr
```

**Why 0xC0 is preferred over two `MSR` writes:**
- `0xC0 = 0x80 | 0x40`. A single `ORR` sets both I-bit and F-bit in **one cycle**, atomically.
- If we performed two separate `MSR` calls, an **interrupt could fire between them**, breaking the "critical section" guarantee. ARM exception entry itself modifies CPSR (`I=1` automatically), but the goal here is software-level atomicity of the bit-set.
- Further, after the first `MSR` with `I=1`, the IRQ line is masked, but FIQ could still fire — making the second `MSR` non-atomic. Single-instruction masking guarantees no external event slips through.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Full IRQ Handler & Nested Interrupt Pattern*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Draw and explain the complete ARM7 **nested-enabling IRQ handler** showing all register save/restore steps. What does the `^` suffix mean in `LDMFD sp!, {r0-r12, lr}^`?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Demonstrate with assembly code how the FIQ-banked registers `r8_fiq–r12_fiq` allow FIQ handlers to be written **without saving** these registers, and explain why this makes FIQ "faster" than IRQ.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Nested-enabling IRQ handler (already given in §3.6):**

The `^` suffix in `LDMFD` is the **Force-User-mode-register-transfer** qualifier. It tells the ARM core that:
- The registers `r0–r12` being loaded must come from the **User-mode banked set** (because the handler runs in IRQ mode but must restore the *original* User-mode context).
- `lr` is loaded from the **banked `lr_irq`** register, not the user-mode `lr`.

This is essential because when the exception entered, ARM automatically swapped `r13` (SP) and `r14` (LR) to the **IRQ-bank** versions. The `^` suffix reverses this swap during restoration.

**(b) FIQ Banked-Register Advantage:**

When an FIQ is taken, ARM7TDMI automatically swaps to **FIQ mode**, giving the handler *private* copies of registers:
- `r8_fiq, r9_fiq, r10_fiq, r11_fiq, r12_fiq` (5 extra banked)
- `r13_fiq` (SP), `r14_fiq` (LR), `SPSR_fiq`

A minimal FIQ handler therefore needs **zero context save** for `r8–r12`:

```armasm
        AREA    FIQ_Handler, CODE, READONLY
        EXPORT  FIQ_Handler
FIQ_Handler
        ; No STMFD needed — r8-r12 are already private to FIQ mode!
        ; ISR body uses r8..r12 freely without clobbering user state.
        ; ... perform urgent task here ...

        ; Fast return (note: +4 correction not needed for FIQ on ARM7
        ; if handler does not modify lr; we keep it for safety)
        SUBS    pc, lr, #4       ; restore SPSR → CPSR, return
```

**Why this is faster:** A typical IRQ handler takes ~12–20 cycles to push `r0–r12, lr` onto the stack. FIQ saves those cycles because **r8–r12 are already isolated by hardware banking**. This deterministic, low-jitter response is exactly why FIQ is reserved for the *single most time-critical* source in an embedded design.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Writing `0x80` for FIQ or `0x40` for IRQ** — bit positions are swapped more often than you think. Memorize: **I = bit 7 = 0x80; F = bit 6 = 0x40.**
> 2. **Forgetting the `_c` qualifier in `MSR CPSR_c, Rd`** — using just `MSR CPSR, Rd` will overwrite the flag byte `N Z C V` and the upper 24 bits, causing immediate corruption of condition codes. Examiners deduct **2 marks** here.
> 3. **Trying to write CPSR in User mode** — this triggers an *Undefined Instruction trap*. Examiners look for the privileged-mode prerequisite explicitly.
> 4. **Using `MOV pc, lr` instead of `SUBS pc, lr, #4` for exception return** — this skips SPSR restoration, leaving the CPU in IRQ mode after "return." Catastrophic in OS contexts.
> 5. **Confusing FIQ and IRQ vector addresses** — `0x18` for IRQ, `0x1C` for FIQ. The 4-byte gap exists for the full 32-bit branch instruction.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ ARM7 supports **7 exception types**; IRQ and FIQ are the two external, maskable ones.
- ✅ **Vector addresses:** IRQ = `0x00000018`, FIQ = `0x0000001C`.
- ✅ CPSR **I-bit (bit 7, mask 0x80)** masks IRQ; **F-bit (bit 6, mask 0x40)** masks FIQ. **1 = masked.**
- ✅ To enable: `BIC Rd, Rd, #mask` (clear bit). To disable: `ORR Rd, Rd, #mask` (set bit).
- ✅ Always use the **`MRS` → `BIC/ORR` → `MSR CPSR_c, …`** read-modify-write triplet.
- ✅ CPSR writes are **privileged** — User-mode code must trap to a privileged routine (e.g., via SWI).
- ✅ FIQ is faster than IRQ because of **five extra banked registers** (`r8_fiq–r12_fiq`) and being last in the vector table (no extra branch needed).
- ✅ For exception return: **`SUBS pc, lr, #4`** — the `S` suffix atomically restores SPSR → CPSR.
- ✅ The **`^` suffix** in `LDM/STM` forces User-mode register transfer for IRQ/FIQ/SVC handlers.
- ✅ Combined mask `0xC0` disables **both** IRQ and FIQ in a single atomic instruction.
- ✅ Bare-metal LPC2148 startup leaves CPU in **Supervisor (SVC) mode**, making `MSR CPSR_c` legal throughout the application.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison Between Exceptions and Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Comparison Between Exceptions and Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXCEPTION (KTU Definition):**
> An **Exception** is a *synchronous* event that alters the normal sequential flow of program execution, directly caused by the executing instruction itself. The processor detects the condition during instruction execution and immediately transfers control to a predefined exception handler. In the **ARM Cortex-M** architecture (the canonical architecture for PECPT631), exceptions include Reset, NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt), HardFault, MemManage, BusFault, UsageFault, SVCall, DebugMonitor, PendSV, and SysTick.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **INTERRUPT (KTU Definition):**
> An **Interrupt** is an *asynchronous* event generated by an external hardware peripheral or an internal I/O device that requests the CPU to halt its current activity and service the requesting device. Interrupts are typically *maskable* (can be enabled/disabled) and are processed through the **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** in ARM Cortex-M systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy:**

Imagine a head chef (the **CPU**) working on a complex dish (the **main program**):

- An **Exception** is like the chef *realizing mid-recipe* that they are out of a critical ingredient, or burning the sauce — the problem is **caused by the current cooking action** itself. The chef must immediately stop and address this self-induced problem before continuing. (Synchronous, instruction-caused)

- An **Interrupt** is like the restaurant's phone suddenly **ringing** from the dining area — a customer complaint, an order change, or a delivery arriving. This event is **completely unrelated** to the dish currently being prepared. The chef pauses, takes the call, handles it, and returns to cooking. (Asynchronous, hardware-triggered)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Both exceptions and interrupts use the **same NVIC mechanism** in ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers. The architectural distinction is *semantic* (synchronous vs asynchronous, source-based), not hardware-based. The Vector Table contains handlers for both.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Weightage Items)

- **Interrupt Latency:** *typically* **12 clock cycles** in ARM Cortex-M3/M4 for tail-chaining
- **Interrupt Response Time:** **6 cycles** (stacking) + vector fetch
- **Number of Interrupt Sources:** Cortex-M4 supports up to **240** interrupts
- **Priority Levels:** **4 to 8 bits** of priority (implementable in NVIC)
- **NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt):** Cannot be masked, highest priority after Reset
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Exception Mechanism

The execution flow of an **Exception** in ARM Cortex-M follows a deterministic sequence:

1. **Instruction Fetch/Decode** → The CPU fetches and begins decoding the instruction.
2. **Exception Detection** → During execution, the processor detects the fault condition (e.g., divide by zero, undefined opcode, memory access violation).
3. **Exception Entry Sequence (Hardware-Automatic):**
   - **Stacking:** The CPU automatically pushes **8 registers** onto the stack: `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0` (in that order).
   - **Vector Fetch:** The CPU reads the exception vector from the Vector Table.
   - **Register Update:** `LR` is loaded with a special **EXC_RETURN** value; `IPS` is updated with exception number.
4. **Exception Handler Execution** → Control transfers to the ISR (Interrupt Service Routine).
5. **Exception Return** → Triggered by writing `EXC_RETURN` to PC; CPU unstacks and resumes.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Interrupt Mechanism

1. **Peripheral Asserts IRQ Line** → A peripheral (e.g., Timer, USART, GPIO) sets its interrupt request flag.
2. **NVIC Acceptance** → The NVIC evaluates pending interrupts against current priority and PRIMASK/FAULTMASK/BASEPRI registers.
3. **Interrupt Entry** → If accepted, the same hardware sequence as exceptions occurs (stacking, vector fetch, EXC_RETURN).
4. **ISR Execution** → The peripheral's specific ISR runs.
5. **Interrupt Clear & Return** → The ISR must clear the pending bit before returning.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table (Board Exam Favorite)

| **Parameter** | **Exception** | **Interrupt** |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger Nature** | **Synchronous** — caused by instruction | **Asynchronous** — caused by external event |
| **Source** | Generated by CPU during instruction execution | Generated by on-chip peripherals or external pins |
| **Predictability** | Deterministic — occurs at known instruction boundaries | Non-deterministic — can occur at any clock cycle |
| **Examples** | Reset, NMI, HardFault, UsageFault, SVCall, PendSV, SysTick | External IRQ0–IRQ239 (GPIO, Timer, USART, ADC, etc.) |
| **Maskability** | Most are non-maskable (NMI, HardFault); some maskable | Maskable via PRIMASK register; enabled/disabled per-IRQ |
| **Priority** | Fixed priority hierarchy (Reset > NMI > HardFault > ...) | Programmable (preemption + sub-priority) |
| **Vector Table Entry** | System Handler Vector (offset 16+) | Device-specific Vector (offset 16+) |
| **Handling Time** | Must be handled immediately | Can be deferred by priority masking |
| **Programmer Visibility** | Often implicit (compiler/runtime generated) | Explicit (programmer writes ISR handlers) |
| **NVIC Handling** | Routed via NVIC | Routed via NVIC |
| **Re-entrancy Concern** | Low (predictable timing) | High (nested interrupts possible) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Interrupt Timing Equations

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` for absolute values to preserve markdown table syntax.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| **Interrupt Latency** | $T_{latency} = T_{stack} + T_{fetch} + T_{decode}$ | Time from IRQ assertion to ISR start |
| **Stacking Time (Cortex-M3/M4)** | $T_{stack} = 8 \times T_{mem} + 6 \text{ cycles}$ | 8 registers + control overhead |
| **Total Worst-Case Latency** | $T_{max} = 12 \text{ cycles} + N_{blocked}$ cycles | Where $N_{blocked}$ = cycles spent in higher-priority ISR |
| **Tail-Chain Latency** | $T_{tail} = 6 \text{ cycles}$ | Cost when switching between two ISRs without unstacking |
| **NVIC Cycles Per Priority** | $T_{priority} = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ | Time to evaluate pending priorities |
| **Priority Bits Available** | $N_{levels} = 2^{P}$ | Where $P$ = priority register bits (4–8) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial automation, automotive ECUs (Electronic Control Units), and IoT edge devices, distinguishing exceptions from interrupts is **critical for system reliability**:

- **Airbag Deployment System (Automotive):** Uses NMI (a system exception) for crash sensors — must be non-maskable and immediate.
- **Motor Control (PMSM/FOC):** Uses timer interrupts at fixed 10 kHz — synchronous control loop is essential.
- **HardFault Handler:** Catches illegal memory access; engineers must implement fault recovery to prevent system lockup.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Interrupt Response Time

The total time from an interrupt request assertion until the first instruction of the ISR executes is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Define the components of latency:**

$$T_{response} = T_{detection} + T_{completion} + T_{stacking} + T_{fetch}$$

Where:
- $T_{detection}$ = time for NVIC to recognize pending IRQ
- $T_{completion}$ = time for CPU to finish current instruction (worst case = divide instruction, ~12 cycles)
- $T_{stacking}$ = time to push 8 registers on stack
- $T_{fetch}$ = time to fetch vector address from Vector Table

**Step 2 — Substitute ARM Cortex-M3/M4 typical values:**

$$T_{detection} = 1 \text{ cycle (synchronous to clock)}$$
$$T_{completion} = 12 \text{ cycles (worst case for divide)}$$
$$T_{stacking} = 8 \text{ register pushes} + 2 \text{ control cycles} = 10 \text{ cycles}$$
$$T_{fetch} = 1 \text{ cycle (Vector Table in flash with 0 wait state)}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the components:**

$$T_{response} = 1 + 12 + 10 + 1$$
$$T_{response} = 24 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to time at $f_{CLK} = 72$ MHz:**

$$T_{response} = \frac{24}{72 \times 10^{6}} \text{ seconds}$$
$$T_{response} = 333.33 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5 — Worst-case with preemption blocked:**

$$T_{worst} = T_{response} + T_{current\_ISR} + T_{higher\_priority\_ISR}$$
$$T_{worst} = 333 \text{ ns} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{ISR_i}$$

This derivation is essential for **hard real-time systems** where deterministic response is mandated.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Source Classification

| **Vector Number** | **Exception/Interrupt Name** | **Type** | **Priority** | **Maskable?** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial SP (Stack Pointer) | Exception (System) | — | No |
| 1 | Reset | Exception | -3 (Highest) | No |
| 2 | NMI | Exception | -2 | No |
| 3 | HardFault | Exception | -1 | No |
| 4 | MemManage | Exception | Programmable | Yes |
| 5 | BusFault | Exception | Programmable | Yes |
| 6 | UsageFault | Exception | Programmable | Yes |
| 7–10 | Reserved | — | — | — |
| 11 | SVCall | Exception | Programmable | Yes |
| 12 | DebugMonitor | Exception | Programmable | Yes |
| 13 | Reserved | — | — | — |
| 14 | PendSV | Exception | Programmable | Yes |
| 15 | SysTick | Exception | Programmable | Yes |
| 16+ | External IRQs | **Interrupt** | Programmable | Yes |

### 3.3 Derivation: Priority Encoding for NVIC

If the NVIC implements **3 bits** of priority (a common configuration in Cortex-M4 with `__NVIC_PRIO_BITS = 3`):

$$N_{priority\_levels} = 2^3 = 8 \text{ distinct priority levels}$$

The priority field in the Interrupt Priority Register (IPR) is stored in the **upper bits**:

$$IPR[7:5] = \text{priority value (0–7)}$$
$$IPR[4:0] = \text{reserved (read as zero)}$$

Lower numerical value = **higher** priority (priority 0 is highest non-system priority).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Exception vs Interrupt Handling Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Execution Starts] --> B{Next Event Type?}
    
    B -->|Instruction causes fault| C[Synchronous Exception Detected]
    B -->|Peripheral asserts IRQ line| D[Asynchronous Interrupt Received]
    
    C --> C1[CPU pushes 8 registers on stack]
    D --> D1[NVIC checks priority vs PRIMASK]
    
    D1 --> D2{Priority Acceptable?}
    D2 -->|No| D3[Wait or remain pending]
    D2 -->|Yes| D4[NVIC sends IRQ to CPU]
    
    C1 --> E[Vector Table Lookup]
    D4 --> E
    
    E --> F[Fetch Handler Address]
    F --> G[Load LR with EXC RETURN value]
    G --> H[Update IPSR with vector number]
    H --> I[Branch to ISR Handler]
    
    I --> J[ISR Executes User Code]
    J --> K{Return Triggered?}
    
    K -->|Write EXC RETURN to PC| L[Unstack 8 registers]
    L --> M[Restore PC, PSR, and R0-R3, R12]
    M --> N[Resume Main Program]
    
    D3 --> O[Continue Main Program]
    O --> B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — NVIC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ExternalIRQ[External Interrupt Sources]
        IRQ1[IRQ0 GPIO]
        IRQ2[IRQ1 Timer0]
        IRQ3[IRQ2 USART1]
        IRQ4[IRQ3 ADC1]
        IRQ5[IRQn Peripherals]
    end
    
    subgraph InternalExceptions[Internal Exception Sources]
        EX1[Reset Handler]
        EX2[NMI]
        EX3[HardFault]
        EX4[SVCall]
        EX5[SysTick]
        EX6[PendSV]
    end
    
    subgraph NVIC_Block[NVIC Controller Block]
        ENAB[Enable Registers]
        PEND[Pending Registers]
        ACTIVE[Active Registers]
        PRIO[Priority Registers 4 to 8 bits]
    end
    
    subgraph CPU_Block[ARM Cortex-M Core]
        CPUCORE[CPU Pipeline]
        STACK[Stack Pointer]
        VECTAB[Vector Table in Flash]
    end
    
    IRQ1 --> ENAB
    IRQ2 --> ENAB
    IRQ3 --> ENAB
    IRQ4 --> ENAB
    IRQ5 --> ENAB
    
    EX1 --> ENAB
    EX2 --> ENAB
    EX3 --> ENAB
    EX4 --> ENAB
    EX5 --> ENAB
    EX6 --> ENAB
    
    ENAB --> PEND
    PEND --> PRIO
    PRIO --> CPUCORE
    
    CPUCORE --> STACK
    VECTAB --> CPUCORE
    ACTIVE --> CPUCORE
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Interrupt Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Inactive
    Inactive --> Pending : Peripheral sets IRQ flag
    Pending --> Active : NVIC accepts priority
    Pending --> Inactive : Software clears pending bit
    Active --> Active : Preempted by higher priority
    Active --> Inactive : ISR returns EXC RETURN
    Inactive --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between exceptions and interrupts in ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Exception** | **Interrupt** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Synchronous (instruction-caused) | Asynchronous (event-driven) |
| **Source** | Internal CPU faults (e.g., divide-by-zero) | External/internal peripherals |
| **Examples** | Reset, NMI, HardFault, SysTick | Timer, USART, GPIO, ADC |
| **Maskability** | Most are non-maskable | Maskable via PRIMASK |

*(1 mark for nature distinction, 1 mark for source/examples, 1 mark for maskability with valid example)*

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**"What is the role of NVIC in handling exceptions and interrupts? Mention the number of registers pushed during exception entry."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** is a hardware unit integrated into ARM Cortex-M cores that manages both exceptions and interrupts. It handles enable/disable control, priority resolution, and vectoring to the correct handler. *(2 marks)*

2. During exception entry, **8 registers** are automatically pushed onto the stack by hardware: `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0`. *(1 mark)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the exception handling mechanism in ARM Cortex-M architecture with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the Vector Table. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare exceptions and interrupts. Explain priority handling in NVIC with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer for Question A(a):**

**Exception Handling Mechanism (7 marks):**

The ARM Cortex-M exception handling is a **hardware-automatic** process with the following steps:

1. **Exception Trigger:** A fault condition is detected during instruction execution (e.g., undefined opcode, memory violation). *(1 mark)*

2. **Register Stacking:** The CPU automatically pushes 8 registers onto the current stack:
   - Order: `xPSR → PC → LR → R12 → R3 → R2 → R1 → R0`
   - This preserves the execution context. *(1 mark)*

3. **Vector Table Lookup:** The CPU uses the exception number to compute the offset (`4 × exception_number`) into the Vector Table stored at address `0x00000000`. *(1 mark)*

4. **Handler Address Fetch:** The 32-bit handler address is loaded into the PC. *(0.5 mark)*

5. **Special Register Updates:**
   - `LR` ← `EXC_RETURN` value (e.g., `0xFFFFFFF9` for returning to Thread mode using MSP)
   - `IPSR` ← exception number *(0.5 mark)*

6. **Handler Execution:** The exception handler runs. *(0.5 mark)*

7. **Exception Return:** Triggered by executing `BX LR` or `POP {PC}` with EXC_RETURN. The hardware unstacks the 8 registers in reverse order. *(1 mark)*

**Vector Table (1 mark):**
The Vector Table is a 32-bit aligned array in memory starting at address 0. The first entry is the **Initial Stack Pointer** value. Subsequent entries contain handler addresses for Reset, NMI, HardFault, etc. The table size = `(16 + number_of_IRQs) × 4 bytes`.

---

**Model Answer for Question A(b):**

**Comparison (3.5 marks):**
Refer to the comparison table from Section 2.3 — explicitly highlight:
- Synchronous vs asynchronous
- CPU-internal vs peripheral sources
- Maskability differences

**Priority Handling in NVIC (3.5 marks):**

1. **Priority Bits:** NVIC implements **4 to 8 bits** of priority (configurable via `__NVIC_PRIO_BITS`). For a 3-bit implementation, 8 priority levels exist. *(1 mark)*

2. **Lower numerical value = higher priority.** Priority 0 is the highest non-system priority. *(0.5 mark)*

3. **Preemption:** A higher-priority interrupt can preempt a lower-priority one. Preemption is controlled by the upper bits of the priority field. *(1 mark)*

4. **Sub-priority:** When two interrupts of the same preemption level occur simultaneously, sub-priority (lower bits) determines which runs first. *(0.5 mark)*

5. **Example:** Set Timer0 priority = `0x20` (preemption=1, sub-priority=0), USART1 priority = `0x40` (preemption=2, sub-priority=0). If USART1 IRQ fires while Timer0 ISR is executing, Timer0 will complete first because it has higher preemption priority. *(0.5 mark)*

---

**Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC) and its key features. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the interrupt response sequence in ARM Cortex-M. Calculate the worst-case interrupt latency for a system clock of 72 MHz. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer for Question B(a):**

**NVIC Architecture (7 marks):**

The NVIC is tightly integrated with the ARM Cortex-M core. Key components: *(1 mark for block diagram description)*

1. **Interrupt Set-Enable Registers (ISER):** Allow software to enable specific IRQs. Writing 1 to a bit enables that interrupt. *(1 mark)*

2. **Interrupt Clear-Enable Registers (ICER):** Disable specific IRQs. *(0.5 mark)*

3. **Interrupt Set-Pending (ISPR) / Clear-Pending (ICPR):** Allow software to force-trigger or clear pending status. *(1 mark)*

4. **Active Bit Registers:** Track which interrupts are currently being serviced, enabling nested interrupts. *(0.5 mark)*

5. **Priority Registers (IPR):** 8 bits per IRQ (only upper 4–8 bits implemented depending on configuration). *(1 mark)*

6. **Mask Registers:** `PRIMASK` (masks all maskable interrupts), `FAULTMASK` (masks faults), `BASEPRI` (masks interrupts below a threshold). *(1 mark)*

7. **Vector Table Integration:** NVIC reads handler addresses directly from the Vector Table. *(0.5 mark)*

8. **Tail-Chaining:** When one ISR completes and another is pending, NVIC skips unstacking/stacking, saving 6 cycles. *(0.5 mark)*

---

**Model Answer for Question B(b):**

**Interrupt Response Sequence (4 marks):**

1. **IRQ Assertion:** Peripheral asserts its interrupt request line.
2. **NVIC Detection:** NVIC synchronizes the request to the clock and checks enable status.
3. **Priority Resolution:** Compares pending IRQ priority against current execution priority.
4. **Preemption Decision:** If pending priority is numerically lower (higher importance), preemption occurs.
5. **Completion of Current Instruction:** CPU finishes current instruction (up to 12 cycles for divide).
6. **Stacking:** Hardware pushes 8 registers onto stack (12 cycles including alignment).
7. **Vector Fetch:** Handler address fetched from Vector Table.
8. **ISR Execution:** Handler runs.
9. **Return:** On writing EXC_RETURN, unstacking occurs.

**Worst-Case Latency Calculation (3 marks):**

$$T_{worst} = T_{complete} + T_{stack} + T_{fetch} + T_{branch}$$

$$T_{worst} = 12 + 12 + 1 + 1 = 26 \text{ cycles}$$

Converting to time at $f_{CLK} = 72$ MHz:

$$T_{worst} = \frac{26}{72 \times 10^6}$$
$$T_{worst} = 361.11 \text{ ns}$$

Therefore, the worst-case interrupt response time is approximately **361 nanoseconds**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing "interrupt latency" with "interrupt response time." Latency = time to *recognize*; Response = time to *first ISR instruction*.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to specify the priority bits when calculating priority levels. Always state `__NVIC_PRIO_BITS` value.
> - **Mistake 3:** Writing "EXC_RETURN = some address" — it is a **special constant**, not an actual memory address.
> - **Mistake 4:** Stacking order is often given incorrectly. The correct ARM Cortex-M stacking sequence is `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0` (highest register pushed first).
> - **Mistake 5:** Writing 4 or 16 registers — it is precisely **8 registers**.
> - **Mistake 6:** Saying "Reset is an interrupt" — Reset is an **exception**, the highest-priority one.
> - **Mistake 7:** Unit conversion errors: forgetting to invert frequency ($1/f$ not $f$) when converting cycles to seconds.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Bullet Points — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- **Synchronous vs Asynchronous:** Exceptions are *synchronous* (caused by instruction); Interrupts are *asynchronous* (caused by peripherals).

- **ARM Cortex-M Vector Table:** Starts at address `0x00000000`; entry 0 = Initial SP, entry 1 = Reset Handler.

- **8 Registers Stacked on Entry:** `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0` (in that hardware-defined order).

- **EXC_RETURN:** Special value loaded into LR upon exception; common values are `0xFFFFFFF1` (return to Handler mode) and `0xFFFFFFF9` (return to Thread mode using MSP).

- **NVIC Priority:** Lower numerical value = higher priority. Priority 0 is the highest user-configurable priority.

- **Tail-Chaining:** Saves **6 cycles** when switching directly between ISRs without full unstacking.

- **Late Arrival:** If a higher-priority IRQ arrives during stacking of a lower-priority one, the higher-priority one is serviced first.

- **PRIMASK Register:** Setting it to 1 blocks all maskable interrupts and exceptions (except NMI and HardFault).

- **NMI:** The highest-priority maskable* (non-maskable) exception, vector number 2.

- **PendSV:** Used for deferred context switching in RTOS — typically set to lowest priority.

- **SysTick:** 24-bit down-counter exception generated by the system timer, vector number 15.

- **HardFault:** Catches all fault classes that cannot be disabled; vector number 3, priority -1 (fixed).

- **Number of Interrupts:** Cortex-M4 supports up to **240 external interrupts** (IRQ0 to IRQ239).

- **Interrupt Latency Formula:** $T_{latency} = T_{stack} + T_{fetch} + T_{decode}$ (typical: 12 cycles minimum).

- **Vector Fetch:** Always takes 1 cycle if Vector Table is in flash with 0 wait states.

- **ARM Cortex-M uses only MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** in Handler mode; PSP (Process Stack Pointer) is for Thread mode in RTOS applications.

- **Exceptions vs System Exceptions:** Reset, NMI, HardFault are *system exceptions*; SysTick and PendSV are also exceptions but configurable.

- **Maskability Summary:** Non-maskable = Reset, NMI, HardFault. Maskable = all other faults and all IRQs.

- **Tail-Chain vs Late Arrival:** Both are NVIC optimizations but function differently — tail-chain is post-ISR, late arrival is during pre-stacking.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupt Handling Schemes - Nested Interrupt Handler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Handling Schemes - Nested Interrupt Handler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Nested Interrupt Handler** is an interrupt servicing mechanism in which a currently executing Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) can itself be interrupted by another interrupt request of **higher priority**, allowing the Central Processing Unit (CPU) to suspend the lower-priority ISR, service the higher-priority interrupt, and then correctly resume the original ISR from where it was preempted. This preemption-based hierarchical dispatching forms the backbone of **deterministic real-time embedded response** in advanced microcontroller architectures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Nested interrupt handling is fundamentally governed by the microcontroller's **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** in ARM Cortex-M class devices, or equivalent programmable priority controllers in PIC32, dsPIC, and MSP430 families. Students must understand that nesting is **hardware-assisted**, not purely software-controlled.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surgeon in Operation"

Imagine a **senior surgeon** performing a scheduled operation in Operation Theatre-1:

- The surgeon (CPU core) is executing the **ongoing procedure** (current code or lower-priority ISR).
- A **critical emergency case** arrives at the hospital (high-priority interrupt request).
- The hospital protocol (hardware priority logic) immediately directs the surgeon to **pause** the current procedure.
- The surgical team **carefully covers the patient and notes the exact point of intervention** (context saving — pushing registers, Program Counter (PC), Link Register (LR), and processor status onto the stack).
- The surgeon rushes to Theatre-2 to handle the emergency (executes the higher-priority ISR).
- If **another, even more critical emergency** arrives (nested interrupt), the same procedure repeats — this is **n-level deep nesting**.
- Once all emergencies are handled, the surgeon **returns to Theatre-1, removes the drapes, and resumes** the original procedure exactly from where it was stopped (context restoration — popping the stack frame).

This is precisely how a Nested Interrupt Handler operates inside an advanced microcontroller.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine)** | The dedicated function executed in response to an interrupt | Atomic unit of interrupt servicing |
| **Preemption** | Suspension of a running ISR by a higher-priority interrupt | Enables real-time response |
| **Interrupt Latency** | Time from IRQ assertion to first instruction of ISR | Critical for hard real-time systems |
| **Context Save/Restore** | Push/Pop of CPU registers on stack | Maintains state integrity |
| **Interrupt Priority** | Numeric level assigned to each IRQ source | Determines nesting order |
| **Tail-Chaining** | Back-to-back ISR execution without full unstacking | Reduces overhead latency |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard nesting depth in most ARM Cortex-M controllers is **8 to 16 levels**, but theoretically bounded only by available stack memory. The Cortex-M3/M4 hardware automatically stacks **R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR** — eight words — on interrupt entry.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Stepwise Logic Flow

The nested interrupt mechanism operates through the following rigorously ordered sequence:

1. **Interrupt Request (IRQ) Assertion:** A peripheral sets its Interrupt Request Flag (IRF) and signals the Interrupt Controller (NVIC or equivalent).
2. **Priority Arbitration:** The controller compares the incoming IRQ's priority against the *currently active* priority threshold. If the new IRQ's priority is **numerically lower (higher in urgency)**, it is granted preemption rights.
3. **Hardware Stack Push:** The CPU automatically saves the current execution context — the **eight critical registers** — onto the Main Stack Pointer (MSP). This requires **8 CPU clock cycles** on Cortex-M3/M4.
4. **Vector Fetch & PC Load:** The Interrupt Controller supplies the address of the new ISR, and the CPU loads it into the Program Counter.
5. **ISR Execution Begins:** The pre-empted lower-priority ISR remains suspended on the stack; its stack frame is preserved.
6. **Further Nesting Check:** While inside the new ISR, the controller continues to monitor for even higher-priority requests.
7. **Return & Unstack:** When the higher-priority ISR completes (EXC_RETURN triggered), the CPU performs a 4-cycle unstack of the previous context and resumes.

### 2.2 Why Nested Handling Matters — The Engineering 'Why'

- **Determinism:** Guarantees worst-case interrupt response time, mandatory for control loops in **motor drives, airbag systems, and pacemakers**.
- **Efficiency:** Avoids polling loops that waste CPU cycles.
- **Modularity:** Each peripheral driver can be designed independently with its own ISR.
- **Real-Time Compliance:** Enables adherence to **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** and **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** task scheduling assumptions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** for interrupt latency and response time analysis. These appear frequently in Part-B numerical problems.

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Interrupt Latency ($t_{lat}$) | $t_{lat} = t_{detect} + t_{stack} + t_{vector}$ | Clock cycles |
| Stack Push Time (Cortex-M3/M4) | $t_{stack} = 8 \times T_{clk}$ | **8 registers**, fixed hardware cost |
| Vector Fetch Time | $t_{vector} = T_{clk}$ (flash with 0 wait state) | Up to **3 cycles** if flash wait states exist |
| Worst-Case ISR Response ($t_{resp}$) | $t_{resp} = t_{lat} + t_{ISR}$ | Clock cycles |
| Maximum Nested Latency ($L_{max}$) | $L_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{ISR,i} + N \cdot t_{context}$ | $N$ = nesting depth |
| Stack Memory Required ($M_{stack}$) | $M_{stack} = 8 \cdot N \cdot W_{reg} + t_{safety}$ | Bytes; $W_{reg}$ = register width in bytes |
| Effective Throughput Gain (Tail-Chain) | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{t_{unstk} + t_{stack}}{t_{ISR}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |

**Critical Constants to Memorise:**

- Cortex-M3/M4 **hardware stack frame size** = **8 words (32 bytes on Cortex-M4 with FPU disabled)**.
- With FPU enabled, the stack frame extends to **26 words (104 bytes)**.
- Default **PRIGROUP** field reset value = **0**, meaning 0 bits of group priority and 4 bits of sub-priority.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Worst-Case Interrupt Latency in a Nested System

**Problem Context:** A Cortex-M4 based microcontroller operates at $f_{clk} = 72 \text{ MHz}$. There are three interrupt sources active: Timer2 (low priority), UART1 (medium priority), and an External IRQ (highest priority). Each ISR execution time is given. Derive the worst-case latency observed by the highest priority IRQ.

**Given:**

- $T_{Timer2} = 4 \text{ \mu s}$
- $T_{UART1} = 2 \text{ \mu s}$
- $T_{EXTI} = 1.5 \text{ \mu s}$
- Stack push cycles = **8**
- Vector fetch = **1 cycle**

**Step 1: Compute Clock Period**

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s}$$

This gives the fundamental time quantum of the system.

**Step 2: Compute Hardware Stack Push Duration**

$$t_{stack} = 8 \times T_{clk} = \frac{8}{72 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s}$$

Numerically evaluating the denominator:

$$t_{stack} = \frac{8}{72} \times 10^{-6} = 0.1111 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

Thus:

$$t_{stack} = 0.1111 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 3: Compute Vector Fetch Duration**

$$t_{vector} = 1 \times T_{clk} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s} = 0.0139 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Latency Formula**

The interrupt latency for any incoming IRQ is the sum of stack push, vector fetch, and detection time. For Cortex-M, detection is essentially 0 (interrupt sampled every cycle):

$$t_{lat} = t_{stack} + t_{vector}$$

$$t_{lat} = 0.1111 + 0.0139 = 0.125 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 5: Compute Worst-Case Latency for the Highest Priority IRQ**

In the **worst case**, the EXTI arrives just as both Timer2 and UART1 ISRs are about to begin. The system must first complete any in-progress lower-priority ISR before reaching EXTI. However, since EXTI has the **highest priority**, it can preempt even mid-execution:

$$L_{max,EXTI} = t_{lat} = 0.125 \text{ \mu s}$$

This is independent of the lower-priority ISR execution times **because of preemption**.

**Step 6: Compute Worst-Case Latency for the Lowest Priority IRQ (Timer2)**

The Timer2 ISR, however, can only run after any pending UART1 and EXTI are serviced. Therefore:

$$L_{max,Timer2} = t_{lat} + T_{UART1} + T_{EXTI} + 2 \cdot t_{context}$$

$$L_{max,Timer2} = 0.125 + 2.0 + 1.5 + 2 \times 0.125$$

$$L_{max,Timer2} = 0.125 + 2.0 + 1.5 + 0.25 = 3.875 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 7: Interpretation & Engineering Insight**

> [!NOTE]
> This analytical result is **highly significant for real-time design**. The lowest-priority interrupt suffers **31× the latency** of the highest-priority interrupt. Designers must therefore carefully map **urgency** to **priority** — placing motor control loops and safety interrupts at the highest priority levels.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Priority vs. Latency Trade-off

| Priority Level | Max Observed Latency | Determinism Class | Suitable Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Highest (level 0) | $0.125 \text{ \mu s}$ | Hard real-time | Emergency stop, motor trip |
| Mid (level 2) | $1.625 \text{ \mu s}$ | Firm real-time | UART, communication |
| Low (level 4) | $3.875 \text{ \mu s}$ | Soft real-time | LED toggle, status polling |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flow Diagram — Nested Interrupt Dispatch Sequence

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the decision logic executed by the hardware interrupt controller upon each IRQ assertion, demonstrating how nested preemption is decided.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Boot and Main Loop Execution] --> B[IRQ Source Asserts Interrupt Request]
    B --> C{Compare Priority Against Currently Active Priority}
    C -->|New IRQ Priority Numerically Higher Urgency| D[Preempt Current Execution Context]
    C -->|New IRQ Priority Numerically Lower Urgency| E[Place IRQ in Pending Register Hold Queue]
    D --> F[CPU Hardware Push of 8 Critical Registers onto MSP Stack]
    F --> G[Vector Table Lookup for New ISR Address]
    G --> H[Load ISR Address into Program Counter PC]
    H --> I[Execute Higher Priority ISR Body]
    I --> J{Another Higher Urgency IRQ Arrives During Execution}
    J -->|Yes Nested Preemption Detected| D
    J -->|No Further Preemption| K[Complete Current ISR Execution]
    K --> L[Trigger EXC RETURN to Initiate Unstack]
    L --> M[Restore 8 Registers from MSP Stack]
    M --> N[Return to Original Preempted Context]
    N --> O[Resume Lower Priority ISR or Main Thread]
    E --> P[Re evaluate Priority When Current ISR Finishes]
    P --> C
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — NVIC Priority Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Peripheral A IRQ Source] --> NVIC[NVIC Priority Comparator Block]
    P2[Peripheral B IRQ Source] --> NVIC
    P3[Peripheral C IRQ Source] --> NVIC
    P4[Peripheral D IRQ Source] --> NVIC
    NVIC --> ARB[Arbitration and Mask Logic Unit]
    ARB --> VEC[Interrupt Vector Table in Flash Memory]
    VEC --> CPU[CPU Core Executes ISR with Automatic Stack Push]
    CPU --> STK[Main Stack Pointer MSP in SRAM]
    CPU --> RET[EXC RETURN Driven Return Path]
    RET --> STK
```

### 4.3 Stack Frame Visualization — Two-Level Deep Nesting

```
        HIGH MEMORY (Lower Address in Full Descending Stack)
        +-------------------------------+
        |  EXTI ISR's Local Variables   |  <-- SP after EXTI entry
        +-------------------------------+
        |  EXTI's saved R0..R3, R12,    |
        |  LR, PC, xPSR  (8 words)     |
        +-------------------------------+  <-- Nested stacking boundary
        |  UART1 ISR's Local Variables  |  <-- SP after UART1 entry (preempted)
        +-------------------------------+
        |  UART1's saved R0..R3, R12,   |
        |  LR, PC, xPSR  (8 words)     |
        +-------------------------------+
        |  Main thread's Local Stack    |
        +-------------------------------+
        LOW MEMORY (Higher Address)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The figure above illustrates **physical nesting** on the stack. Note that each nested ISR adds **8 words (32 bytes)** automatically by hardware before any software-driven register save occurs. This is why Cortex-M3/M4 ISRs are typically written with minimal register pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define nested interrupt handling. Why is it essential in real-time embedded systems?*

**Model Answer:**

A nested interrupt handler allows a currently executing Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) to be interrupted by a higher-priority IRQ. The CPU suspends the lower-priority ISR, automatically saves its execution context onto the stack via hardware stacking, executes the higher-priority ISR, and then returns to resume the preempted ISR.

It is essential in real-time systems because it guarantees **deterministic response time** for critical events. Without nesting, a long-running low-priority ISR could delay safety-critical interrupts (e.g., emergency shutdown), violating hard real-time deadlines and potentially causing system failure or physical harm.

*(Expected length: 3–4 lines. Marks awarded for: correct definition = 2 marks, real-time justification = 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between vectored and polled interrupt handling schemes.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Vectored Interrupt Handling | Polled Interrupt Handling |
|---|---|---|
| IRQ Identification | Hardware supplies ISR address via vector table | Software checks each peripheral flag sequentially |
| Response Time | Deterministic and fast (few cycles) | Variable and slow (proportional to flag count) |
| CPU Overhead | Minimal — hardware auto-dispatch | High — CPU cycles consumed in polling loop |
| Suitability | Real-time multi-source systems | Simple systems with few interrupt sources |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently **forget the hardware stack frame of 8 words** when calculating memory or latency — this costs 2 marks.
> 2. Confusing **priority numeric value with priority urgency** is the most common error. On ARM Cortex-M, **lower numeric priority = higher urgency**.
> 3. Do not write ISRs that call blocking functions (e.g., `printf`, long `delay` loops) — this destroys nesting benefit and is heavily penalized in design questions.
> 4. When computing worst-case latency, **always include the preempted lower-priority ISR execution times**, not just the latency constant.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC) in an ARM Cortex-M microcontroller. Describe the role of the **Interrupt Priority Register**, **Vector Table**, and **Interrupt Set-Enable Register**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Cortex-M4 system clocked at 50 MHz services three ISRs in a nested priority configuration:
- ISR-A (priority level 1): execution time 3 microseconds
- ISR-B (priority level 2): execution time 5 microseconds
- ISR-C (priority level 3): execution time 8 microseconds

Assuming hardware stack push of 8 words and vector fetch of 1 cycle, calculate:
(i) The interrupt latency for each level.
(ii) The worst-case observed latency for ISR-C.
(iii) The total stack memory consumed if maximum nesting depth is 3. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The NVIC is a tightly coupled peripheral inside the Cortex-M core that manages all IRQ sources. Its major blocks are:

1. **Interrupt Priority Registers (IPR):** Each IRQ source has an 8-bit priority field, but only the upper N bits are implemented (configurable via PRIGROUP). Lower numeric value means higher urgency.
2. **Vector Table:** A table of 32-bit ISR addresses stored in flash, beginning at address 0x00000000. On IRQ acceptance, the CPU fetches the ISR address directly from this table.
3. **Interrupt Set-Enable Register (ISER) and Clear-Enable Register (ICER):** These allow software to individually mask or unmask each of the interrupt lines.
4. **Interrupt Pending Set/Reset Registers (ISPR/ICPR):** These track pending IRQs awaiting service.
5. **Active Bit Register:** Tracks which IRQs are currently being serviced to support nesting logic.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for a functional representation.

When an IRQ is asserted, the NVIC arbitrates priorities, performs hardware stacking (8 words on MSP), fetches the vector, and starts the ISR — all without software intervention.

*(Awarding: 3 marks block diagram, 2 marks register explanations, 2 marks operation sequence.)*

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**(i) Interrupt Latency Calculation:**

Clock period:

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 0.02 \text{ \mu s}$$

Stack push duration:

$$t_{stack} = 8 \times T_{clk} = 8 \times 0.02 = 0.16 \text{ \mu s}$$

Vector fetch duration:

$$t_{vector} = 1 \times T_{clk} = 0.02 \text{ \mu s}$$

Total base latency:

$$t_{lat} = t_{stack} + t_{vector} = 0.16 + 0.02 = 0.18 \text{ \mu s}$$

This is identical for all three ISRs since it depends only on hardware, not priority.

**(ii) Worst-Case Latency for ISR-C (lowest priority):**
