ISR-C can be delayed by ISR-A and ISR-B if they become pending. Worst-case latency:

$$L_{max,C} = t_{lat} + T_{ISR-A} + T_{ISR-B} + 2 \times t_{context\_save}$$

$$L_{max,C} = 0.18 + 3.0 + 5.0 + 2 \times 0.18$$

$$L_{max,C} = 0.18 + 3.0 + 5.0 + 0.36 = 8.54 \text{ \mu s}$$

**(iii) Total Stack Memory for Maximum Nesting Depth = 3:**

Each ISR context requires 8 words × 4 bytes = 32 bytes (Cortex-M4, no FPU).

$$M_{stack} = 3 \times 32 = 96 \text{ bytes}$$

If local variables within ISRs use additional stack, this must be added on top.

*(Awarding: 3 marks for part (i), 2 marks for part (ii), 2 marks for part (iii).)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **hardware stacking mechanism** of ARM Cortex-M during nested interrupt entry. Why is this design superior to software-based context saving? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the **Tail-Chaining** and **Late Arrival** optimization techniques used in NVIC for reducing interrupt overhead. Illustrate with a timing diagram description. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

During nested interrupt entry, the Cortex-M hardware automatically pushes the following 8 registers onto the MSP stack: **R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR (R14), PC (R15), and xPSR**. This process takes exactly 8 clock cycles (single-cycle bus write).

**Why hardware stacking is superior:**

1. **Determinism:** The 8-cycle cost is fixed and predictable, regardless of ISR code.
2. **No prologue required:** ISR author does not manually push registers, reducing code size and bugs.
3. **Atomicity:** Context save cannot be interrupted mid-way.
4. **Performance:** Software push of 8 registers would consume 16+ cycles and pipeline stalls.

With FPU enabled, additional lazy stacking extends the frame to 26 words, but the **FPU registers are stacked only on actual use**, preserving performance.

*(Awarding: 3 marks for register listing, 4 marks for advantages.)*

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Tail-Chaining:**
When an ISR completes and there is a pending IRQ in the NVIC, instead of fully unstacking and then re-stacking for the next ISR, the NVIC skips the unstack-stack cycle and directly fetches the next vector. This saves **up to 12 cycles per back-to-back interrupt**, dramatically improving throughput.

**Late Arrival:**
If a higher-priority IRQ arrives **during** the stacking phase of a currently entering lower-priority ISR, the NVIC abandons the lower-priority fetch and immediately services the late-arriving higher-priority IRQ. The original lower-priority vector is still preserved and serviced later. This minimizes latency for true urgent events.

**Timing Impact (textual representation):**

```
Standard:   |---Stack A---|--ISR A--|---Unstack A---|---Stack B---|--ISR B--|
Tail-Chain: |---Stack A---|--ISR A--|              |---ISR B Entry (Vector only)--|--ISR B--|
Late Arr.:  |--Stack A start--|LATE IRQ|--ISR Late--|--Complete Stk A--|--ISR A--|
```

*(Awarding: 3 marks tail-chaining explanation, 3 marks late-arrival explanation, 1 mark timing impact.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Nested Interrupt Handler** = preemption-based hierarchical ISR servicing, enabled by NVIC or equivalent priority controller.
- **Hardware stacking** of 8 registers (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) takes **8 cycles** on Cortex-M3/M4.
- **Lower numeric priority value = higher urgency** in ARM Cortex-M (e.g., priority 0 is highest).
- **Tail-Chaining** reduces back-to-back interrupt overhead to nearly zero context switch cost.
- **Late Arrival** mechanism ensures truly urgent IRQs are not delayed by an in-progress stacking sequence.
- **Worst-case latency** = $t_{lat}$ + sum of all preempting higher-priority ISR execution times + context save overheads.
- **Maximum stack consumption** = nesting depth × 32 bytes (no FPU) or × 104 bytes (FPU enabled).
- ISRs must be **short, non-blocking, and re-entrant safe** to fully leverage nested handling.
- The **PRIGROUP field** in AIRCR configures how the 8-bit priority field splits into group priority and sub-priority.
- **Vector table relocation** via VTOR allows the table to be moved to SRAM for bootloader applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Always pair your explanation with a **priority table or timing diagram**. Examiners award marks generously for visual representation in interrupt-related questions, even in Part-A short answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Nested Interrupt Handler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Non-Nested Interrupt Handler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Non-Nested Interrupt Handler

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

A **Non-Nested Interrupt Handler** (also called a *flat* or *single-level* interrupt service routine) is an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) in which **all other interrupt sources are globally masked** for the entire duration of the current ISR's execution. Once the microcontroller enters the handler, the **Global Interrupt Enable (GIE)** bit is cleared (or equivalently, interrupts are disabled) so that no other interrupt — irrespective of its priority — can preempt the currently executing ISR. Only after the ISR completes its execution (via the `RETFIE` instruction in PIC, or `BX LR` / exception return in ARM) is the GIE bit re-enabled, allowing pending interrupts to be serviced sequentially.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned to the PIC18F/dsPIC and ARM Cortex-M0 architectures, a non-nested handler:
- Saves only the **context of the foreground (main) program** and the currently executing ISR.
- Does **NOT re-enable interrupts** during the ISR body.
- Uses a **single hardware stack frame** per interrupt invocation.
- Guarantees **deterministic, non-preemptive execution** of critical sections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> *"A Non-Nested Interrupt Handler is an ISR design where, upon entry, the Global Interrupt Enable bit is cleared so that no further interrupts can interrupt the current handler, and the interrupt is re-enabled only upon exit via RETFIE."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **single-window bank teller** (the CPU). A customer walks in and rings the service bell (an **interrupt request flag**, e.g., `PIR1<INT1IF>`). You stop what you are doing, press the **"Do Not Disturb"** sign on your window (clear the **GIE bit**), and attend only to that one customer. While you are helping them, no other customer — even a VIP — is allowed to interrupt you. You finish the work, ring the bell again (set **GIE = 1** via `RETFIE`), and only then attend to the next person in line.

If another customer had interrupted you mid-way (a **nested** interrupt), it would be like a VIP jumping the queue — useful in real life, but mathematically harder to analyze, slower (because of double context-saves), and prone to stack overflow if customers keep jumping. The **non-nested** approach is simpler, safer, and is the *default* way KTU examiners expect students to write ISRs unless the question explicitly demands nesting.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Registers

The following standard registers/metrics are central to a non-nested handler in the **PIC18F family** (the KTU-default architecture for PECPT631 Module 4):

| Symbol | Full Form | Default / Typical Value | Role |
|--------|-----------|------------------------|------|
| **GIE** | Global Interrupt Enable | Cleared (`0`) inside ISR | Master switch |
| **PEIE** | Peripheral Interrupt Enable | Cleared (`0`) inside ISR | Sub-master switch |
| **IPEN** | Interrupt Priority Enable | `0` for non-priority (compat. mode) | Disables priority |
| **RCON<IPEN>** | Reset/Control register | `0` | Compatibility mode selector |
| **Stack depth** | Hardware call-return stack | **31 levels** (PIC18F) | Limits nesting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IPEN = 0?** In PIC18F, when **IPEN = 0** (Compatibility Mode), the architecture *forces* all interrupts into a single, non-nested, "flat" priority group. This is precisely the non-nested handler model. The moment you set **IPEN = 1**, you transition to the *priority* (potentially nested) model.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

A Non-Nested Interrupt Handler in PIC18F (or ARM Cortex-M with PRIMASK) executes the following **strict sequence**:

**Step 1 — Hardware Detection:**
The peripheral sets its **Interrupt Flag (IF)** bit (e.g., `PIR1<ADIF>`, `PIR2<TMR3IF>`). If both the corresponding **Interrupt Enable (IE)** bit and the **GIE/PEIE** master bits are set, the CPU completes the current instruction, then vectors to the ISR.

**Step 2 — Hardware-Automatic Context Save (Partial):**
The PIC18F hardware automatically pushes the **Program Counter (PC)** onto the hardware return-address stack. **No other registers are auto-saved** — this is a critical KTU point.

**Step 3 — Software-Required Context Save:**
Inside the ISR, the programmer **must manually save** any registers that will be modified (`WREG`, `STATUS`, `BSR`, `PROD`, `PCLATH`, `TABLAT`, `TBLPTR`). This is typically done via `MOVWF` into a temporary register or a software stack.

```assembly
PUSH:
    MOVWF W_TEMP       ; Save W register
    MOVFF STATUS, STATUS_TEMP
    MOVFF BSR, BSR_TEMP
    MOVFF PCLATH, PCLATH_TEMP
    MOVFF FSR0L, FSR0L_TEMP
    MOVFF FSR0H, FSR0H_TEMP
    MOVFF FSR1L, FSR1L_TEMP
    MOVFF FSR1H, FSR1H_TEMP
    MOVFF PRODL, PRODL_TEMP
    MOVFF PRODH, PRODH_TEMP
```

**Step 4 — Clear Conditional Flag:**
The corresponding **IF** bit must be **cleared in software** before re-enabling interrupts (or before exit, depending on peripheral). Failure to do this creates an infinite re-entry loop — a classic KTU pitfall.

**Step 5 — Execute ISR Body:**
The actual interrupt-handling code runs. Crucially, **GIE remains cleared** (or `PRIMASK = 1` in ARM) throughout. No other interrupt can preempt.

**Step 6 — Software Context Restore:**
```assembly
POP:
    MOVFF PRODH_TEMP, PRODH
    MOVFF PRODL_TEMP, PRODL
    MOVFF FSR1H_TEMP, FSR1H
    MOVFF FSR1L_TEMP, FSR1L
    MOVFF FSR0H_TEMP, FSR0H
    MOVFF FSR0L_TEMP, FSR0L
    MOVFF PCLATH_TEMP, PCLATH
    MOVFF BSR_TEMP, BSR
    MOVFF STATUS_TEMP, STATUS
    MOVF  W_TEMP, W
```

**Step 7 — Return and Re-Enable:**
The `RETFIE` (RETurn From Interrupt Enable) instruction **atomically** pops PC from the hardware stack AND sets **GIE = 1**, re-arming the interrupt subsystem.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It's Necessary | What Fails Without It |
|------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Hardware PC push | So we know where to resume | Program counter corruption → crash |
| Software context save | ISR may clobber main's registers | Main program computes wrong results |
| Clear IF flag | Otherwise ISR re-triggers instantly | Infinite ISR loop / system hang |
| No GIE inside body | Defines "non-nested" semantics | Becomes a *nested* handler accidentally |
| RETFIE | Atomic PC-pop + GIE-set | Race condition: pending ISR missed |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Interrupt Timing

For latency and timing calculations (frequently asked in KTU 2024 numerical questions):

$$
T_{latency} = T_{inst\_finish} + T_{vector} + T_{context\_save}
$$

$$
T_{ISR} = T_{context\_save} + T_{body} + T_{context\_restore} + T_{RETFIE}
$$

$$
\text{Max nesting depth (hardware-limited)} = \text{Stack depth} = \mathbf{31} \text{ (PIC18F4520)}
$$

$$
f_{cy} = \frac{f_{osc}}{4}
$$

$$
T_{cy} = \frac{4}{f_{osc}} \quad [\text{seconds}]
$$

$$
T_{ISR,total} = N_{inst} \times T_{cy}
$$

> **No vertical pipe characters used — table syntax is preserved.**

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|-----------|--------|---------|------|---------------------|
| Instruction cycle time | $T_{cy}$ | $4/f_{osc}$ | s | $f_{osc} \le \mathbf{40\,\text{MHz}}$ (PIC18F4520) |
| ISR total time | $T_{ISR}$ | $N \times T_{cy}$ | s | Must be $<$ next event period |
| Hardware stack depth | $N_{stack}$ | **31** levels | levels | Non-nested → max 1 used |
| Worst-case latency | $T_{lat,max}$ | $4 \cdot T_{cy} + T_{vector}$ | s | $T_{vector} = \mathbf{4 \cdot T_{cy}}$ (PIC18) |
| Priority disabled | IPEN | `0` | — | Forces non-nested behavior |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Non-nested handlers are the **industry default** for safety-critical, hard-real-time systems:
- **Automotive ECUs** (ABS, airbag controllers) — predictable worst-case latency.
- **Medical devices** (insulin pumps, pacemakers) — never miss a deadline.
- **Industrial PLCs** — deterministic scan times.
- **RTOS kernels** — the OS-tick interrupt itself runs as a non-nested handler before scheduling.

Where nesting is *required* (multi-rate motor control, voice+data DSP), engineers switch to ARM Cortex-M with **NVIC priority grouping** — covered later in the KTU syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Non-Nested ISR Frequency

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A PIC18F4520 is clocked at $f_{osc} = 20\,\text{MHz}$. An interrupt fires every $T_{event} = 100\,\mu s$. The ISR body (excluding context save/restore) executes $N_{body} = 80$ instructions. Context save = 14 instructions, context restore = 14 instructions. Verify whether a non-nested handler can keep up, and compute the maximum sustainable interrupt rate.

**Step 1 — Compute Instruction Cycle Time**

$$
f_{osc} = 20 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}
$$

$$
T_{cy} = \frac{4}{f_{osc}} = \frac{4}{20 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{s} = \mathbf{200\,\text{ns}}
$$

This is the time for **one** PIC instruction (most are single-cycle; branches and table reads take 2).

**Step 2 — Total Instruction Count Per ISR**

$$
N_{total} = N_{save} + N_{body} + N_{restore} + N_{RETFIE}
$$

$$
N_{total} = 14 + 80 + 14 + 2 = \mathbf{110 \text{ instructions}}
$$

(`RETFIE` is 2-cycle on PIC18F.)

**Step 3 — Total ISR Execution Time**

$$
T_{ISR} = N_{total} \times T_{cy} = 110 \times 200 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
T_{ISR} = 22{,}000 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s} = \mathbf{22\,\mu s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare With Event Period**

$$
T_{event} = 100\,\mu s \quad ; \quad T_{ISR} = 22\,\mu s
$$

Since $T_{ISR} \ll T_{event}$, the system keeps up **comfortably**. The "headroom" for the main loop is:

$$
T_{main,available} = T_{event} - T_{ISR} = 100 - 22 = \mathbf{78\,\mu s}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Sustainable Interrupt Rate (non-nested, no main code)**

$$
f_{ISR,max} = \frac{1}{T_{ISR}} = \frac{1}{22 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
f_{ISR,max} \approx \mathbf{45{,}454\,\text{Hz}} \;\;(\approx 45.5\,\text{kHz})
$$

**Step 6 — CPU Utilization**

$$
U_{CPU} = \frac{T_{ISR}}{T_{event}} \times 100\% = \frac{22}{100} \times 100\% = \mathbf{22\%}
$$

The main program can use the remaining 78%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In a *non-nested* design, the worst-case latency is bounded by $T_{ISR}$ itself, **not** by cascaded ISRs. This is why non-nested handlers are used in hard real-time safety systems — the worst-case blocking time is provably finite.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Handler Models

| Property | Non-Nested Handler | Nested Handler | Vectored Handler |
|----------|--------------------|----------------|------------------|
| GIE inside ISR | Cleared | Set (selectively) | Set per-priority |
| Context save depth | **1 frame** | Up to N frames | Up to N frames |
| Stack usage | **Minimum (1 level)** | Up to 31 levels | Up to 31 levels |
| Latency | Deterministic, flat | Priority-dependent | Priority-dependent |
| Code complexity | **Lowest** | Medium | High |
| KTU typical marks weight | **High** (Module 4 focus) | Medium | Low |
| Hardware required | Any PIC/8051 | PIC18 with IPEN=1 | ARM NVIC |
| Risk of stack overflow | **Negligible** | High if unbounded | High if unbounded |
| Determinism | **Provable** | Conditional | Conditional |

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Context Save Register Count (PIC18F4520)

The PIC18F4520 manual mandates saving the following **mandatory** registers for an ISR to be re-entrant-safe:

$$
N_{ctx,save} = \text{count}(\text{WREG}, \text{STATUS}, \text{BSR}, \text{PCLATH}, \text{FSR0L}, \text{FSR0H}, \text{FSR1L}, \text{FSR1H}, \text{PRODL}, \text{PRODH}) = \mathbf{10}
$$

For a fully re-entrant *math-using* ISR, add `TABLAT` and the `TBLPTRH/L` pair → **13 registers**.

Each `MOVFF` (move from file to file) takes **2 instruction cycles**. Therefore:

$$
T_{ctx,save} = N_{regs} \times 2 \times T_{cy} = 10 \times 2 \times 200\,\text{ns} = \mathbf{4\,\mu s}
$$

$$
T_{ctx,restore} = \mathbf{4\,\mu s} \text{ (symmetric)}
$$

$$
T_{ctx,overhead} = T_{ctx,save} + T_{ctx,restore} = \mathbf{8\,\mu s}
$$

This overhead is the **price of non-nesting** — but it is paid once per interrupt, not recursively.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Non-Nested Handler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Program Loop] --> B[Peripheral Event Occurs e.g. Timer Overflow]
    B --> C{IF bit set AND IE set AND GIE set}
    C -- No --> A
    C -- Yes --> D[Hardware Auto Push PC to Stack]
    D --> E[CPU Vectors to ISR Address 0x08 or 0x18]
    E --> F[Software Context Save 10 registers]
    F --> G[Clear IF Flag in Software]
    G --> H[Execute ISR Body GIE remains 0]
    H --> I[Software Context Restore]
    I --> J[RETFIE Instruction Atomic PC Pop plus GIE 1]
    J --> K[Return to Main Program]
    K --> A
    H -.->|Other interrupt arrives| L[IRQ Pending Flag Set but Ignored]
    L --> K
```

### 4.2 State Diagram — ISR Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Foreground: Power On Reset
    Foreground --> InterruptDetect: GIE=1, IE=1, IF=1
    InterruptDetect --> ISREntry: HW push PC
    ISREntry --> ContextSave: vector taken
    ContextSave --> ISRBody: regs saved
    ISRBody --> ContextRestore: body done
    ContextRestore --> ISRExit: regs restored
    ISRExit --> Foreground: RETFIE sets GIE=1
    ISRBody --> PendingBlocked: other IRQ arrives, GIE=0
    PendingBlocked --> ISRExit: waits until RETFIE
    ISRExit --> InterruptDetect: next ISR serviced
```

### 4.3 Register-Level Save Area (Software Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAM[RAM GPR Bank 0]
        W_T[0x50 W_TEMP]
        ST_T[0x51 STATUS_TEMP]
        BS_T[0x52 BSR_TEMP]
        PC_T[0x53 PCLATH_TEMP]
        F0L[0x54 FSR0L_TEMP]
        F0H[0x55 FSR0H_TEMP]
        F1L[0x56 FSR1L_TEMP]
        F1H[0x57 FSR1H_TEMP]
        PDL[0x58 PRODL_TEMP]
        PDH[0x59 PRODH_TEMP]
    end
    ISR[ISR Entry] --> W_T
    W_T --> ST_T
    ST_T --> BS_T
    BS_T --> PC_T
    PC_T --> F0L
    F0L --> F0H
    F0H --> F1L
    F1L --> F1H
    F1H --> PDL
    PDL --> PDH
    PDH --> BODY[ISR Body Executes]
    BODY --> PDH
    PDH --> F1H
    F1H --> F1L
    F1L --> F0H
    F0H --> F0L
    F0L --> PC_T
    PC_T --> BS_T
    BS_T --> ST_T
    ST_T --> W_T
    W_T --> RETFIE[RETFIE Exit]
```

### 4.4 Timing Diagram — Non-Nested vs. Nested Interrupt Behavior

```mermaid
gantt
    title Non-Nested Handler — Interrupt Timeline (GIE State)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section CPU Main
    Main Running (GIE=1) :a1, 0, 10
    section Interrupt Event
    IRQ0 arrives at t=10 :crit, 10, 0
    section ISR0 (GIE=0)
    Context Save :a2, 10, 2
    ISR0 Body :a3, 12, 6
    Context Restore :a4, 18, 2
    RETFIE (GIE=1) :a5, 20, 0
    section Pending IRQ
    IRQ1 blocked from t=10 to t=20 :a6, 10, 10
    section Main resumed
    Main Running (GIE=1) :a7, 20, 4
    section IRQ1 serviced after RETFIE
    IRQ1 ISR executes :a8, 24, 5
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The second interrupt `IRQ1` arrives at $t=10$ while `ISR0` is still running. Because `GIE=0` inside `ISR0`, `IRQ1` is **buffered in its IF flag** but does *not* trigger until `RETFIE` at $t=20$. This is the *defining* property of non-nested handling.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Define a **Non-Nested Interrupt Handler**. Why is it preferred for hard real-time systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Non-Nested Interrupt Handler is an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) in which, upon entry, the **Global Interrupt Enable (GIE)** bit is automatically (or via `BCLR INTCON, GIE`) cleared, preventing any other interrupt — irrespective of priority — from preempting the currently executing handler. The ISR runs to completion, performs its context restore, and then uses the **`RETFIE`** instruction to atomically pop the return address from the hardware stack and re-set the **GIE** bit.

It is preferred for hard real-time systems because:
1. The **worst-case interrupt latency** is bounded and equal to the longest single ISR execution time, not a sum of nested ISRs.
2. Only **one context-save frame** is needed, eliminating stack-overflow risk.
3. The behavior is **deterministic and provable**, satisfying schedulability analysis (Rate Monotonic, Deadline Monotonic).

*(Full 3 marks awarded only if both the definition and the *two* real-time advantages are mentioned.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** List the **mandatory registers** to be saved by software on entering an ISR in PIC18F, and justify why they cannot be auto-saved by hardware.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Mandatory registers: **WREG, STATUS, BSR, PCLATH, FSR0L, FSR0H, FSR1L, FSR1H, PRODL, PRODH** (10 registers; +3 if using table-read math).

Hardware does not auto-save them because:
1. PIC18F was designed for **minimal hardware context overhead** — auto-save would slow every interrupt by ~20 cycles.
2. Some registers (e.g., `WREG`, `STATUS`) are **frequently used by the main program** in non-trivial patterns; the hardware cannot know which the ISR will clobber.
3. It gives the **programmer full control** over optimization — an ISR that doesn't touch, say, `FSR1`, can skip saving it.

*(Award full marks only if all 10 registers are listed and at least two justifications are given.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **complete execution flow** of a Non-Nested Interrupt Handler in PIC18F4520. Clearly mark the steps where (i) the IF flag is cleared, and (ii) GIE is re-enabled. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ADC conversion-complete interrupt (`PIR1<ADIF>`) on a PIC18F clocked at $f_{osc} = 40\,\text{MHz}$ triggers an ISR. The ISR performs the following: context save (12 instructions), ADC read (5 instructions), scaling math (30 instructions), storage to RAM (4 instructions), context restore (12 instructions), `RETFIE` (2 cycles). Calculate: (i) $T_{cy}$, (ii) total ISR time, (iii) the maximum ADC sampling rate if the CPU spends **at most 30%** of its time in the ISR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flowchart (already drawn in Section 4.1 of these notes — students should redraw it for full marks):**

Verbal description for the answer script:
1. Main loop executes with `GIE = 1`.
2. Peripheral event sets `IF` bit.
3. Hardware checks `IF AND IE AND GIE` → if true, current instruction finishes.
4. Hardware pushes **PC** onto the 31-level hardware return-stack.
5. CPU vectors to ISR address **0x08** (high vector) or **0x18** (low vector).
6. Software saves 10 mandatory registers via `MOVFF`.
7. **(i) IF flag cleared** in software (`BCLR PIR1, ADIF`).
8. ISR body executes with **`GIE = 0`** — no preemption possible.
9. Software restores 10 registers in reverse order.
10. **`RETFIE`** executes — (ii) atomic PC-pop **plus** `GIE = 1`.
11. Execution resumes at the instruction after the original interrupt point.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *"RETFIE re-enables interrupts at the start of ISR"* — this is **wrong** and costs 2 marks. RETFIE only executes *at the end*. Also, students forget that **IF flag must be cleared in software** (not just by hardware).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Compute $T_{cy}$:**

$$
f_{osc} = 40 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}
$$

$$
T_{cy} = \frac{4}{f_{osc}} = \frac{4}{40 \times 10^{6}} = \mathbf{0.1\,\mu s} = \mathbf{100\,ns}
$$

**(ii) Total ISR Time:**

$$
N_{total} = 12 + 5 + 30 + 4 + 12 + 2 = \mathbf{65\,\text{instructions}}
$$

$$
T_{ISR} = 65 \times 100\,\text{ns} = \mathbf{6.5\,\mu s}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Sampling Rate at 30% CPU Budget:**

$$
T_{sampling,min} = \frac{T_{ISR}}{0.30} = \frac{6.5\,\mu s}{0.30} \approx \mathbf{21.67\,\mu s}
$$

$$
f_{sampling,max} = \frac{1}{T_{sampling,min}} = \frac{1}{21.67 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
f_{sampling,max} \approx \mathbf{46{,}154\,\text{Hz}} \;\;(\approx 46.2\,\text{kHz})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Errors:**
> - Using $T_{cy} = 1/f_{osc}$ instead of $4/f_{osc}$ → loses 1 mark.
> - Forgetting that `RETFIE` is **2 cycles**, not 1 → loses 0.5 marks.
> - Using 50% instead of 30% budget → loses 1 mark.
> - Reporting answer in Hz without unit → loses 0.5 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Supplementary]`

**(a)** Compare **Non-Nested** and **Nested** Interrupt Handlers in PIC18F under the following heads: (i) GIE handling, (ii) context save stack usage, (iii) hardware stack depth implications, (iv) suitability for hard real-time, (v) code complexity, (vi) re-entrancy risk. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete PIC18F assembly **Non-Nested Interrupt Handler** for `INT0` (external interrupt on RB0). The ISR should: toggle PORTC bit 0, increment a counter at `0x100`, and exit cleanly. Show initialization code for `TRISB`, `TRISC`, `INTCON`, and `INTCON2`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Head | Non-Nested | Nested |
|------|-----------|--------|
| (i) GIE inside ISR | **Cleared (0)** throughout | May be set per-priority |
| (ii) Stack frames | **1 frame max** | Up to N frames |
| (iii) HW stack depth | Uses **1 of 31** | Risk of all-31 overflow |
| (iv) Real-time suitability | **Excellent (deterministic)** | Conditional on priority analysis |
| (v) Code complexity | **Low** | Medium-high |
| (vi) Re-entrancy | **Safe** | Requires explicit design |

*(Award 1 mark per correct row × 6 rows = 6 marks; 1 mark for overall conclusion.)*

**(b) PIC18F Assembly Code:**

```assembly
; ========== Non-Nested ISR for INT0 (RB0) ==========
; Vector at 0x08 for High Priority, but here IPEN=0
; so all interrupts share 0x08 vector (Compatibility Mode)

    ORG 0x0000
    GOTO MAIN

    ORG 0x0008              ; Interrupt vector
    GOTO ISR_INT0

; ----------- Context Save RAM Locations (Bank 0) -----------
    CBLOCK 0x060
        W_TEMP
        STATUS_TEMP
        BSR_TEMP
    ENDC

; ============== MAIN PROGRAM ==============
MAIN:
    ; --- Initialize Ports ---
    MOVLW 0x0F
    MOVWF TRISB           ; RB0..RB3 = inputs, RB4..RB7 = outputs
    CLRF  TRISC           ; PORTC all outputs

    ; --- Initialize INTCON ---
    MOVLW B'00010000'     ; INTEDG0=1 (rising edge), TMR0IF=0, INT0IF=0
    MOVWF INTCON
    BCF   INTCON2, INTEDG0  ; (Alternative: rising edge)
    BSF   INTCON, INT0IE  ; Enable INT0 interrupt
    BSF   INTCON, GIE     ; Global Interrupt Enable = 1

    ; --- Counter at 0x100 ---
    CLRF  0x100           ; Clear counter
    CLRF  0x101           ; High byte of counter

MAIN_LOOP:
    BRA   MAIN_LOOP       ; Idle (real main work goes here)

; ============== ISR (NON-NESTED) ==============
ISR_INT0:
    ; --- Context Save (mandatory minimum) ---
    MOVWF W_TEMP
    MOVFF STATUS, STATUS_TEMP
    MOVFF BSR, BSR_TEMP

    ; --- Clear IF flag IMMEDIATELY ---
    BCF   INTCON, INT0IF  ; Clear INT0 flag

    ; --- ISR Body (GIE=0, no nesting) ---
    BTG   PORTC, 0        ; Toggle RC0

    INCF  0x100, F         ; Increment low byte of counter
    BTFSC STATUS, Z        ; Check if rolled over
    INCF  0x101, F         ; If yes, increment high byte

    ; --- Context Restore ---
    MOVFF BSR_TEMP, BSR
    MOVFF STATUS_TEMP, STATUS
    MOVF  W_TEMP, W

    ; --- Return & Auto-Re-Enable GIE ---
    RETFIE                ; PC pop + GIE=1 (atomic)

    END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting `BCF INTCON, INT0IF`** inside the ISR — causes infinite re-entry. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. **`RETFIE FAST` or `RETURN`** instead of `RETFIE` — interrupt will never re-fire. *(−2 marks)*
> 3. **Not saving `STATUS`** — bank bits get corrupted; wrong RAM is accessed. *(−2 marks)*
> 4. **Setting `IPEN = 1` accidentally** — this converts the design to a priority (potentially nested) model, contradicting the question. *(−2 marks)*
> 5. **Not setting `TRISB, 0` = 1** (input) — INT0 cannot detect edge. *(−1 mark)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Non-Nested Interrupt Handler"**

- ✅ **Definition:** ISR where `GIE = 0` throughout body; only `RETFIE` re-enables.
- ✅ **Two master bits in PIC18F:** `GIE` (global) and `PEIE` (peripheral).
- ✅ **Compatibility Mode** = `IPEN = 0` → *forces* non-nested behaviour.
- ✅ **Hardware auto-saves ONLY the PC**; everything else is the programmer's job.
- ✅ **Mandatory software-saved registers (10):** `WREG, STATUS, BSR, PCLATH, FSR0L/H, FSR1L/H, PRODL/H`.
- ✅ **IF flag must be cleared in software** to avoid infinite re-entry.
- ✅ **`RETFIE`** is the only instruction that *atomically* pops PC and sets `GIE = 1`.
- ✅ **Worst-case ISR time formula:** $T_{ISR} = N_{total} \times T_{cy}$ where $T_{cy} = 4/f_{osc}$.
- ✅ **Hardware stack depth = 31** (PIC18F4520); non-nested uses only 1.
- ✅ **ARM Cortex-M equivalent:** `PRIMASK = 1` inside ISR; CPSIE I on exit.
- ✅ **Real-time suitability:** Hard real-time = non-nested (deterministic); Soft real-time = nested acceptable.
- ✅ **Pitfall to avoid:** Never `BSF INTCON, GIE` *inside* an ISR body — it converts your handler into a nested one silently.

> **End of Module 4 — Non-Nested Interrupt Handler Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Interrupt Stack Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Interrupt Stack Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In advanced microcontroller architectures (specifically the **ARM Cortex-M** family and similar RISC-based cores), a **Basic Interrupt Stack Design** refers to the dedicated, hardware-managed region of SRAM (Static RAM) reserved for the temporary storage of the processor's execution context when an interrupt request (IRQ) or exception is serviced. This memory region operates on the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle and is governed by a dedicated hardware register known as the **Stack Pointer (SP)**, which in the ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS) is physically mapped to **R13**.

When an interrupt is accepted by the Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC), the processor automatically pushes a structured set of registers onto the stack — a process known as **stacking**. Upon returning from the interrupt service routine (ISR) via a special return instruction (e.g., `EXC_RETURN` in Cortex-M), the registers are automatically popped back — a process called **unstacking**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):**
> The basic interrupt stack is *not* a software abstraction. In Cortex-M cores, hardware automatically saves **xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0** — an **8-word hardware-stacked frame** — upon exception entry. This is a critical distinction from older architectures (8051, AVR) where context saving was purely the programmer's responsibility.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Busy Office Worker's Inbox Tray"

Imagine a busy office worker (the **CPU core**) who is in the middle of writing a long report (the **main program execution**). Suddenly, the phone rings (the **interrupt request**). The worker cannot lose track of what they were writing — they must remember:
- The exact sentence they were on (the **Program Counter / PC**)
- Which pen they were holding (the **working registers**)
- Which file folder was open (the **status flags / xPSR**)

So the worker pulls out an **Inbox Tray (the Stack)** from the drawer and drops all their current work papers into it, *one on top of the other*. The tray gets a marker (the **Stack Pointer**) pointing to the top paper. Now the worker answers the phone (the **ISR runs**). When finished, the worker lifts the papers back from the tray in *reverse order* (LIFO), restoring their desk to the exact previous state, and resumes the report.

> [!NOTE]
> The "Inbox Tray" analogy clarifies three critical rules of interrupt stacking:
> 1. **LIFO order** is mandatory — restoring happens in the exact reverse sequence of saving.
> 2. **Automatic hardware intervention** — the office worker (CPU) doesn't manually shuffle papers; the desk mechanism (hardware) does.
> 3. **Fixed tray size (Stack Depth)** — if too many interrupts occur simultaneously, the tray can overflow onto the floor (a **stack overflow crash**).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Standard Value (Cortex-M3/M4) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware-stacked frame size** | **8 words = 32 bytes** | Fixed cost per interrupt entry |
| **Stack word width** | **32 bits (4 bytes)** | Matches native data bus |
| **Stack Pointer alignment** | **8-byte aligned (dual-word)** | ARM AAPCS requirement |
| **Main Stack Pointer (MSP)** | Reset default SP | Used by Kernel & all exceptions |
| **Process Stack Pointer (PSP)** | Thread mode only | Used by user application tasks |
| **Stack growth direction** | **Full-descending** | SP decrements on PUSH |
| **Stack empty register value** | **0xA5A5A5A5** (initialization pattern) | Used to detect un-used stack |
| **Recommended stack size** | **≥ 256 bytes minimum** | For simple RTOS-less applications |

> [!WARNING]
> The values listed above apply to **32-bit ARM Cortex-M** cores. For Cortex-M0/M0+, the hardware-stacked frame is **8 words** but accessed as individual 32-bit pushes. For 16-bit controllers (e.g., MSP430), the frame size differs (typically 6 words for the basic context).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Anatomy of an Interrupt Stack Frame

When an exception occurs and is accepted by the NVIC, the Cortex-M core executes an implicit **hardware stacking sequence**. This is not executed as software instructions; the hardware physically writes to memory pointed to by SP.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Pre-emption Decision (NVIC Arbitration):**
   - The NVIC compares the priority of the incoming IRQ against the currently active exception's **BASEPRI / PRIMASK / FAULTMASK** registers.
   - If the new IRQ has **numerically lower priority value** (higher logical priority), it pre-empts the current execution.

2. **Stack Pointer Selection:**
   - The core checks the **CONTROL[1]** bit to determine which SP is in use.
   - For **all exceptions (Handlers)**: **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** is *always* used, regardless of thread mode SP selection.

3. **Hardware Push Sequence (Descending Stack):**
   - SP is decremented by 4 bytes for each push.
   - Eight registers are pushed in this fixed order:
     1. `xPSR` — Program Status Register (flags, interrupt number)
     2. `PC` — Program Counter (return address of interrupted instruction)
     3. `LR` — Link Register (return mode)
     4. `R12` — General-purpose scratch register
     5. `R3, R2, R1, R0` — Argument and result registers (AAPCS)

4. **Vector Fetch:**
   - The NVIC fetches the ISR address from the **Vector Table** in Flash/ROM.
   - PC is loaded with the ISR entry point.

5. **ISR Execution:**
   - The ISR runs. It may push additional registers (`R4-R11`) manually if required.

6. **Hardware Pop Sequence (on `EXC_RETURN`):**
   - On encountering `BX LR` or `POP {PC}` with LR containing `EXC_RETURN`, the hardware reverses the push.
   - Registers are popped in reverse order: `R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR`.
   - SP increments back to its original value.

## 2.2 Stack Memory Layout — Visual Block Diagram

```
HIGH ADDRESS  (0x2002 0000 + Stack_Size)
  ┌─────────────────────────────┐  ← Initial SP after reset
  │      Free Stack Space       │
  │                             │
  ├─────────────────────────────┤
  │      [Unused area —         │
  │       filled with 0xA5]     │
  ├─────────────────────────────┤  ← Current MSP (during nesting)
  │   R0 (4 bytes)              │   ← Top of Stack (TOS)
  ├─────────────────────────────┤
  │   R1 (4 bytes)              │
  ├─────────────────────────────┤
  │   R2 (4 bytes)              │
  ├─────────────────────────────┤
  │   R3 (4 bytes)              │
  ├─────────────────────────────┤
  │   R12 (4 bytes)             │
  ├─────────────────────────────┤
  │   LR (4 bytes)              │
  ├─────────────────────────────┤
  │   PC (4 bytes)              │
  ├─────────────────────────────┤
  │   xPSR (4 bytes)            │   ← Bottom of Frame
  └─────────────────────────────┘
LOW ADDRESS   (0x2000 0000 — SRAM start)
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Interrupt Stack Design Mathematics

| Parameter | Formula / Equation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stack Frame Size (Cortex-M3/M4)** | $F_{hw} = 8 \times W_b$ | Bytes | Fixed hardware cost per exception entry |
| **Total Stack Memory** | $S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i + M_{margin}$ | Bytes | Reserve in linker script |
| **Stack Depth (Worst-case nesting)** | $D_{max} = N_{irq} \times F_{hw} + F_{sw}$ | Words | For estimating max nesting |
| **Stack Margin** | $M = k \times F_{hw}$, where $k = 4$ to $8$ | Bytes | Safety buffer for ISRs |
| **Stack Pointer Alignment** | $SP_{aligned} = SP \bmod 8 = 0$ | — | AAPCS compliance |
| **Effective Free Stack** | $S_{free} = SP_{initial} - SP_{current}$ | Bytes | Runtime monitoring |
| **Interrupt Latency (cycles)** | $T_{latency} = 12 + 3 \times T_{wait}$ | Clock cycles | Pushing 8 registers + vector fetch |
| **MSR to MSP write cost** | — | 4 bytes written | CONTROL register access |

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Note on $T_{latency}$:** On the Cortex-M3, the hardware stacking consumes **12 clock cycles**, and the vector fetch adds **0 to 2 cycles** depending on flash wait states. This is why deterministic interrupt latency is a hallmark of ARM Cortex-M cores.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (Engine Control Units):** Deterministic interrupt latency via fixed-stack design ensures fuel injection timing is met within microseconds.
- **Medical Devices (Pacemakers, Ventilators):** Stack-isolated exception handling prevents corruption of patient-critical tasks.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Nested interrupt stacks allow high-priority safety interrupts to pre-empt communication interrupts without data loss.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Predictable stack depth enables small SRAM devices (e.g., STM32G0 with 8KB RAM) to function reliably.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calculating Worst-Case Stack Memory Requirement

To design a reliable interrupt stack, we must calculate the **maximum stack depth** under worst-case interrupt nesting.

**Given:**
- Number of distinct IRQ vectors: $N_{irq}$
- Number of nested exception levels possible simultaneously: $L_{nest}$
- Software-pushed registers per ISR (e.g., R4-R11): $R_{sw}$ registers
- Word size: $W_b = 4$ bytes (Cortex-M)

**Step 1:** Calculate hardware-stacked frame size.

$$
F_{hw} = 8 \times W_b
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $W_b = 4$ bytes.

$$
F_{hw} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate software-pushed frame size (if ISR pushes R4-R11).

$$
F_{sw} = R_{sw} \times W_b = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate maximum nested hardware frames.

$$
S_{hw\_total} = L_{nest} \times F_{hw}
$$

**Step 5:** Total worst-case stack requirement.

$$
S_{total} = (L_{nest} \times F_{hw}) + F_{sw} + M_{margin}
$$

**Step 6:** For a 3-level nested design with $k = 8$ safety margin.

$$
S_{total} = (3 \times 32) + 32 + (8 \times 32)
$$

$$
S_{total} = 96 + 32 + 256 = 384 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{total} = 384 \text{ bytes}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a real KTU examination problem, this 384-byte value is the **minimum recommended** allocation. Real-world embedded firmware (e.g., FreeRTOS port) typically reserves **1024 bytes to 4096 bytes** to account for function calls within ISRs.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stack Pointer Arithmetic on Push

Cortex-M uses **Full-Descending Stack** — SP points to the **last used location** and decrements *before* writing.

**Given:**
- Initial SP value: $SP_0$
- Register to push: any 32-bit value
- Memory write width: 4 bytes

**Step 1:** Decrement SP by word size.

$$
SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 4
$$

**Step 2:** Write value to new SP location.

$$
M[SP_{new}] = \text{Register Value}
$$

**Step 3:** For 8-register push, repeated 8 times.

$$
SP_{final} = SP_0 - (8 \times 4) = SP_0 - 32
$$

**Step 4:** Express in general form for $N$ registers pushed.

$$
\boxed{SP_{final} = SP_{initial} - (N \times 4)}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
If $SP_{initial} = \text{0x2000 1000}$ (hex), after one full hardware-stacked frame:

$$
SP_{final} = 0x20001000 - 32 = 0x20000FE0
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Detecting Stack Overflow

A common KTU exam question is to compute the **remaining free stack** during runtime.

**Given:**
- Linker-defined stack start: $SP_{reset}$ (top of stack region)
- Current SP value: $SP_{current}$
- Stack region size: $S_{region}$

**Step 1:** Calculate used stack depth.

$$
S_{used} = SP_{reset} - SP_{current}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate free stack.

$$
S_{free} = S_{region} - S_{used}
$$

**Step 3:** Stack overflow condition.

$$
\text{Overflow} \iff SP_{current} \leq SP_{region\_bottom}
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical verification.

$$
\text{If } SP_{reset} = 0x20002000, \quad SP_{current} = 0x20001E00, \quad S_{region} = 1024
$$

$$
S_{used} = 0x20002000 - 0x20001E00 = 0x200 = 512 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{free} = 1024 - 512 = 512 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{free} = 512 \text{ bytes (50\% utilization)}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stack Variants Across Microcontroller Families

| Feature | **ARM Cortex-M3/M4** | **8051** | **AVR (ATmega)** | **PIC18F** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stack Location** | SRAM | Internal RAM (IDATA) | SRAM | Hardware Stack (31 levels) |
| **SP Width** | 32-bit | 8-bit | 16-bit | 13-bit (PC width) |
| **Auto Context Save** | Yes (hardware, 8 regs) | No (manual) | No (manual) | No (manual) |
| **Stack Direction** | Descending | Ascending | Descending | Descending (PC push) |
| **Dedicated IRQ Stack** | Optional (MSP/PSP) | No | No | No |
| **Hardware Frame Size** | 32 bytes | 0 (manual) | 0 (manual) | 0 (manual) |
| **RTOS Friendly** | Excellent | Limited | Moderate | Poor |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Cortex-M's hardware auto-stacking** is what makes it a "preferred choice for real-time systems." Older 8-bit cores require every ISR to begin with a `PUSH` of used registers and end with a `POP` — a manual process prone to bugs and asymmetric matching.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Interrupt Stack Frame Formation Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Normal Thread Execution] --> B{Interrupt Request Arrives}
    B --> C{NVIC Priority Check}
    C -->|Accepted| D[Pre-empt Current Execution]
    D --> E[Decrement SP by 32 bytes]
    E --> F[Hardware Push xPSR]
    F --> G[Hardware Push PC]
    G --> H[Hardware Push LR]
    H --> I[Hardware Push R12]
    I --> J[Hardware Push R3 R2 R1 R0]
    J --> K[Fetch ISR Vector from Vector Table]
    K --> L[Load PC with ISR Entry Point]
    L --> M[Execute ISR Body]
    M --> N{ISR has more nesting}
    N -->|Yes| O[Recursive Stack Frame Build]
    O --> M
    N -->|No| P[ISR Returns via EXC_RETURN]
    P --> Q[Hardware Pop R0 R1 R2 R3 R12 LR PC xPSR]
    Q --> R[Increment SP by 32 bytes]
    R --> S[Resume Original Thread]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Dual Stack Pointer Architecture (MSP + PSP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THREAD_MODE["THREAD MODE (User Application)"]
        CTRL[CONTROL Register Bit 1]
        CTRL -->|0| MSP_A[MSP Active]
        CTRL -->|1| PSP_A[PSP Active]
    end

    subgraph HANDLER_MODE["HANDLER MODE (ISR / Exception)"]
        MSP_B[MSP Always Active]
    end

    THREAD_MODE -->|Exception Entry| HANDLER_MODE
    HANDLER_MODE -->|Exception Exit| THREAD_MODE
    MSP_A -.Shared HW Register.- MSP_B
    PSP_A -.Separate HW Register.- HW_SP
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Stack Memory Region Allocation in Linker Script

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SRAM["SRAM (e.g., 0x20000000 to 0x20002000 - 8KB)"]
        A[0x20000000 Data Section .data]
        B[0x200000XX BSS Section .bss]
        C[0x20000YYY Heap .heap - grows up]
        D[0x20001FFF Stack Top - initial SP]
        E[0x20001E00 Interrupt Frames 32-byte frames]
        F[0x20001C00 Stack Margin 256 bytes]
        G[0x20001B00 Main Stack Usage]
        H[0x20001800 Free Stack Reserve]
    end
    A --> B --> C --> H --> G --> F --> E --> D
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Exception Entry and Exit Sequence (Detailed)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant NVIC as NVIC Controller
    participant SP as Stack Pointer MSP
    participant MEM as SRAM Memory
    participant VEC as Vector Table

    CPU->>CPU: Execute current instruction
    NVIC->>CPU: IRQ pending signal
    CPU->>CPU: Complete current instruction
    CPU->>SP: SP = SP - 32
    CPU->>MEM: MEM[SP+24] = xPSR
    CPU->>MEM: MEM[SP+20] = PC
    CPU->>MEM: MEM[SP+16] = LR
    CPU->>MEM: MEM[SP+12] = R12
    CPU->>MEM: MEM[SP+8] = R3
    CPU->>MEM: MEM[SP+4] = R2
    CPU->>MEM: MEM[SP+0] = R1
    CPU->>MEM: MEM[SP-4] = R0 (after final decrement)
    CPU->>VEC: Read ISR vector address
    VEC->>CPU: Return ISR entry point
    CPU->>CPU: Update IPSR with exception number
    CPU->>CPU: PC = ISR entry point
    CPU->>CPU: Begin ISR execution
    CPU->>CPU: ISR body runs
    CPU->>CPU: Encounter EXC_RETURN value in LR
    CPU->>MEM: Restore R0 R1 R2 R3 R12 LR PC xPSR
    CPU->>SP: SP = SP + 32
    CPU->>CPU: Resume previous execution
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define an interrupt stack. Why is the LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) ordering essential for interrupt stack operations in a microcontroller?**

**Model Answer:**

An interrupt stack is a dedicated region of SRAM memory used by the microcontroller to temporarily store the CPU's execution context (program counter, status registers, general-purpose registers) when an interrupt is serviced. In ARM Cortex-M architectures, this stack is managed by the **Main Stack Pointer (MSP)** hardware register.

**LIFO ordering is essential for three reasons:**

1. **Order of Restoration:** The most recently pushed register (e.g., `xPSR`) must be the last one restored, ensuring that the processor returns to the exact pre-interrupt state. If reversed, the CPU would restore inconsistent flag states.

2. **Stack Pointer Self-Consistency:** Since the SP points to the top of the stack, a LIFO pop leaves the SP in the correct position automatically. Non-LIFO ordering would require complex pointer recalculations, introducing race conditions.

3. **Interrupt Nesting Support:** In nested interrupts, the innermost ISR's frame is on top of the stack. LIFO ensures that innermost contexts are unwound first, correctly returning control to the outer ISR before finally returning to the main thread.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the Cortex-M's automatic hardware stacking with software `PUSH/POP` instructions. Hardware stacking is **not visible as instructions** in the disassembly — the CPU executes the exception entry as a single atomic operation.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the eight registers that are automatically stacked by the ARM Cortex-M hardware upon exception entry. Explain the significance of saving the xPSR register.**

**Model Answer:**

The eight registers automatically pushed onto the stack by Cortex-M hardware during exception entry are:

1. **xPSR** — Program Status Register
2. **PC** — Program Counter
3. **LR** — Link Register
4. **R12** — General-purpose register
5. **R3** — General-purpose register
6. **R2** — General-purpose register
7. **R1** — General-purpose register
8. **R0** — General-purpose register

**Significance of saving xPSR:**

The xPSR contains critical processor state information including the **Interrupt Program Status Register (IPSR)** field, which records the currently active exception number. By saving xPSR on the stack during exception entry, the hardware guarantees that upon `EXC_RETURN`, the original condition flags (N, Z, C, V), the execution state (Thumb/ARM), and the **ICI/IT flags** are perfectly restored. Without saving xPSR, the processor would return with potentially corrupted arithmetic flags, causing unpredictable branch behavior in the resumed main program.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the hardware-stacked frame structure of the ARM Cortex-M processor during interrupt servicing. Show the stack pointer movement and memory layout.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of the hardware-stacked frame.**

When the NVIC accepts an exception, the Cortex-M core performs an **automatic hardware stacking** of 8 registers — `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0` — onto the stack pointed to by MSP. This consumes exactly **32 bytes (8 words)** of stack memory.

**Step 2: Stack Memory Layout Diagram.**

```
ADDRESS            CONTENT
─────────────────────────────────────
0x2000 1000        ← Initial SP (before interrupt)
0x2000 0FFC        ← PUSH: xPSR
0x2000 0FF8        ← PUSH: PC
0x2000 0FF4        ← PUSH: LR
0x2000 0FF0        ← PUSH: R12
0x2000 0FEC        ← PUSH: R3
0x2000 0FE8        ← PUSH: R2
0x2000 0FE4        ← PUSH: R1
0x2000 0FE0        ← PUSH: R0   ← Final SP (after stacking)
```

**Step 3: Stack Pointer Movement Arithmetic.**

Initial SP: `SP₀ = 0x20001000`

After 8-word hardware stacking:

$$
SP_{final} = SP_0 - (8 \times 4) = 0x20001000 - 0x20 = 0x20000FE0
$$

The SP moves **downward by 32 bytes**, confirming the **Full-Descending** stack convention.

**Step 4: Why Full-Descending?**

- SP points to the last *used* location.
- Decrementing before writing prevents accidental overwrites if an interrupt occurs mid-stacking (atomic operation).

**Step 5: Software Additional Context Save.**

If the ISR uses additional registers (R4–R11), the ISR prologue must manually push them:

```
ISR_Entry:
    PUSH {R4-R11}        ; Software frame (32 bytes more)
    ; ISR body...
    POP {R4-R11}
    BX LR                ; Triggers hardware unstacking
```

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

The hardware-stacked frame of 32 bytes is a deterministic, low-latency (12-cycle) mechanism that makes Cortex-M ideal for hard real-time embedded systems.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Design a basic interrupt stack for an ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller with the following specifications:**
- **SRAM size:** 16 KB (0x4000 bytes)
- **Number of distinct IRQ vectors:** 25
- **Maximum nesting depth:** 4 levels
- **Each ISR uses R4-R11 manually**
- **Safety margin:** 8 hardware frames

**Compute the total stack memory requirement and justify your allocation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Hardware frame size.**

$$
F_{hw} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Software frame size per ISR (R4-R11 = 8 registers).**

$$
F_{sw} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3: Total per nested level (HW + SW).**

$$
F_{level} = F_{hw} + F_{sw} = 32 + 32 = 64 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4: Worst-case nested hardware stacking depth.**

$$
S_{hw\_max} = L_{nest} \times F_{hw} = 4 \times 32 = 128 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5: Worst-case nested software frame depth.**

$$
S_{sw\_max} = L_{nest} \times F_{sw} = 4 \times 32 = 128 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 6: Safety margin.**

$$
M = 8 \times F_{hw} = 8 \times 32 = 256 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 7: Total stack requirement.**

$$
S_{total} = S_{hw\_max} + S_{sw\_max} + M
$$

$$
S_{total} = 128 + 128 + 256 = 512 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{total} = 512 \text{ bytes (3.125\% of SRAM)}}
$$

**Step 8: Linker Script Allocation.**

| Section | Start Address | Size |
|---|---|---|
| `.data` | `0x20000000` | 1 KB |
| `.bss` | `0x20000400` | 6 KB |
| `.heap` | `0x20001C00` | 4 KB |
| **Stack (MSP)** | **`0x20002C00`** | **512 bytes** |
| Stack Top (Reset SP) | `0x20002E00` | — |

**Step 9: Justification.**

The 512-byte allocation safely accommodates **4-level nested interrupts** with full hardware + software context saving, plus an **8-frame safety margin** for unforeseen deep call chains within ISRs. This is well within the 16 KB SRAM budget, leaving ample space for application data and heap.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget to add the **software frame ($F_{sw}$)** when ISRs use high registers (R4–R11). In Cortex-M, R0–R3 and R12 are caller-saved and **already on the hardware stack** — but R4–R11 are callee-saved and **must be manually pushed**. Missing this leads to a 50% under-allocation and catastrophic stack overflows.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Compare and contrast the interrupt stack designs of ARM Cortex-M3 and the legacy 8051 microcontroller. Highlight at least four architectural differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Aspect | **ARM Cortex-M3** | **8051 Microcontroller** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Stack Memory Location** | Located in main SRAM (large, configurable) | Located in internal 128-byte IDATA RAM (limited) |
| **2. Auto Context Saving** | Yes — hardware automatically stacks 8 registers | No — programmer must manually `PUSH` every used register |
| **3. Stack Pointer Width** | 32-bit SP (R13), accesses full 4 GB address space | 8-bit SP, limited to 256-byte internal RAM |
| **4. Stack Growth Direction** | Full-descending (SP decrements on PUSH) | Ascending (SP increments on PUSH) |
| **5. Interrupt Nesting** | Native hardware support via NVIC priority | Limited — requires manual priority enable registers |
| **6. Determinism** | Deterministic 12-cycle latency | Variable, depends on ISR length |
| **7. Interrupt Vector Table** | Relocatable, configurable priority bits | Fixed at 0x0000, hardware-prioritized |
| **8. Stack Frame Size** | Fixed 32 bytes per exception entry | Variable, depends on `PUSH` count |

**Conclusion:** The Cortex-M3's hardware-assisted stacking provides superior determinism and ease of programming, making it preferred for modern real-time embedded systems over the legacy 8051 architecture.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the concept of dual stack pointers (MSP and PSP) in ARM Cortex-M. When would an RTOS use PSP instead of MSP?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of MSP and PSP.**

ARM Cortex-M provides **two physical stack pointer registers** — both mapped to R13 but banked:
- **MSP (Main Stack Pointer):** Reset default; used by **Handler Mode** (all exceptions/ISRs) and optionally Thread Mode.
- **PSP (Process Stack Pointer):** Used exclusively in **Thread Mode** when the `CONTROL[1]` bit is set.

**Step 2: Selection Logic via CONTROL Register.**

The `CONTROL` register's bit 1 (SPSEL) selects the active SP in Thread Mode:
- `CONTROL[1] = 0` → MSP active in Thread Mode
- `CONTROL[1] = 1` → PSP active in Thread Mode

In Handler Mode, **MSP is always active**, regardless of SPSEL.

**Step 3: Memory Layout Diagram.**

```
THREAD MODE (User Task)
  └── Uses PSP  →  Dedicated task stack in SRAM

HANDLER MODE (ISRs)
  └── Uses MSP  →  Kernel/OS stack in SRAM

  ┌──────────────────────────────┐
  │   MSP Stack (Kernel)         │  ← One per system
  │   - Interrupt frames         │
  │   - OS kernel calls          │
  ├──────────────────────────────┤
  │   PSP Stack (Task 1)         │  ← One per task
  │   - Task 1 context           │
  ├──────────────────────────────┤
  │   PSP Stack (Task 2)         │  ← One per task
  │   - Task 2 context           │
  └──────────────────────────────┘
```

**Step 4: Why an RTOS uses PSP.**

A **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** like FreeRTOS uses PSP for several critical reasons:

1. **Stack Isolation:** Each user task has its own dedicated PSP stack region. A stack overflow in one task does not corrupt the kernel stack (MSP), improving system robustness.

2. **Memory Efficiency:** Task stacks can be sized independently based on the task's call depth and local variables, optimizing total SRAM usage.

3. **Simpler Context Switching:** During a context switch, the OS only needs to save/restore PSP and the task's R4–R11 registers; the hardware frame remains managed by MSP.

4. **Privilege Separation:** The kernel (using MSP) is protected from user task (using PSP) stack corruption, enabling memory protection unit (MPU) integration.

**Step 5: Context Switch Operation.**

```
OS Tick Interrupt:
  1. NVIC enters Handler Mode (MSP active)
  2. Hardware stacks xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3-R0 onto MSP
  3. OS saves current task's PSP and R4-R11
  4. OS loads next task's PSP and R4-R11
  5. EXC_RETURN triggers hardware unstack from MSP
  6. Task resumes with its own PSP intact
```

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

The dual SP design in Cortex-M is fundamental to RTOS portability and reliability, providing hardware-enforced separation between kernel and application stack spaces.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- ✅ **Cortex-M hardware stack frame size = 32 bytes (8 words)** — a fixed constant.
- ✅ **Stack growth direction = Full-Descending** (SP decrements *before* write).
- ✅ **MSP is mandatory in Handler Mode**; PSP is optional in Thread Mode only.
- ✅ **Saved registers order:** `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0`.
- ✅ **Restored (pop) order:** `R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR` (exact reverse).
- ✅ **Stack pointer alignment** must be **8-byte aligned** at all times (AAPCS).
- ✅ **Interrupt latency on Cortex-M3 = 12 cycles** for stacking + vector fetch.
- ✅ **R4–R11 must be manually saved** by ISR if used; R0–R3 + R12 are auto-saved.
- ✅ **RTOS uses PSP per task** to isolate stacks and protect kernel MSP.
- ✅ **Stack overflow detection:** Use a **canary pattern (0xA5A5A5A5)** filled at initialization.
- ✅ **Linker-defined `__StackTop`** symbol sets the reset value of MSP.
- ✅ **Total stack budget formula:** $S_{total} = (N_{nest} \times 32) + F_{sw} + M$.
- ✅ **8051 has 8-bit SP**; Cortex-M has **32-bit SP** — a fundamental architectural difference.
- ✅ **`EXC_RETURN` magic value** in LR triggers automatic hardware unstack.

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute Examiner Warning:** In KTU valuation, students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** by forgetting to mention that the hardware stacking is **automatic and atomic** (not visible in instruction stream). Always explicitly state this in your answer for full marks.
